### [1] [ICG: Improving Cover Image Generation via MLLM-based Prompting and Personalized Preference Alignment](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.27374)
*Zhipeng Bian,Jieming Zhu,Qijiong Liu,Wang Lin,Guohao Cai,Zhaocheng Du,Jiacheng Sun,Zhou Zhao,Zhenhua Dong*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: They propose ICG, an end-to-end framework that links multimodal large language models with diffusion models to generate personalized, high-quality cover images without needing labeled data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Personalized cover images are important for user engagement and recommendation quality on digital platforms, but current AI systems underexploit this, often relying on handcrafted prompts and disconnected modules, and lacking good supervision signals for personalization.

Method: ICG uses a multimodal LLM to extract semantic features from item titles and reference images via meta tokens, then refines these with user embeddings to form a personalized context that is injected into a diffusion model through an adapter. Training uses multi-reward learning: public aesthetic and semantic relevance rewards plus a learned personalized preference model derived from user behavior, allowing end-to-end optimization without ground-truth labels.

Result: Experiments show that ICG improves image quality, semantic alignment with the content, and personalization, and that these gains translate into stronger user appeal and better offline recommendation accuracy compared to prior pipelines.

Conclusion: ICG serves as a plug-and-play adapter that effectively bridges MLLMs and diffusion models, works with standard checkpoints, and can optimize personalized cover generation without explicit labeled supervision, making it practical for real-world recommendation and content platforms.

Abstract: Recent advances in multimodal large language models (MLLMs) and diffusion models (DMs) have opened new possibilities for AI-generated content. Yet, personalized cover image generation remains underexplored, despite its critical role in boosting user engagement on digital platforms. We propose ICG, a novel framework that integrates MLLM-based prompting with personalized preference alignment to generate high-quality, contextually relevant covers. ICG extracts semantic features from item titles and reference images via meta tokens, refines them with user embeddings, and injects the resulting personalized context into the diffusion model. To address the lack of labeled supervision, we adopt a multi-reward learning strategy that combines public aesthetic and relevance rewards with a personalized preference model trained from user behavior. Unlike prior pipelines relying on handcrafted prompts and disjointed modules, ICG employs an adapter to bridge MLLMs and diffusion models for end-to-end training. Experiments demonstrate that ICG significantly improves image quality, semantic fidelity, and personalization, leading to stronger user appeal and offline recommendation accuracy in downstream tasks. As a plug-and-play adapter bridging MLLMs and diffusion models, ICG is compatible with common checkpoints and requires no ground-truth labels during optimization.

</details>


### [2] [LCO: LLM-based Constraint Optimization for Safer Agentic LLMs in Real-world Tasks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.27375)
*Jiayong Wan,Jiawei Chen,Zhaoxia Yin,Liu Shuyuan,Hang Su*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: They introduce LLM-based Constraint Optimization (LCO), a framework that reduces in-context reward hacking by having LLMs explicitly reason about safety constraints and using evolutionary sampling to keep actions within a safe yet performant solution space, showing substantial reductions in toxicity and harmful optimization without hurting task performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: As LLMs are increasingly used as autonomous agents that continuously interact with their environments, they tend to over-optimize proxy objectives (like click-through or engagement) in-context, leading to in-context reward hacking (ICRH) that can cause harmful side effects (e.g., more toxic content) even without adversarial prompts. Existing safety defenses focus on adversarial inputs or static alignment and thus do not adequately address this dynamic, self-driven over-optimization problem. The paper is motivated by the need for a practical, training-free method to constrain such behavior while retaining high task performance.

Method: They propose LLM-based Constraint Optimization (LCO), a training-free framework composed of two main modules: (1) a self-thought module that prompts the LLM to proactively deliberate about and integrate potential safety constraints into its plan before executing actions; and (2) an evolutionary sampling module that uses the LLM itself to carry out crossover and mutation over candidate solutions, iteratively generating and selecting variants that both satisfy safety constraints and maintain or improve task performance. This process effectively constrains the search space of the LLM’s in-context optimization to a safer region without changing the model’s weights.

Result: In experiments on two settings—output-refine and policy-refine—the LCO framework significantly mitigates in-context reward hacking. On a tweet engagement optimization task using GPT-4, LCO reduces the Toxicity Growth Rate (TGR) by 39%, indicating much lower increases in toxicity while still optimizing for engagement. On a separate policy optimization benchmark, LCO reduces the ICRH Occurrence Rate by 15.23%, again without degrading the underlying task performance metrics. These results demonstrate that LCO can curb harmful side effects of in-context optimization while preserving effectiveness.

Conclusion: The paper concludes that LLM-based Constraint Optimization (LCO) is an effective, fine-tuning-free approach to mitigating in-context reward hacking in LLM agents. By combining explicit self-reflection on safety constraints with an evolutionary sampling procedure that keeps optimization within a safe solution space, LCO substantially improves safety outcomes such as toxicity and harmful policy behaviors, without sacrificing task performance. The work suggests that structured, prompt-level control and sampling strategies can be a powerful complement to traditional alignment and training-based safety methods for LLM agents.

Abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) are increasingly acting as autonomous agents, but their continuous interaction with the environment can lead to in-context reward hacking (ICRH), a phenomenon where LLMs iteratively optimize their behavior to maximize proxy objectives, inadvertently producing harmful side effects. Existing defense methods are insufficient to address this risk, as ICRH arises not from adversarial inputs but from the model's own over-optimization. To mitigate this issue, we propose \textbf{LLM-based Constraint Optimization (LCO)}, a framework that effectively reduces ICRH without model fine-tuning. LCO consists of two modules: \textit{self-thought module}, which guides the LLM to proactively deliberate and integrate potential safety constraints before execution; and \textit{evolutionary sampling module}, which employs LLM-based crossover and mutation to constrain the model's actions within a safe solution space while maintaining task performance. Experimental results demonstrate that LCO substantially alleviates ICRH in both output-refine and policy-refine scenarios. In particular, on the tweet engagement optimization task, LCO achieves a 39% reduction in the Toxicity Growth Rate (TGR) on GPT-4, while on the policy optimization benchmark, it reduces the ICRH Occurrence Rate by 15.23%, demonstrating safety improvement without sacrificing task performance.

</details>


### [3] [Unlocking Fine-Grained and Within-Utterance Speaking Style Control in Prompt-Based Text-to-Speech Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.27376)
*Jaehoon Kang,Yejin Lee,Yoonji Park,Kyuhong Shim*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: This paper improves prompt-based text-to-speech (TTS) by enabling smooth, controllable style changes across and within utterances using embedding-space interpolation and decoder attention/KV-cache manipulation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing prompt-based TTS models can control speaking style with natural language prompts but only at a coarse, global level for an entire utterance. They lack fine-grained, continuous control over style attributes (e.g., gender, pitch, speaking rate) across utterances and cannot handle time-varying style transitions within a single utterance, which are important for practical applications like expressive narration or dialogue. The paper aims to remove these limitations.

Method: 1) Inter-utterance style interpolation: The authors compute direction vectors in the prompt embedding space between contrastive style prompts (e.g., male↔female, slow↔fast) and perform simple linear interpolation along these directions to gradually change style attributes between utterances. 2) Intra-utterance style transition: They analyze the autoregressive TTS decoder and find it has a strong attention bias toward early text tokens, causing the beginning of the utterance to dominate the rest. To counter this, they introduce two mechanisms: KV-cache swapping (replacing past key–value states to reflect new style prompts during generation) and sliding-window attention masking (restricting attention to a moving local window over tokens) so that style can change mid-utterance.

Result: For inter-utterance interpolation, the system obtains 99–100% success in gender conversion, allows pitch changes up to 36 Hz, and modifies speaking rate by up to 1.6 syllables per second, with smooth transitions. For intra-utterance transitions, it preserves speaker similarity in the range 0.81–0.91 (indicating the voice identity is largely maintained) and achieves high perceptual smoothness scores between 3.48 and 4.48 (on a subjective scale), suggesting that within-utterance style changes are both natural and continuous.

Conclusion: The proposed techniques extend existing prompt-based TTS with both inter-utterance and intra-utterance fine-grained style control, without needing to redesign or retrain the base model. Embedding-space direction interpolation enables smooth, continuous style changes across utterances, while KV-cache swapping and sliding-window attention overcome decoder attention bias to support natural, time-varying style transitions within a single utterance. These methods yield strong objective and subjective results, making prompt-based TTS more practical for expressive and dynamic speech generation.

Abstract: While prompt-based text-to-speech (TTS) models enable natural language-driven speaking style control, they often provide limited fine-grained control and apply a single global style across an utterance. This restricts practical use cases that require continuous style attribute interpolation across utterances and time-varying style transitions within a single utterance. In this paper, we propose novel techniques to achieve both capabilities in existing prompt-based TTS models. For inter-utterance style interpolation, we compute direction vectors between contrastive style prompts in the embedding space and perform simple interpolation, enabling smooth transitions between style characteristics. For intra-utterance style transition, we first identify a strong attention bias toward early tokens in autoregressive TTS decoders, causing the initial audio realization to dominate subsequent generation. To mitigate this effect, we introduce KV-cache swapping and sliding-window attention masking. Experiments demonstrate that our proposed inter-utterance interpolation achieves a 99-100% success rate in gender conversion, up to 36 Hz pitch variation, and up to 1.6 syllables-per-second speed change. Our intra-utterance transition maintains a speaker similarity of 0.81-0.91 and achieves perceptual smoothness scores of 3.48-4.48.

</details>


### [4] [RAG-Coding: Enhancing LLM Medical Coding with Structured External Knowledge](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.27377)
*Yidong Gan,David D. Nguyen,Yang Lin,Peter Zhong,Thanh Vu,Long Duong,Yuan-Fang Li*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: RAG-Coding is an agent-based, retrieval-augmented LLM system that automates ICD-10-CM coding by grounding decisions in official coding resources, achieving better performance than LLM baselines and comparable F1 to the SOTA PLM-ICD, and releases an updated, guideline-aligned MDACE-2025 dataset.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: ICD-10-CM coding is complex, requires adherence to detailed official guidelines, and existing automated methods either lack access to up-to-date external coding knowledge or do not fully leverage LLMs’ reasoning abilities, leading to suboptimal accuracy and clinical compliance. There is also a need for datasets that reflect the latest ICD-10-CM guideline revisions to ensure that models are evaluated against current clinical practice.

Method: The paper proposes RAG-Coding, an agentic retrieval-augmented generation framework that coordinates four LLM-based agents. These agents retrieve, cross-reference, and ground their coding decisions in external knowledge sources such as the official ICD-10-CM tabular list and guidelines. The system is evaluated on the MDACE dataset across multiple LLM backbones, with ablation studies to quantify the contribution of each component and the use of external knowledge. Additionally, the authors construct MDACE-2025 by re-annotating the existing dataset using expert coders and the latest 2025 ICD-10-CM guidelines, including more fine-grained labels.

Result: On the MDACE dataset, RAG-Coding improves over the best LLM-based baseline by 8–13% in micro-F1 and 2–8% in macro-F1 across different LLM backbones. When compared with the state-of-the-art PLM-ICD model, RAG-Coding attains substantially higher micro recall (+11%), while PLM-ICD maintains higher micro precision (+6%), resulting in similar overall micro- and macro-F1 scores. Ablation studies show incremental performance gains from each added component, underscoring the impact of incorporating external knowledge sources.

Conclusion: Grounding LLM-based ICD-10-CM coding in structured external knowledge through an agentic RAG framework significantly enhances coding performance and clinical guideline compliance, especially in terms of recall, relative to standard LLM baselines. While precision–recall tradeoffs remain when compared to specialized PLM models like PLM-ICD, the approach is competitive overall and more adaptable. The release of MDACE-2025 provides a more fine-grained, guideline-current benchmark that supports future research on clinically valid, up-to-date automated coding systems.

Abstract: We present RAG-Coding, an agentic method for automated ICD-10-CM coding. RAG-Coding orchestrates four large language model (LLM) agents and grounds their coding decisions in external knowledge sources (e.g. the official coding tabular list and guidelines). By retrieving and cross-referencing relevant knowledge in these sources, the agents enhance coding accuracy and ensure clinical compliance. On the MDACE dataset, RAG-Coding outperforms the best LLM-based baseline by 8-13\% in micro-F1 and 2-8\% in macro-F1 across multiple LLM backbones. Compared to the state-of-the-art pretrained language model method, PLM-ICD, RAG-Coding exhibits higher micro recall (+11\%), while PLM-ICD exhibits higher micro precision (+6\%), yielding comparable micro- and macro-F1. Ablations show stepwise gains, highlighting the importance of incorporating external knowledge. We also release MDACE-2025, updating the original dataset with expert re-annotations with the latest 2025 ICD-10-CM guidelines. This update features more fine-grained code labels and enables evaluation against current clinical standards.

</details>


### [5] [OralAgent: Integrating Reasoning, Tools, and Knowledge for Interactive Dental Image Analysis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.27378)
*Jing Hao,Siyuan Dai,Yongxin Zhang,Yuci Liang,Jiamin Wu,Jiahao Bao,Yuxuan Fan,Zanting Ye,Yanpeng Sun,Xinyu Zhang,Ming Hu,Liang Zhan,James Kit Hon Tsoi,Linlin Shen,Junjun He,Kuo Feng Hung*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: Introduces OralAgent, an end-to-end dental AI agent that unifies multimodal clinical reasoning, tool use, and knowledge-grounded retrieval, achieving state-of-the-art performance on multiple dental benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing dental AI models are narrow: they are focused on single tasks or single imaging modalities and work in isolation, which does not match complex, real-world dental clinical workflows that require multimodal reasoning, multi-step decisions, and integration of domain knowledge. The authors want a more holistic, practical system that can autonomously assist diagnosis and treatment planning across diverse oral healthcare scenarios.

Method: Design an AI agent, OralAgent, that orchestrates multimodal reasoning, planning, and tool-based decision-making within an automated framework. It integrates 22 specialized visual analysis tools to process diverse dental images and connects to a large corpus of dental knowledge (368 textbooks) via retrieval-augmented generation. The system can autonomously plan multi-step workflows: interpret images, query knowledge, decide which tools to call, and combine outputs. They also build OralCorpus, a large bilingual domain corpus to support RAG, and OralQA-ZH, a Chinese multiple-choice benchmark to test multidisciplinary dental knowledge. The model is evaluated on several benchmarks for image analysis and knowledge QA.

Result: OralAgent achieves state-of-the-art performance on MMOral-Uni, MMOral-OPG (dental imaging benchmarks), and OralQA-ZH (knowledge QA benchmark), demonstrating superior accuracy over existing methods. The experiments show it can effectively utilize tools and retrieved knowledge, yielding strong performance, better interpretability (through its reasoning traces and tool calls), and good adaptability to varied dental tasks and imaging modalities.

Conclusion: A unified dental AI agent that combines multimodal analysis, tool use, and knowledge retrieval is both feasible and beneficial. OralAgent outperforms prior task-specific models on multiple benchmarks and better reflects real-world clinical workflows. The supporting resources—OralCorpus and OralQA-ZH—provide valuable infrastructure for future research on retrieval-augmented and knowledge-intensive dental AI. The work suggests that agentic, tool-augmented architectures can significantly advance practical AI assistance in oral healthcare.

Abstract: Dental image analysis plays a pivotal role in supporting accurate diagnosis and treatment planning in oral healthcare. Although recent advances have produced dental AI models for specific tasks and individual imaging modalities, their isolated designs limit practical use in real-world clinical workflows. In this paper, we present OralAgent, the first dental-specialized AI agent that unifies multimodal reasoning, tool-based decision-making, and knowledge-grounded retrieval within an end-to-end automated framework. It integrates 22 visual analysis tools and 368 widely-used classical dental textbooks, enabling autonomous reasoning, planning, tool use, knowledge retrieval, and multi-step workflow execution. Furthermore, we introduce OralCorpus, a large-scale, high-quality bilingual textual resource containing 134.8M tokens curated for dental retrieval-augmented generation (RAG). To evaluate models' multidisciplinary dental knowledge, we construct OralQA-ZH, a Chinese multiple-choice question benchmark consisting of 798 items across eleven oral subspecialties. Extensive experiments demonstrate that OralAgent achieves state-of-the-art performance on the MMOral-Uni, MMOral-OPG, and OralQA-ZH benchmarks, highlighting its effectiveness, interpretability, and adaptability in real-world clinical settings. The code and models are publicly available at https://github.com/isjinghao/OralAgent.

</details>


### [6] [BioELX: Cross-lingual Biomedical Entity Linking via Alias-based Retrieval and LLM Ranking](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.27380)
*Yi Wang,Corina Dima,Liangyu Zhong,Steffen Staab*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper presents BioELX, a training-free (no task-specific supervision) two-stage cross-lingual biomedical entity linking framework that substantially improves performance, especially for low-resource languages, by enhancing multilingual candidate retrieval and using an LLM-based ranker for context-aware disambiguation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Cross-lingual biomedical entity linking is crucial for clinical and biomedical NLP but suffers from a lack of expert-annotated training data, particularly in low-resource languages. Existing methods often depend on SapBERT trained mostly on English aliases, which limits generalization to non-English mentions and hampers context-aware disambiguation. The authors aim to build a system that works well across languages, especially low-resource ones, without requiring costly task-specific annotation.

Method: BioELX is a two-stage framework. Stage 1 improves candidate retrieval by retraining/enriching a SapBERT-based retriever with multilingual aliases extracted from Wikidata, thereby better covering non-English surface forms. Stage 2 performs context-aware candidate ranking and disambiguation with a pre-trained large language model used as a ranker, which jointly encodes the mention context and each candidate entity description/alias, and is applied without any supervised fine-tuning on labeled BEL data. The combination yields better cross-lingual retrieval and more accurate disambiguation without task-specific annotation.

Result: On five benchmarks (XL-BEL, EMEA, Patent, WikiMed-DE, and MedMentions), BioELX achieves new state-of-the-art performance. On the XL-BEL benchmark, it improves average Recall@1 by 19.2 points overall, with particularly large gains for low-resource languages such as Turkish (+21.6), Korean (+22.1), and Thai (+30.8). It also provides consistent performance gains on the EMEA (+6.2 Recall@1), Patent (+5.4), and WikiMed-DE (+12.8) datasets compared to prior methods.

Conclusion: The study concludes that high-performance cross-lingual biomedical entity linking is possible without task-specific annotated corpora by (1) augmenting SapBERT training with Wikidata-based multilingual aliases for better cross-lingual candidate retrieval and (2) leveraging a pre-trained LLM as a context-aware, zero-shot ranker. This approach particularly benefits low-resource languages and establishes a new state of the art across multiple biomedical EL benchmarks, indicating a promising direction for low-supervision, multilingual biomedical NLP systems.

Abstract: Cross-lingual biomedical entity linking (BEL) maps mentions in any language to unique identifiers in a biomedical knowledge base (KB), supporting clinical and biomedical NLP applications. However, expert-annotated training data for BEL are costly, especially for low-resource languages. Moreover, many cross-lingual BEL systems rely on SapBERT-based retrievers trained on predominantly English aliases in the KB, leading to poor generalization to unseen non-English mentions and limited context-aware disambiguation. We propose BioELX, a two-stage cross-lingual BEL framework that requires no task-specific annotated training corpora. In Stage~1, we enrich SapBERT training with Wikidata-derived multilingual aliases and use the resulting retriever to improve cross-lingual candidate retrieval. In Stage~2, we perform context-aware disambiguation with a pre-trained LLM ranker that jointly considers the mention context and candidate, eliminating the need for supervised training. Experiments on five benchmarks (XL-BEL, EMEA, Patent, WikiMed-DE, and MedMentions) show that BioELX achieves new state-of-the-art performance. It improves average Recall@1 on XL-BEL by +19.2, with especially large gains for low-resource languages, e.g., +21.6 on Turkish, +22.1 on Korean, +30.8 on Thai, and delivers consistent improvements on EMEA (+6.2), Patent (+5.4), and WikiMed-DE (+12.8). Code and resources will be released upon publication.

</details>


### [7] [Bridging the Stability-Expressivity Gap: Synthetic Data Scaling and Preference Alignment for Low-Resource Spoken Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.27383)
*Yizhong Geng,Yanliang Li,Jinghan Yang,Tianhan Jiang,Boxun An,Ya Li,Xiaoyu Shen*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper analyzes a trade-off in spoken language models for low-resource languages, where synthetic data improves phonetic stability but harms prosodic expressivity, and proposes two self-alignment methods (DGSA and TDSC) to restore expressivity and stability, achieving state-of-the-art performance and zero-shot voice cloning for Lao.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Spoken Language Models avoid explicit grapheme-to-phoneme conversion and are promising for speech synthesis, but low-resource languages suffer from a lack of transcribed speech. Practitioners compensate using synthetic data, which provides phonetic supervision. However, this synthetic dependence may hide fundamental limitations, especially regarding naturalness and expressiveness of speech. The paper is motivated by understanding and overcoming this limitation in order to make SLMs more effective for low-resource languages.

Method: The authors first identify and characterize a Stability-Expressivity Gap caused by synthetic training data: increasing synthetic data improves phonetic stability but degrades prosodic richness, leading to "Synthetic Erosion". To address this, they propose two self-alignment frameworks: (1) Disentanglement-Guided Self-Alignment (DGSA), which leverages a separation between prosody and timbre representations to re-align the model in a way that preserves expressivity while maintaining phonetic stability; and (2) Temperature-Driven Self-Critique (TDSC), which, for extremely low-resource settings, explores generations at different sampling temperatures and automatically filters them to keep stable yet expressive outputs, essentially using the model’s own generations as curated training signals.

Result: Using DGSA and TDSC, the models achieve improved phonetic accuracy and prosodic expressivity compared to baselines, mitigating the Stability-Expressivity Gap. The approach surpasses strong commercial speech synthesis systems, such as ElevenLabs and Gemini Pro, in relevant benchmarks. Additionally, it demonstrates the first zero-shot voice cloning capability for the Lao language, indicating strong generalization in a very low-resource scenario.

Conclusion: Reliance on synthetic data in SLM training for low-resource languages introduces a fundamental trade-off between stability and expressivity, leading to expressive collapse if unaddressed. By introducing self-alignment frameworks—DGSA for prosody-timbre disentanglement and TDSC for temperature-based self-critique—the paper shows it is possible to maintain or even enhance expressivity while preserving phonetic stability. These techniques not only close the Stability-Expressivity Gap but also set new performance levels against commercial systems and enable impactful applications like zero-shot voice cloning for previously unsupported languages such as Lao.

Abstract: Spoken Language Models (SLMs) have emerged as a promising paradigm for speech synthesis by bypassing explicit grapheme-to-phoneme pipelines. However, their effectiveness in low-resource languages remains fundamentally limited by the scarcity of transcribed speech. In practice, synthetic data has become the primary strategy for scaling SLMs in such settings, providing reliable phonetic supervision when real data is insufficient. In this work, we show that this reliance introduces a fundamental trade-off, which we term the Stability-Expressivity Gap: while synthetic data improves phonetic accuracy, it progressively suppresses prosodic variability, ultimately leading to a collapse of expressivity (Synthetic Erosion). To bridge this gap, we propose two self-alignment frameworks. Disentanglement-Guided Self-Alignment (DGSA) recovers expressivity for complex languages by exploiting prosody-timbre separation. For regimes where authentic references are exceptionally limited, Temperature-Driven Self-Critique (TDSC) stabilizes generation through automated exploration and filtering. Our approach outperforms strong commercial systems, including ElevenLabs and Gemini Pro, and enables the first zero-shot voice cloning capability for Lao.

</details>


### [8] [From AR to Diffusion: Efficiently Adapting Large Language Models with Strictly Causal and Elastic Horizons](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.27387)
*Xiangyu Ma,Teng Xiao,Zuchao Li,Lefei Zhang*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces FLUID, a framework to adapt autoregressive (AR) language models to diffusion-based text generation without retraining from scratch, achieving SOTA performance with far lower training cost.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Diffusion-based text generators can generate tokens in parallel but typically require bidirectional attention and pretraining from scratch, which is expensive and prevents reuse of strong GPT-style autoregressive priors. The motivation is to enable efficient parallel text generation while leveraging existing AR checkpoints.

Method: They propose FLUID, which enforces Strictly Causal Alignment so that diffusion models can share the same causal structure as GPT-style AR models, allowing direct initialization from AR checkpoints. Additionally, they introduce Elastic Horizons, an entropy-aware mechanism that adjusts the denoising step size (stride) dynamically based on local information density instead of a fixed diffusion schedule.

Result: Experiments show that FLUID reaches state-of-the-art results among diffusion-based text generation methods while cutting training costs by orders of magnitude, demonstrating that AR foundations can be effectively combined with efficient parallel generation.

Conclusion: FLUID successfully reconciles autoregressive priors with diffusion-based parallel generation via strictly causal alignment and adaptive denoising schedules, enabling SOTA performance with dramatically reduced pretraining requirements.

Abstract: Diffusion models promise efficient parallel text generation but rely on bidirectional attention, creating a structural mismatch with pre-trained Autoregressive (AR) models. This incompatibility precludes reusing robust AR priors, necessitating prohibitive pre-training from scratch. To bridge this gap, we propose FLUID, a framework that efficiently adapts AR backbones to the diffusion paradigm. By enforcing Strictly Causal Alignment, FLUID enables seamless initialization from standard GPT-style checkpoints, circumventing the need for massive pre-training. Furthermore, we introduce Elastic Horizons, an entropy-driven mechanism that dynamically modulates denoising strides based on local information density rather than fixed schedules. Experiments demonstrate that FLUID achieves state-of-the-art performance while reducing training costs by orders of magnitude, effectively reconciling established AR foundations with efficient parallel generation. Our code is available at https://github.com/Oli-lab-nun/FLUID/tree/main.

</details>


### [9] [Modeling Community Attitude through Reaction Tone: A Human-AI Collaborative Framework for Evaluating LLM Alignment with Linguistic Behaviors in Online Communities](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.27388)
*Nuan Wen,Xuezhe Ma*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: Introduces CARE, a framework to evaluate how well LLMs simulate community reactions to real-world news and finds a persistent gap between LLM outputs and real communities’ discourse.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing evaluations of LLMs in social analysis reduce social identity to fixed labels and ignore how communities dynamically react to real events, raising doubts about whether LLM-based simulations can capture rich, context-sensitive community behavior.

Method: Propose CARE (Community-Aware Reaction Evaluation), which collects real online community reactions to news events and compares them to LLM-simulated discourse. It defines a fine-grained set of illocutionary tones and underlying attitudes, validated through human-AI collaboration, and uses these to benchmark and diagnose model behavior.

Result: CARE reveals a consistent ‘realism gap’: merely prompting LLMs with explicit community labels or descriptions does not substantially improve how realistically they simulate community reactions. The analysis also shows differing behavioral signatures across frontier models, evidencing that they respond in systematically different ways when simulating communities.

Conclusion: Current LLM alignment and prompting strategies are inadequate for faithfully modeling the sociolinguistic dynamics and context-dependent reactions of online communities; more sophisticated methods are needed to close the realism gap identified by CARE.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly utilized as proxies for computational social analysis; yet, their ability to faithfully represent the "thick descriptions" (Geertz, 1973) of human communities remains a critical challenge. Current evaluations often reduce social identity to static labels, sidelining how real-world groups navigate social shifts. To bridge this gap, we introduce CARE (Community-Aware Reaction Evaluation), a reaction-centered framework that benchmarks LLM-simulated discourse against the authentic, event-contingent responses of distinct communities to real-world news. By characterizing a fine-grained spectrum of illocutionary tones and the underlying attitudes they manifest--validated through human-AI collaboration--our diagnosis reveals a persistent "realism gap": steering LLMs with explicit community prompts fails to inherently improve simulation fidelity. Analysis further identifies divergent behavioral signatures among frontier models, suggesting that current alignment strategies remain insufficient for capturing the sociolinguistic dynamics of online groups.

</details>


### [10] [EvoSpec: Evolving Speculative Decoding via Real-Time Vocabulary and Parameter AdaptationTarget](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.27390)
*Shuyu Zhang,Lingfeng Pan,Qicheng Wang,Yaqi Shi,Yueyang Tan,Ruyu Yan,Jiaqi Chen,Lixing Du,Lu Wang*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: EvoSpec improves speculative decoding for LLMs by dynamically adapting vocabulary and parameters, maintaining high acceptance rates and speeding up inference in specialized domains.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Static pruning of the output projection layer reduces computational cost in speculative decoding but fails in specialized or topic-shifting settings because it cannot track dynamic shifts in token distributions, leading to low acceptance rates and reduced speedups.

Method: EvoSpec dynamically evolves the draft model via two main components: (1) context-aware retrieval of important long-tail tokens using semantic and statistical indexing to adapt the vocabulary in real time; and (2) a lightweight online alignment strategy with curriculum learning to continually reduce the distribution gap between draft and target models during inference.

Result: In evaluations on specialized domains such as coding, law, and medicine, EvoSpec outperforms static baselines. On the EAGLE-3 benchmark, it provides a 1.13x speedup over the state-of-the-art static method FR-Spec while using 27% less memory than standard online adaptation approaches.

Conclusion: Dynamic, context-aware evolution of the draft model in speculative decoding mitigates the limitations of static pruning, yielding higher efficiency and lower memory cost in specialized-domain inference without sacrificing acceptance rate.

Abstract: Speculative decoding accelerates Large Language Model inference via a draft-then-verify paradigm, yet the output projection layer becomes a bottleneck as vocabulary sizes scale. While existing static pruning methods effectively reduce this overhead, they suffer from precipitous drops in acceptance rate in specialized domains or topic-switching scenarios due to their inability to capture dynamic distribution shifts. To address this, we introduce EvoSpec, a framework that enables real-time evolution of the draft model through dynamic vocabulary and parameter adaptation. Unlike static or purely retrieval-based approaches, EvoSpec employs a context-aware mechanism that retrieves critical long-tail tokens via efficient semantic and statistical indexing. Furthermore, we propose a lightweight online alignment strategy utilizing curriculum learning to continually minimize the distributional gap between the draft and target models. Extensive evaluations across specialized domains (coding, law, and medicine) confirm that EvoSpec overcomes the limitations of static baselines. On EAGLE-3, it achieves a 1.13x speedup in these settings over the state-of-the-art static baseline FR-Spec, with 27\% lower memory overhead than standard online adaptation.

</details>


### [11] [StoryMI: Steerable Multi-Agent Therapeutic Dialogue Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.27393)
*Qingyu Meng,Min Chen,Dingming Liu,Yifan Mo,Yue Su,Xin Sun,Koen Hindriks,Jiahuan Pei*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: StoryMI is a multi-agent LLM framework that generates clinically plausible, MI-adherent psychotherapy dialogues using situational stories and controllable MI strategies, evaluated with MI-specific and lexical metrics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing LLM-based dialogue systems for motivational interviewing lack rich situational grounding, fine-grained and dynamic control over counseling strategies, and evaluation aligned with clinical MI standards, limiting their realism, adherence to MI, and clinical usefulness.

Method: The authors design StoryMI, a framework with multiple specialized LLM agents: one expands questionnaire-based client profiles into narrative situational stories; therapist and client agents generate utterances labeled with MI codes; an interaction agent selects MI codes and orchestrates multi-turn exchanges to control macro-level counseling strategies. They also define a two-level evaluation protocol combining lexical metrics, MI-specific macro-strategy measures, and LLM-as-judge plus human expert assessments, then simulate 6K MI dialogues from 1K questionnaire-story pairs across 12 MI codes and 13 symptom domains and benchmark six LLMs.

Result: Using StoryMI to provide situational grounding plus macro-level strategic control leads to higher MI adherence and more clinically plausible dialogues across the benchmarked LLMs, as shown by MI-specific metrics and expert/LLM judgments on the 6K-sample simulated dialogue dataset.

Conclusion: A structured, multi-agent LLM workflow that grounds conversations in detailed situational stories and explicitly controls MI strategies can more faithfully emulate motivational interviewing, producing psychotherapy dialogues with better MI adherence and clinical plausibility; the released code and data support further research and benchmarking.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) can generate fluent dialogue, but prior works lack situational grounding, dynamic strategy control, and evaluation aligned with clinical standards in motivational interviewing (MI). We introduce StoryMI, a multi-LLM agent framework for controllable MI dialogue generation, where questionnaire-based client profiles are expanded into situational stories that provide narrative context for the dialogue. Therapist and client agents generate MI-coded utterances guided by MI codes selected by the interaction agent, while an interaction agent dynamically coordinates exchanges to control MI strategies during a multi-turn conversation. We propose a two-level evaluation protocol: lexical metrics and MI-specific measures of macro-level counseling strategies, alongside LLM-as-judge and human expert assessments. We construct a dataset of 6K simulated MI dialogues grounded in 1K questionnaire-story pairs, covering 12 MI codes and 13 symptom domains, and benchmark six open- and closed-source LLMs. Our results show that situational grounding and macro-level control can improve MI adherence and clinical plausibility, demonstrating the effectiveness of a structured multi-agent workflow for psychotherapy dialogue generation. We provide code and data for reproducibility.

</details>


### [12] [Debate Helps Weak Judges Reward Stronger Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.27483)
*Ethan Elasky,Frank Nakasako,Naman Goyal*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: Study of when AI debate protocols improve oversight vs consultancy baselines on code/logic tasks with verifiable answers.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Debate has been proposed as a scalable oversight method for powerful AI systems, but empirical results are mixed, especially when judges lack hidden information or are weaker than the debaters. The authors want to understand under what conditions debate actually helps and why it sometimes gives null or negative effects.

Method: Run proposer–critic debate experiments on programmatically verifiable code and logic tasks with model pairings of stronger debaters and weaker judges. Compare a debate protocol to a consultancy baseline, measure critic and judge classification ability, vary how judges treat critic messages (as claims to verify vs testimony), and ablate rebuttal rounds to test their contribution.

Result: Debate improves judge performance relative to consultancy only when the critic has strictly higher classification ability than the judge and when the judge interprets critic statements as verifiable claims rather than as testimony. In the model pairings where this holds (three of five), gains are statistically significant and correspond to the most capable pairings. In the two pairings where critic and judge abilities are similar, debate yields null effects and even reduces judge verification behavior. Removing rebuttal rounds does not significantly change judge performance, indicating that a single independent critique provides most of the benefit.

Conclusion: Effective, training-free scalable oversight via debate in verifiable domains requires a competence gap in favor of the critic and a judge that verifies critic claims. A simpler “answer–critique–judge” primitive captures most of debate’s benefits at lower cost, and a pre-deployment audit checking whether the critic outperforms the judge and whether the judge actually verifies critic claims can predict when debate will be helpful.

Abstract: Despite theoretical promise, debate as a scalable oversight protocol has produced mixed empirical results: gains in some settings, and null effects in others, especially when the judge does not have information hidden from it. We study proposer-critic debate in a stronger-debater/weaker-judge setting on programmatically verifiable code and logic tasks. Debate helps the judge over a consultancy baseline when the critic provides a usable advantage: the critic's classification ability must exceed the judge's, and the judge must treat critic speeches as claims to verify rather than testimony to summarize.
  On the three of five pairings where the condition holds, proposer-critic debate's gains are statistically significant over consultancy, and these pairings are the most capable model pairings. On the two non-responder pairings in our set, debate produces null effects, and judge verification rates drop by tens of percentage points once a critic enters the transcript. In these cases the critic's binary-classification ability and the judge's are within noise of each other, and the critic's disagreement is parsed as testimony rather than a claim to check.
  Ablating rebuttal rounds from debate produces no measurable change in judge performance: a single independent critique recovers the bulk of debate's benefit at lower inference cost. These findings suggest a cheaper primitive for training-free scalable oversight in verifiable domains (answer, critique, judge) and a pre-deployment audit (does the critic beat the judge, and will the judge verify it?) that predicts when debate will help.

</details>


### [13] [PAST2HARM: A Simple Adaptive Past Tense Attack for Jailbreaking Multimodal AI](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.27545)
*Snehasis Mukhopadhyay*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper presents PAST2HARM, an adaptive jailbreak framework that exploits past-tense reformulations to bypass safety safeguards in multimodal text-to-image models, achieving high attack success and revealing serious weaknesses in current defenses.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Multimodal text-to-image AI systems can generate more impactful harm than text-only systems, yet jailbreak attacks and defenses for them are underexplored and immature. Prior work shows past-tense phrasing can evade safeguards, suggesting a systematic vulnerability that needs to be characterized, quantified, and used to stress-test safety mechanisms across leading models.

Method: The authors design PAST2HARM, a black-box, gradient-free jailbreak framework that reformulates harmful prompts into past-tense, historically anchored narratives. The framework has two key dimensions: (1) breadth via temporal deepening, where prompts are incrementally reframed with stronger historical and archival context to erode refusal boundaries; and (2) depth via iterative escalation after a model initially complies, progressively pushing for more harmful content. They introduce a scalar severity metric scored by an LLM judge to quantify harmfulness per turn and analyze how vulnerability evolves over the dialogue. The framework is tested on three state-of-the-art multimodal models (Gemini Nano Banana Pro, GPT Image 2, and SD XL).

Result: PAST2HARM attains high attack success rates—83% on Gemini Nano Banana Pro, 67% on GPT Image 2, and 100% on SD XL—despite operating in a black-box setting. Adversarial prompts show strong transferability, with cross-model success above 50%. Analysis reveals that mid-conversation turns are the most vulnerable: harmfulness rises, then plateaus, and finally inverts semantically as safety mechanisms reassert. The attacks produce a wide range of harmful imagery categories. The authors also compile and release a benchmark dataset of prompts, reformulations, and outputs for future red-teaming and alignment research.

Conclusion: The study demonstrates that current multimodal safety training and refusal mechanisms are fundamentally brittle to adaptive, temporally framed past-tense jailbreaks. Mid-conversation interactions constitute critical vulnerability windows. The authors argue that stronger, more robust multimodal safety training, improved conversational defenses, and better evaluation tools are urgently needed, and they provide a benchmark resource to support continued red-teaming and alignment work.

Abstract: Jailbreak attacks on multimodal AI systems remain underexplored, even though unsafe image generation can have more severe consequences than unsafe text and current defenses are relatively immature. We introduce PAST2HARM, a simple yet effective adaptive jailbreak framework that bypasses refusal training in state of the art multimodal text to image models. Building on prior findings that past tense reformulations can evade safeguards, PAST2HARM systematically exploits this vulnerability in multimodal generative AI.
  We characterize the attack along two dimensions. First, breadth: through temporal deepening, the framework incrementally strengthens historical anchoring and archival cues, eroding refusal boundaries across models with varying alignment strength. Second, depth: via iterative escalation after initial compliance, we probe the upper bound of harmful generation, measuring severity using a scalar severity jailbreak metric evaluated by a language model acting as a judge. We find that mid conversation turns form peak vulnerability windows, where harmfulness increases before plateauing and eventually undergoing semantic inversion.
  We evaluate PAST2HARM on three models Gemini Nano Banana Pro, GPT Image 2, and SD XL achieving attack success rates of 83 percent, 67 percent, and 100 percent in a black box, gradient free setting. Adversarial prompts also transfer across models, with cross model success rates above 50 percent. The attack elicits diverse harmful outputs, including explicit sexual content, political disinformation, historical denial narratives, hate speech, and self harm glorification. We further release a curated benchmark of prompts, reformulations, and outputs as a resource for red teaming and alignment. Our results expose fundamental brittleness in current safeguards and highlight the need for stronger multimodal safety training.

</details>


### [14] [The Future of Facts: Tracing the Factual Generation-Verification Gap](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.27564)
*Tim R. Davidson,Anja Surina,Caglar Gulcehre*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper studies why language models are better at checking facts than producing them (the generation–verification gap) and how this gap develops during training.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Language models are increasingly used as interfaces to factual knowledge, but they more reliably verify given answers than generate correct ones. This gap powers many recent techniques (self-correction, debate, etc.), yet its specific behavior for factual knowledge and during different training phases is not well understood. The authors aim to clarify how this factual generation–verification gap arises, evolves, and behaves under continued training and model updates.

Method: The authors empirically track and compare generation vs. verification performance on factual questions for four open-source model families, each at two parameter scales. They analyze models across three training regimes: initial knowledge acquisition, continual learning, and post-hoc updating. For each regime, they design benchmarks where the model must either (a) freely generate an answer or (b) verify/assess candidate answers. They then measure accuracy, robustness to new training, and behaviors after targeted factual updates. They also run natural experiments on larger frontier models, examining how similar patterns appear at scale and probing biases in verification for well-covered facts.

Result: Across model families and sizes, three consistent findings emerge: (1) During training, models learn to verify candidate factual answers correctly earlier than they learn to generate those answers on their own. (2) Under continual learning, verification capabilities remain accurate and stable, while generation performance degrades more easily or lags. (3) When models are updated with new factual information, they may enter a “multi-verse” state where they can verify both the old and the new answer as correct depending on context, suggesting partial, overlapping representations of conflicting facts. Experiments on large frontier models show similar patterns and reveal lingering verification biases in favor of widely seen or well-covered facts.

Conclusion: The paper concludes that the factual generation–verification gap is a stable and systematic property of language model training, not just a computational quirk or prompt artifact. Verification is acquired earlier, is more robust under continual learning, and can persist in conflict with updated knowledge, creating multi-verse states. These findings help explain why verification-based strategies (self-checking, critique, debate) are effective and suggest that training and update methods should explicitly account for the asymmetric development and robustness of generation vs. verification abilities in factual domains.

Abstract: Language models are becoming the default interface to factual knowledge, yet they often verify outputs more reliably than they generate them. This generation-verification gap (GV-gap) underlies many recent advances in self-improvement and reasoning, but its dynamics on factual knowledge specifically remain poorly understood. We focus on the training mechanisms underlying factual GV-gaps, distinguishing them from their computational and aesthetic counterparts. We trace generation and verification capabilities through three training phases (acquisition, continual learning, and updating) across four open-source model families at two scales each. Three findings recur across models: (i) verification is consistently learned before generation; (ii) verification is more robust to continual learning than generation; and (iii) factual updates can leave models in a "multi-verse" state, simultaneously verifying both old and new answers as correct. Natural experiments on frontier models reproduce these dynamics at scale and reveal residual verification biases on well-covered facts.

</details>


### [15] [Can Hallucinations Be Useful? Solving Multi-Hop Questions With SLMs By Chaining System-I/II Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.27596)
*Saptarshi Sengupta,Suhang Wang*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper proposes an “answer first, reason later” framework for small language models (SLMs), where the model gives a fast initial answer and then selectively performs deeper, retrieval-augmented reasoning only when needed, improving multi-step QA performance over traditional “think first” methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Small Language Models are attractive due to speed and low hardware requirements, but they hallucinate more than LLMs, making them unreliable for complex multi-step reasoning where early errors propagate. Existing methods mitigate hallucination by forcing the model to think in detail before answering, but this may be unnecessary or inefficient if the model’s initial answer is already reliable or if its hallucinations can be harnessed productively. The paper aims to exploit SLMs’ initial responses more effectively to achieve better reasoning with less overhead.

Method: The authors introduce a cognitively-inspired two-system framework: (1) System-I: the SLM first produces a quick, zero-shot answer to the question, effectively an initial hypothesis; (2) System-II: conditioned on this hypothesis, the system performs deeper, slower reasoning by retrieving external evidence from a knowledge source and refining or verifying the answer. This inverts the standard think-first-then-answer pipeline into an answer-first-reason-later process. The framework selectively invokes System-II based on the initial answer’s confidence and uses the retrieved evidence to correct or reinforce the initial guess.

Result: Across several multi-step question-answering benchmarks, the proposed answer-first framework yields higher accuracy than prior methods that enforce think-first reasoning and iterative retrieval before answering. The results show that SLMs’ initial confident answers are often correct, and that starting from these hypotheses plus targeted retrieval can reduce hallucination impact and improve final performance.

Conclusion: The study concludes that forcing SLMs to always think extensively before answering is not optimal. Instead, leveraging their fast, reasonably calibrated initial answers and then performing targeted, retrieval-augmented reasoning leads to better multi-step QA performance. Hallucinations are not purely detrimental; they can serve as useful hypotheses that guide effective evidence retrieval. The cognitively-inspired combination of rapid System-I guesses and deliberate System-II reasoning provides a more efficient and accurate approach for using SLMs on complex reasoning tasks.

Abstract: Recently, there has been increased interest in Small Language Models (SLMs), which are fast, show good performance, and have lower hardware demands than large language models (LLMs). However, SLMs hallucinate more frequently than LLMs, impacting their ability to solve complex multi-step reasoning problems as early mistakes cascade to the final response. To address this, existing works think-first followed by iterative retrieval to reduce hallucination. We argue that the think-first strategy is not always necessary as we find that: (i) SLMs are often accurately confident in their initial answer and, (ii) hallucinations can actually be beneficial for honing in on the true answer. As such, we position our work as an inversion of this strategy, i.e., answer first-reason later. We propose a cognitively-inspired framework where the model is first allowed to quickly answer the question (System-I (zero-shot)) and then resorts to deeper thinking (System-II) based on evidence retrieved from a knowledge source using the initial hypothesis. By combining System-I and System-II style thinking, we show that our method can outperform prior work that takes the traditional think-first route on various multi-step question-answering benchmarks.

</details>


### [16] [Simorgh at SemEval-2026 task 7: Region-Aware Hybrid Retrieval for Low-Resource Cultural Reasoning in Multilingual Question Answering](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.27636)
*Hadi Bayrami Asl Tekanlou,Mahdi Bakhtiyarzadeh,Jafar Razmara*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper evaluates culturally grounded multiple-choice QA across 30 languages and shows that a region-aware hybrid retrieval method improves LLM cross-lingual stability but still suffers from data imbalance across languages.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLMs perform well on general reasoning tasks but struggle with culturally grounded knowledge, especially in low-resource languages with limited digital/textual data. There is a need to systematically evaluate and improve LLM performance on culture-specific questions across many languages and socio-cultural domains.

Method: The authors use the BLEnD benchmark, a multilingual multiple-choice QA corpus spanning 30 languages and socio-cultural domains (cuisine, sports, family, etc.). They design a region-aware hybrid retrieval system that combines BM25 lexical retrieval with dense semantic retrieval, weighted by regional heuristics. Retrieved passages are transformed into a structured prompt for the Qwen3-14B quantized model, and answers are chosen deterministically via logit-based selection instead of open-ended decoding. They compare this retrieval-augmented setup against pure parametric inference of the LLM.

Result: The hybrid region-aware retrieval-augmented approach improves performance and cross-lingual stability on culturally grounded QA compared with using the LLM without retrieval. Nonetheless, performance disparities persist: high-resource languages still significantly outperform low-resource ones, indicating uneven gains from retrieval augmentation.

Conclusion: Retrieval augmentation with a region-aware hybrid retriever can enhance cross-lingual cultural QA performance and stability, but it does not fully compensate for underlying training data imbalances across languages. More fundamental solutions are needed to close the performance gap for low-resource languages and culturally grounded knowledge.

Abstract: Although Large Language Models (LLMs) demonstrate excellent capabilities and performance for general reasoning tasks within the general public domain, they may face challenges with culturally grounded knowledge within languages with limited digital and textual data. In this paper, we investigate culturally grounded multiple-choice question answering with the BLEnD benchmark, which consists of a multilingual corpus of 30 languages and covers various socio-cultural domains, such as cuisine, sports, family, etc. We propose a region-aware hybrid retrieval approach that combines BM25 lexical matching and dense semantic similarity with regional weighting heuristics to improve the relevance of the answer. The retrieved documents are used to construct a structured prompt for the Qwen3-14B quantized model with logit-based deterministic answer selection. The experimental results show improvements to cross-lingual stability with the hybrid retrieval approach over pure parametric inference for culturally grounded question answering. However, there are still notable performance gaps between languages with more and less training data. This shows that the limitations of the retrieval augmentation approach are not entirely overcome by the training data imbalance problem.

</details>


### [17] [Learning to Translate from Soft to Hard LLM Prompts](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.27642)
*Pitipat Kongsomjit,Suryansh Goyal,Jacob Whitehill*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper improves interpretability and performance of soft prompt tuning by learning a dedicated natural-language translator that converts soft prompts into high-quality text prompts, which can then be used across models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Soft prompt tuning is parameter-efficient but hard to interpret and reuse, and existing automatic soft-prompt-to-text methods (e.g., InSPEcT) are training-free and limited in quality. The authors want a better way to convert learned soft prompts into understandable, portable natural language prompts that preserve or improve task performance.

Method: They train a specialized natural language translation model that takes soft prompts as input and outputs natural language verbalizations. They evaluate this model on multiple Datasets of Datasets (DoDs), performing both quantitative and qualitative comparisons against prior training-free translation approaches like InSPEcT.

Result: Their trained translator generates more fluent and accurate natural language prompts than existing methods, and these translated prompts improve task performance, sometimes even surpassing few-shot learning when used with larger, closed-API LLMs. The translated prompts also in some cases outperform the original soft prompts when transferred to these larger models.

Conclusion: Optimizing a dedicated translator from soft prompts to text improves both interpretability and downstream performance. Soft prompts learned on small open-source models can be converted into portable natural language prompts that work even better on larger closed models, suggesting a practical, interpretable, and model-agnostic way to transfer task-specific knowledge.

Abstract: Soft prompt tuning is a parameter-efficient method for adapting LLMs to specific tasks, but suffers from a lack of interpretability. Building on recent work on interpreting soft prompts (Ramati et al., 2024), we explore how training a dedicated soft prompt to natural language translation model can yield higher translation quality. In particular, in both quantitative and qualitative comparisons on multiple Datasets of Datasets (DoDs), we demonstrate that our translator produces fluent, accurate verbalizations that outperforms existing training-free methods like InSPEcT. In addition to advancing interpretability, our work suggests a promising downstream application: soft prompts optimized on small, open-source models can be translated into portable text prompts that, when deployed on larger closed-API models, exceed the performance of the original soft prompt and, in some cases, even few-shot learning.

</details>


### [18] [Disentangling Language Roles in Multilingual LLM Task Execution](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.27649)
*Qishi Zhan,Minxuan Hu,Seoyeon Jang,Lei Zhao,Ziheng Chen,Man Liang,Xinyue Xiang,Jiaxin Liu,Guansu Wang,Liang He*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces MTM-Bench, a benchmark that systematically tests multilingual LLMs when instruction, content, and response languages differ, revealing that performance depends most on the response language role rather than just the number of language mismatches.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand and evaluate how multilingual LLMs handle realistic scenarios where the language of instructions, input content, and required output differ, which existing benchmarks do not fully or cleanly isolate in a controlled, fully crossed way.

Method: They construct MTM-Bench, a benchmark over English, Spanish, and Chinese that enumerates all 27 possible language triplets (instruction, content, response). They design 2,430 instances per model across specific task families: semantic reversal, final-state extraction, and language purity with update realization. They then evaluate 20 frontier and open-weight LLMs using decomposed metrics (semantic correctness, target-language adherence, constraint satisfaction, contamination ratio, and joint success) and validate the automated scoring with a targeted human audit.

Result: The study finds that performance degradation is structured by the role a language plays (instruction, content, or response) rather than simply the number of mismatches. The response-language role is the main driver of variation, and even a single mismatch in the response language causes most of the degradation. Comparisons between response-only and full-mismatch setups show that more mismatches do not always mean greater difficulty, and model rankings differ across conditions. Different task families expose different failure channels, indicating that looking only at semantic correctness misses important aspects of multilingual robustness.

Conclusion: MTM-Bench reveals that multilingual LLM robustness is heavily determined by the response language role and that mismatch count is not a simple predictor of difficulty. Reliable multilingual task execution requires evaluation beyond semantic correctness, using decomposed metrics that account for language roles and different failure modes. The benchmark provides a controlled framework to better diagnose and compare multilingual capabilities of LLMs.

Abstract: Multilingual LLMs are increasingly used when instruction, source content, and required response languages do not coincide. Existing benchmarks have expanded multilingual instruction-following evaluation, but they rarely isolate these three roles within a fully crossed design. We introduce MTM-Bench, a controlled benchmark for language-conditioned task execution in which each instance is defined by a triplet \((L_{\text{instr}}, L_{\text{content}}, L_{\text{resp}})\). Across English, Spanish, and Chinese, MTM-Bench enumerates all 27 triplets and contains 2{,}430 instances per model across semantic reversal, final-state extraction, and language purity with update realization. We evaluate 20 frontier and open-weight LLMs using decomposed metrics for semantic correctness, target-language adherence, constraint satisfaction, contamination ratio, and joint success, with scoring validated by a targeted human audit. The fully crossed design reveals that degradation is organized by the role a language occupies in the task structure, not merely by mismatch count. The response-language role is the dominant axis of variation, and a single response-slot mismatch accounts for most degradation. The response-only and full-mismatch comparison suggests that mismatch count is not a monotonic predictor of difficulty, with model-level ordering varying across systems. Task families fail through distinct channels, showing that semantic correctness alone does not capture reliable multilingual task execution.

</details>


### [19] [Cultural Fidelity in English-to-Hindi Translation: A Preservation-Fluency Frontier for Gender Recoverability](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.27654)
*Samyak Savi,Chavi Gupta,Shreyas Gantayet,Tanay Sodha,Dhruv Kumar*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper examines how English-to-Hindi generative translation systems handle explicitly gendered cues, finding that they often neutralize gender and proposing two reranking interventions to better preserve gender, trading off fluency for fidelity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand and improve how generative translation systems handle culturally significant gender cues when translating from English to Hindi, particularly because current systems often erase explicit gender information through specific syntactic constructions.

Method: The authors construct a 37,345-instance benchmark across twelve categories where English sources explicitly encode gender. They evaluate five English-to-Hindi systems on whether they preserve recoverable gender cues. They then design and test two inference-time reranking strategies: Source-Aware Reranker (SAR), which prefers outputs avoiding gender-neutralizing syntax, and Phenomenon-Aware Reranker (PAR), which enforces gender preservation via targeted lexical choices even if ergative syntax is retained. Performance is assessed through automatic metrics (target-subset accuracy) and human evaluations of gender preservation and fluency.

Result: Existing systems frequently neutralize gender in English-to-Hindi translation using ergative and honorific constructions. Applying PAR substantially increases gender preservation accuracy for GPT-4o-mini (from 11.07% to 54.47%) and Sarvam (from 15.99% to 49.66%). Human evaluation shows gender preservation rising from 10.3% to 81.3% with PAR, but with a drop in mean fluency scores from 4.36 to 3.37. SAR and PAR reveal a tradeoff surface between gender fidelity and fluency rather than a single optimal solution.

Conclusion: Culturally meaningful aspects of language, like gender, are often lost in current English-to-Hindi generative translation systems due to grammatical conventions. Mechanism-aware, inference-time reranking (SAR and PAR) can recover much of this lost information, but at a cost to fluency and stylistic naturalness. Effective culturally situated generation thus requires explicit, principled tradeoffs among fidelity to source cues, grammatical fluency, and cultural or stylistic expectations, rather than relying on default system behavior.

Abstract: Generative translation systems are cultural technologies because they decide how socially meaningful cues are rendered within culturally specific grammatical systems. We study one concrete notion of successful cultural translation: when an English source explicitly encodes gender, an English-to-Hindi translation should preserve the recoverability of that cue unless the source itself is ambiguous. We evaluate this criterion on a 37,345-instance benchmark spanning twelve categories and show that five systems frequently erase gender through ergative and honorific constructions. We then introduce two mechanism-aware inference-time interventions. The first, the Source-Aware Reranker (SAR), prefers candidates that avoid gender-neutralizing syntax. The second, the Phenomenon-Aware Reranker (PAR), preserves gender through targeted lexical marking even when ergative syntax remains. Across GPT-4o-mini and Sarvam, PAR improves target-subset accuracy from 11.07% to 54.47% and from 15.99% to 49.66%, respectively. Human evaluation shows that PAR increases gender preservation from 10.3% to 81.3%, but reduces mean fluency from 4.36 to 3.37. These findings place the two interventions on a preservation and fluency frontier rather than supporting a single dominant solution, and show how culturally situated generation can require explicit tradeoffs among fidelity, fluency, and stylistic naturalness.

</details>


### [20] [TRACES: Proactive Safety Auditing for Multi-Turn LLM Agents via Trajectory-State Modeling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.27690)
*Jiaqian Li,Yanshu Li,Boxuan Zhang,Ruixiang Tang,Kuan-Hao Huang*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: TRACES is a proactive, representation-based auditor that predicts safety risks during an LLM agent’s multi-step trajectory using hidden states, enabling earlier and more accurate detection of unsafe behavior.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing safety checks focus on final outputs, but LLM agents act over many steps with tools and environments, where risks may appear early and remain invisible in the final answer. Post-hoc, reactive auditing misses opportunities to intervene while unsafe behavior is developing. There is a need for a method that monitors the unfolding internal process and predicts risk proactively without requiring costly, ambiguous step-level labels.

Method: They introduce TRACES, a proactive auditor that observes an LLM agent’s hidden representations at each step (prefix-level trajectory states). TRACES learns latent mechanism features from these step representations and models their temporal dynamics to estimate whether the ongoing partial trajectory is drifting toward unsafe behavior. It is trained using weak supervision only at the full-trajectory level (safe/unsafe labels) but is designed to output dense, per-prefix risk scores, bypassing the need for explicit step-level annotations.

Result: On multiple agent safety benchmarks, TRACES achieves better performance than baselines at predicting whether an entire trajectory is safe or unsafe and is more effective at discriminating risk early in the trajectory, i.e., proactively. This demonstrates that representation-based, prefix-level risk estimation can outperform traditional reactive safety checks that only inspect final outputs.

Conclusion: Proactive, representation-based auditing via TRACES can detect and quantify safety risk as an agent’s trajectory unfolds, rather than only at the end. The learned risk states are not only useful for auditing but can also inform training of safer agents, indicating that such proactive mechanisms are a promising direction for improving long-horizon agent safety.

Abstract: LLM agents increasingly operate through multi-turn tool use and environment interaction, where safety risks often emerge from intermediate steps long before they surface in the final outcome. Reactive auditing is therefore insufficient: post-hoc diagnosis frequently misses the chance to flag risks while they are unfolding. We propose TRACES, a representation-based proactive auditor that learns prefix-level trajectory risk states from the hidden representations of an observer LLM. TRACES induces latent mechanism features from step representations and models their temporal evolution to estimate whether a partial trajectory is drifting toward unsafe behavior. To sidestep the cost and ambiguity of step-level risk annotation, TRACES is trained with weak trajectory-level supervision while still producing dense prefix-level risk estimates. Across multiple agent safety benchmarks, TRACES improves both full-trajectory safety prediction and proactive risk discrimination. Our analyses further suggest that these risk states can help train a safer agent, highlighting the broader potential of proactive auditing for long-horizon agent safety.

</details>


### [21] [Chain-based Adaptive Reconfiguration Over Lattices for Hallucination Reduction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.27706)
*Joan Vendrell Gallart,Solmaz Kia,Russell Bent,Michael Grosskopf*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: CAROL is a probabilistic, Markov-chain-based framework that reduces hallucinations in large language models by iteratively refining answers to be semantically consistent with a trusted context, yielding provably near-optimal, more reliable outputs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Large language models often hallucinate—produce fluent but incorrect content—especially when only token-level uncertainty or likelihood is used to judge reliability. Existing mitigation techniques (e.g., likelihood thresholds, simple self-consistency checks, or retrieval augmentation) lack a principled, semantic uncertainty measure and often separate hallucination detection from mitigation. The paper aims to provide a unified, theoretically grounded framework that measures semantic uncertainty relative to a trusted context and actively steers generation away from hallucinations at test time while remaining computationally efficient.

Method: The authors propose CAROL (Chain-based Adaptive Reconfiguration Over Lattices), which models candidate responses as nodes in a lattice of textual sequences. They define a semantic uncertainty score based on how consistent a generated response is with a trusted context (e.g., documents, tools, or prior turns), rather than on raw token probabilities. This induces a string-submodular objective over the response space. Using this objective, CAROL performs a Markov chain accept-reject process: the system proposes edits or alternative continuations, accepts changes that improve semantic consistency according to the submodular objective, and rejects those that don’t, yielding an iterative refinement procedure. Theoretical analysis provides convergence and near-optimality guarantees for the resulting chain-based optimization. Implementation supports question answering and multi-agent reasoning tasks, comparing CAROL against likelihood-based decoding and retrieval-augmented baselines.

Result: Across question answering and multi-agent reasoning benchmarks, CAROL substantially reduces hallucination rates while improving overall reliability and interpretability of model outputs. It outperforms likelihood-based and retrieval-augmented methods in terms of hallucination reduction and semantic correctness, while offering competitive runtime and computational cost. Qualitative analyses indicate that CAROL’s iterative refinement steps are interpretable and trackable as the system moves responses toward higher semantic consistency with the trusted context.

Conclusion: CAROL provides a unified, semantically grounded framework for both detecting and mitigating hallucinations in large language models. By defining a semantic uncertainty measure and casting hallucination mitigation as a string-submodular optimization solved via a Markov chain accept-reject process, it offers provable convergence and near-optimality guarantees and yields more reliable, interpretable outputs at test time. The approach shows practical gains on QA and reasoning tasks without large computational overhead, suggesting a promising direction for principled test-time control of hallucinations in LLMs.

Abstract: We introduce CAROL (Chain-based Adaptive Reconfiguration Over Lattices), a probabilistic framework for test-time hallucination reduction in large language models. Rather than relying on token-level uncertainty, CAROL defines a semantic uncertainty measure based on the consistency between generated responses and a trusted context, inducing a string-submodular objective over a lattice of textual sequences. This formulation enables hallucination mitigation to be cast as a Markov chain accept-reject process with provable convergence and near-optimality guarantees, allowing the model to iteratively refine outputs toward semantic consistency. By operating at the level of meaning, CAROL unifies hallucination detection and mitigation within a single framework. Empirical results on question answering and multi-agent reasoning benchmarks show that CAROL significantly reduces hallucinations and improves reliability and interpretability compared to likelihood-based and retrieval-augmented baselines, while maintaining competitive computational efficiency.

</details>


### [22] [ReverseMath: Answer Inversion for Scalable and Verifiable Mathematical Problem Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.27709)
*Raoyuan Zhao,Yihong Liu,Yupei Du,Hinrich Schütze,Michael A. Hedderich*

Main category: cs.CL
