Surprisingly, these deceptive patterns remain 100\% in low-resource languages, revealing fundamental limitations in current CoT-based oversight. Our results reveal that CoT monitoring is fundamentally fragile under linguistic distribution shift, providing a substantially weaker safety signal than what English-only studies suggest. These findings underscore an urgent need to develop robust CoT monitors and to accelerate research into white-box monitoring techniques, especially to improve CoT monitorability in mid- and low-resource languages. Our code is available \href{https://multilingual-cot-monitoring.github.io/}{\textcolor{blue}{here}}.

</details>


### [42] [AI Research Agents Narrow Scientific Exploration](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.27905)
*Yixuan Tang,Yi Yang*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper empirically evaluates how AI research agents generate scientific ideas and finds that they tend to stay close to existing literature, concentrating on local elaboration rather than expanding into novel, diverse research directions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand whether AI research agents genuinely broaden the space of scientific exploration or merely intensify work around existing topics, as these systems are increasingly used for idea generation and early-stage research planning.

Method: The authors treat AI research agents as scientific search systems. Using four AI-agent frameworks and six LLMs, they generate 37,802 AI-created research ideas, all seeded from common starting literature within citation-defined subfields of AI/ML. They then compare these ideas to (1) human-authored papers in the same areas, (2) later human follow-on work that cites the same seed papers, and (3) the seed literature itself, analyzing concentration, novelty, distance from prior work, and citation impact.

Result: AI-generated ideas are more topically concentrated than human-authored work, stay closer to the seed literature compared with subsequent human follow-on papers, resemble human papers that later receive fewer citations, and—when they differ from prior work—do so mainly via recombinations of existing methods rather than proposing fundamentally new research questions.

Conclusion: Current AI research agents mainly facilitate local elaboration around existing research rather than driving broader, more exploratory scientific innovation. They are currently better for deepening and recombining known directions than for opening up genuinely new areas of inquiry.

Abstract: AI research agents can now generate research ideas, design experiments, run code, and draft papers, raising the possibility of large-scale AI-assisted scientific discovery. Many current agent frameworks explicitly encourage the generation of novel and high-impact ideas. Yet it remains unclear whether AI-assisted ideation broadens scientific exploration or mainly concentrates around existing work. We study AI research agents as scientific search systems. Using four AI research-agent frameworks and six large language models, we generate 37,802 scientific ideas from shared seed literature across citation-defined research areas in AI and machine learning. We then compare the resulting AI ideas against human-authored papers from the same research areas, follow-on human research emerging from the same seed literature, and the seed literature itself. Across experiments, four consistent patterns emerge. First, AI-generated ideas are substantially more concentrated than human-authored papers from the same research areas. Second, AI-generated ideas remain much closer to their starting literature than later human follow-on work does. Third, papers most similar to AI-generated ideas tend to receive lower subsequent citations. Fourth, when AI-generated ideas differ from prior work, the differences arise primarily from recombining existing technical methods rather than introducing fundamentally new research questions. Overall, current AI research agents appear better suited to local elaboration than to broadening scientific exploration.

</details>


### [43] [ESC-Skills: Discovering and Self-Evolving Skills for Emotional Support Conversations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.27908)
*Jie Zhu,Huaixia Dou,Shuo Jiang,Junhui Li,Lifan Guo,Feng Chen,Chi Zhang,Fang Kong*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces ESC-Skills, a framework that models emotional support conversations as reusable, interpretable skills and iteratively refines them through simulation, leading to better, more controllable emotional support dialogue systems.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current emotional support conversation systems are mostly end-to-end and opaque: they directly generate responses or use only coarse strategies, which makes it hard to interpret their behavior, improve specific skills, or ensure safety and consistency across different user types. The authors want a structured, skill-centric approach that can analyze what works or fails in emotional support and support systematic improvement and control.

Method: 1) Represent fine-grained support interactions as Intervention Units (IUs), each linking a seeker’s state, a support intervention (system action), and the emotional outcome. 2) Extract IUs from both successful and failed ESC dialogues and aggregate them into an ESC-Skills Bank that encodes for each skill: guidance on how to intervene, when it is applicable, expected emotional effects, and potential risks. 3) Use a multi-profile self-evolution framework where an ESC agent interacts with diverse simulated seeker profiles and is evaluated under the SAGE protocol. 4) Analyze these simulated interactions to detect missing skills, unsafe behaviors, and profile-specific failures. 5) Refine and expand the Skills Bank using simulation-based verification, iterating until the skills are more robust and comprehensive.

Result: Experiments show that the ESC-Skills framework leads to higher-quality responses at the turn level and better emotional trajectories at the dialogue level compared to baselines. It also makes the system’s behavior more interpretable and controllable, as the responses can be traced back to explicit skills with known conditions, expected outcomes, and risks.

Conclusion: A skill-centric, IU-based and self-evolving framework can significantly improve emotional support dialogue systems over purely end-to-end approaches, yielding better emotional outcomes and clearer interpretability and control. The released ESC-Skills Bank, code, and prompts provide a practical resource for building and studying safer, more robust emotional support agents.

Abstract: Existing emotional support conversation (ESC) systems mainly rely on end-to-end response generation or coarse strategy supervision, offering limited interpretability and little support for systematic skill improvement. We propose ESC-Skills, a skill-centric framework that discovers and self-evolves executable emotional support skills. We first model localized support interactions as Intervention Units (IUs), which capture state--action--outcome dynamics between seeker states, support interventions, and post-response emotional changes. Based on IUs extracted from both successful and failed ESC dialogues, we construct the ESC-Skills Bank, a repository of executable emotional support skills containing intervention guidance, applicability conditions, expected outcomes, and potential risks. To further improve robustness, we introduce a multi-profile self-evolutionary refinement framework in which an ESC agent interacts with diverse simulated seeker profiles under SAGE evaluation. The resulting interaction traces are analyzed to identify missing skills, unsafe interventions, and profile-specific failure patterns, which are then used to refine the Skills Bank through simulation-based verification. Experimental results demonstrate that ESC-Skills improves both response-level quality and dialogue-level emotional outcomes while providing more interpretable and controllable support behaviors. We will release the code, prompts, and ESC-Skills Bank at https://github.com/aliyun/qwen-dianjin.

</details>


### [44] [Let the Results Speak: A Replication-First Paradigm for LLM Behavioral Benchmarking](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.27914)
*Yuming,Huang,Yao Liu,Lei Wang,Junchen Wan*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: New paradigm to evaluate subjective LLM behaviors like empathy using replication-based validation instead of single human raters.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Subjective traits of LLMs (empathy, restraint, emotional tone) are hard to measure reliably: human raters disagree, LLM-as-judge can be circular, and relying on one rater group fails when humans themselves lack consensus.

Method: They propose a 'replication-first' evaluation paradigm that validates a rating instrument through four properties: (1) reliability across multiple runs, (2) replication across architecturally distinct judge models, (3) calibration using earlier-cohort judge models, and (4) pre-registered hypotheses and predictions. They apply it to emotional accompaniment, letting the evaluation rubric self-evolve from data over iterations, converging on 9 dimensions without predefining them. They pre-register 10 hypotheses and 11 predictions before collecting test data.

Result: Applied to 49 LLMs across 8 model families, the paradigm reveals differences hidden by aggregate scores. For example, on 'advice-restraint' (avoiding unsolicited solutions in empathic settings), newer models like gpt-5 and Opus-4.7 show significant drops relative to their predecessors despite flat aggregate metrics. These effects are robust across three user-proxy swaps, a stack of judge models from 5 families, a 17‑month training-cohort gap, and 74 held-out real ESConv conversations, achieving high reliability (ordinal Krippendorff’s alpha = 0.91) and strong correlations (rho 0.749–0.850).

Conclusion: The replication-first framework provides a more trustworthy way to evaluate subjective LLM behavior, distinguishing real capability changes from measurement artifacts. It also serves as a diagnostic for when current rubrics hit their limits, indicating whether to refine the rubric or redesign scenarios or model rosters.

Abstract: Subjective evaluation of LLM behavior -- empathy, restraint, calibrated emotional tone -- is hard. Human inter-rater agreement on such qualities saturates near rho ~ 0.45, and an LLM-as-judge proxy alone risks circularity: a judge sharing the target's training cohort cannot independently verify it. Anchoring validity to a single human-rater consensus does not extend to capabilities where humans themselves disagree.
  We propose a replication-first paradigm: instead of anchoring on one rater group, we certify the instrument via four orthogonal properties -- reliability across K runs, cross-instrument replication across architecturally distinct judges, historical-footprint calibration via judges from earlier training cohorts, and pre-registered prediction. We test it on emotional accompaniment by letting the rubric self-evolve data-driven across iterations: the dimensions are not pre-stipulated and the procedure stabilizes to a 9-dimension set. Pre-registration applies to 10 falsifiable hypotheses and 11 forward predictions, committed before any test data was collected.
  Applied to 49 models across 8 families, the paradigm surfaces what aggregate scores hide. On advice-restraint -- whether a model refrains from giving unsolicited solutions in empathic contexts -- gpt-5 falls 1.87 points from gpt-4.1 and Opus-4.7 falls 0.629 from Opus-4.6, while aggregate scores stay flat. The regression survives three user-proxy swaps (95% of magnitude), replicates across a 5-family judge stack and a 17-month cohort gap, and persists on 74 held-out real ESConv conversations (rho in [0.749, 0.850]); the instrument reaches ordinal Krippendorff alpha = 0.91. As a by-product, the paradigm acts as a saturation-source diagnostic, separating instrumental ceilings (breakable by rubric refinement) from structural ceilings (needing scenario or roster intervention).

</details>


### [45] [GeneralThinker: Domain-General Reasoning through Likelihood-Guided Answer-Conditioned Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.27934)
*Shengmin Piao,Sanghyun Park*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: They propose GeneralThinker, an on-policy RL framework that provides dense, token-level credit assignment for reasoning in language models, based on answer likelihood rather than domain-specific verifiers, achieving state-of-the-art average performance on 11 reasoning benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing RL-for-reasoning methods depend on domain-specific verifiers and sparse rewards, and only provide coarse-grained credit assignment at the trajectory/response level, limiting applicability and training efficiency for complex reasoning tasks.

Method: Reformulate reasoning supervision as dense, answer-conditioned optimization: for each generated reasoning trajectory, compute the likelihood of the correct final answer under the model, then back out token-wise compatibility scores to serve as fine-grained rewards. Use an on-policy training loop and stabilize optimization with clipping and direction-preserving modulation so that token-level updates are bounded and do not overreact to noisy signals.

Result: On 11 benchmarks across mathematics, STEM, and general reasoning, the GeneralThinker approach yields the best average performance compared with prior RL and reasoning baselines, showing that dense token-level credit assignment without verifiers can be effective.

Conclusion: Fine-grained, answer-conditioned token-level credit assignment can improve language model reasoning without domain-specific verifiers, but it requires carefully controlled modulation (e.g., clipping and direction-preserving constraints) to avoid destabilizing training; with these controls, GeneralThinker delivers robust gains across diverse reasoning benchmarks.

Abstract: Reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards improves language model reasoning, but its reliance on domain-specific verifiers, sparse outcome rewards, and coarse-grained credit assignment limits its applicability. We introduce GeneralThinker, an on-policy framework that reformulates reasoning supervision as dense answer-conditioned optimization, enabling response-level evaluation and token-level credit assignment without domain-specific verifiers. GeneralThinker evaluates generated reasoning trajectories using the likelihood of the ground-truth answer and derives token-wise compatibility signals for fine-grained credit assignment. To stabilize optimization, it constrains token-level updates through clipping and direction-preserving modulation. Across 11 benchmarks spanning mathematics, STEM, and general reasoning, GeneralThinker achieves the best average performance. Further analyses show that uncontrolled token-level modulation can destabilize training, whereas controlled modulation makes fine-grained credit assignment consistently effective.

</details>


### [46] [DisasterBench: Benchmarking LLM Planning under Typed Tool Interface Constraints](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.27957)
*Zhitong Chen,Kai Yin,Weifeng Zhang,Zhiyuan Wang,Xiangjue Dong,Chengkai Liu,Zhewei Liu,Yiming Xiao,Ali Mostafavi,James Caverlee*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: DisasterBench is a benchmark to test how well LLM-based planners and multi-agent systems can coordinate complex disaster-response tool workflows, focusing on executable, step-wise plans rather than just semantically plausible ones.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: In real-world disaster response, many heterogeneous AI tools (e.g., satellite imagery analysis, flood prediction, damage assessment) need to be combined into coherent multi-step workflows under time pressure. LLMs are increasingly used as orchestrators of these tools, but existing evaluations mostly check if the chosen tools are semantically reasonable, not whether the resulting workflows are executable with correct parameter bindings and dependency handling. There is a lack of benchmarks and diagnostics that expose where and why planning fails in such structured, tool-heavy settings.

Method: The authors build DisasterBench, a benchmark centered on structured multi-agent planning over a set of disaster-response tools that are semantically similar but differ in their operational details. The benchmark requires LLMs to generate multi-step, structured workflows that correctly select tools, bind parameters, and propagate dependencies. They also introduce First-Point-of-Failure (FPoF), an evaluation protocol/metric that identifies the earliest root-cause error in the generated workflow, allowing step-level attribution and distinguishing primary errors (tool mismatch, wrong parameters) from secondary cascading failures. They then systematically evaluate different planning methods and LLM capacities on this benchmark, comparing planning effectiveness, types of errors, and the impact of reasoning styles (e.g., verbose chain-of-thought) on structured planning quality.

Result: The experiments show: (1) Planning performance is highly sensitive to model capacity; larger models plan more effectively. (2) The dominant first errors are tool mismatches (choosing the wrong but semantically similar tool) and parameter-binding mistakes, indicating that semantic understanding alone is insufficient without precise grounding to tool specifications and consistent execution constraints. (3) Encouraging verbose intermediate reasoning can backfire: it can cause instruction clashes between free-form reasoning text and required structured output formats, which disrupts plan generation and harms execution quality.

Conclusion: There is a fundamental gap between semantic reasoning (choosing seemingly appropriate tools and steps) and execution-grounded coordination (producing workflows that actually run correctly with proper bindings and dependencies). DisasterBench and FPoF expose this gap and suggest that future planning frameworks must jointly model semantic task intent, tool execution constraints, and workflow-level consistency, rather than treating semantic reasoning and tool execution as separate concerns. The work provides open-source code, data, and evaluation resources to support further research on robust LLM-based planners for disaster-response pipelines.

Abstract: Disasters cause severe societal impacts, demanding rapid coordination of heterogeneous AI tools, from satellite analysis to flood prediction and damage assessment, into coherent multi-step workflows. As LLMs increasingly serve as orchestrators of such pipelines, effective coordination requires more than selecting semantically plausible tools: LLMs must generate executable workflows with correct parameter binding and dependency propagation.
  We introduce DisasterBench, a benchmark for evaluating structured multi-agent planning over semantically similar but operationally distinct disaster-response tools. To enable step-level failure attribution, we further propose First-Point-of-Failure (FPoF), which localizes the earliest root cause in a predicted workflow, separating primary errors from downstream cascading effects.
  Our evaluation reveals three findings: planning method effectiveness depends strongly on model capacity; tool mismatch and parameter-binding errors dominate first failures, revealing semantic grounding and execution consistency as distinct bottlenecks; and verbose intermediate reasoning can create instruction clash with structured output requirements, disrupting plan generation.
  Together, these findings highlight a fundamental gap between semantic reasoning and execution-grounded coordination, underscoring the need for planning frameworks that jointly model semantic intent, execution constraints, and workflow consistency.
  Code, data, and evaluation resources are available at: https://github.com/TamuChen18/DisasterBench_Open

</details>


### [47] [Pressure-Testing Deception Probes in LLMs: Scaling, Robustness, and the Geometry of Deceptive Representations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.27958)
*Sachin Kumar*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper studies why linear probes for detecting deception in LLMs look extremely good on standard benchmarks but fail under distribution shift, and shows their fragility comes from narrow training distributions rather than fundamental architectural limits or simple entropy effects.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Linear probes on LLM activations are widely proposed as metrics for deception detection and often report very high AUROC, but these metrics collapse when evaluated on data with different styles or distributions. There is confusion about what these probes are actually learning (deception vs. artifacts such as style or entropy) and why performance degrades under shift. The paper aims to systematically stress-test and diagnose probe behaviors across multiple model sizes to understand whether deception is represented as a simple linear direction, subspace, cone, or entropy proxy, and whether failures are due to geometry or training distribution issues.

Method: The authors use the Gemma 3 model family (1B–27B parameters) and construct a series of controlled experiments: (1) testing a single linear direction hypothesis vs multi-dimensional probes; (2) cross-domain transfer using transfer matrices to assess geometric vs layer-mismatch causes of failure; (3) multi-dimensional probe analysis with permutation-based null baselines to identify genuine signal dimensions; (4) entropy-residualization to test whether probes are just reading off token entropy; and (5) evaluations under 8 different stylistic shifts, including distractor styles, and style-augmented training of probes. They vary probe dimensionality (k) to see how many directions are needed to capture deception-related signal.

Result: On clean in-distribution data, probes achieve nearly perfect AUROC (≥0.998). Under stylistic shifts, performance collapses, but when probes are trained with style-augmented data they recover near-perfect detection (mean AUROC 0.979–0.983) on unseen styles. Single-direction probes capture only 0.61–0.80 AUROC, rejecting the hypothesis that deception lives in one linear direction. Cross-domain transfer failures are due to geometric misalignment rather than layer mismatches. Entropy correlation is modest (max |ρ|=0.454), and residualizing out entropy barely changes performance (max Δ-AUROC=0.004), rejecting the entropy-proxy hypothesis. Deception does not appear as a statistically significant linear subspace when tested per-domain (k* = 0), yet using higher-dimensional probes (k ≥ 5) still enables recovery of deception signal through many individually weak features.

Conclusion: Probe-based deception metrics are highly fragile because they are trained on narrow data distributions and overfit to stylistic or domain-specific patterns, not because LLMs lack a usable representation of deception or because deception is purely an entropy effect. Deception is not captured by a single direction or clean linear subspace but is instead encoded in a distributed, multi-dimensional pattern of weak features that probes can exploit when given sufficient dimensionality and stylistic diversity. The observed inverse scaling of probe performance with model size is therefore a consequence of training-distribution artifacts, and with style-augmented training high performance can be recovered even for large models, undermining claims that larger models are inherently harder to probe for deception.

Abstract: Linear probes trained on LLM activations are increasingly proposed as deception-detection metrics, yet report AUROC exceeding 0.96 on clean benchmarks while collapsing under distributional shift. This paper systematically pressure-tests probe-based metrics across the Gemma 3 model family (1B-27B parameters), diagnosing why they fail rather than merely documenting that they fail. We test four hypotheses about deception encoding: (1) single linear direction, (2) multi-dimensional subspace, (3) convex conic hull, (4) entropy proxy. Our design includes cross-domain transfer matrices, multi-dimensional probe analysis with permutation null baselines, entropy-residualization tests, and distractor evaluations across 8 stylistic shifts. We find that: (a) probes achieve near-perfect AUROC (>=0.998) on clean data but collapse under stylistic shifts; style-augmented probes recover near-perfect detection (mean AUROC 0.979-0.983) on unseen styles; (b) the single-direction hypothesis is rejected (k=1 captures only 0.61-0.80 AUROC), with cross-domain transfer failure confirmed as geometric rather than layer-mismatch-driven; (c) the entropy-proxy hypothesis is rejected (max |rho|=0.454, max Delta-AUROC after residualization=0.004); and (d) deception does not form a significant linear subspace (per-domain k*=0), yet multi-dimensional probes (k>=5) recover the signal through distributed sub-threshold features. Probe fragility reflects distributional narrowness rather than an architectural limitation: style-augmented probes recover near-perfect detection at both 4B and 27B, establishing that the inverse scaling pattern is a training-distribution artifact rather than a genuine scale-dependent phenomenon.

</details>


### [48] [Boundary Suppression Asymmetry in Post-trained Assistants: Over-expansion as a Controllability Cost](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.27969)
*Jiarui Han*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper studies how post-training language models that are optimized to avoid under-answering can become harder to control when users request shorter or narrower answers.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To investigate whether safety and helpfulness tuning that pushes assistants to be very thorough and proactive creates asymmetric controllability costs, making it difficult to suppress certain over-helpful behaviors when users explicitly ask for concise or narrow responses.

Method: The authors use prompt-side probes across several response dimensions and compare controlled assistant-policy variants derived from a shared base model. They run matched boundary-control evaluations, direct boundary-control comparisons between standard and minimal-boundary policies, and mechanism-oriented probes testing factors like output length, EOS behavior, uncertainty handling, and continuation bias. They also perform robustness checks across shared-system and larger-scale settings.

Result: They find that anti-underanswering policies (designed to avoid being too brief or unhelpful) are harder to rein in than baseline policies when users try to narrow the answer. Minimal-boundary variants avoid the upward shift in over-answering. Mechanistic tests rule out simple explanations like just having longer default outputs, end-of-sequence failures, uncertainty compensation, or local continuation bias. The results instead suggest overshooting content budgets and persistence in continuation as key factors.

Conclusion: Post-training that strongly discourages under-answering introduces direction-specific controllability costs: it remains easy to elicit helpful, expansive behavior, but becomes harder to locally suppress these "too-much assistant" tendencies when users want stricter boundaries, due to mixed planning and stopping dynamics in the model's behavior.

Abstract: Post-trained language-model assistants are often optimized to avoid under-answering, encouraging complete, helpful, cautious, and proactive responses. We ask whether this optimization creates asymmetric controllability costs: when users explicitly request narrower answers, which assistant behaviors remain suppressible, and which continue to shape the response? We study this problem as boundary-suppression asymmetry. Prompt-side probes across multiple high-level response dimensions suggest a selective cost, concentrated around `too-much assistant' directions such as over-completion, extra help, and anti-underanswering.
  Using controlled assistant-policy variants derived from a shared base model, we find that anti-underanswering policies are harder to pull back than the baseline under matched boundary-control evaluations, while minimal-boundary variants generally avoid this anti-side upward shift in the direct boundary-control comparisons. Mechanism-oriented probes point beyond longer default outputs, pure EOS failure, uncertainty compensation, and local continuation bias, while robustness checks preserve the main anti-over-baseline ordering under shared-system and larger-scale settings. The evidence supports a mixed planning/stopping account, where content-budget overshoot and continuation persistence jointly make boundary correction harder. Overall, post-training may create direction-specific controllability costs: some helpful assistant tendencies remain easy to invoke, yet harder to locally suppress.

</details>


### [49] [Semantic Flow Regularization: Teaching LLMs to Generate Diverse Yet Coherent Responses](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.27971)
*Kerui Peng,Feifei Li,Xingyu Fan,Wenhui Que*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper identifies and addresses 'Cross-Style Collapse'—a loss of diversity when fine-tuning LLMs for personas or tones—by introducing Semantic Flow Regularization (SFR), an auxiliary flow-matching objective that improves diversity and quality without inference-time cost.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Fine-tuning large language models for persona- or tone-conditioned responses often leads to highly uniform, low-diversity outputs across styles, which harms user experience and controllability. The authors want to understand why this collapse happens under standard cross-entropy training and to develop a method that restores diverse, style-faithful generation without increasing inference-time complexity.

Method: They first analyze how the standard cross-entropy objective, combined with shared representations, encourages suppression of diverse continuations across styles, leading to what they call Cross-Style Collapse. To fix this, they propose Semantic Flow Regularization (SFR), an auxiliary training objective: using a sentence encoder, they compute continuous semantic embeddings of future text segments and train a separate flow-matching head (via conditional flow matching) to predict a stochastic path (flow) over these embeddings conditioned on the context. This flow source is inherently multimodal, so matching it preserves diversity in the backbone representations. The flow head is used only in training and is removed at inference, so generation cost is unchanged. They also compare SFR to Multi-Token Prediction and show it is a special, degenerate case of SFR under certain conditions.

Result: On a large-scale industrial persona-dialogue setup using Qwen3-32B with 9 personas, SFR improves output diversity, style fidelity, and overall response quality over standard supervised fine-tuning (SFT). On the public LiveCodeBench-v5 benchmark with Qwen2.5-Coder-7B-Instruct, SFR consistently boosts pass@k metrics, indicating that the benefits extend beyond stylized dialogue to code generation. On MBPP, a controlled comparison shows that conventional Multi-Token Prediction is subsumed by SFR as a limiting case, further validating the generality of their formulation.

Conclusion: Cross-entropy training in shared-representation, style-conditioned settings leads to a systematic reduction in output diversity (Cross-Style Collapse). Semantic Flow Regularization offers a simple, training-only auxiliary objective that injects and preserves multimodality in the model’s semantics, improving both diversity and quality without any extra inference-time cost. Its effectiveness on dialogue personas and code benchmarks, plus the theoretical link to Multi-Token Prediction, suggests SFR is a broadly applicable and more principled way to mitigate diversity collapse in fine-tuned LLMs.

Abstract: When large language models are fine-tuned to generate persona- or tone-conditioned responses, their output diversity is severely limited--a failure we term Cross-Style Collapse. We trace this collapse to the cross-entropy objective, which under shared representations tends to suppress diverse continuations. We propose Semantic Flow Regularization (SFR), a lightweight auxiliary objective that supervises the backbone with continuous sentence-encoder embeddings of future segments via conditional flow matching. The stochastic flow source preserves multi-modality by construction; the flow-matching head is discarded at inference, adding zero deployment cost. On a large-scale industrial dialogue dataset (Qwen3-32B, 9 personas), SFR improves output diversity, style fidelity, and response quality over SFT. We further validate on the public LiveCodeBench-v5 (Qwen2.5-Coder-7B-Instruct), where SFR consistently improves pass@k, confirming generality beyond stylized dialogue. A controlled comparison on MBPP reveals Multi-Token Prediction to be a degenerate special case of SFR.

</details>


### [50] [Periodic RoPE for Infinite Context LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.27980)
*Simin Huo*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: They propose a new positional encoding scheme and architecture (MiniWin) that, in principle, allows LLMs to work with effectively infinite context without degradation beyond the pre-trained length.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLMs struggle with very long inputs because standard positional encodings like RoPE break down when sequences exceed their pre-trained length, causing performance degradation (“position exhaustion”). Existing attempts to extend context (e.g., to 1M tokens) still face this fundamental limitation, preventing truly infinite context processing.

Method: Introduce Periodic RoPE (P-RoPE), a modified positional encoding that repeats periodically, paired with an architectural design combining: (1) sliding window attention (SWA) layers using P-RoPE to model local dependencies and relative positions within each window, and (2) global attention layers with no positional encoding (NoPE) to allow unconstrained, long-range interactions. By stacking these two layer types, the model never needs to extrapolate positional encodings beyond their training range.

Result: They build a model called MiniWin using this design and empirically compare it to MiniMind, a baseline using standard GPT architectures. MiniWin shows better efficiency and stability on long-context tasks, handling very long inputs more reliably than the baseline.

Conclusion: Combining periodic positional encodings for local windows with position-free global attention provides a way to sidestep position exhaustion and theoretically supports infinite context windows. The MiniWin experiments suggest this is a practical path toward LLMs capable of stable, efficient infinite-context understanding.

Abstract: The ability to process ultra-long contexts is crucial for large language models (LLMs) to perform long-horizon tasks. While recent efforts have extended context windows to 1M and beyond, model performance degrades when sequence length exceeds the pre-trained range of positional encodings (e.g., RoPE), i.e., position exhaustion. This fundamental limitation must be overcome to achieve a truly infinite context. To address it, we propose Periodic RoPE (P-RoPE), a positional encoding mechanism designed to circumvent this exhaustion. It operates in conjunction with sliding window attention (SWA) to capture local dependencies and relative positions within each window. This local layer is then complemented by a global attention layer with No Positional Encoding (NoPE), enabling unbounded interaction across the entire sequence without positional constraints. By stacking these two types of layers, the model avoids the need for positional extrapolation to generalize longer and theoretically supports an infinite context window. Empirical results show that our model, MiniWin, outperforms MiniMInd with standard GPT architectures in long-context efficiency and stability. Our work provides a possible pathway toward LLMs with genuine infinite-context understanding. The code is available at \href{https://github.com/Cominder/miniwin}{https://github.com/Cominder/miniwin}.

</details>


### [51] [KVoiceBench, KOpenAudioBench, and KMMAU: Agent-Driven Korean Speech Benchmarks for Evaluating SpeechLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.27984)
*Haechan Kim,Seungjun Chung,Inkyu Park,Jihoo Lee,Jonghyun Lee*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces new Korean speech benchmarks to more reliably evaluate multilingual speech language models, revealing model weaknesses that English-only evaluation misses.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing evaluations for speech language models focus mainly on English and often rely on automatic benchmark transfer (ASR, MT, TTS), which distorts language-specific instructions, answer constraints, and speech properties. This makes it hard to fairly assess multilingual capabilities, especially for languages like Korean where speaker attributes, accents, and paralinguistic cues are important.

Method: The authors design two human-in-the-loop benchmark construction frameworks: (1) a framework that transfers existing SpokenQA benchmarks from a source language into a target language while preserving instructions and answer constraints; (2) a framework that converts target-language ASR corpora into audio-understanding benchmarks by leveraging text transcriptions and speaker metadata. Using these procedures, they build three Korean benchmarks: KVoiceBench and KOpenAudioBench for Korean SpokenQA, and KMMAU for Korean audio understanding.

Result: They construct and release three Korean speech benchmarks with a total of 12,345 samples. Eight recent SpeechLMs are evaluated on these benchmarks. The experiments show large and model-dependent performance gaps between English and Korean, and different ranking orders across SpokenQA vs audio-understanding tasks.

Conclusion: English-centric benchmarks are insufficient to characterize multilingual SpeechLM performance. The proposed human-agent frameworks enable higher-fidelity construction of non-English spoken QA and audio-understanding benchmarks. The new Korean benchmarks expose complementary weaknesses across models and task types that are not visible under English-only evaluation, underscoring the need for multilingual, modality-faithful benchmarks.

Abstract: Speech language models (SpeechLMs) have achieved substantial progress by extending large language models (LLMs) to the speech modality. However, SpeechLM evaluation remains heavily centered on English, limiting reliable assessment of multilingual speech capabilities. Straightforward benchmark transfer through ASR, translation, normalization, and TTS can corrupt language-specific instructions, answer constraints, and spoken forms; for audio understanding, transferring source-language audio also fails to preserve target-language speaker attributes, accents, and paralinguistic properties. To address these limitations, we propose two human-agent benchmark-construction frameworks: one transfers source-language SpokenQA benchmarks into target-language SpokenQA benchmarks, and the other converts target-language ASR corpora into audio understanding benchmarks using transcriptions and speaker metadata. Using these frameworks, we construct and publicly release three Korean speech benchmarks: KVoiceBench and KOpenAudioBench for Korean SpokenQA, and KMMAU for Korean audio understanding, comprising 12,345 samples in total. We evaluate eight recent SpeechLMs and find that English-Korean performance gaps vary substantially across models and task families, and that SpokenQA and audio understanding rankings diverge, revealing complementary weaknesses invisible to English-only evaluation.

</details>


### [52] [An Evolutionary Approach for Designing Stable and Highly Expressible Low-Immunogenicity Therapeutic mRNA Sequences](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.27986)
*Dhawa Sang Dong,Mausam Gurung,Suraj Kandel*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: They propose a two-stage, fully in‑silico pipeline that first generates and then evolutionarily optimizes mRNA sequences to balance translation efficiency, structural stability, and low immunogenicity, outperforming single‑objective baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current computational mRNA design tools either over‑stabilize transcripts (hurting translation) or optimize only codon usage without considering RNA structure or innate immune activation. There is a need for a rational, multi‑objective, data‑driven method that can jointly optimize translation efficiency, structural robustness, and reduced immunogenic motifs for therapeutic mRNA, especially vaccines.

Method: The authors build a two-stage framework. Stage 1 uses a pretrained CodonTransformer, a BERT-like language model trained on coding sequences, to generate synonymous mRNA sequences for a given antigen while preserving biological plausibility. Stage 2 applies a genetic algorithm that performs codon-aware crossover and synonymous mutation under human codon usage constraints. Candidate sequences are scored using a composite fitness function that integrates translation-related indices (CAI, tAI, codon-pair bias), RNA structure metrics (local/global MFE from RNAfold, GC content, ribosomal accessibility via unpaired_30, and base-pairing fraction), and immunogenicity proxies (CpG/UpA motif frequencies aggregated into an immune penalty). The GA iteratively evolves populations over many generations, selecting for high fitness under this multi-objective score.

Result: Across later generations (around the 38th–42nd), the evolved mRNAs show consistent improvements: CAI increases to ~0.73–0.74 and tAI to ~0.63–0.64 (over 6% gains), codon-pair bias remains high (~0.97), 5′-end ribosomal accessibility improves with unpaired_30 ~0.87, and global MFE converges to a moderate range (~−346 to −356 kcal/mol) corresponding to roughly 84% base-paired structures. Immune-stimulatory CpG/UpA motifs are reduced, lowering the average immune penalty to 27.3. Compared with LinearDesign, which yields overly stable (MFE < −2000 kcal/mol) and potentially poorly translated RNAs, and BiLSTM-CRF, which maximizes CAI (~0.96–0.98) without structural constraints, the proposed framework achieves a better balance across all objectives.

Conclusion: The two-stage BERT-GA pipeline enables rational, multi-objective in-silico optimization of mRNA therapeutics, striking a practical compromise between translation efficiency, structural stability, and reduced immunogenicity that existing single-objective methods fail to achieve. This demonstrates that combining deep generative sequence modeling with evolutionary search and composite fitness functions is an effective strategy for designing optimized therapeutic mRNAs.

Abstract: Messenger RNA (mRNA) sequences as therapeutics require optimized design to ensure efficient translation, structural stability, and minimal immunogenicity. This study presents a two-stage in-silico framework that integrates deep learning and evolutionary computation for rational mRNA optimization instead of existing state-of-the-art models. In the first stage, a pretrained CodonTransformer (BERT-like Large Language Model) generates biologically coherent mRNA sequences encoding the target antigen. In the second stage, a genetic algorithm (GA) evolves these candidate sequences through codon-aware crossover and synonymous mutation guided by human codon usage preferences. Fitness functions for evaluation combined translation-related metrics (CAI, tAI, codon-pair bias), mRNA structural stability (local and global MFE via RNAfold, GC content), and reduced immunogenicity (CpG/UpA motif frequency). Over successive generations (38th, 40th, and 42nd), the GA improved (achieved CAI values of 0.73 to 0.74 and tAI values of 0.63 to 0.64) CAI and tAI by over 6% and codon-pair bias is high and consistent (0.97 ) and improved ribosomal accessibility at the 5' end, with an unpaired_30 fraction reaching 0.87; Global Minimum Free Energy (MFE) converged to a balanced range of -346 to -356 kcal/mol, achieving approximately 84% base-paired structural stability, and reduced immune-stimulatory motifs - lowering the average immune penalty to 27.3 in the final generation. Linear Design produces hyper-stable transcripts (MFE < - 2000 kcal/mol) that risk translation inefficiency due to extreme rigidity, and BiLSTM-CRF focuses solely on high CAI (0.96 to 0.98) without structural constraints, our framework achieves an optimal translation-stability equilibrium, highlighting the proposed BERT-GA framework as an effective, data-driven approach for the design and optimization of in-silico mRNA sequences.

</details>


### [53] [Auditing Stance Asymmetry in Generative Explanations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.27988)
*Jiarui Han*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces Symmetry Decomposition Evaluation (SDE), a method to detect and analyze asymmetric explanatory stances (who gets blame, legitimacy, or context) in language models’ open-ended outputs between different groups or “sides.”


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing bias evaluations focus on bounded settings (e.g., word associations, simple substitutions, toxicity) and miss subtler asymmetries in open-ended explanations, where a model can be polite yet consistently portray one side as more legitimate or reasonable. The authors want a way to systematically reveal and study these stance-bearing asymmetries.

Method: They define “stance-bearing asymmetry” in generative explanations and propose SDE: constructing paired scenarios with (1) concrete group labels, (2) structural-role rewrites (swapping roles or contexts while holding structure constant), and (3) explicit prompts for supporting or counter-evidence. They create a controlled 32-family prototype benchmark of such pairs, then analyze how differences between model explanations change or persist under these decompositions. They also perform targeted case reviews and compare human-judge interpretations across different operationalizations and scoring schemes.

Result: Using the 32-family SDE suite, they find that not all surface asymmetries behave the same: some disappear or weaken when structural roles or required evidence are controlled, while others remain robust, indicating persistent differences in how models assign blame, legitimacy, or contextual understanding to different sides. They also observe that human-judge evaluations vary with how tasks are framed, and that scalar bias scores often obscure the nuanced distinctions that readers actually use to perceive stance in explanations.

Conclusion: The paper argues that bias evaluation for generative models should be reframed as auditing explanatory stance rather than just measuring surface toxicity or association biases. SDE offers a decomposition-based methodology to inspect what stance each side receives, how that stance shifts when structure and evidence are controlled, and where automatic or scalar scoring becomes unreliable, highlighting the need for more nuanced, decomposition-aware evaluation of open-ended model behavior.

Abstract: Bias evaluation for language models has made substantial progress on bounded comparisons, such as overt derogation, stereotype association, or label-sensitive differences under controlled substitutions. Open-ended explanations raise a different problem: they guide interpretation by assigning responsibility, legitimacy, context, and grievance. A model can avoid hostile language while making one side structurally understandable and another personally at fault, overreacting, or less worth taking seriously. We call this stance-bearing asymmetry in generative explanations. We propose Symmetry Decomposition Evaluation (SDE), which tests paired situations with concrete group labels, structural-role rewrites, and explicit support or counter-evidence. In a controlled 32-family prototype suite, this decomposition shows that surface differences are not all alike: some weaken under structural or evidence control, while others remain as stable differences in how the model assigns blame, context, or legitimacy. Targeted case review and judge comparison suggest a broader difficulty for evaluating open-ended framing asymmetries: judge readings shift across operationalizations, and scalar scores can flatten distinctions that readers use to interpret explanatory stance. SDE therefore reframes generative bias evaluation as an audit of explanatory stance -- what stance each side receives, how it changes under decomposition, and where automatic scoring becomes unstable.

</details>


### [54] [Rethinking Visual Neglect: Steering via Context-Preference for MLLM Hallucination Mitigation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.27993)
*Jingwen Wu,Xijun Zhang,Ge Song*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: Proposes CAS, a training-free activation-steering method to control MLLMs' reliance on image vs. parametric/textual knowledge, reducing object hallucinations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Object hallucinations hinder safe deployment of MLLMs. Existing mitigation assumes hallucinations are mainly due to weak visual grounding and tries to increase visual reliance, but empirical evidence on multiple models shows this assumption can be wrong or incomplete: more visual reliance sometimes worsens hallucinations and less can help. Thus, hallucinations likely arise from misbalanced competition among image context, parametric knowledge, and textual context, requiring a finer control mechanism.

Method: Introduce Context-Preference Activation Steering (CAS), a training-free inference-time framework. CAS constructs two Context Preference Vectors (CPVs) that correspond to different reliance preferences (e.g., more image vs. more parametric/textual). CPVs are derived from a small, curated set of conflict samples where image and text/knowledge disagree. During inference, CAS injects signed residuals aligned with these CPVs into mid-early MLP layers via a single forward pass, thereby steering the model's context reliance without retraining or added decoding steps.

Result: Across multiple MLLMs, CAS significantly reduces object hallucinations, demonstrating that explicit control over context reliance is an effective lever. It does so without increasing decoding latency (thanks to single-pass residual injection) and while maintaining the original text generation quality, indicating minimal side effects on general language capabilities.

Conclusion: Hallucinations in MLLMs cannot be fully explained by visual insufficiency; instead, they reflect a complex competition among image, model parameters, and text context. By learning and injecting Context Preference Vectors, CAS provides a practical, training-free way to modulate this competition at inference, substantially mitigating object hallucinations while preserving efficiency and generation quality.

Abstract: Object hallucination remains a primary obstacle to the reliable deployment of Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs). Current inference-time mitigation methods mainly assume hallucinations stem from visual neglect, steering models to enhance visual reliance. In contrast, our systematic interventions on multiple MLLMs show that pushing toward more visual reliance may exacerbate hallucinations on some models, while less may mitigate hallucinations. This result suggests that attributing hallucinations solely to visual insufficiency is underdetermined. We argue that the image, as a context, simultaneously competes with the model's parametric knowledge and the textual context. For this, we propose a training-free framework, Context-Preference Activation Steering (CAS). It extracts two semantically distinct Context Preference Vectors (CPVs) via two small sets of designed conflict samples and applies them via single-pass signed residual injection at mid-early MLP layers during inference to control information reliance. Experiments show that CAS substantially mitigates object hallucinations without increasing decoding latency and preserves native text-generation quality.

</details>


### [55] [Where Does Toxicity Live? Mechanistic Localization and Targeted Suppression in Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.27997)
*Himanshu Beniwal,Mayank Singh*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: They propose two retraining-free methods to locate and suppress toxicity inside large language models by targeting specific layers and neurons, reducing toxic outputs while preserving overall performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLMs often produce toxic or harmful content, and existing mitigation strategies are either expensive (full retraining) or operate as black-box filters on outputs, offering little understanding of how toxicity is internally represented. The authors want a more mechanistic, efficient, and transparent way to reduce toxicity.

Method: They develop two frameworks, Meow2X and TRNE. Both analyze activation differences between toxic and neutral prompts to localize where toxicity resides in the network (particular layers and neurons). Meow2X suppresses these toxic circuits at inference time via scaling activations, while TRNE applies minimal rank-one weight edits to the model, all done without gradient descent or full retraining. They evaluate across multiple language models, benchmarks, and configurations, using two different safety evaluators to assess toxicity and quality.

Result: Across five language models, two benchmarks, and 90 experimental configurations, both approaches consistently reduce toxic generations while maintaining language modeling quality. The analysis shows toxicity is concentrated mainly in early MLP layers and that its distribution depends on the model architecture. They also find that relying on a single safety evaluator systematically underestimates remaining toxicity.

Conclusion: Mechanistic, retraining-free interventions at specific layers and neurons can effectively detoxify language models while preserving performance. Toxic behavior is structurally localized (especially in early MLP layers) and should be assessed with multiple evaluators. The proposed frameworks demonstrate a practical path to safer and more interpretable LLMs by directly targeting internal toxic circuits rather than relying solely on output filtering or full model retraining.

Abstract: Large language models frequently generate toxic, hateful, or harmful content, yet existing mitigation methods rely on costly retraining or output-level filtering with no mechanistic insight into where toxicity originates internally. We introduce Meow2X and TRNE, two complementary retraining-free frameworks that localize toxicity to specific layers and neurons by analyzing activation differentials between toxic and neutral prompts, then suppress them via inference-time scaling or minimal rank-one weight edits -- without any gradient descent. Evaluations across five LMs, two benchmarks, and 90 configurations using dual safety evaluators demonstrate consistent toxicity reduction while preserving language modeling quality. Our analysis reveals that toxicity is disproportionately encoded in early MLP layers, varies across architectures, and is systematically underestimated by single-evaluator setups -- underscoring the need for multi-evaluator safety assessment. By bridging mechanistic interpretability with practical detoxification, our framework offers a principled path toward safer, more transparent language models.

</details>


### [56] [ResearchMath-14K: Scaling Research-Level Mathematics via Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28003)
*Guijin Son,Seungyeop Yi,Minju Gwak,Hyunwoo Ko,Wongi Jang,Youngjae Yu*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: They build the largest research-level math problem dataset, study how open models behave on it (including failures like fabricated references), and show that carefully filtered, imperfect solution attempts can still significantly improve math reasoning when used to fine-tune models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current language models are weak on frontier, research-level mathematics, partly because there is no large, dedicated dataset of such problems and no clear understanding of how models behave on them. The authors want to create a benchmark and training resource that reflects real research problems, and to test whether model-generated reasoning, even when flawed, can nevertheless be useful supervision.

Method: 1) Curate 14,056 research-level math problems from academic sources using a multi-agent pipeline, forming the ResearchMath-14k dataset. 2) Generate 220K solution trajectories (ResearchMath-Reasoning) using two open models, and analyze their behavior, focusing on avoidance patterns like refusing to attempt solutions or inventing references. 3) Quantitatively study how different generations of open-weight models behave in terms of reference usage and fabrication. 4) Apply an "agentic filtering" process to remove low-quality or pathological trajectories. 5) Fine-tune a family of Qwen3 models (4B–30B parameters) on the filtered trajectories and evaluate the performance gains over the base models on research-level math tasks.

Result: They obtain ResearchMath-14k, the largest public collection of research-level math problems, plus a 220K-trajectory reasoning dataset from open models. Behavioral analysis reveals systematic avoidance and hallucination patterns, including large increases in both cited and fake references in newer open-weight models. After applying their filtering pipeline and using the resulting data to fine-tune Qwen3 models, they observe an average improvement of 9.2 points compared with the base models on research-level math benchmarks.

Conclusion: Even though current open models often fail on research-level math and exhibit problematic behaviors like non-attempts and fabricated references, their solution attempts still contain enough useful signal to improve mathematical reasoning. With appropriate agentic filtering, these imperfect reasoning traces become effective supervision data. The released ResearchMath-14k dataset establishes a new large-scale benchmark and resource for future work on research-level mathematical reasoning and model training.

Abstract: The frontier of mathematics is defined by problems whose solutions are not yet known, yet it remains unclear whether language models can meaningfully engage with such problems without human intervention. A major obstacle is the lack of large-scale research-level math datasets. To this end, we introduce ResearchMath-14k, a set of $14{,}056$ problems curated from academic sources via a multi-agent pipeline, making it the largest collection of research-level mathematical problems to date. We further generate ResearchMath-Reasoning, $220$K teacher trajectories from two open models, where we observe recurring avoidance behaviors such as non-attempts and fabricated references. Interestingly, across eight open-weight models, newer generations produce $5.6\times$ more references and $5.0\times$ more fake references per trace. After agentic filtering of ResearchMath-Reasoning, fine-tuning Qwen3 models from 4B to 30B parameters improves over base models by $9.2$ points on average. This shows that filtered open-problem attempts can provide useful supervision even without fully correct reasoning traces. We make ResearchMath-14k publicly available for future works on research-level mathematical reasoning.

</details>


### [57] [Beyond Chunk-Local Extraction: Cross-Chunk Graph Augmentation for GraphRAG](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28004)
*Jiaming Zhang,Yibo Zhao,Jing Yu,Jianxiang Yu,Xiang Li*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: CrossAug improves GraphRAG-based question answering by adding missing cross-chunk relations to knowledge graph indices using a GNN to select where an LLM should infer new edges.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: In GraphRAG, entities and relations are extracted per text chunk, so relations that span multiple chunks are missing, which hurts complex, multi-hop QA. Brute-force LLM inference over all chunk combinations is computationally infeasible, so a targeted, efficient method to recover important cross-chunk relations is needed.

Method: CrossAug performs offline graph augmentation in three stages: (1) it generates training data with self-supervised graph corruption, removing some existing relations; (2) it trains a topology-aware graph neural network to predict which subgraphs are likely to be missing relations, based on graph structure; (3) at inference time it scores subgraphs, selects high-scoring regions as candidates for missing cross-chunk relations, and then uses an LLM with textual evidence from multiple chunks to infer and add these cross-chunk relations to the GraphRAG index.

Result: Across three different GraphRAG frameworks and four multi-hop / long-document QA benchmarks, CrossAug consistently improves QA performance compared to baselines without cross-chunk augmentation, indicating more effective retrieval and reasoning over the augmented graphs.

Conclusion: CrossAug demonstrates that selectively augmenting GraphRAG indices with cross-chunk relations, guided by a GNN and completed by an LLM, is an effective and practical way to enhance retrieval-based QA, avoiding the cost of exhaustive LLM relation extraction while capturing important multi-hop structure.

Abstract: GraphRAG extends retrieval-augmented generation by organizing corpora as explicit knowledge graphs, enabling graph-based retrieval for complex question answering. However, existing frameworks extract entities and relations within individual chunks, leaving cross-chunk relations -- those whose evidence spans multiple passages -- systematically absent from the index. Exhaustive LLM-based recovery of such relations is impractical due to the combinatorial explosion of chunk combinations. We present CrossAug, a GNN-guided CROSS-Chunk Graph AUGmentation method that enriches GraphRAG indices with cross-chunk relational structure as an offline step before query-time retrieval. CrossAug derives training supervision through self-supervised graph corruption, uses a topology-aware GNN to score subgraphs for missingness, and applies evidence-grounded LLM completion only to selected high-scoring regions. Experiments on three LLM-based GraphRAG frameworks across four multi-hop and long-document QA benchmarks demonstrate that CrossAug consistently improves performance, confirming the benefit of cross-chunk graph augmentation for retrieval-based question answering. Our code is available at https://github.com/DonFinliani/CrossAug.

</details>


### [58] [Integrated and Cross-Architecture Interpretation of LLM Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28006)
*Leonardo Matthew Yauw,Wei-Bin Kou,Yujiu Yang*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces the IAR framework to interpret how LLMs reason by integrating multiple probing methods and analyzing reasoning-crucial tokens across layers, architectures, and tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing efforts to understand LLM reasoning typically rely on single interpretability probes (such as MIP or DTR), which provide only a partial view and can underestimate the true inferential structure. There is a need for a unified, quantitative methodology that connects output-level indicators of reasoning with internal computation patterns across model layers and across different architectures and domains.

Method: 1) Calibrate Mutual Information Peak (MIP) with a bandwidth parameter and use Tukey IQR-based peak detection to identify reasoning-crucial tokens at the output layer. 2) Perform overlap analysis between MIP-selected tokens and DTR-identified deep-thinking tokens to trace how these reasoning-crucial tokens propagate through layers, examining whether they are associated with computationally intensive processing. 3) Use a Jaccard stability metric over multiple domains and problem instances to test whether the MIP-identified tokens are stable and correlate with high-quality reasoning. The framework is applied to several models and domains to assess its generality.

Result: The IAR framework successfully identifies reasoning-crucial tokens and maps their trajectories across layers, showing how reasoning patterns develop internally. The overlap between MIP and DTR tokens reveals when crucial reasoning tokens are also computation-intensive. The Jaccard stability analysis indicates that MIP-identified tokens are consistent across problem instances and domains, aligning with better reasoning performance. The framework generalizes across three LLM architectures (Qwen-7B, Qwen-14B, Llama-8B) and four reasoning domains (mathematics, code, logic, common sense).

Conclusion: IAR offers a unified, architecture-agnostic approach to interpreting LLM reasoning, going beyond single-probe analyses by integrating output-layer token importance, internal computation depth, and stability across tasks. This integrated methodology yields more reliable insight into how reasoning emerges and evolves in LLMs and can serve as a general tool for reasoning interpretability research across different models and domains.

Abstract: Understanding how LLMs reason is hindered by a practical asymmetry: while their generated outputs are observable, the underlying reasoning patterns remain opaque. Relying on single probes, such as Mutual Information Peak (MIP) or Deep-Thinking Ratio (DTR), risks underestimating the genuine inferential structure. To response this deficiency, we present an Integrated, cross-Architecture Reasoning (IAR) framework, designed to provide a unified approach to LLM reasoning interpretability. Specifically, we first propose to use bandwidth-calibrated MIP coupled with Tukey IQR peak-detection to isolate reasoning-crucial tokens at the output layer. Second, we performed an overlap analysis between MIP-picked tokens and DTR-deep tokens to trace the cross-layer trajectories of those tokens. This also discloses whether reasoning-crucial tokens are computation-intensive as well, further facilitating to understand how reasoning patterns evolve across model layers. Finally, we apply a Jaccard stability metric over multi-domain problems to verify if the MIP-identified tokens are reasoning quality-guaranteed. Extensive experiments on three models (Qwen-7B, Qwen-14B, and Llama-8B) across four domains (mathematics, code, logic, and common sense) demonstrate IAR's generalizable interpretation capabilities across architectures.

</details>


### [59] [MemGuard: Preventing Memory Contamination in Long-Term Memory-Augmented Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28009)
*Hyeonjeong Ha,Jeonghwan Kim,Cheng Qian,Jiayu Liu,William M. Campbell,Yue Wu,Yuji Zhang,Kathleen McKeown,Dilek Hakkani-Tur,Heng Ji*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: MemGuard proposes a type-aware memory system for LLMs that separates different kinds of user memories to prevent them from contaminating one another, yielding more reliable long-term reasoning with less retrieved context.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current memory-augmented LLMs store diverse information types—such as stable user attributes, temporary situations, and behavioral guidelines—in a single undifferentiated memory space. This leads to heterogeneous memory contamination, where context-bound or functionally incompatible memories are wrongly reused as general evidence, causing hallucinations and unreliable behavior. The paper aims to make long-term LLM memory more reliable by addressing this structural problem.

Method: The authors design MemGuard, a type-aware memory framework for LLMs. At write time, each memory is annotated with an explicit functional role (e.g., stable fact, episodic event, rule). The system maintains explicit relations between memories of different types while keeping their storage logically separated. During retrieval, the framework selectively pulls only the memory types functionally necessary for the current query, and then composes them into the prompt, thereby enforcing type boundaries and reducing cross-type contamination.

Result: On hallucination and long-horizon conversation benchmarks, MemGuard significantly improves memory reliability (up to 28.27% gain) while also being more efficient, retrieving up to 5.8 times fewer memory tokens than baseline memory systems. This indicates better precision in which memories are deemed relevant and used.

Conclusion: Separating heterogeneous memory into functionally defined types and controlling how they are written, related, and retrieved leads to more reliable long-term reasoning in memory-augmented LLMs. Principled organization and selective use of memory are critical for scaling LLMs beyond fixed context windows without amplifying hallucinations or misuse of past information.

Abstract: Memory-augmented large language models extend reasoning beyond a fixed context window by maintaining long-term memory across interactions. However, existing memory systems often collapse stable user facts, episodic events, and behavioral rules into a shared space, allowing functionally distinct memories to be retrieved and used as interchangeable evidence. We identify this failure mode as heterogeneous memory contamination, where context-specific events become overgeneralized claims, or semantically relevant but functionally incompatible memories mislead generation. To this end, we introduce MemGuard, a type-aware memory framework that preserves functional memory boundaries during memory construction and retrieval. It assigns each memory an explicit functional role at write time, maintains relations across type-isolated memories, and selectively composes evidence only from necessary memory types, reducing contamination from irrelevant or functionally incompatible evidence. Across hallucination and long-horizon conversation benchmarks, MemGuard improves memory reliability by up to 28.27% while retrieving up to 5.8x fewer memory tokens than prior methods. These results suggest that reliable long-term reasoning depends on principled organization and selective use of heterogeneous memory.

</details>


### [60] [KSAFE-MM: A Multimodal Safety Benchmark via Localized Contextualization for Korean Cultural Risks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28013)
*Yongwoo Kim,Sojung An,Yunjin Park,Jungwon Yoon,Dujin Lee,HyunBeom Cho,Jaewon Lee,Wonhyuk Lee,Youngchol Kim,JeongYeop Kim,Donghyun Kim*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper proposes KSAFE-MM, a Korean multimodal safety benchmark that evaluates both general and culture-specific risks, showing current MLLMs are more vulnerable to culturally grounded multimodal jailbreak attacks than to generic ones, and that safer models often over-refuse benign queries.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing multimodal LLM safety benchmarks are largely English-centric and emphasize generic risks, failing to capture culture-specific vulnerabilities, especially for languages like Korean and for multimodal signals such as local visual cues. There is therefore a gap in evaluating how MLLMs behave in non-English, culturally grounded contexts, particularly under multimodal jailbreak attacks, which can undermine safety in real-world deployments.

Method: The authors build KSAFE-MM, a Korean multimodal safety benchmark with two components: (1) KSAFE-MM-G, which assesses globally shared safety risks in Korean by linguistically contextualizing generic safety queries into Korean and placing them in multimodal settings; and (2) KSAFE-MM-C, which targets culture-specific vulnerabilities by constructing localized visual queries from real Korean contexts and pairing them with jailbreak-style textual prompts to create challenging multimodal attack scenarios. They then benchmark 12 state-of-the-art MLLMs on these datasets, measuring attack success rates (ASR) under standard and jailbreak-style prompts, and analyzing the trade-off between safety (low ASR) and over-refusal on benign inputs.

Result: Experiments on 12 SOTA MLLMs show that models are significantly more vulnerable to culturally grounded multimodal attacks than to generic safety queries. Jailbreak strategies, particularly ProgramExecution, greatly increase attack success rates, achieving up to 74.2% ASR versus 13.4% with standard queries. Additionally, models that achieve lower ASR (i.e., appear safer) tend to exhibit high levels of over-refusal, declining to respond even to benign inputs, revealing a systematic trade-off between safety and usability.

Conclusion: KSAFE-MM demonstrates that existing English-centric, generic safety benchmarks underestimate the real safety risks of MLLMs in non-English and culturally specific multimodal contexts. The strong performance gaps under localized, jailbroken attacks and the observed safety–over-refusal trade-off indicate that culturally grounded, multimodal safety evaluation is crucial. The authors argue for future safety research and benchmark design that explicitly incorporates local cultural cues, languages like Korean, and realistic multimodal jailbreak settings.

Abstract: Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) exacerbate safety risks by introducing vulnerabilities across multiple modalities, such as language and vision. Current MLLM safety evaluation tools, however, suffer from major limitations: 1) English-centric dataset construction, and 2) a focus on generic risks that are not tied to local cultural contexts. This paper introduces KSAFE-MM, a benchmark for Korean multimodal safety evaluation that covers both general safety risks and culture-specific vulnerabilities. KSAFE-MM consists of two parts, KSAFE-MM-G and KSAFE-MM-C. KSAFE-MM-G evaluates globally shared risks in Korean contexts through linguistic contextualization, which transforms generic safety queries into contextually grounded multimodal samples. KSAFE-MM-C targets culture-dependent MLLM safety vulnerabilities using localized visual queries derived from real-world contexts. It pairs these visual queries with jailbreak-style textual queries to cover multimodal safety risks involving cultural visual cues and malicious textual intent. Together, these components provide a general-to-local construction pipeline for evaluating both globally shared safety risks and culture-specific vulnerabilities. We evaluate 12 state-of-the-art MLLMs on KSAFE-MM and reveal that models exhibit greater vulnerability to culturally grounded attacks than to generic ones. Notably, jailbreaking strategies substantially amplify attack success rates, with ProgramExecution yielding up to 74.2% ASR compared to 13.4% for standard queries. Furthermore, we identify a systematic trade-off between safety and over-refusal, where models achieving low ASR tend to exhibit excessive refusal behavior on benign queries. These findings highlight the urgent need for culturally grounded safety evaluation beyond English-centric benchmarks.

</details>


### [61] [ROSD: Reflective On-Policy Self-Distillation for Language Model Reasoning across Domains](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28014)
*Ziqi Zhao,Xinyu Ma,Liu Yang,Yujie Feng,Daiting Shi,Jingzhou He,Xin Xin,Zhaochun Ren,Xiao-Ming Wu*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces Reflective On-policy Self-Distillation (ROSD), a method that improves large language models’ reasoning by correcting only the identified erroneous span of on-policy rollouts using reflection-guided supervision, leading to better in-domain performance and stronger out-of-domain generalization than standard OPSD.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing on-policy self-distillation (OPSD) methods give dense token-level supervision for on-policy rollouts, but they show limited improvement on in-domain reasoning and generalize poorly to out-of-domain tasks. Two diagnosed problems are: (1) conditioning the self-teacher on a full verified solution makes the student imitate whole reference trajectories instead of learning how to correct specific mistakes; (2) distilling over the entire response can overwrite correct reasoning prefixes, reinforcing overfitting to the training distribution and harming generalization.

Method: The authors propose Reflective On-policy Self-Distillation (ROSD). For each model rollout, a self-reflector module analyzes the solution, extracts a concise corrective idea, and pinpoints the first span where the reasoning goes wrong. The corrective idea is then used to condition a self-teacher model that generates targeted supervision focused on how to fix that specific type of error. Distillation is applied only on the localized erroneous span, not on the entire response, which preserves already-correct reasoning prefixes while injecting corrections where they are needed. This yields reflection-guided, error-localized on-policy self-distillation.

Result: Across multiple reasoning benchmarks, covering both in-domain and out-of-domain settings, ROSD leads to higher in-domain reasoning accuracy than standard OPSD methods and substantially better out-of-domain generalization performance. The experimental results demonstrate that targeting only the erroneous spans with reflection-guided supervision is more effective than full-response imitation-based distillation.

Conclusion: ROSD reframes on-policy self-distillation from global imitation of reference solutions to localized correction of identified reasoning errors. By using a self-reflector to derive corrective ideas and localize the first erroneous span, and restricting distillation to that span, the method improves reasoning quality without overwriting valid prefixes. This design yields stronger in-domain gains and significantly improved out-of-domain generalization compared to standard OPSD, suggesting that reflection-guided, error-localized supervision is a more robust way to improve LLM reasoning.

Abstract: On-policy self-distillation (OPSD) improves the reasoning performance of large language models (LLMs) by providing dense token-level supervision for on-policy rollouts. However, existing OPSD methods often yield limited gains on in-domain reasoning and generalize poorly to out-of-domain problems. We identify two key causes: conditioning the self-teacher on a verified solution encourages imitation of training-domain reference trajectories rather than error-specific correction, and applying distillation to the full response can overwrite valid reasoning prefixes and reinforce overfitting.
  We propose Reflective On-policy Self-Distillation (ROSD), a framework that turns reference-solution imitation into targeted reasoning correction through reflection-guided, error-localized distillation. For each rollout, ROSD uses a self-reflector to extract a corrective idea and locate the first erroneous span. The corrective idea guides the self-teacher toward targeted supervision, while the localized error span restricts distillation to where correction is needed. This design corrects flawed reasoning while preserving valid prefixes. Experiments on multiple in-domain and out-of-domain reasoning benchmarks show that ROSD yields stronger in-domain reasoning performance overall and substantially better out-of-domain generalization than standard OPSD. Code is available at https://github.com/ZiqiZhao1/ROSD.

</details>
