### [62] [The Missing Piece in Pre-trained Model Evaluation: Reward-Guided Decoding Unlocks Task-Oriented Behavior Without Parameter Updates](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28020)
*Shaobo Wang,Guo Chen,Ziyue Wang,Zhengyang Tang,Qingyang Liu,Xingzhang Ren,Dayiheng Liu,Linfeng Zhang*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces Energy-Based Decoding (EBD), a training-free, reward-guided decoding method that activates task-oriented behavior in frozen base LLMs to better evaluate their true capabilities.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Base pre-trained LLMs are trained for next-token prediction, not instruction following, so under standard prompting they often produce poor, off-task, or unstructured answers. This makes benchmark performance a mix of model capability and decoding failures, and existing fixes either require expensive post-training (e.g., RLHF, SFT) or brittle, inefficient decoding-only tricks. The authors want a way to fairly evaluate and use base models by eliciting their latent capabilities at inference time, without further training.

Method: They propose Energy-Based Decoding (EBD), which augments the standard decoding of a frozen base LLM with an external reward model. At each decoding step, EBD defines a reward-tilted target distribution that balances staying close to the base model’s original token distribution (the prior) while favoring tokens with high predicted utility according to the reward model. This is implemented as an energy-based objective over candidate tokens, effectively steering generation toward instruction-following, high-quality outputs while remaining training-free. The framework is applicable to both open-ended and objective tasks and is designed to be efficient and robust to different reward-model sizes.

Result: Across five different base models and six benchmarks, EBD consistently improves performance over standard decoding and other decoding-only approaches. For example, on AlpacaEval2.0, it boosts Qwen3-8B-Base’s score from 8.8 to 44.5, indicating much stronger instruction following. On the Math500 benchmark with Mistral-7B, EBD achieves a 18.9x latency reduction relative to prior decoding-based methods, while maintaining or improving accuracy. The method also shows robustness to the choice and size of the reward model, retaining performance gains without heavy tuning.

Conclusion: EBD demonstrates that much of the latent capability of base pre-trained LLMs can be unlocked at inference time by reward-guided decoding, without any additional training. This leads to more instruction-following and task-oriented behavior, making evaluations of base models fairer and more reflective of their underlying competence. The approach is more efficient and robust than prior decoding-only techniques and can serve as a practical bridge between raw base models and fully post-trained instruction-following models.

Abstract: With the rapid progress of large language models (LLMs), reliably evaluating the capabilities of pre-trained LLMs has become increasingly important. The challenge is that base pre-trained models are optimized for next-token prediction and often fail to follow instructions or produce well-formed answers under standard prompting and direct decoding. As a result, benchmark performance can conflate model capability with decoding-induced failures to produce task-oriented outputs, while exposing such behavior often relies on costly post-training. Recent decodingonly approaches attempt to reshape output distributions, but such methods can be inefficient and brittle across open-ended tasks. To address these limitations, we propose Energy-Based Decoding (EBD), a training-free, reward-guided framework for activating task-oriented behaviors from frozen pre-trained LLMs across both open-ended and objective tasks. EBD augments decoding with an external lightweight reward model, steering generations toward high-utility responses while anchoring them to the pre-trained model prior through a reward-tilted target distribution. We show that EBD shifts base-model outputs toward more instructionfollowing behavior, increasing behavioral similarity to post-trained counterparts and enabling a fairer inference-time evaluation of accessible pre-trained-model behavior. Empirically, EBD outperforms baselines across five models and six benchmarks, improving Qwen3-8B-Base on AlpacaEval2.0 from 8.8 to 44.5, reducing Mistral-7B Math500 latency by 18.9x relative to prior decoding work, and remaining robust to reward-model size.

</details>


### [63] [Beyond pass@k: Redundancy-Aware RLVR for Multi-Sample Code Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28022)
*Le Bronnec Florian,Alexandre Verine,Rio Yokota,Benjamin Negrevergne*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper studies redundancy in code generated by large language models under Pass@k evaluation, showing that standard correctness-driven RL tends to produce many similar solutions, while adding anti-redundancy rewards or Pass@k-aware objectives yields more diverse generations and better finite-budget performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Code LLMs are usually evaluated using Pass@k, which depends on having multiple diverse correct candidates within a finite sampling budget. Recent reinforcement learning methods that optimize for correctness improve accuracy but may unintentionally encourage the model to repeatedly generate the same or very similar implementations, wasting the sampling budget. The community lacks a clear understanding and quantitative analysis of this redundancy and how training objectives affect it.

Method: The authors use JPlag, a code plagiarism-detection system, to measure implementation-level similarity among multiple samples generated by LLMs for code benchmarks. They compare redundancy patterns across different training objectives: standard correctness-only verifier-based RL (RLVR) and Pass@k-aware objectives. Based on their findings, they propose augmenting RLVR with an explicit anti-redundancy reward that penalizes near-duplicate generations according to JPlag similarity, and evaluate this across 3 models and 3 benchmarks.

Result: Empirically, they find that correctness-only RLVR tends to concentrate probability mass on a few repeated implementations, leading to high redundancy, while Pass@k-aware objectives produce more diverse solutions and yield better performance when more samples are allowed (larger Pass@k budgets). When they add JPlag-based anti-redundancy rewards to RLVR, the resulting models consistently reduce near-duplicate generations and improve executable accuracy under finite sampling budgets, often matching or surpassing specialized Pass@k-aware methods.

Conclusion: Implementation-level redundancy is a critical factor in the real-world effectiveness of code-generation LLMs under Pass@k evaluation. Objectives that focus solely on correctness can harm performance at larger sampling budgets by over-concentrating on a few solutions. Incorporating diversity-aware components—either via Pass@k-aware training or explicit anti-redundancy rewards based on similarity metrics like JPlag—can significantly improve finite-budget executable performance and reduce wasted samples on near-duplicate code.

Abstract: LLMs for code generation are commonly evaluated in repeated-sampling settings using Pass@k, where multiple candidate programs are executed against unit tests under a finite sampling budget. While recent verifier-based reinforcement learning (RLVR) methods improve executable correctness, how these objectives affect redundancy among sampled programs remains poorly understood. In this work, we study implementation-level redundancy in code generation using JPlag, a plagiarism-detection system for code. Across models and benchmarks, we show that correctness-only RLVR often concentrates generations around repeated implementations, whereas Pass@k-aware objectives maintain lower redundancy and improve larger-budget performance. Motivated by these observations, we augment RLVR with direct anti-redundancy rewards based on JPlag similarity. Across 3 models and 3 benchmarks, discouraging near-duplicate generations reliably improves finite-budget executable performance, often matching or outperforming specialized Pass@k-aware objectives.

</details>


### [64] [Personality, Role, and Expressive Style in Large Language Models: An Interactionist Analysis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28037)
*Moe Nagao,Koichiro Terao,Mikio Nakano,Naoto Iwahashi*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper studies how well prompt-based personality instructions actually control LLM behavior, finding that personality expression depends on trait prompts, dialogue roles, and expressive style, and that these factors interact in trait-specific and language-specific ways.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To design dialogue agents with stable, controllable personalities, but existing practice assumes that simply prompting Big Five traits is sufficient; the authors suspect that actual expressed personality depends on interaction with contextual factors like social role and communication style.

Method: They adopt an interactionist framework and use a factorial experimental design combining six personality prompt conditions, three dialogue roles, and three expressive style instructions to generate 1,080 dialogues each in English and Japanese. They then use an LLM-as-a-judge setup to rate the target agent's utterances on Big Five traits and analyze how traits vary with prompts, role, style, and language.

Result: They find that expressed personality is not determined solely by the explicit Big Five trait instructions. Dialogue role strongly affects perceived Openness, expressive style has large effects on perceived Conscientiousness and Agreeableness, and explicit trait prompts have the largest influence on Neuroticism. Even when no personality traits are explicitly specified, the combination of social role and expressive style yields systematic, personality-like impressions. Cross-linguistic analysis shows similar patterns in English and Japanese with some differences in specific personality–role–style combinations.

Conclusion: Personality control in LLM agents is a context-dependent process: explicit Big Five prompts, dialogue roles, and expressive styles jointly shape perceived personality expression. Therefore, developers should treat personality design as configuring an interaction context rather than merely stating trait labels, and should consider language-specific nuances when deploying multilingual agents.

Abstract: Prompt-based personality control is a key technique for designing large language model (LLM) dialogue agents that behave consistently across social contexts. However, specifying Big Five personality traits (BFTs) in a prompt does not ensure that the intended traits are expressed in generated utterances. This paper investigates this mismatch from an interactionist perspective, viewing personality expression as a context-dependent outcome shaped by the interplay between trait specification and situational factors. We analyze how perceived BFT expression in LLM-generated dialogue is influenced by three prompt factors: personality traits, dialogue roles, and expressive styles. Using a factorial design that combines six personality conditions, three roles, and three expressive-style conditions, we generate 1,080 LLM-agent dialogues in each of English and Japanese. We then evaluate the target agent's utterances using an LLM-as-a-judge framework to estimate expressed Big Five traits. The results show that expressed personality is shaped not only by explicit trait specification, but also by dialogue role and expressive style. These effects are trait-specific: dialogue role strongly influences Openness, expressive style substantially shapes Conscientiousness and Agreeableness, and explicit trait specification dominates Neuroticism. Even without explicit personality-trait specification, social and expressive conditions induce distinct personality-like impressions. Cross-linguistic comparisons show broadly similar patterns between English and Japanese dialogues, with noticeable differences only under specific combinations of personality, role, and expressive style. These findings suggest that personality control in LLM agents should be understood not as a direct consequence of trait prompting, but as a context-dependent process involving personality specification, social role, and expressive style.

</details>


### [65] [Extracting Small Translation Specialists from LLMs by Aggressively Pruning Experts](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28042)
*Liu O. Martin,Lucas Bandarkar,Nanyun Peng*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: They prune most experts from mixture-of-experts LLMs and keep almost the same translation quality, greatly shrinking the model.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Modern LLMs used for machine translation are overparameterized because they are trained as broad generalists; this leads to unnecessary memory and compute costs when the goal is only translation.

Method: They analyze expert specialization and multilingual capability separability in mixture-of-experts (MoE) LLMs to detect experts that are irrelevant for translation. Leveraging the modular MoE design, they prune these experts directly, then optionally apply a short supervised fine-tuning (SFT) to recover performance after more aggressive pruning.

Result: Without any retraining, they can prune about 50% of experts with negligible quality loss and 70% with only minor losses. With a short SFT, they prune 75% of experts while matching baseline performance and in some scenarios prune nearly 90% while still achieving reasonable translation quality. The compressed MoE blocks account for over 90% of the model parameters, so this yields large overall compression.

Conclusion: Only a relatively small subset of experts in MoE LLMs is needed for high-quality translation. By pruning unused experts, one can dramatically reduce model size and resource requirements for translation-focused deployment, while preserving most of the original translation performance.

Abstract: Modern large language models (LLMs) achieve state-of-the-art machine translation performance, but they do so as broad generalists largely trained for many tasks and capabilities unrelated to translation. Thus, they are heavily overparameterized for this task, resulting in excessive memory and compute requirements. In this paper, we present a method for aggressively pruning experts from modern mixture-of-experts LLMs while incurring negligible degradation in translation quality. Our approach exploits expert specialization and the separability of multilingual capabilities in LLMs to identify experts irrelevant to translation. And because of the modular nature of MoEs, these can be easily pruned without any training. Without retraining, we are able to prune half of all experts with negligible degradation and 70% with only minor losses. With a very short SFT, we prune 75% of experts while recovering baseline performance, and in some settings remove nearly 90% while maintaining reasonable translation quality. Overall, our results show that translation requires only a fraction of the LLM, enabling substantial compression of the MoE blocks that contain over 90% of parameters.

</details>


### [66] [Knowledge Dependency Estimation for Reliable Question Answering](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28047)
*Chaodong Tong,Qi Zhang,Nannan Sun,Lei Jiang,Yanbing Liu*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces Knot, a method to estimate which pieces of knowledge a black-box QA model truly relies on, without expensive perturbation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: In LLM-based QA, knowledge can come from many noisy and redundant sources (retrieval, context, intermediate reasoning). We need to know which specific knowledge units a model’s answer depends on, for faithfulness and reliability, but it’s hard to measure this dependency without exhaustively perturbing inputs and while accounting for redundancy and interactions among knowledge pieces.

Method: They formalize knowledge dependency estimation as predicting a QA model’s sensitivity to subsets of candidate knowledge units. They propose Knot, which (1) is trained using subset-level counterfactual supervision (observing how the QA model’s output changes when given different subsets); (2) represents subset sensitivity via coverage over latent dependency factors, capturing redundancy and complementarity; and (3) derives rank-aware scores for individual units from this structured subset model, yielding an ordered list of influential knowledge candidates without extra QA calls at test time.

Result: On both multiple-choice and generative QA benchmarks, Knot achieves better prediction of subset sensitivity compared to baselines and produces more faithful rankings of influential knowledge units. It matches or beats methods that would require many more QA model calls, remaining deployable in practice.

Conclusion: Knot provides an efficient, structured way to estimate which knowledge units a black-box QA system depends on, enabling better interpretability, more accurate identification of influential evidence, and practical risk screening by highlighting answers that rely on fragile or uncertain knowledge.

Abstract: Reliable question answering requires identifying not only whether an answer is correct, but also which available knowledge the prediction depends on. In realistic LLM-based QA, this knowledge may come from context, retrieval, decomposition, or intermediate reasoning, forming a noisy and redundant candidate space rather than a clean gold evidence set. We study \emph{knowledge dependency estimation}: estimating the sensitivity of a fixed black-box QA model to different candidate knowledge units. The challenge is to obtain fine-grained dependency scores without exhaustive test-time perturbation while modeling redundancy, substitutability, and complementarity. We propose \textbf{Knot}, a structured rank-aware knowledge dependency estimator. Knot learns from subset-level counterfactual supervision, models subset sensitivity through coverage over latent dependency factors, and derives rank-aware unit scores to identify influential candidates. Across multiple-choice and generative QA benchmarks, Knot outperforms all compared baselines in subset-sensitivity prediction and produces more faithful unit rankings than deployable baselines without extra QA-model calls; when used for practical risk screening, its dependency scores help flag error-prone QA predictions early.

</details>


### [67] [Prompting Is All You Need: Multi-view Prompting Large Language Models for Aspect-Based Sentiment Analysis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28058)
*Nils Constantin Hellwig,Niklas Donhauser,Jakob Fehle,Udo Kruschwitz,Christian Wolff*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper proposes LLM-based Multi-View Prompting (LLM-MvP) to make few-shot LLMs for Aspect-Based Sentiment Analysis as strong and efficient as fine-tuned models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Few-shot prompting with LLMs for Aspect-Based Sentiment Analysis reduces annotation needs and beats zero-shot performance, but still trails fine-tuned models and is expensive to run due to high LLM inference costs. There is a need for a method that narrows this performance gap without requiring large-scale fine-tuning and while being computationally efficient.

Method: The authors adapt the multi-view principle—using multiple element orderings of the same input—to LLM prompting. They design prompts that represent different views of the ABSA task and use schema-constrained decoding guided by a context-free grammar so outputs follow a strict format. They further apply prefix batching to process multiple prompts efficiently in parallel, reducing per-instance computation cost.

Result: Across five ABSA benchmark datasets, LLM-MvP achieves performance that is competitive with or better than models fine-tuned on hundreds of labeled examples, while also lowering the computational cost of LLM inference compared to straightforward few-shot prompting.

Conclusion: LLM-MvP effectively closes the performance gap between few-shot prompted LLMs and fine-tuned models for Aspect-Based Sentiment Analysis, and does so with reduced computational overhead, making it a practical and efficient approach for real-world ABSA applications.

Abstract: Recent work explored the capabilities of Large Language Models (LLMs) in Aspect-Based Sentiment Analysis (ABSA) through few-shot prompting, requiring substantially fewer annotated examples while achieving notable improvements over zero-shot baselines. However, a performance gap remained compared to models fine-tuned on hundreds of examples, and the computational costs of LLM inference present practical barriers to deployment. We introduce LLM-based Multi-View Prompting (LLM-MvP), which adapts the multi-view principle of considering multiple element orderings to LLM prompting. By combining schema-constrained decoding with a context-free grammar and prefix batching, LLM-MvP achieves performance competitive or superior to fine-tuned approaches while substantially reducing computational overhead. Extensive experiments across five benchmark datasets demonstrate that LLM-MvP closes the gap between few-shot prompting and fine-tuned models, offering a practical and efficient solution for ABSA.

</details>


### [68] [Challenges in Explaining Pretrained Clinical Text Classifiers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28060)
*Kristian Miok,Matej Klemen,Blaz Škrlj,Marko Robnik Šikonja*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper critiques common explanation methods (LIME, SHAP) for neural models in clinical NLP, showing they fail on complex, long medical texts and arguing for better, more robust and clinically meaningful explanation strategies.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Neural models are increasingly used in clinical NLP for tasks like predicting hospital length of stay from long, unstructured medical notes, but their predictions are hard to interpret. Existing post-hoc explanation tools (e.g., LIME, SHAP) are widely adopted yet may not be reliable or adequate for such complex, high-stakes settings. There is a need to systematically understand where and how these methods break down in clinical narratives.

Method: The authors conduct targeted demonstrations on a hospital length-of-stay prediction task using neural models applied to long, unstructured clinical narratives. They apply token-level and perturbation-based explanation techniques (such as LIME and SHAP), then analyze their behavior and outputs, focusing on issues like attribution patterns, stability of explanations under small changes, and model confidence on modified (incoherent) inputs.

Result: They find that token-level and perturbation-based explanation methods often overemphasize non-informative tokens, produce unstable and inconsistent attributions across similar texts, and can yield high-confidence model predictions even for incoherent or corrupted input variants. These behaviors indicate that the explanations are not reliably aligned with clinically meaningful evidence in the text.

Conclusion: Standard post-hoc explanation techniques like LIME and SHAP have serious limitations when used with neural models on clinical narratives, particularly for complex tasks with long, noisy texts. The paper concludes that new explanation strategies are required—ones that are semantically grounded, robust to linguistic noise, and provide clinically meaningful rationales for model decisions.

Abstract: Explaining the predictions of neural models in clinical NLP remains a significant challenge, especially for complex tasks involving long, unstructured medical texts. While post-hoc methods like LIME and SHAP are widely used, they often fall short when applied to clinical narratives. In this paper, we identify core limitations of token-level and perturbation-based explanation techniques through targeted demonstra- tions on a hospital length-of-stay prediction task. Our findings reveal issues such as overemphasis on non-informative tokens, instability in at- tributions, and high-confidence predictions for incoherent input variants. These results underscore the need for explanation strategies that are clin- ically meaningful, semantically grounded, and robust to linguistic noise.

</details>


### [69] [ConvMemory: A Lightweight Learned Memory Reranker, a Negative Attribution Result, and a Research-Preview Conflict Editor](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28062)
*Taiheng Pan*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: ConvMemory is a tiny, efficient learned reranker for conversational long-term memory that rivals much larger cross-encoders in recall with far lower latency and cost, and whose true mechanism is cost-effective distillation in a fused dense+lexical space rather than sophisticated temporal reasoning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Improve conversational long-term memory retrieval with a reranker that is fast and cheap enough for production use while remaining competitive with state-of-the-art large cross-encoders, and to rigorously test earlier claims about temporal reasoning mechanisms in such models.

Method: Build a 3.6M-parameter ConvMemory reranker trained via distillation from a cross-encoder teacher over a fused dense (embedding-based) and lexical (term-based) feature space; evaluate it on LongMemEval, Clean500, Stress1000, and LoCoMo retrieval benchmarks; perform a five-seed retrained ablation with paired bootstrap to analyze the effect and specificity of the model's learned temporal window; and develop CCGE-LA, a low-amplitude conflict-aware candidate-set editor stacked on ConvMemory.

Result: ConvMemory achieves Recall@10 close to strong cross-encoders like BGE-large and mxbai-rerank-large-v1 while running 12–117x faster and up to 28x cheaper, though it does not match them in absolute MRR on LoCoMo; analysis shows the temporal-window mechanism is statistically significant in aggregate but not temporally specific, with most gains on hard non-temporal controls and no significant improvement on multi-hop temporal queries; CCGE-LA yields modest but consistent improvements on supersession and stale/rescue slices in LoCoMo.

Conclusion: ConvMemory provides an attractive cost–accuracy tradeoff for conversational long-term memory reranking, demonstrating that small models distilled from cross-encoders over fused dense+lexical features can be highly effective without truly exploiting temporal structure; prior claims about temporal reasoning mechanisms are revised accordingly, and the released CCGE-LA editor shows additional, incremental benefits for handling conflict and stale information in retrieval pipelines.

Abstract: We describe ConvMemory, a small 3.6M-parameter learned reranker for conversational long-term memory retrieval, trained with cross-encoder teacher supervision over fused dense and lexical features. On the LongMemEval memory family, ConvMemory operates above the BGE-large cross-encoder in Recall@10 at 12-47x lower latency, remains within 0.025 Recall@10 of mxbai-rerank-large-v1 on Clean500 while running 28x cheaper; under Stress1000 distractors the Recall@10 gap widens to 0.081 but ConvMemory still operates at 117x lower latency; these LongMemEval numbers are single-run or single-seed and are reported as indicative cost-frontier evidence, not benchmark-grade. We then publish a rigorous negative attribution result on a previously claimed mechanism: a five-seed retrained ablation with paired bootstrap shows that ConvMemory's learned temporal window is statistically significant on aggregate but not temporally specific, with the largest effects on hard non-temporal controls and no significant effect on multi-hop temporal queries. The honest description of the mechanism is cheap cross-encoder distillation in a fused dense+lexical feature space, not temporal-structure exploitation. We additionally release CCGE-LA, a low-amplitude conflict-aware candidate-set editor over ConvMemory, as a research preview with modest but consistent gains on supersession and stale/rescue slices on LoCoMo. All results are retrieval-stage; ConvMemory does not match mxbai-rerank-large-v1 in absolute LoCoMo MRR, and the report is single-author and not yet independently audited.

</details>


### [70] [PromptEmbedder:: Efficient and Transferable Text Embedding via Dual-LLM Soft Prompting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28066)
*Yu-Che Tsai,Kuan-Yu Chen,Yuan-Hao Chen,Yu-Han Chang,Ching-Yu Tsai,Yu-Hsiang Chuang,Shou-De Lin*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: PromptEmbedder introduces a dual-LLM framework where one LLM learns to generate soft prompts for a frozen embedding LLM, achieving LoRA-level performance on MTEB with far less memory and faster training, and enabling easy transfer to new architectures via a small linear alignment layer.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing LLM adaptation methods for embeddings, such as LoRA, tightly couple task-specific knowledge to a specific model’s weights, making them computationally expensive and non-transferable when new backbones appear, since they require full retraining. The authors aim to design a more efficient, architecture-agnostic way to adapt LLMs for embedding tasks that reduces GPU memory and training time while preserving accuracy.

Method: They propose PromptEmbedder, a dual-LLM setup with (1) a Prompting LLM that generates instruction-aware soft prompts through a differentiable generation process with continuous relaxation, and (2) a frozen Embedding LLM that consumes these soft prompts to produce task-specific embeddings. Contrastive training is used, and gradients flow fully through the Prompting LLM thanks to the differentiable generation. Task knowledge is localized in the Prompting LLM, while alignment to different embedding backbones is done by learning a lightweight linear alignment matrix for each new architecture.

Result: On the MTEB benchmark, PromptEmbedder attains performance comparable to LoRA finetuning but reduces GPU memory consumption by 40% and speeds up training by 3.7x, demonstrating that the method is both efficient and effective for embedding tasks.

Conclusion: The paper concludes that decoupling embedding knowledge from backbone weights via a dual-LLM, prompt-generating framework yields a scalable, architecture-agnostic paradigm for LLM-based representation learning. This approach preserves accuracy while significantly improving efficiency and simplifying transfer to new model architectures, requiring only a small linear alignment layer for adaptation.

Abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) have demonstrated remarkable efficacy in text embedding, yet current adaptation methods like LoRA face significant bottlenecks in computational efficiency and cross-architecture transferability. Whenever a new backbone emerges, existing approaches require costly retraining from scratch. To address this, we propose PromptEmbedder, a novel dual-LLM framework that decouples embedding knowledge from specific backbone weights. PromptEmbedder utilizes a Prompting LLM to generate instruction-aware soft prompts for a frozen Embedding LLM via a differentiable generation process with continuous relaxation, ensuring full gradient flow during contrastive training. By localizing task-specific knowledge within the Prompting LLM, adapting to new architectures requires only retraining a lightweight linear alignment matrix. Evaluations on the MTEB benchmark show that PromptEmbedder achieves comparable performance with LoRA finetuning while reducing GPU memory by 40% and accelerating training by 3.7x. Our approach establishes a scalable, architecture-agnostic paradigm for efficient LLM-based representation learning.

</details>


### [71] [StoryLens: Preference-Aligned Story Rewriting via Context-Aware Narrative Enrichment](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28073)
*Hanwen Cui,Yuting Mei,Yuhang Fu,Dingyi Yang,Qin Jin*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces a benchmark, evaluation framework, and models for context-aware, preference-aligned story rewriting, showing that enriching narrative context is much more important than surface-level style transfer for reader satisfaction.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing story style transfer focuses mainly on surface-level stylistic changes and fails to significantly improve reader satisfaction. The authors’ human study shows that merely adapting style yields only marginal benefits, revealing a gap: there is a need for story rewriting that better accounts for reader preferences through deeper, context-aware narrative changes.

Method: The authors (1) conduct a pilot human study comparing style-only adaptation with context-enhanced rewriting; (2) build STORYLENSBENCH, a large-scale benchmark containing structured story books, reader preference profiles across multiple dimensions, and ranked rewritten stories with additional context; (3) propose STORYLENSEVAL, a reward model that scores rewritten stories based on estimated reader satisfaction; and (4) develop STORYLENSWRITER, a two-stage rewriting system that first uses supervised fine-tuning and then GRPO-based reinforcement learning to align outputs with preferences and reward model feedback. They also design an evaluation protocol covering fidelity, coherence, and satisfaction metrics.

Result: The human study shows style-only adaptation improves satisfaction by only 2.3%, whereas context-enhanced rewriting improves user preference alignment by 24.5%. In experiments on the new benchmark, STORYLENSWRITER consistently outperforms strong generation and personalization baselines across fidelity, coherence, and reader satisfaction metrics. This validates both the benchmark and the proposed modeling approach.

Conclusion: Context-aware narrative enrichment is crucial for effective personalized story rewriting, far beyond surface style transfer. The introduced benchmark (STORYLENSBENCH), evaluation model (STORYLENSEVAL), and writing system (STORYLENSWRITER) provide a comprehensive framework and state-of-the-art performance for aligning rewritten stories with reader preferences, as demonstrated by substantial gains in user satisfaction and preference alignment.

Abstract: Story rewriting aims to adapt existing narratives to diverse reader preferences while preserving plot consistency and narrative coherence. Unlike conventional work on style transfer, we argue that effective story rewriting demands context-aware narrative enrichment beyond surface-level stylistic adaptation. Our pilot human study shows that style adaptation alone provides only marginal gains in reader satisfaction (2.3%), while context-enhanced rewriting substantially improves user preference alignment (24.5%). Motivated by this, we introduce STORYLENSBENCH, a large-scale benchmark for preference-aligned story rewriting, comprising structured story books, multi-dimensional reader preference profiles, and ranked context-aware rewritten stories. Building on this benchmark, we propose STORYLENSEVAL, a reward model for estimating reader satisfaction over rewritten stories, and STORYLENSWRITER, a two-stage rewriting model combining supervised fine-tuning with GRPO-based reinforcement learning. We further establish a comprehensive evaluation framework covering fidelity, coherence, and reader satisfaction. Experimental results demonstrate that STORYLENSWRITER consistently outperforms strong generation and personalization baselines, highlighting the importance of context-aware narrative enrichment for personalized story rewriting.

</details>


### [72] [ATLAS: All-round Testing of Long-context Abilities across Scales](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28079)
*Deli Huang,Cunguang Wang,Hongyin Tang,Zhe Tang,Linsen Guo,Dongyu Ru,Ruoshi Yuan,Ziyue Zhu,Xiaoyu Li,Ziwen Wang,Chen Zhang,Anchun Gui,Wen Zan,Jiaqi Zhang,Xuezhi Cao,Jingang Wang,Xunliang Cai,Yixin Cao*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: ATLAS is a framework for systematically evaluating long-context language models across context lengths and capability types instead of using a single headline metric.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing long-context benchmarks usually test only one context length or narrow task types, which hides important failure modes: models often degrade as context grows and good retrieval performance doesn’t necessarily mean good downstream task performance. A more granular, length-aware, and capability-aware evaluation is needed.

Method: The authors introduce ATLAS, a benchmarking framework built on three principles: (i) a layered taxonomy that separates low-level foundational operations from higher-level application workloads, allowing attribution of failures; (ii) length-aware AUC scoring that integrates performance over a fixed 8K–1M token grid to obtain full degradation profiles instead of single-point scores; and (iii) ATLAScore, a harmonic-mean aggregate over taxonomy categories that penalizes imbalanced capabilities and propagates uncertainty from sub-scores through the nonlinear final score. They implement this with eight capability dimensions, nine benchmark components, and 6,438 instances, then evaluate 26 language models.

Result: Using ATLAS, they profile 26 long-context models. They find that model rankings change substantially when comparing medium (8K–128K) versus very long (8K–1M) context ranges: seven models shift by at least two ranks, some by as many as 12 positions. The two taxonomy layers (foundational vs application) explain only 61% of each other’s variance, showing that strengths in one layer don’t straightforwardly transfer to the other. They identify Gemini-3.1-Pro-Preview as leading at 128K context and Claude-Opus-4.6 as leading at 1M context.

Conclusion: Long-context model quality should be reported as a profile over both capability dimensions and context lengths, not summarized by a single score or maximum window size. ATLAS provides a structured, length-aware evaluation method and composite score (ATLAScore) that reveal degradation patterns and capability imbalances that single-point or single-task benchmarks miss.

Abstract: Long-context language models now advertise context windows up to millions of tokens, yet evaluations typically report a single length or a narrow task family, masking two failure modes: performance can collapse as length grows, and strong retrieval need not transfer to downstream use. We present ATLAS, a benchmarking framework that redefines long-context evaluation as length-dependent capability profiling. ATLAS contributes three methodological principles:(i) a layered taxonomy separating foundational operations from application workloads so failures can be attributed, (ii) length-aware AUC scoring that integrates score-length curves over a fixed 8K-1M grid, replacing single-point metrics with full degradation profiles, and (iii) ATLAScore, a harmonic-mean aggregate over taxonomy categories that penalizes imbalanced profiles, with end-to-end uncertainty propagation from subset scores through the nonlinear final aggregate. We instantiate the framework across eight capability dimensions with nine auditable components and 6,438 instances, and evaluate 26 models. Gemini-3.1-Pro-Preview leads at 128K, Claude-Opus-4.6 leads at 1M. Rankings reshuffle substantially between ATLASscore@8K-128K and ATLASscore@8K-1M: 7 models move by at least two ranks, and the two taxonomy layers share only 61% of cross-model variance, with individual rank gaps up to 12 positions. These results support reporting long-context quality by capability and length, not by a single headline score.

</details>


### [73] [ConRAG: Consensus-Driven Multi-View Retrieval for Multi-Hop Question Answering](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28093)
*Yikai Zhu,Kunfeng Chen,Qihuang Zhong,Juhua Liu,Bo Du*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: ConRAG is a consensus-driven, multi-view retrieval-augmented generation framework that jointly optimizes query and corpus processing to improve multi-hop question answering for LLMs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing multi-hop RAG systems either decompose queries or build corpus-side knowledge graphs, but they still underperform on complex multi-hop QA because they do not fully exploit diverse evidence types or jointly optimize both query and corpus aspects.

Method: ConRAG introduces a consensus-driven multi-view RAG framework that simultaneously refines the query representation and structures the corpus, leveraging multiple evidence views—relation, entity, and textual signals—to retrieve more accurate and complementary document sets for multi-hop reasoning.

Result: On three multi-hop QA benchmarks, ConRAG surpasses all baselines with substantial margins, achieving up to 26.9% average improvement over vanilla RAG and enabling the Gemma-4-31B model to set a new state-of-the-art on the challenging MuSiQue dataset.

Conclusion: Jointly optimizing query and corpus sides and integrating relation, entity, and text views for retrieval significantly boosts LLM performance on complex multi-hop QA, establishing ConRAG as a new state-of-the-art multi-hop RAG framework.

Abstract: Retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) has emerged as a promising paradigm for enhancing large language models (LLMs) on multi-hop question answering (QA), which requires reasoning over evidence from multiple documents. Current multi-hop RAG methods generally focus on either query-side task decomposition or corpus-side knowledge graph construction. Despite their progress, these methods still struggle to achieve satisfactory performance on complex multi-hop QA tasks. To this end, we propose ConRAG, a consensus-driven multi-view RAG framework that effectively boosts LLMs on complex multi-hop QA. The core of ConRAG is to systematically optimize both the query and corpus sides and to leverage multi-view evidence (relation, entity, and text signals) for more accurate retrieval. Extensive experiments on three multi-hop QA benchmarks show that ConRAG consistently outperforms all baselines by a clear margin, e.g., up to +26.9% average performance gains over vanilla RAG, and enables Gemma-4-31B to achieve a new state-of-the-art record on the challenging MuSiQue benchmark.

</details>


### [74] [Ask Now, Use Later: Benchmarking the Proactivity Gap in Long-Lived LLM Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28108)
*Bin Wu,Guanyun Zou,Bingbing Wang,Huan Zhao,Chuan Shi*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces ATRBench, a benchmark to evaluate how well long-lived LLM agents proactively ask users for reusable preferences they’ll need in future sessions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Long-lived LLM agents should manage ongoing user preferences across sessions, but they rarely ask clarifying or anticipatory questions about persistent preferences that aren’t needed for the immediate task, leading to a “proactivity gap.” The authors want a concrete way to study and measure this gap.

Method: They define the Ask-to-Remember (ATR) problem: deciding when to ask users now for reusable preferences that are not required for the current task but may matter later. To evaluate ATR, they construct ATRBench, where each user has hidden ground-truth preferences, and success requires agents to proactively query and store those preferences rather than just recall what was stated. They then test eight frontier LLM agents under default and prompted settings, and compare them to an oracle that is directly given the relevant preference.

Result: On ATRBench, all eight frontier LLM agents perform at least 62 points worse than an oracle that is given the relevant user preference a priori, and various prompting strategies only marginally improve performance. Detailed diagnostics show the main weakness is in acquiring (proactively asking for) user preferences rather than storing or using them.

Conclusion: ATRBench exposes a large proactivity gap in current long-lived LLM agents: they are poor at deciding when and what future-useful preferences to ask users about. The benchmark provides a controlled, diagnostic environment for studying and improving proactive preference acquisition in such agents.

Abstract: A long-lived LLM agent, such as OpenClaw, earns its value by acting on a user's preferences and constraints across sessions, not just the current request. Yet today's agents keep what a user volunteers but rarely ask for what stays unspoken, leaving a proactivity gap in long-lived LLM agents: an agent cannot act on a preference it never obtained. As users delegate more of their affairs to agents, the impact of this gap grows. We isolate one concrete, controllable slice of this gap as Ask-to-Remember (ATR): the agent decides whether to ask now for a reusable user preference that the current task does not need but a later session with the same user will. ATR is hard even to evaluate: the right question is underdetermined and its payoff deferred to tasks that may never arise. ATRBench, to the best of our knowledge the first ATR benchmark, makes it measurable by fixing each user's preferences as hidden ground truth, so success demands asking, not recall. Across eight frontier LLM agents, defaults fall at least 62 points below an oracle handed the relevant preference, and prompting closes little of it. Diagnostics identify acquisition as the bottleneck. ATRBench surfaces this proactivity gap in current agents and offers a diagnostic testbed for closing it.

</details>


### [75] [LegalGraphRAG: Multi-Agent Graph Retrieval-Augmented Generation for Reliable Legal Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28120)
*Zerui Chen,Qinggang Zhang,Zhishang Xiang,Zhimin Wei,Linfeng Gao,Xiao Huang,Zhihong Zhang,Jinsong Su*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: LegalGraphRAG is a domain-specific GraphRAG framework for legal reasoning that uses a hierarchical legal knowledge graph and a multi-agent validation pipeline to achieve more accurate and trustworthy legal judgments than existing GraphRAG methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard GraphRAG models represent knowledge in a mostly flat graph and directly feed retrieved context to an LLM. In legal domains, this is problematic because legal corpora are heterogeneous (cases, statutes, interpretations) and span multiple abstraction levels (facts, rules, principles). A flat graph cannot separate or align these levels well, which hurts retrieval quality. Moreover, legal decisions require transparent, evidence-backed reasoning, while traditional RAG pipelines do not rigorously verify retrieved evidence, leading to opaque and potentially unreliable outputs. The paper aims to address these domain-specific issues to make RAG suitable for high-stakes legal applications.

Method: The paper proposes LegalGraphRAG, which has two main ingredients: (1) a hierarchical legal graph that organizes legal knowledge across multiple levels of abstraction—such as factual case details, applied rules, and high-level legal principles—capturing relationships between cases, statutes, and interpretations and enabling retrieval at the appropriate granularity; and (2) a multi-agent reasoning system with three specialized roles: a Researcher agent that retrieves candidate evidence from the hierarchical graph, an Auditor agent that validates and cross-checks this evidence against original source documents to filter out incorrect or unsupported items, and an Adjudicator agent that synthesizes the verified evidence into a final legal judgment or analysis. This pipeline aims to combine structured retrieval with explicit verification and structured decision making.

Result: In experiments on legal reasoning tasks, LegalGraphRAG achieves state-of-the-art performance compared to existing GraphRAG baselines. It yields more accurate legal analyses and improves trustworthiness, as measured by metrics that likely reflect correctness, faithfulness to sources, and possibly interpretability or citation quality. The system consistently outperforms prior graph-based retrieval approaches that rely on flatter knowledge graphs and single-step LLM reasoning without rigorous evidence verification.

Conclusion: LegalGraphRAG demonstrates that domain-specific adaptations of GraphRAG—namely hierarchical legal knowledge modeling and a multi-agent verification pipeline—can substantially improve both the accuracy and reliability of LLM-based legal reasoning. By differentiating levels of legal abstraction and enforcing an evidence-auditing process before judgment, the framework addresses key challenges of heterogeneity and transparency in legal corpora, making retrieval-augmented generation more suitable for high-stakes legal decision support. The released code and datasets facilitate further research and application in this area.

Abstract: Graph-based Retrieval-Augmented Generation (GraphRAG) advances flat document retrieval by structuring knowledge as relational graphs, enabling more coherent and effective reasoning. However, applying it to specific domains like legal reasoning faces critical challenges. (i) Legal corpora are heterogeneous, containing multi-granular knowledge from cases, articles and interpretations. A flat knowledge graph cannot adequately differentiate between factual details, applied rules, and abstract principles, limiting accurate retrieval. (ii) Reliable legal judgment demands transparent, evidence-based reasoning. Traditional RAG passes retrieved context directly to an LLM without verification, resulting in opaque, error-prone reasoning. To this end, we propose LegalGraphRAG, a framework designed for reliable legal reasoning. Our approach introduces two core components: a hierarchical legal graph that hierarchically organizes legal sources to enable retrieval at appropriate abstraction levels, and a multi-agent system for reliable legal reasoning, where a Researcher retrieves candidate evidence, an Auditor rigorously verifies its validity against source documents, and an Adjudicator synthesizes the set of verified evidence to render a final judgment. Extensive experiments show that LegalGraphRAG achieves the state-of-the-art performance, outperforming existing GraphRAG baselines in accurate and trustworthy legal analysis. Our code, datasets and implementation details are available at https://github.com/XMUDeepLIT/LegalGraphRAG.

</details>


### [76] [Risk-aware Selective Prompting for Hallucination Mitigation in Large Vision-Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28123)
*Yuang Huang,Yafeng Zhang,Yu Zilan*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: Study of when and how prompt-based verification helps reduce hallucinations in LVLMs, and a new selective, risk-aware prompting strategy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Prompt-based verification is widely used to combat hallucinations in large vision-language models, but when it actually helps or harms is not well understood, creating potential performance risks.

Method: Systematic empirical study of verification prompting across two representative LVLM architectures and multiple hallucination benchmarks, analyzing performance vs input difficulty and internal model behaviors like attention redistribution and entropy patterns. Then design a training-free Risk-aware Selective Prompting (RSP) method that uses pre-generation uncertainty signals to decide whether to apply verification prompting.

Result: Always-on verification prompting corrects more errors on difficult inputs but introduces a stable amount of new errors across all difficulty levels, so it helps on hard cases but can hurt easy ones. Verification prompts cause a conservative output shift, redistributing attention from visual to instruction tokens and yielding a characteristic middle-layer entropy pattern. RSP selectively triggers verification based on uncertainty, reduces the negative impact of always-on prompting, preserves baseline performance, and shows that good selection signals differ by architecture.

Conclusion: Verification prompting is a risk-bearing, input-dependent intervention rather than uniformly beneficial, driven by instruction-conditioned attention shifts. Selectively applying it via uncertainty-based Risk-aware Selective Prompting can retain its benefits on hard inputs while avoiding degradation on easier ones, though the best selection strategy is architecture-specific.

Abstract: Prompt-based verification is widely used to mitigate hallucinations in large vision-language models (LVLMs), yet when it helps remains poorly understood. We systematically study verification prompting across two representative LVLM architectures and hallucination benchmarks, and find that it is a risk-bearing intervention: its corrections increase with input difficulty, while newly introduced errors persist across difficulty levels. As a result, always-on prompting helps on hard inputs but offers little benefit -- and can harm -- easier ones. Our analysis further shows that this behavior is associated with a conservative output shift. Verification prompts redistribute attention from visual tokens toward instruction tokens and induce a distinct middle-layer entropy pattern absent in a neutral-prompt control, suggesting instruction-conditioned attention redistribution rather than uniformly improved visual grounding. Motivated by this input-dependent risk, we propose Risk-aware Selective Prompting (RSP), a training-free approach that uses pre-generation uncertainty signals to trigger verification selectively. RSP mitigates the degradation of always-on prompting while preserving baseline performance, and reveals that effective selection signals vary across architectures.

</details>


### [77] [Chinese Word Boundary Recovery through Character Alignment Projection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28128)
*Lusha Wang,Yuchen Li,Su Yuan,Jungyeul Park*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper proposes an alignment-based projection method to recover correct Chinese word boundaries in noisy learner text, and shows it is more robust than direct segmentation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Chinese word segmentation fails frequently on non-standard, noisy input such as learner Chinese, causing broken word boundaries that harm downstream annotation and fair evaluation. There is a need for methods and benchmarks that can stabilize segmentation when the source text is corrupted but a corrected counterpart is available.

Method: Formulate word boundary recovery as a character-level alignment and projection task between a noisy source sentence and a cleaner target sentence. First, align the two character strings; then, project the word boundaries from the clean target back onto the noisy source to obtain recovered word spans. Construct two evaluation resources: (1) a manually checked learner Chinese benchmark based on MuCGEC, and (2) a synthetic benchmark derived from Chinese Penn Treebank with controlled noise. Compare the proposed two-step projection approach against direct segmentation on these benchmarks.

Result: Experiments demonstrate that standard segmentation approaches applied directly to noisy learner input suffer from compound fragmentation and over-segmentation. The proposed two-step alignment-projection method substantially reduces over-segmentation errors by leveraging the corrected target sentences to restore coherent source-side word spans on both real learner and synthetic benchmarks.

Conclusion: Word boundary recovery, when a clean counterpart is available, is a different problem from ordinary segmentation. Alignment-based projection offers a principled and empirically effective way to stabilize Chinese word annotation and evaluation under noisy input, improving robustness to learner errors and other character-level divergences.

Abstract: Chinese word segmentation is especially fragile in non-standard text, where language learner errors and other character-level divergences disrupt the word boundaries assumed by downstream annotation and evaluation. This paper formulates Chinese word boundary recovery as an alignment-based projection task. Given a noisy source sentence and a cleaner target counterpart, we first align the two strings at the character level and then project target-side word boundaries back onto the source. Beyond the recovery method itself, we introduce two evaluation resources: a manually checked learner Chinese benchmark based on MuCGEC and a controlled synthetic benchmark derived from the Chinese Penn Treebank. Experiments show that direct segmentation remains vulnerable to compound fragmentation in learner input, whereas the proposed two step projection method corrects many over-segmentation errors by using the corrected target to recover source-side word spans. The results show that word boundary recovery is distinct from ordinary segmentation and that alignment projection provides a principled mechanism for stabilizing Chinese annotation and evaluation under noisy input.

</details>


### [78] [Better heads do not guarantee better binarized constituency parsing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28131)
*Zeyao Qi,Yige Chen,Eitan Klinger,Vivaan Wadhwa,Jungyeul Park*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper tests whether using learned, dependency-based headedness for punctuation-aware tree binarization improves constituency parsing, and finds that it generally does not.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To investigate if more accurate, learned head assignments—especially ones induced from dependency structures—can improve the supervision signal for binary constituency parsers, particularly in handling punctuation.

Method: They compare rule-based versus learned, dependency-induced head rules for punctuation-aware tree binarization, evaluate intrinsic head prediction quality, then debinarize and measure constituency parsing performance, including punctuation-sensitive F1, across treebanks and in cross-treebank transfer settings.

Result: Learned heads greatly outperform rule-based ones in intrinsic head prediction but do not translate into consistent improvements in constituency parsing after debinarization; in some cases, especially under punctuation-conditioned and macro-averaged punctuation-sensitive F1 evaluation and under cross-treebank transfer, they underperform traditional rule-based binarization.

Conclusion: More linguistically grounded or accurate headedness is not guaranteed to be optimal for training parsers when used as a binarization control signal; better head prediction quality does not necessarily yield better punctuation-sensitive constituency parsing, yielding an important negative result for parser design.

Abstract: We revisit punctuation-aware tree binarization for constituency parsing and ask whether dependency-induced headedness improves binary parser supervision. Although learned heads substantially outperform rule-based heads in intrinsic head prediction, they do not yield consistent parsing gains after debinarization. In particular, punctuation-conditioned evaluation shows that learned headedness underperforms rule-based binarization in macro-average punctuation-sensitive $F_1$, despite a small overall gain on CTB. Similar instability appears under cross-treebank transfer. These results suggest that \ycc{linguistically grounded} headedness is not necessarily parser-optimal when used as a binarization control signal. The paper presents a negative result: better head prediction does not imply better punctuation-sensitive constituency parsing.

</details>


### [79] [DEPART: DEcomposing PARiTy across Multilingual LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28163)
*Manan Uppadhyay,Prashant Kodali,Pranjal Chitale,Reshma Ramaprasad,Himanshu Beniwal,Sunayana Sitaram*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper analyzes why multilingual language models perform differently across languages and introduces a statistical framework to explain and decompose these performance gaps.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing multilingual LLM leaderboards report accuracy per language but do not explain why performance disparities occur, making it hard to identify or mitigate systemic biases. The authors want to move from passive reporting to an explanatory, diagnostic approach that attributes variance in performance to specific, interpretable sources.

Method: The authors first use non-parametric, distribution-free statistical tests (Friedman and Kruskal–Wallis) to confirm that observed performance gaps across languages are systematic and not due to random sampling noise. They then propose a two-step Bayesian hierarchical modeling framework. Step 1 isolates the portion of performance variance attributable solely to language identity and regresses this on observable language features such as script, language family, and typological distance, as well as representational similarity to English inside the model. Step 2 models the full three-way (model × benchmark × language) performance cube to decompose variance into components tied to model identity, language, benchmark, and their interactions, comparing profiles for understanding (NLU) vs reasoning tasks.

Result: They find that language identity contributes systematic variance, and that observable language features explain most of that language-driven variation: 79% of language-level variance in understanding tasks and 92% in reasoning tasks. Among predictors, a model’s internal representational similarity to English is the strongest driver across both task types. In the full variance decomposition, they show that for NLU tasks, model identity is the primary source of performance variance (66.7%), whereas for reasoning tasks, the interaction between benchmark and model is dominant (46.3%), indicating different underlying structures of difficulty and model behavior across task types.

Conclusion: Performance disparities in multilingual LLMs are structured and explainable, not mere noise. Language properties—especially how similarly a language is internally represented relative to English—account for most language-level variance, and the main drivers of overall variance differ between understanding and reasoning tasks. This reframes multilingual evaluation as an explainable, diagnostic process that provides concrete levers (e.g., targeting specific language families, scripts, or benchmark–model interactions) to address and mitigate language-based performance gaps.

Abstract: Multilingual Large Language Models (mLLMs) leaderboards report per-language accuracy but rarely explain why disparities emerge, leaving systemic biases unattributed and offering practitioners no actionable levers. We first establish that these gaps are systematic rather than artifacts of sampling noise via distribution-free Friedman and Kruskal--Wallis tests, then introduce a two-step Bayesian hierarchical framework that decomposes multilingual performance variance into interpretable components. First, isolating the variance attributable to language identity, we show that observable language features (script, family, typological distance) explain $R^2_{\text{ling}} = 79\%$ of this variance on understanding tasks and $92\%$ on reasoning, with a model's internal representational similarity to English emerging as the dominant predictor across both task buckets. Second, decomposing the full (model$\times$benchmark$\times$language) cube, we find that NLU and reasoning have fundamentally divergent variance profiles: model identity dominates understanding ($66.7\%$ of variance), whereas the benchmark$\times$model interaction dominates reasoning ($46.3\%$). Together these results recast multilingual evaluation from passive performance mapping into an explainable, diagnostic framework with concrete levers for targeting the root drivers of language disparity.

</details>


### [80] [SuperValid: Capability-Aligned OOD Validation for Generalizable Downstream Scaling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28179)
*Quanen Sun,Changxin Tian,Ke Shi,Cai Chen,Cunyin Peng,Jia Liu,Kunlong Chen,Zhiqiang Zhang*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper proposes SuperValid, a capability-level validation framework for large language models that better predicts downstream performance across architectures, scales, and training data, enabling improved model selection and training decisions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing scaling law work either uses benchmark-level downstream scores, which can be noisy and scenario-specific, or IID validation loss, which fails to capture changes in capability when training distributions shift. This makes it hard to reliably predict real-world performance and guide training. The authors want a more general, capability-centric way to connect training signals to downstream capabilities.

Method: They introduce SuperValid, which constructs out-of-distribution but capability-aligned validation datasets. For each capability domain (e.g., reasoning, coding), they distill core concepts from multiple benchmarks, then expand these into diverse, knowledge-rich validation texts. They then compute the cross-entropy loss of models on this synthetic validation set and analyze its correlation with downstream benchmark performance across models, sizes, and data distributions.

Result: Across 17 benchmarks grouped into 6 capability domains, the loss on SuperValid strongly and consistently correlates with downstream scores, more robustly than traditional IID validation loss or per-benchmark signals, and generalizes across architectures, model scales, and training data variations.

Conclusion: Studying scaling at the capability level via synthetic, OOD but capability-aligned validation data yields a more stable and general predictor of downstream performance. SuperValid can serve as a training-free metric that is available during training, supporting better model selection, early stopping, and scaling decisions without extensive benchmark evaluations.

Abstract: Scaling laws guide large language model training by relating compute to cross-entropy loss, and recent work further extends them to predict downstream benchmark performance. However, prior approaches face generalization limitations from two aspects: focusing on benchmark-level performance introduces scenario-specific artifacts, while relying on IID validation loss fails to track capability improvements when training distributions vary. In this work, we argue that downstream scaling should be studied at the capability level, which captures shared skill factors across related tasks while abstracting away benchmark-specific noise. We propose SuperValid, a framework that synthesizes OOD (out-of-distribution), capability-aligned validation data by distilling core concepts from benchmarks within a capability domain and expanding them into diverse, knowledge-rich texts. Extensive experiments spanning 17 benchmarks grouped into 6 capability domains show that SuperValid loss exhibits strong and stable correlation with downstream performance across models of different architectures, scales, and training data distributions. As a training-free metric computable during training without benchmark evaluation, SuperValid enables effective model selection, early stopping, and scaling decisions.

</details>


### [81] [When Confidence Misleads: Suffix Anchoring and Anchor-Proximity Confidence Modulation for Diffusion Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28181)
*Jungwon Park,Jimyeong Kim,Jungmin Ko,Nojun Kwak,Wonjong Rhee*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper identifies failures of confidence-based position selection in fully non-autoregressive diffusion language models and proposes a training-free decoding tweak, Suffix-Anchored Confidence Modulation, that uses a short suffix anchor plus locally modulated confidence to encourage complete outputs without premature decoding near the anchor, improving reasoning and code benchmarks while keeping parallelism.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Fully non-autoregressive diffusion language models decode all tokens in parallel by iterative denoising, so they must decide which token positions to finalize at each step. Existing training-free strategies rely directly on model confidence, assuming high-confidence tokens (including EOT) can be safely fixed. However, this can lead to early EOT selection and incomplete outputs, and naive fixes like adding a suffix anchor to push for completion create new issues: overconfidence near the anchor and premature decoding of nearby tokens. The paper is motivated by understanding and correcting these confidence pathologies during inference without retraining the model.

Method: The authors empirically analyze confidence behavior in fully non-AR diffusion decoding, showing that EOT tokens and anchor-adjacent tokens can become misleadingly high-confidence. They then introduce Suffix-Anchored Confidence Modulation: (1) append a short suffix anchor to the prompt to bias the model toward producing a complete response; and (2) apply a decoding-time confidence modulation schedule around the anchor positions, reducing or reshaping their confidence based on the current decoding progress so that these tokens are not finalized too early. The method is entirely training-free and only affects inference-time token selection in the diffusion denoising loop.

Result: On benchmarks spanning text-only reasoning, vision-language reasoning, and code generation, the proposed method consistently improves over vanilla confidence-based fully non-AR decoding. It also outperforms a simpler explicit EOT-suppression heuristic while maintaining the high parallelism and speed advantages of fully non-autoregressive generation. Quantitatively, scores on these benchmarks increase, and qualitatively, generations are more complete and less truncated without sacrificing efficiency.

Conclusion: Confidence, as directly output by diffusion language models, can be systematically misleading for position selection in fully non-autoregressive decoding, especially for EOT and tokens near suffix anchors. A lightweight, training-free adjustment—Suffix-Anchored Confidence Modulation—can correct these issues by combining a short suffix anchor with localized confidence modulation during decoding. This yields more complete and accurate generations while preserving the efficiency benefits of parallel diffusion decoding, suggesting that better-calibrated decoding-time confidence control is key for robust fully non-AR language generation.

Abstract: Diffusion language models decode text by iteratively denoising masked token sequences, making the choice of which positions to decode a central inference-time decision. Most training-free decoding strategies use model confidence for position selection, assuming that high-confidence positions are ready to be decoded. In this work, we revisit this assumption by studying when confidence misleads fully non-autoregressive (fully non-AR) decoding. EOT tokens can receive high confidence and cause incomplete generation; inserting a suffix anchor can mitigate this issue but introduces local overconfidence near the anchor, causing anchor-adjacent tokens to be decoded too early. To address these issues, we propose Suffix-Anchored Confidence Modulation, a simple training-free method that inserts a short suffix anchor to encourage response completion and modulates confidence near the anchor according to decoding progress. This preserves the response-completion benefit of suffix anchoring while reducing premature decoding of anchor-adjacent tokens. Across text-only reasoning, vision-language reasoning, and code-generation benchmarks, our method consistently improves confidence-based fully non-AR decoding, outperforms explicit EOT suppression, and preserves the parallel decoding advantage of fully non-AR generation.

</details>


### [82] [BenGER: Benchmarking LLM Systems on Subsumption-Based Legal Reasoning in German Law](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28183)
*Sebastian Nagl,Ann-Kristin Mayrhofer,Martin Heidebach,Aleyna Koçak,Anne Zettelmeier,Elly Breu,Angelina Greiner,Sofija Milijas,Matthias Grabmair*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: BenGER is a benchmark dataset to evaluate large language models on German-law legal reasoning, with comprehensive human and LLM-judge evaluations showing strong performance of closed models and benefits of human–AI co-writing.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: There is a lack of standardized, realistic benchmarks to assess how well large language models can perform subsumption-based legal reasoning in German law, especially in comparison to human students and practitioners. Existing evaluations often use artificial tasks, lack robust grading protocols, and do not explore how LLMs can be used collaboratively with humans. The authors aim to create a rigorous, education-aligned benchmark and evaluation framework to address these gaps.

Method: The authors construct BenGER, a dataset with three components: (1) 596 exam-style free-text legal case tasks covering different levels of German legal education, and (2) 531 short doctrinal reasoning tasks. They then evaluate 12 contemporary LLMs, including closed flagship, efficient, and open-weight models, using both automated metrics and human-judge assessments. They gather a controlled validation subset of human-written solutions produced under timed conditions in two settings: unaided humans and humans collaborating with AI (human–AI co-creation). They design an LLM-as-a-Judge framework with grading rubrics, and cross-validate it against a multi-rater human grading protocol where each solution is reviewed by three blind human graders plus one informed creator. They analyze inter-rater agreement and compare model performance to human baselines.

Result: The study finds that the LLM-as-a-Judge approach is nearly as reliable as adding an additional human blind reviewer: replacing one blind human reviewer with the LLM judge does not reduce agreement with the full human pool more than simply removing that reviewer (Calderon correlation r=0.96 vs. r=0.96, on a matched sample of 30). Among the evaluated models, closed flagship LLMs achieve the best performance across all components of the BenGER dataset. Additionally, human–AI co-creation of legal solutions substantially outperforms work done by humans without AI assistance on the benchmark tasks.

Conclusion: BenGER provides a robust benchmark for assessing subsumption-based legal reasoning of LLMs in German law and enables systematic comparison between models and human performance. The validated LLM-as-a-Judge framework can serve as a scalable alternative or complement to human grading, achieving human-level agreement. The results suggest that state-of-the-art closed LLMs are currently the strongest performers on German legal reasoning tasks and that collaborative workflows where humans leverage AI tools yield higher-quality legal analysis than unaided human work. This has implications for legal education, evaluation practices, and practical integration of LLMs into legal reasoning tasks.

Abstract: We introduce the BenGER (Benchmark for German Law) dataset for evaluating LLM systems on subsumption-based legal reasoning in German law. The BenGER dataset consists of three components: 596 exam-style free-text legal case tasks across multiple levels of legal education and 531 short doctrinal reasoning tasks. We evaluate 12 contemporary LLM systems -- closed flagship, efficiency-oriented, and open-weight -- across automatic and judge-based metrics. On a controlled validation subset of timed human-written solutions under both unaided and human--AI co-creation conditions, we contextualise model performance against these human baselines. We introduce a rubric-aligned LLM-as-a-Judge framework cross-validated against a multi-rater human-grading protocol (three blind reviews plus one author-informed creator review per solution). Our results show that replacing a blind human reviewer with the LLM judge degrades agreement with the full human pool no more than removing that reviewer altogether (Calderon r=0.96 vs.~r=0.96, matched n=30), that closed-flagship systems lead the leaderboard across all corpora, and that human--AI co-creation substantially outperforms unaided human work.

</details>


### [83] [Framing Matters: Addressing Framing Sensitivity in Decision-Making through Behaviorally-Grounded Value Alignment](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28188)
*Seojin Hwang,Minju Kim,Junhyuk Choi,JeongHyun Park,Hwanhee Lee*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper shows that large language models are highly sensitive to fact-preserving framing in legal-style decisions, introduces a benchmark (Fragile) to measure this effect, and proposes a representation-level method (Valign) that reduces such framing-induced decision flips.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLMs are being used in high-stakes domains like legal reasoning, where decisions must be consistent across inputs that are factually equivalent but phrased differently. Empirically, the authors find that current models can change their decisions when only the framing, not the facts, changes. This inconsistency is dangerous in legal and similar applications, so there is a need to both measure and mitigate framing sensitivity in a controlled, systematic way.

Method: The authors construct Fragile, a benchmark that varies inputs along three controlled, fact-preserving framing dimensions: (1) value-tinted narration, (2) temporal slice, and (3) narrative vividness. They then evaluate LLMs on this benchmark and measure the decision flip rate across different framings of the same factual situation. They test prior mitigation strategies at the prompt and activation levels and show these approaches fail or even worsen sensitivity. To address this, they design Valign, a representation-level method that: (a) anchors model decisions to a stable value prior, (b) steers hidden states toward directions aligned with the model’s own value-consistent representations, and (c) projects out directions related to temporal and vividness sensitivity from hidden states.

Result: On the Fragile benchmark, state-of-the-art LLMs exhibit a high average decision flip rate of 28.6% under fact-preserving reframings, indicating strong susceptibility to framing. Prior interventions at the prompt and activation level not only fail to reduce this flip rate but sometimes increase it. Valign, in contrast, consistently lowers the rate of framing-induced decision flips across the evaluated models and settings, indicating improved robustness to semantic framing while preserving factual content.

Conclusion: The work demonstrates that LLM decisions in high-stakes settings can be substantially distorted by purely semantic framing, even when facts are unchanged. Simple surface-level or shallow internal interventions are insufficient and can exacerbate the problem. Effective mitigation requires directly modifying the internal representation pathways associated with framing. Valign provides a concrete representation-level approach that reduces framing sensitivity on the Fragile benchmark, suggesting a promising direction for building more reliable and consistent decision-making LLMs.

Abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) are increasingly deployed in high-stakes decision-making settings such as legal reasoning, where consistency under factually equivalent inputs is critical. However, we find that fact-preserved but differently framed inputs can significantly destabilize LLM decisions.
