To systematically investigate this problem, we introduce Fragile, a large-scale benchmark that isolates fact-preserving semantic framing across three controlled dimensions: value-tinted narration, temporal slice, and narrative vividness. Our experiments reveal a high susceptibility of LLMs to framing, with an average decision flip rate of 28.6%. We find that simple prior prompt-level and activation-level interventions not only fail to suppress framing sensitivity but actively amplify it. We therefore propose Valign, a representation-level method that explicitly targets these framing dimensions by anchoring decisions to a stable value prior, steering hidden states toward the model's value-consistent direction, and projecting out temporal-vividness-sensitive directions from the model's hidden states. Valign consistently reduces framing-induced decision flips, demonstrating that robust mitigation requires directly targeting the internal pathways in which framing operates.

</details>


### [84] [The Harder Text Embedding Benchmark (HTEB): Beyond One-dimensional Static Robustness](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28190)
*Manuel Frank,Haithem Afli*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: They propose a new, dynamic benchmark (HTEB) that tests embedding model robustness along multiple axes using LLM-generated input transformations, revealing weaknesses that single static scores hide.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing embedding benchmarks like MTEB give one static score per model, implicitly assuming robustness is a single scalar and potentially hiding that models may fail differently under different input variations. The authors want to expose and measure these nuanced robustness properties that matter for real-world deployment.

Method: They design the Harder Text Embedding Benchmark (HTEB), which dynamically perturbs evaluation inputs at test time using an LLM with stochastic transformations. These transformations are organized into three intuitive robustness axes: Lexical/Stylistic changes, Length changes, and Language changes. They validate the semantic adequacy of these transformations with 4,800 human ratings on an English subset, then evaluate 16 open-weight embedding models on 32 datasets spanning 42 languages under these transformed conditions and compare performance to the original, untransformed benchmark scores.

Result: They observe three main empirical findings: (1) Models show distinct, partially independent robustness profiles along the three axes, indicating robustness is multidimensional. (2) Larger models within each of three model families obtain higher absolute scores, but scaling does not eliminate the performance gap between original and transformed data; scale helps most on the Language axis. (3) English datasets degrade more under HTEB transformations than multilingual datasets. These results show that current single-score benchmarks miss important robustness failures.

Conclusion: HTEB provides a dynamic, multidimensional robustness evaluation framework for text embeddings that uncovers axis-specific strengths and weaknesses of models that static benchmarks like MTEB overlook. The authors argue that future evaluation of embedding models should adopt such multidimensional, dynamic robustness measurements instead of relying on a single static score.

Abstract: Embedding benchmarks like MTEB report a single score per model, implicitly treating robustness as a static, scalar property. We argue that embedding robustness is multidimensional, since models respond differently to different types of variation, and requires dynamic evaluation to expose failures hidden by static benchmarks. We introduce the Harder Text Embedding Benchmark (HTEB), a dynamic evaluation framework that challenges model robustness along three practically interpretable axes (Lexical/Stylistic, Length and Language) by stochastically transforming inputs at evaluation time with an LLM. Evaluating 16 open-weight embedding models on 32 datasets covering 42 languages under transformations validated by 4,800 human ratings on an English subsample, we find three patterns: (1) Models exhibit specific, partly decoupled robustness profiles across axes. (2) Across three model families, scale increases absolute scores but does not close the gap between original and transformed evaluations. Here, scaling tends to improve specifically the Language axis. (3) English datasets are more sensitive to HTEB transformations than multilingual datasets. This demonstrates that HTEB identifies strengths and weaknesses of models along deployment-relevant axes, challenging current embedding benchmarks and arguing for multidimensional, dynamic robustness evaluation.

</details>


### [85] [Pruning and Distilling Mixture-of-Experts into Dense Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28207)
*Junhyuck Kim,Jihun Yun,Haechan Kim,Gyeongman Kim,Joonghyun Bae,Jaewoong Cho*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: They propose a framework to convert Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) language models into standard dense models by selecting, grouping, and concatenating experts, then refining with knowledge distillation, achieving better accuracy and efficiency than standard dense pruning at the same parameter count.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: MoE models dominate frontier LMs due to their efficiency, but deployment is difficult on memory-constrained devices because all experts must reside in memory. Existing MoE compression still leaves an MoE structure with this fundamental limitation. The motivation is to obtain dense models with similar quality to MoE teachers while eliminating MoE-specific memory and infrastructure overhead.

Method: 1) Start from a trained MoE LM. 2) Score experts with various criteria (7 methods, including a new diversity-aware scoring) to select which experts to keep. 3) Group selected experts using different grouping strategies (5 options). 4) Concatenate grouped experts into a single dense feed-forward network, optionally applying different magnitude scaling strategies (2 options). 5) Perform knowledge distillation from the original MoE teacher on ~4B tokens to refine the resulting dense model. They systematically sweep 350 configurations on Qwen3-30B-A3B and test transferability on other MoE models.

Result: Their diversity-aware expert scoring consistently beats prior scoring methods on multiple MoE models (Qwen3-30B-A3B, DeepSeek-V2-Lite, GPT-OSS-20B). At matched parameter count, the resulting dense models from MoE-to-dense conversion outperform traditional dense-to-dense pruning by +6.3 percentage points in average downstream accuracy while also achieving ~1.6x faster training wall-clock for the distillation stage.

Conclusion: It is feasible and effective to convert trained MoE language models into compact dense models. Expert scoring, especially diversity-aware scoring, is key to performance. Compared at the same size, MoE-to-dense distilled models are more accurate and faster to obtain than sparsified dense models, making this a promising path for memory-constrained deployment of frontier MoE LMs.

Abstract: Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) is now the dominant architecture for frontier language models, yet it requires all expert parameters to be loaded in memory, making it less preferable for memory-constrained deployment. Existing compression methods reduce the number of experts but the output remains an MoE model with the same fundamental limitation. We present the first systematic framework for converting a trained MoE into a standard fully dense architecture: experts are scored, selected, and grouped, then concatenated into a dense FFN and refined by knowledge distillation from the MoE teacher. We evaluate 7 scoring, 5 grouping, and 2 magnitude scaling methods across a range of selected expert counts on Qwen3-30B-A3B, yielding 350 configurations. We find that the choice of scoring method is the most impactful, with our novel diversity-aware scoring consistently outperforming prior methods on Qwen3-30B-A3B, DeepSeek-V2-Lite, and GPT-OSS-20B. Under a controlled comparison at matched parameter count, MoE-to-dense outperforms dense-to-dense pruning by +6.3 pp in average downstream accuracy after ~4B-token distillation at 1.6x faster training wall-clock speed.

</details>


### [86] [When Helpful Context Leaks: Privacy Risks in Domain-Adapted ASR](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28211)
*Maike Züfle,Jan Niehues*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper uncovers and analyzes a new privacy risk in customized speech LLMs: they can leak sensitive, domain-specific words from prompts or fine-tuning data by incorrectly transcribing phonetically similar words, and it studies how prompting and fine-tuning contribute to this leakage and how to mitigate it.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: As speech LLMs are used in professional domains (e.g., with proprietary or sensitive audio/context), users routinely provide domain-specific prompts or fine-tune models with private data. Existing work focuses on conventional privacy attacks (e.g., membership inference), but the authors notice a more subtle risk: once a model is adapted to expect certain domain-specific terms, it may hallucinate or substitute those terms into transcripts, even when they are not spoken, thereby leaking confidential terminology or content.


Method: The authors design a controlled experimental setting where they can systematically test for this leakage behavior. They create a dataset where spoken words have phonetically similar counterparts that appear only in the customisation data (prompts, fine-tuning data, or both). They then adapt speech LLMs through (1) context prompting with domain-specific terms and (2) supervised fine-tuning on proprietary-style recordings, and in some conditions combine both. They measure how often the model transcribes the domain-specific (sensitive) word instead of the actually spoken word, defining and quantifying a leakage rate. They also test a prompt-level mitigation technique that aims to constrain or reduce such substitution and examine how it affects transcription accuracy and leakage under the different customisation strategies.

Result: Both prompting and fine-tuning introduce non-trivial privacy leakage: models more frequently output domain-specific, sensitive terms instead of the true spoken words. This effect increases when prompting and fine-tuning are combined, suggesting they compound the risk. The tested prompt-level mitigation can reduce leakage, but not without affecting accuracy. Across all approaches, fine-tuning alone (without additional in-context domain prompts at inference) yields the best trade-off between retaining task performance and limiting leakage.

Conclusion: Customising speech LLMs with domain-specific information is inherently privacy-sensitive, not only because of what is stored in model weights or prompts, but also because customisation shifts the model’s transcription biases in ways that can actively leak private terminology. Developers and practitioners must therefore treat domain adaptation as a potential privacy attack surface, carefully select customization strategies, and potentially favor fine-tuning without explicit in-context domain prompts to balance performance and privacy. The authors provide datasets and code to support future work on understanding and mitigating such leakage risks.

Abstract: SpeechLLMs are increasingly deployed in professional settings where domain customisation is standard practice: users supply context in prompts with sensitive information, fine-tune on proprietary recordings, or both. We identify and systematically investigate an overlooked privacy risk of such customisation: a model adapted to recognise domain-specific terminology can be nudged into transcribing a phonetically similar word from its context or training data, even when a different word is spoken, thereby leaking private information. To evaluate this risk, we construct a controlled dataset and measure leakage rates across two customisation mechanisms, prompting and fine-tuning. Both mechanisms cause measurable leakage, compounding when combined. We evaluate a prompt-level mitigation strategy and analyse the accuracy-leakage trade-off across customisation approaches, finding that fine-tuning without context prompts offers the best balance. We release our code and dataset publicly.

</details>


### [87] [Analyzing Quality-Latency-Resource Trade-offs in a Technical Documentation RAG Assistant Using LoRA Adaptation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28222)
*Evgenii Palnikov,Elizaveta Gavrilova*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper analyzes how different LoRA configurations affect the trade-off between answer quality, latency, and resource use in a Kubernetes-docs RAG system based on Llama 3.x models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: RAG systems over technical documentation must balance answer quality with practical constraints like latency, memory, and training cost, but there is limited systematic evidence on how LoRA adapter design (rank, target modules) impacts these trade-offs in real-world setups such as Kubernetes documentation QA.

Method: The authors build a manually verified benchmark of 5,144 Kubernetes documentation QA pairs and run a controlled study on a fixed, strong hybrid-retrieval pipeline (dense + sparse BGE-M3, RRF, cross-encoder reranking). They train and evaluate 20 LoRA configurations on Llama-3.2-3B-Instruct and Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct, varying LoRA rank and which attention modules they target. They measure token-level F1, LLM-judged groundedness/correctness (pass@4), inference latency, memory footprint, and training cost, reporting all metrics with bootstrap 95% CIs and performing Pareto-front analysis and parameter-matched controls.

Result: Across the 20 LoRA settings, adapters that modify only the q and v attention projections systematically lie on or near the Pareto front, yielding better quality-resource trade-offs than alternatives, largely independent of model size; meanwhile, the choice between 3B and 8B models primarily shifts which latency/memory regime is achievable rather than changing relative adapter rankings. A parameter-matched control suggests that the q/v superiority cannot be explained only by parameter count.

Conclusion: In documentation-grounded RAG for Kubernetes docs, carefully choosing LoRA target modules matters: restricting adaptation to q and v projections delivers structurally better quality-latency-resource trade-offs than other configurations, with model size mainly setting the overall operating regime. The authors release their benchmark, selected adapters, and code to support further research on RAG-LoRA trade-offs.

Abstract: We study quality-latency-resource trade-offs in a documentation-grounded retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) system that uses Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) of the generator. We build a manually verified benchmark of 5,144 question-answer pairs over the official Kubernetes documentation and combine it with a fixed hybrid-retrieval pipeline (BGE-M3 dense, BGE-M3 native sparse, Reciprocal Rank Fusion, cross-encoder reranking). Over this benchmark we ablate 20 LoRA configurations on Llama-3.2-3B-Instruct and Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct across rank and target-module choices, and evaluate each on token-level F1, LLM-judged groundedness and correctness (pass@4), inference latency, inference memory, and training cost, all reported with bootstrap 95% confidence intervals. Pareto analysis shows that LoRA adapters acting only on the q and v attention projections consistently dominate the front, while the 3B/8B choice mainly defines operating regime. A param-matched control comparison further indicates that the q/v advantage is structural rather than purely parametric. The benchmark, selected adapters, and code are available at https://github.com/EugPal/rag-lora-tradeoffs.

</details>


### [88] [Supervised Semantic Differential for Cross-Cultural Concept Analysis: A Case Study of Human Affect](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28225)
*Jan Sikora,Paweł Lenartowicz,Hubert Plisiecki*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: They propose a method to compare psychological meaning across languages using multilingual word embeddings and supervised semantic gradients, finding that affective meanings are broadly shared but with systematic cross-cultural differences.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional cross-cultural comparisons of psychological meaning mostly rely on direct word-level translations, which miss how concepts are structured and related within each language. The authors want a more nuanced, data-driven way to test whether semantic dimensions like Valence, Arousal, and Dominance are organized similarly or differently across languages.

Method: They extend the Supervised Semantic Differential (SSD) method to a cross-lingual setting by using aligned multilingual word embeddings. They estimate supervised semantic gradients (directions in embedding space corresponding to affective dimensions), then compare these gradients across languages using permutation tests and bootstrap confidence intervals. Residual differences between languages are interpreted by clustering words around the difference gradient to see what types of meanings diverge.

Result: The method successfully recovered affective dimensions (Valence, Arousal, Dominance) across Polish, English, and French lexicons. Cross-lingual comparisons showed that Valence was largely aligned across languages, whereas Arousal and Dominance showed more structured and interpretable differences. These differences involved themes such as bodily threat, aesthetic stimulation, internal emotionality, macro-level authority, everyday control, as well as some corpus-specific artifacts.

Conclusion: Cross-lingual SSD is an effective and interpretable framework for testing how well semantic dimensions align across languages, detecting where they diverge, and generating concrete hypotheses about cross-cultural differences in psychological meaning. The findings suggest that while core affective valence is shared across cultures, dimensions like arousal and dominance show culturally structured variation and are sensitive to corpus properties.

Abstract: Cross-cultural comparison of psychological meaning requires methods that go beyond word-level translation and examine how semantic dimensions are organized across languages. We introduce a cross-lingual extension of the Supervised Semantic Differential (SSD), which estimates supervised semantic gradients in embedding space and compares them across aligned multilingual word embeddings. The method tests gradient alignment and difference using permutation procedures and bootstrap intervals, and interprets residual differences through clustering around the difference gradient. We demonstrate the approach on Polish, English, and French affective norm lexicons, modeling Valence, Arousal, and Dominance where available. Affective dimensions were significantly recoverable across languages and model settings. Cross-lingual comparisons showed broad alignment together with structured residual differences: Valence appeared mostly shared, whereas Arousal and Dominance produced more interpretable contrasts involving bodily threat, aesthetic stimulation, internal emotionality, macro-level authority, and everyday control. Several clusters also reflected corpus-specific artifacts, underscoring the need for cautious interpretation. Cross-lingual SSD offers an explainable framework for testing semantic alignment, identifying divergence, and generating hypotheses about cross-cultural differences in psychological meaning.

</details>


### [89] [Why We Need Speech to Evaluate Speech Translation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28227)
*Maike Züfle,Danni Liu,Vilém Zouhar,Jan Niehues*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper finds that current metrics for evaluating speech translation do not effectively capture speech-specific information like gender and prosody, and proposes new speech-conditioned quality estimation models, but shows that even these struggle without better data and speech encoders.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: As speech translation models begin to preserve more speech-specific information (such as speaker gender, prosody, and emphasis), existing evaluation metrics, which focus mostly on text content, fail to measure these aspects. There is a need to understand how well current metrics handle speech-specific phenomena and to improve them so that speech translation systems can be properly assessed and optimized.

Method: The authors first perform a meta-evaluation of existing text- and speech-based quality estimation metrics on two contrastive datasets specifically designed to test gender agreement and prosody in speech translation. They then introduce SpeechCOMET, a family of quality estimation models that incorporate speech encoders, and also evaluate a state-of-the-art SpeechLLM as an automatic judge. They compare the performance of these new models and the SpeechLLM against text-based COMET on both standard quality estimation tasks and on speech-specific phenomena.

Result: The study shows that both existing text- and speech-based metrics perform poorly on evaluating gender agreement and prosody, even when they have direct access to the speech signal. SpeechCOMET models and the SpeechLLM match or surpass text-based COMET on standard quality estimation benchmarks, but they still fail to consistently judge speech-specific aspects. The authors identify three main reasons: current speech encoders do not reliably preserve speech-specific features; models often ignore the speech source signal during evaluation; and the training data used for quality estimation lacks sufficient examples of speech-specific phenomena.

Conclusion: The paper concludes that simply adding speech encoders or using SpeechLLMs is not enough to properly evaluate speech-specific information in speech translation. Meaningful progress will require purpose-built training data focusing on speech-specific phenomena and the development of models that truly condition on and exploit speech information. The authors release their models and code to facilitate future research in this direction.

Abstract: Speech translation models are increasingly capable of preserving speech-specific information (e.g., speaker gender, prosody, and emphasis), yet evaluation metrics remain blind to such phenomena. We meta-evaluate both text- and speech-based quality estimation metrics on two contrastive datasets targeting gender agreement and prosody, and find that both fall short, even when given direct access to the speech signal. We then train SpeechCOMET, a family of quality estimation models with speech encoders, and evaluate a state-of-the-art SpeechLLM as a judge. Both match or exceed text-based COMET on standard quality estimation, but neither consistently assesses speech-specific phenomena. We identify three causes: (1) speech-specific features are not reliably preserved in current encoders, (2) models tend to ignore the speech source signal, and (3) quality estimation training data contains too few relevant examples. We release all models and code, and argue that progress requires dedicated speech-specific training data and models that genuinely condition on speech.

</details>


### [90] [When Seekers Are Hard to Help: Evaluating Emotional Support Dialogue Systems in Worst-Case Interactions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28228)
*Jiajie Yang,Yangchun Li,Guanyi Chen,Rui Fan,Xin Bai,Tingting He*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper critiques current evaluation of Emotional Support Dialogue Systems (ESDSes) using overly cooperative LLM-simulated users and proposes a worst-case evaluation framework with difficult seeker simulations and new metrics, showing large performance drops and offering a way to improve robustness via worst-case training data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Most ESDSes are evaluated and even trained with simulated help-seekers that are cooperative, clear, and quickly accepting of support, which does not reflect challenging real-world users who may be disengaged, resistant, or emotionally volatile. This risks overly optimistic evaluation and systems that fail in realistic difficult scenarios. The authors aim to understand how ESDSes behave under such worst-case interactions and to build an evaluation framework that captures these challenging conditions.

Method: (1) Expert simulation study: eight experienced counselling professionals simulate difficult seekers, interact with existing Chinese ESDSes, rate them with scales, and are interviewed. From this, the authors derive key worst-case seeker behaviours and system limitations. (2) They design an LLM-based worst-case seeker simulator that reproduces such difficult behaviours. (3) They define four worst-case-oriented evaluation metrics: Deep Emotional Understanding, Guided Exploration, Balanced Emotional Support, and Authentic and Grounded Support. (4) They evaluate 17 ESDS and general LLM systems using this framework, then use worst-case simulations to generate additional training data for smaller models to test robustness gains.

Result: Under the proposed worst-case evaluation, nearly all 17 tested systems show large drops in performance relative to their behaviour with cooperative users. Large general-purpose LLMs tend to handle difficult seekers better than specialized ESDSes but still fail to consistently maintain engagement or significantly improve users’ emotional states. Training smaller models with worst-case simulated interactions yields improved robustness when facing difficult seekers.

Conclusion: Standard evaluation using cooperative, average-case simulated seekers gives an overly optimistic view of ESDS capabilities. Worst-case interactions—characterised by low engagement, resistance, limited disclosure, emotional volatility, or rigid negativity—reveal serious weaknesses even in strong models. The proposed LLM-based worst-case seeker simulator and four targeted metrics provide a more realistic, stress-test-style evaluation. Moreover, such worst-case simulations are not only useful for evaluation but also as training data that can make smaller ESDS models more robust to difficult real-world help-seeking scenarios.

Abstract: Emotional Support Dialogue Systems (ESDSes) are increasingly evaluated and trained with LLM-simulated seekers. However, such simulated seekers often behave as cooperative, average-case users who disclose clearly, respond constructively, and accept support within a few turns. This can lead to overly optimistic evaluation and obscure whether ESDSes can handle difficult help-seeking interactions. In this work, we study ESDS evaluation under worst-case interactions, where seekers are hard to help due to low engagement, resistance, limited self-disclosure, emotional volatility, or rigid negative interpretations. We first conduct an expert simulation study with eight experienced counselling professionals, who simulate difficult seekers, interact with existing Chinese ESDSes, provide scale ratings, and participate in semi-structured interviews. Based on this study, we derive worst-case seeker behaviours and identify key limitations of current systems. We then propose a worst-case evaluation framework consisting of an LLM-based worst-case seeker simulator and four worst-case-oriented metrics: Deep Emotional Understanding, Guided Exploration, Balanced Emotional Support, and Authentic and Grounded Support. Evaluating 17 systems, we find that nearly all models suffer substantial performance drops under worst-case interactions. Large general-purpose LLMs are generally more robust than specialised ESDSes, but even the strongest models struggle to sustain engagement and improve seekers' emotional states. Finally, we show that worst-case simulation can also generate useful training data, improving the robustness of smaller models.

</details>


### [91] [Building Community-Centred NLP Resources for Puno Quechua](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28253)
*Elwin Huaman,Adrian Gamarra Lafuente,Johanna Cordova,Anna Korhonen*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: First ASR resources and benchmarks for Puno Quechua, including a large speech corpus, model evaluations, and open releases.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Under-resourced languages like Puno Quechua need digital and ASR resources tailored to their speakers to support preservation and use, but such resources have been missing.

Method: They collected 66 hours of Puno Quechua speech via participatory design, manually transcribed/validated 36 hours, built an ASR corpus, and systematically benchmarked and fine-tuned modern ASR models (Whisper-base, wav2vec2-base, XLS-R-300M) with and without continued pre-training.

Result: They created the largest single-variety Quechua speech corpus, conducted the first systematic ASR benchmark for Puno Quechua, and produced fine-tuned models with evaluated performance.

Conclusion: Puno Quechua now has foundational open ASR resources—corpora, benchmarks, and models—that can support future research, technology development, and language preservation efforts.

Abstract: The preservation of under-resourced languages requires digital tools and resources shaped by and for their speakers. We present the first dedicated ASR resources for Puno Quechua (ISO 639-3: qxp): (1) the largest speech corpus for any single Quechua variety, consisting in 66 hours of recordings for scripted and spontaneous speech (including 36 hours of manually transcribed and validated data), collected via a participatory design campaign; (2) the first systematic ASR benchmark for Puno Quechua, evaluating state-of-the-art models and fine-tuning Whisper-base, wav2vec2-base, and XLS-R-300M, with and without continued pre-training (CPT); (3) an open release of all datasets and fine-tuned models.

</details>


### [92] [PrunePath: Towards Highly Structured Sparse Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28283)
*Zhexuan Gu,Zixun Fu,Yancheng Yuan*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: PrunePath is a structured sparsification framework for FFN layers in language models that adaptively selects experts per token using a probability budget, yielding better sparsity–performance trade-offs and real inference speedups.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Feed-forward networks dominate parameters and FLOPs in LLMs, but existing pruning/sparsification often fails to yield real hardware speedups or flexible control over sparsity at inference time. There is a need for a method that creates hardware-friendly, structured sparsity with a tunable sparsity level from a single model checkpoint.

Method: Starting from MoEfication (which turns FFNs into mixtures-of-experts), the paper replaces independent expert-wise thresholding with a softmax-normalized routing distribution. For each token, experts are activated greedily until the cumulative probability mass of selected experts passes a preset threshold (a token-level probability budget). This produces adaptive expert counts per token and exposes a direct sparsity knob. They also implement optimized Triton kernels for KV-cache decoding that exploit the structured expert sparsity for memory and speed gains.

Result: On a range of NLU, NLG, and instruction-following benchmarks, PrunePath attains better accuracy at the same sparsity (or higher sparsity at fixed accuracy) compared to static pruning and prior MoEfication-based sparsification. In practical deployment settings the method yields measurable decoding speedups and KV-cache memory reductions due to its structured expert sparsity and custom kernels.

Conclusion: PrunePath provides an effective, deployment-oriented way to sparsify FFN layers in large language models, offering a controllable sparsity–performance trade-off and converting token-wise expert sparsity into real inference-time gains via tailored kernels. It is better suited than previous methods for building highly sparse, efficient LLMs from a single trained checkpoint.

Abstract: Feed-forward networks (FFNs) dominate the parameter count and computation of modern language models, yet existing pruning methods often struggle to convert sparsity into hardware-friendly inference efficiency gains. We introduce \textbf{PrunePath}, a budget-adaptive structured sparsification framework for FFN layers. Built on MoEfication, PrunePath replaces independent expert-wise thresholding with a softmax-normalized routing distribution and activates important experts under a cumulative-mass threshold. This formulation imposes a token-level probability budget, enabling adaptive expert counts and a direct inference-time sparsity knob from a single checkpoint. Across NLU, NLG, and instruction-tuning evaluations, PrunePath achieves a favorable sparsity--performance trade-off compared with existing static pruning and MoEfication-based methods. We further implement Triton kernels for KV-cache decoding to translate the resulting structured sparsity into practical memory savings and measurable decoding-speed improvements. These results demonstrate the superior performance of PrunePath for building highly sparse, deployment-friendly large language models.

</details>


### [93] [CIRF: Tokenizing Chain-of-Thoughts into Reusable Functional Units for Efficient Latent Reasoning in Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28292)
*Yukyung Lee,Yumeng Shen,Jinhyeong Park,Hyein Yang,Jun-Hyung Park*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: They introduce CIRF, a method to make chain-of-thought reasoning cheaper and more adaptive by compressing reasoning steps into reusable functional tokens.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Implicit CoT can cut inference costs versus explicit step-by-step rationales, but current methods do not align well with human-written CoT traces and cannot adapt the amount of reasoning to problem complexity. The authors want a framework that keeps the benefits of CoT while being faster and more controllable.

Method: They define CIRF, which converts explicit chain-of-thought traces into sequences of semantically coherent reasoning units, each mapped to a discrete functional token. The model is fine-tuned to autoregressively produce these functional tokens (and optionally their intermediate results) before outputting the final answer. This imposes a latent, modular reasoning structure that can be trained in parallel and used adaptively at inference.

Result: On math, symbolic, and commonsense reasoning benchmarks, CIRF achieves better accuracy-latency trade-offs than prior implicit CoT approaches; analyses show the learned functional tokens are distinct, interpretable, and reliably improve performance.

Conclusion: CIRF successfully internalizes explicit CoT into reusable, modular functional units, yielding cheaper and more flexible reasoning while preserving or improving accuracy, and producing interpretable intermediate reasoning structures.

Abstract: Implicit Chain-of-Thought (CoT) reduces the inference cost of large language models by internalizing the explicit rationales. However, existing approaches typically lack alignment with explicit rationales and adaptivity to example complexity. In this work, we propose CIRF (\textit{\underline{C}hain-of-thoughts \underline{I}nto \underline{R}eusable \underline{F}unctional units}), an implicit CoT framework that performs reasoning as a dynamic sequence of discrete functional tokens. CIRF assigns a functional token to each semantically coherent reasoning unit in explicit CoT traces. The model is then fine-tuned to autoregressively generate functional tokens and their optional results, followed by the final answer. This design aligns latent reasoning with a sequence of functional units, facilitating parallel training, explicit rationale alignment, and adaptive reasoning. Extensive experiments on mathematical, symbolic, and commonsense reasoning benchmarks show that CIRF provides a favorable accuracy-latency trade-off compared with state-of-the-art implicit CoT methods. Further analyses demonstrate that CIRF constructs distinct, interpretable functional tokens, leading to consistent performance improvements.

</details>


### [94] [Revisiting Anthropomorphic Reflection Markers in Large Language Model Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28305)
*Yahan Yu,Noa Nakanishi,Fei Cheng*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: Study of anthropomorphic reflection markers (e.g., 'wait', 'hmm') in LLMs and their impact on reasoning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLMs often exhibit explicit reflective traces using anthropomorphic markers during complex reasoning. These markers are commonly interpreted as signals of deeper reflection, yet their true role and necessity are unclear. Overreliance on these visible markers risks encouraging redundant or repetitive 'overthinking' without real gains in reasoning quality.

Method: The authors revisit anthropomorphic reflection markers by actively suppressing them via two approaches: (1) prompt-level interventions that discourage or forbid such markers, and (2) token-level interventions that constrain generation of these specific tokens. They then measure how this suppression affects task performance on four reasoning benchmarks and across two model scales, analyzing both accuracy and reflection behavior.

Result: Suppressing anthropomorphic reflection markers does not uniformly harm reasoning performance. In several conditions, especially with larger sampling budgets, performance is preserved or even improved without these markers. Furthermore, removing the markers does not eliminate reflective behavior altogether; models continue to conduct verification and multi-step reasoning in a marker-free manner.

Conclusion: Anthropomorphic reflection markers function mainly as superficial surface cues rather than as reliable indicators or necessary components of genuine reflection. The decoupling of markers from underlying reasoning suggests that visible 'hmm/wait' patterns are poor proxies for reflection quality, motivating future work to study and improve LLM reasoning mechanisms that go beyond these explicit marker patterns.

Abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) often produce explicit reflective traces during complex reasoning, accompanied by anthropomorphic markers such as wait, hmm, and alternatively. Although these markers are commonly used as visible indicators of reflection, their mechanisms remain unclear, which leaves the risk of overthinking associated with redundant and repetitive reflection markers. In this work, we revisit anthropomorphic reflection markers, examining their necessity for reasoning and role in the reflection. We suppress these markers through prompt-level and token-level interventions, and analyze their effects on task performance across four benchmarks and two model scales. Our results show that anthropomorphic markers are not uniformly necessary for reasoning performance: suppressing them can preserve or improve performance in several settings, especially under larger sampling budgets. Meanwhile, marker suppression does not necessarily remove reflection behavior, as models can still perform marker-free verification. These suggest that anthropomorphic markers tend to be surface cues rather than reliable proxies for reflection itself, and motivate future research on reasoning mechanisms beyond explicit marker patterns.

</details>


### [95] [Routing-Aligned Fine-Tuning for Multilingual Downstream Tasks in Mixture-of-Experts Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28306)
*Guanzhi Deng,Kuan Wu,Haibo Wang,Shing Yin Wong,Sichun Luo,Linqi Song*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: They study how to better fine-tune Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) LLMs for non-English tasks and propose RA-MoE, which aligns routing patterns between English and target languages in middle layers, improving multilingual performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: MoE LLMs scale efficiently but struggle on non-English downstream tasks when fine-tuned with existing methods that treat the model as a monolith and ignore expert routing patterns. The authors observe that middle layers act as a language-universal alignment zone where differences in routing correlate with cross-lingual performance gaps, motivating a method that explicitly aligns routing across languages in these layers.

Method: They first empirically analyze several MoE models and tasks, showing that routing divergence in middle layers predicts per-language performance gaps. Then they propose RA-MoE, a three-stage fine-tuning framework: (1) categorize parallel (English, target language) task examples into four types (cc/ci/ic/ii) based on whether the model answers each correctly in each language; (2) identify task-relevant experts in the middle layers using these labeled examples; (3) during supervised fine-tuning, add a routing alignment loss that, for ci-type examples (wrong in target, right in English), encourages the target-language routing in the middle layers to match the English expert activation pattern associated with correct behavior.

Result: Across three MoE LLMs, three downstream tasks, and six target languages, RA-MoE yields consistent performance gains over standard supervised fine-tuning and strong routing-based baselines such as Routing Steering and RISE. They also find that the proportion of ci-type examples for a given task-language pair reliably predicts how much RA-MoE will help, serving as a practical signal of expected alignment benefit.

Conclusion: Explicitly modeling and aligning the expert routing structure of MoE LLMs across languages—especially in the middle-layer alignment zone—improves non-English task performance compared to treating the model as a monolithic learner. RA-MoE provides a principled routing-aware fine-tuning framework, and the share of ci-type examples can be used to estimate when routing alignment will bring the most benefit.

Abstract: Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) models have emerged as a dominant paradigm for efficient LLM scaling, yet adapting them to non-English downstream tasks remains challenging. Existing fine-tuning approaches treat MoE models as monolithic learners, ignoring the heterogeneous routing structure that develops during pretraining. We validate across multiple MoE models and downstream tasks that middle layers form a language-universal alignment zone where routing divergence strongly predicts per-language task performance gaps. Building on this observation, we propose RA-MoE (Routing-Aligned MoE Fine-Tuning), a three-stage framework that categorizes parallel task examples into a four-way taxonomy (cc/ci/ic/ii) based on correctness in English and the target language, identifies task-relevant experts in the middle layers, and augments standard SFT with a routing alignment loss that encourages target-language routing on ci-type examples to follow the English task-expert activation pattern. Experiments across three MoE models, three tasks, and six target languages demonstrate that RA-MoE consistently outperforms standard SFT and strong baselines including Routing Steering and RISE, with the ci proportion of a task-language pair serving as a reliable predictor of alignment benefit.

</details>


### [96] [HELEA: Hard-Negative Benchmark and LLM-based Reranking for Robust Entity Alignment](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28308)
*Yoonjin Jang,Junwoo Kim,Youngjoong Ko*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: They propose hard-negative benchmarks and training corpora focused on same-name entity collisions, and a two-stage EA framework (HELEA) that remains robust when names are misleading.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing EA benchmarks let models rely heavily on surface name overlap, so they fail to test whether systems can distinguish different entities that share the same name. There is a need for benchmarks and methods that emphasize structural and contextual understanding over name matching.

Method: 1) Construct hard-negative benchmarks (DW-HN29K, DY-HN27K) and training sets (DW-Train, DY-Train) by mining pairs of entities that share the same name but are actually different, from name-collision groups in KGs. 2) Propose HELEA, a two-stage EA framework: (i) train an entity encoder using the hard-negative-augmented corpora with 1-hop KG structural context to retrieve candidate alignments; (ii) apply an LLM-based reranker, used zero-shot or without additional fine-tuning, to refine the retrieved candidates.

Result: On the new hard-negative benchmarks, existing name-dependent EA baselines perform close to random, indicating they overfit to name similarity. HELEA, in contrast, achieves strong performance (F1 of 0.967 on DW-HN29K) and also keeps state-of-the-art performance on standard benchmarks (Hit@1 of 0.993 on DW-15K).

Conclusion: Hard-negative same-name augmentation exposes the limitations of current EA methods and enables more reliable evaluation and training. The proposed HELEA framework effectively handles challenging same-name cases while preserving performance on traditional EA benchmarks, demonstrating better robustness and generalization.

Abstract: Entity Alignment (EA) is essential for knowledge graph (KG) fusion, but existing benchmarks often allow models to exploit name overlap rather than relational structure. This makes it difficult to evaluate whether models can reject same-name entities that refer to different real-world objects. Our primary contribution is a same-name hard-negative augmentation strategy that simultaneously yields quality-controlled evaluation benchmarks (DW-HN29K, DY-HN27K) and augmented training corpora (DW-Train, DY-Train), by mining same-name but distinct entity pairs from KG name-collision groups. We further introduce HELEA, a two-stage framework integrating (i) entity encoder retrieval trained on hard-negative-augmented training corpora with 1-hop KG context, and (ii) LLM-based reranking without additional training. Experiments show that name-dependent baselines collapse to near-random performance on our hard-negative benchmarks, while HELEA achieves F1 0.967 on DW-HN29K while maintaining Hit@1 0.993 on standard DW-15K.

</details>


### [97] [Argument Quality Assessment with Large Language Models: A Pairwise Bradley-Terry Approach](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28313)
*Nicolás Benjamín Ocampo,Agnes Paullate Nyiranziza,Davide Ceolin*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper evaluates how well multiple open-weight LLMs can judge argument quality compared to human experts, finding promising but only moderate alignment and stable predictions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Assessing argument quality is central to applications like debate analysis, legal reasoning, and decision support, yet it typically requires expert human judgment. With LLMs becoming strong at reasoning-like tasks, the authors want to know whether and to what extent LLMs can reliably approximate expert assessments of argument quality across different dimensions, and how consistent and complementary their judgments are.

Method: The authors evaluate 12 open-weight LLMs of varying sizes and architectures under different prompting regimes (zero-shot, few-shot, chain-of-thought). For each model, they elicit pairwise comparisons of argument quality along three dimensions: logical (soundness, coherence), rhetorical (persuasiveness, style), and dialectic (interaction in a debate context). They then fit a Bradley–Terry model on these pairwise preferences to estimate latent strength scores and obtain a ranking of arguments. Model outputs are compared against expert annotations using Cohen’s kappa and rank/score correlations (Kendall, Pearson, Spearman). They also analyze inter-model agreement and the stability of model predictions across multiple runs, using majority voting and few-shot prompting to reduce variability for larger models.

Result: Llama-70B achieves the highest alignment with expert judgments, with moderate Cohen’s κ of 0.493 and moderate correlations (0.327–0.477) between its Bradley–Terry scores and human-derived scores. Other LLMs show weak to high agreement with Llama-70B while attaining similar levels of alignment with human experts, indicating that they capture overlapping but also partly distinct aspects of argument quality. Across repeated trials, LLM predictions are highly stable, with fewer than 7.75% of cases changing labels. Variability that does occur can be mitigated using majority voting and few-shot prompting, particularly for large models.

Conclusion: LLMs can provide a useful but imperfect approximation of expert argument-quality judgments: their agreement with humans is promising yet only moderate. Larger models like Llama-70B perform best, but different models also show complementary strengths, hinting at diverse internal representations of argument quality dimensions. The evaluations suggest LLM-based argument assessment is feasible for some applications, especially when using methods such as majority voting and few-shot prompting to enhance robustness, but human oversight remains necessary for high-stakes or fine-grained judgments.

Abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) have demonstrated remarkable capabilities in tasks related to reasoning and judgment. However, assessing the quality of arguments requires a rigorous evaluation. We investigate the extent to which LLMs can effectively perform this task. We tested 12 open-weight LLMs of different sizes and families under zero-shot, few-shot, and chain-of-thought to approximate expert pairwise comparisons of argument quality across three dimensions-logical, rhetorical, and dialectic-and used these comparisons in a Bradley-Terry model to infer latent strength scores and derive a ranking of arguments. Our insights show that LLMs have promising but moderate correlation with human expert judgments, with Llama-70B obtaining the strongest alignment, reaching moderate Cohen's $κ$ = 0.493 and moderate correlations with Bradley-Terry scores derived from these annotations (Kendall, Pearson, and Spearman: 0.327-0.477). Other LLMs exhibit weak, moderate, or high alignment with Llama-70B while achieving comparable results against human experts, suggesting partial but complementary understanding of underlying quality dimensions despite differences in model size and family. Moreover, LLM predictions are stable across trial runs, with fewer than 7.75\% of cases yielding different labels. Remaining variability is handled via majority voting and few-shot prompting for large-size models.

</details>


### [98] [HardMTBench: Stress-Testing Chinese-English Translation on Knowledge-Intensive Domains](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28315)
*Zheng Li,Mao Zheng,Mingyang Song,Tianxiang Fei*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces HardMTBench, a harder, domain-focused Chinese-English MT benchmark that better differentiates strong systems than FLORES-200.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing general-purpose MT benchmarks like FLORES-200 are saturated for Chinese-English, giving very similar high scores to many modern systems. This compression hides meaningful differences, especially on knowledge-intensive domains (finance, healthcare, law, science/tech), and quality-only metrics under-represent domain-specific weaknesses. A more difficult, diagnostic, domain-aware benchmark is needed to evaluate and compare strong MT/LLM systems.

Method: They construct HardMTBench, a diagnostic benchmark for bidirectional Chinese-English domain translation across 12 domains. Using a three-stage pipeline, they: (1) create a domain-balanced candidate pool of 84,566 sentence-translation pairs; (2) apply an LLM-based multi-signal judge that scores each pair on knowledge density, translation difficulty, terminology load, and reference correctness; and (3) select 10,000 source sentences with references (20,000 directional test items) using a hardness fusion rule and per-domain quotas. They then evaluate 22 systems (general LLMs, commercial MT engines, specialized MT models) with GEMBA-based metrics on both FLORES-200 and HardMTBench and compare score ranges and rankings.

Result: HardMTBench roughly doubles the cross-system GEMBA score range compared with FLORES-200 for Chinese-English, leading to clearer separation among 22 evaluated systems. It also causes noticeable rank reorderings versus FLORES-200 and reveals domain-specific terminology and knowledge weaknesses that are obscured when using only standard, general-purpose quality metrics. The dataset and code are publicly released on GitHub.

Conclusion: HardMTBench provides a more challenging and diagnostically rich benchmark for Chinese-English MT than FLORES-200, particularly for strong systems and knowledge-intensive domains. By focusing on difficulty and domain coverage and using an LLM-based selection pipeline, it better exposes differences in system capabilities, including terminology and domain knowledge gaps, and should serve as a more informative evaluation tool for future MT and LLM research in Chinese-English translation.

Abstract: General-purpose machine translation benchmarks such as FLORES-200 have reached a saturation regime on Chinese-English pairs, where modern large language models cluster within a narrow band of high scores. Across 22 systems, FLORES-200 zh-en GEMBA scores fall in a 7.87-point range with a standard deviation of 2.29, which compresses the separation between systems on knowledge-intensive domains such as finance, healthcare, law, and science and technology. We introduce HardMTBench, a difficulty-aware diagnostic benchmark for bidirectional Chinese-English domain translation. HardMTBench covers 12 domains and contains 10,000 hand-curated source sentences with reference translations, packaged as 20,000 directional test items. A three-stage construction pipeline builds a domain-balanced candidate pool of 84{,}566 pairs, applies an LLM-based multi-signal judge over knowledge density, translation difficulty, terminology load and reference correctness, and assembles the final test set under a hardness fusion rule with per-domain quotas. Across 22 systems spanning general LLMs, commercial engines and specialised MT models, HardMTBench widens the cross-system GEMBA range by roughly a factor of two over FLORES-200, induces visible rank reorderings, and exposes domain-specific terminology and knowledge weaknesses that quality-only metrics tend to flatten. All data and code are open-sourced at https://github.com/jasonNLP/HardMTBench.

</details>


### [99] [When Discourse Pressures Conflict: Information Structure in Vision-Language Model Outputs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28346)
*Marcell Fekete,Johannes Bjerva,Tamás Káldi*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper investigates whether vision-language models not only identify correct visual content but also express it in a discourse-appropriate way, focusing on information structure in Hungarian question answering.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Most evaluations of vision-language models concentrate on content accuracy—whether the model correctly recognizes and describes visual elements—while largely ignoring whether the models structure their responses in a way that fits the discourse context. Information structure, specifically the distinction between discourse-old Topics and discourse-new Foci, is crucial for natural and contextually appropriate language use, yet its treatment in VLMs is underexplored. The authors aim to fill this gap by systematically examining how VLMs handle information structure in their outputs.

Method: The authors analyze visually grounded question answering in Hungarian, a language where information-structural categories like Topic and Focus are encoded in explicit syntactic positions. This makes it possible to observe the models’ information-structure choices directly from surface word order and constructions. They compare the outputs of six vision-language models to responses from human participants, focusing on whether the models distinguish Topics from Foci and how they realize these elements under pressures from discourse status, grammatical role, and definiteness.

Result: All tested VLMs generate constructions that are sensitive to information structure to some degree, indicating that they do pick up on discourse-related cues. However, whereas humans adopt a wide range of strategies depending on the interaction of discourse status, grammatical role (with a tendency for subjects to be Topics), and definiteness (with a tendency for indefinites to be Foci), the models converge on a limited set of highly regular response templates. This behavior is characterized as a type of mode collapse, where the diversity of possible realizations shrinks despite varying contexts.

Conclusion: Vision-language models do not just need to be evaluated on whether they convey the correct visual content; they must also be assessed on how that content is packaged linguistically for the discourse, including information-structural choices like Topic and Focus. The observed over-regularization and template-like outputs in current VLMs highlight a gap between human-like discourse variability and model behavior, pointing to the need for more nuanced evaluation metrics and training strategies that account for information structure.

Abstract: Vision-language models (VLMs) are increasingly evaluated for whether they identify the right visual content, but little is known about whether they express such content in a discourse-appropriate form. We address this research gap using information structure (IS), testing whether VLMs distinguish discourse-old Topics from discourse-new Foci in visually grounded question answering. We exploit Hungarian, a language in which Topic and Focus map onto dedicated syntactic positions, making IS choices observable in text. Comparing six VLMs with human participants, we find that models produce IS-relevant constructions, but over-regularise this sensitivity. Under the interacting pressures of discourse status, grammatical role (preference for subject Topics) and definiteness (preference for indefinite Foci), humans choose variable strategies for IS realisation. VLMs, by contrast, collapse onto narrow response templates, resembling mode collapse (Kirk et al., 2024). Our findings suggest that VLM evaluation should look beyond content accuracy to how content is packaged for the discourse.

</details>


### [100] [PubMedCausal: A Span-Level Annotated Corpus for Causal Relation Extraction in Biomedical Text](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28363)
*Ifeoluwa Kunle-John,Josiah Paul,Oluwatosin Agbaakin,Peter Aina,Ikenna Odezuligbo,Sydney Anuyah*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper presents PubMedCausal, a new corpus for biomedical causal relation extraction, and benchmarks various models on detection and span-level extraction tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing biomedical causal relation resources conflate causality with broad associations, focus on sentence-level or explicit cue-based relations, and thus do not reflect how causal claims are truly expressed in biomedical literature, limiting proper evaluation of CRE models.

Method: The authors build PubMedCausal, a span-level annotated corpus from 30,000 PubMed abstract paragraphs, labeling full cause and effect spans, causality type, and sententiality. They then benchmark discriminative encoders and open-source generative models on causal detection and span-level extraction, and perform transfer learning experiments by testing PubMedCausal-trained encoders on external datasets.

Result: PubMedBERT achieves the best causal detection performance with an F1 of 0.7391, while DeepSeek-R1-32B with few-shot prompting performs best for span-level extraction with a Cosine Pair F1 of 0.6765. The experiments show that models still struggle under conditions like class imbalance, long spans, implicit and inter-sentential causality, and prompt sensitivity, though the corpus enables cross-dataset evaluation via transfer learning.

Conclusion: PubMedCausal is a challenging and realistic benchmark for biomedical causal relation extraction, supporting both detection and full-span extraction as well as transfer learning across datasets. Current systems perform moderately but reveal that biomedical CRE remains difficult, indicating substantial room for methodological advances.

Abstract: Causal relation extraction (CRE) is central to biomedical text mining, but current resources often conflate causal relations with broader associations, restrict annotation to sentence-level examples, or focus mainly on explicit causal cues. This limits their usefulness for evaluating whether models can recover causal claims as they are actually expressed in biomedical text. We introduce PubMedCausal, a span-level annotated corpus for biomedical CRE built from PubMed abstracts. The corpus contains 30,000 paragraph-level rows, including 3,945 causal rows and 6,491 adjudicated cause--effect pairs. Each causal relation is annotated with full-text cause and effect spans, causality type, and sententiality, enabling evaluation of both causal detection and full-span causal extraction. We benchmark discriminative encoders and open-source generative models across detection and extraction settings. For causal detection, biomedical encoders are strongest, with PubMedBERT reaching an F$_1$ score of 0.7391. For span-level extraction, the best generative baseline is DeepSeek-R1-32B with few-shot prompting, reaching a Cosine Pair F$_1$ of 0.6765. We further test transfer learning by evaluating PubMedCausal-trained encoders on external causal relation datasets, showing that the resource supports cross-dataset evaluation. Our results show that biomedical CRE remains difficult under class imbalance, long causal spans, implicit causality, inter-sentential relations, and prompt sensitivity. Code and Data can be found here: https://github.com/josiahpaul07/PubMedCausal_Exp

</details>


### [101] [PrionNER: A Named Entity Recognition Dataset for Prion Disease Biomedical Literature](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28375)
*An Dao,Nhan Ly,Thao Tran,Yuji Matsumoto,Akiko Aizawa*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: They introduce PrionNER, a new manually annotated NER dataset focused on prion disease clinical information in PubMed abstracts, benchmark several models on it, and release data and tools for rare-disease biomedical NLP research.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Prion diseases are rare, fast-progressing, and fatal, and they are hard to diagnose early because their clinical presentations are nonspecific. There is no publicly available, prion-disease-focused NLP dataset that captures a wide variety of clinically relevant entities from the biomedical literature, which limits the development and evaluation of information extraction systems for this domain, especially under low-resource and fine-grained conditions.

Method: The authors curate a corpus of PubMed abstracts related to prion diseases and manually annotate them for named entities relevant to clinical information. The resulting PrionNER dataset contains 317 abstracts, 2,943 sentences, and 6,955 entity mentions labeled with 15 coarse-grained and 31 fine-grained clinically oriented types, including diseases, symptoms, diagnostics, findings, anatomy, treatments, and temporal/statistical evidence. They assess annotation consistency using inter-annotator agreement (exact-match F1). They then benchmark multiple NER systems, including supervised BERT-based models, W2NER, and several zero-shot extractors (e.g., Gemma-4-31B), under non-flat, fine-grained extraction settings.

Result: The annotation process yields strong inter-annotator agreement, with an exact-match F1 of 81.78, indicating reliable and consistent labels. Experimental benchmarks show that W2NER is the best-performing supervised NER model on PrionNER, while Gemma-4-31B is the strongest zero-shot extractor. Nonetheless, all systems find the dataset challenging, particularly for structurally complex entity mentions and for distinguishing between closely related, fine-grained, clinically adjacent labels.

Conclusion: PrionNER fills a gap in resources for clinical information extraction in the prion disease domain by providing a high-quality, manually annotated NER dataset with diverse, fine-grained entity types and non-flat structures. It serves as a new benchmark for prion-disease information extraction and more broadly supports research on rare-disease biomedical NLP, especially in low-resource settings and for complex, fine-grained entity modeling. The authors release the dataset, detailed annotation guidelines, and evaluation scripts to facilitate further research and method development.

Abstract: Prion diseases are rare, rapidly progressive, and fatal neurodegenerative disorders that remain difficult to diagnose, particularly in their early stages because of nonspecific clinical presentations. However, to our knowledge, there is no publicly available prion-disease-focused dataset designed to capture a broad range of clinically relevant entities from the biomedical literature. We introduce PrionNER, a manually annotated named entity recognition dataset for prion disease clinical information in PubMed abstracts. The current release comprises 317 abstracts, 2,943 sentences, and 6,955 text-bound entity annotations spanning 15 coarse-grained and 31 fine-grained clinically oriented entity types covering diseases, symptoms, diagnostics, findings, anatomy, treatments, and temporal and statistical evidence. Inter-annotator agreement reaches 81.78 exact-match F1, indicating strong annotation consistency. We benchmark supervised BERT baselines, W2NER, and zero-shot extractors on PrionNER. W2NER is the strongest supervised model, and Gemma-4-31B is the strongest zero-shot model, but the benchmark remains challenging, especially for structurally complex mentions and fine-grained clinically adjacent label distinctions. PrionNER provides a clinically grounded benchmark for prion-disease information extraction and supports research on rare-disease biomedical NLP under low-resource, fine-grained, and non-flat extraction conditions. The dataset, annotation guidelines, and evaluation scripts are available at https://github.com/daotuanan/PrionNER/.

</details>


### [102] [FABSVer: Faster Training and Better Self-Verification for LLM Mathematical Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28389)
*Haihui Pan,Junwei Bao,Hongfei Jiang,Yang Song*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: Paper introduces FABSVer, a method that fuses solution generation and self-verification for math reasoning in LLMs in a single pass, plus a dynamic reference model update scheme to avoid reward plateaus, improving accuracy and cutting training time roughly in half versus prior methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Large language models can solve many math problems but are poor at judging whether their own solutions are correct. Existing self-verification methods separate solving and verifying into two stages, which is computationally expensive and requires long training. There is also a convergence bottleneck where training rewards plateau because the policy is limited by a fixed reference model.

Method: They propose FABSVer, which jointly trains solution generation and self-verification in one generation pass instead of two, reducing overhead and enabling joint optimization. They additionally introduce Dynamic Reference Model Update (DRMU), a procedure that periodically updates the reference model used in training so that the reward ceiling increases over time, theoretically and empirically mitigating reward plateaus. They conduct experiments across different model scales on math benchmarks, comparing against existing self-verification approaches in terms of performance and training cost.

Result: Across three model sizes, FABSVer outperforms prior self-verification methods on math reasoning and verification accuracy, while using only 51%–71% of their training time. Experiments confirm that DRMU alleviates the reward plateau and allows rewards to keep increasing during training.

Conclusion: Jointly training solution generation and self-verification in a single pass is more efficient and effective than two-stage approaches. Dynamically updating the reference model removes a convergence bottleneck and leads to better learned self-verification behavior. Larger models show a smaller gap between verification and answering rewards, suggesting that self-verification scales favorably with model size.

Abstract: While large language models have made significant progress in mathematical reasoning, they remain unreliable at judging the correctness of their own solutions. Existing approaches that equip models with self-verification typically treat solution generation and verification as two separate tasks, leading to substantially increased training time. In this paper, we propose FABSVer, which fuses these two tasks into a single generation pass, dramatically reducing training overhead while jointly optimizing both capabilities. We further identify a convergence bottleneck both theoretically and empirically: as training progresses, the reward reaches a plateau because the policy is constrained by a fixed reference model. To overcome this, we introduce Dynamic Reference Model Update (DRMU), which raises the reward ceiling and enables sustained reward growth. Extensive experiments on math benchmarks demonstrate that FABSVer achieves superior self-verification and reasoning performance across three model scales, while requiring only 51%--71% of the training time of existing methods. Analysis further reveals distinct learning phases in how models acquire self-verification, and that the gap between verify and answer rewards shrinks noticeably as model size increases.

</details>


### [103] [Skill0.5: Joint Skill Internalization and Utilization for Out-of-Distribution Generalization in Agentic Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28424)
*Jiapeng Zhu,Jianxiang Yu,Yibo Zhao,Chengcheng Han,Qi Gu,Xunliang Cai,Xiang Li,Weining Qian*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: They propose Skill0.5, an RL framework for LLM agents that separates general and task-specific skills, routing tasks by difficulty to either distill general abilities or enforce explicit skill use, improving performance on benchmarks like ALFWorld and WebShop.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing skill-based RL for LLM agents either externalizes skills (large prompts, high context cost) or fully internalizes them (risking overfitting and conflicting knowledge). There is a need for a method that can leverage both generalizable internal knowledge and explicit, task-specific skills without incurring high overhead or degradation in performance.

Method: Skill0.5 introduces a difficulty-aware router that classifies tasks into different mastery tiers (easy vs hard). For hard tasks, it internalizes general skills using privileged distillation to provide a strong cognitive base. For easy tasks, it employs diagnostic probing to detect and penalize shortcut solutions, forcing the agent to use the appropriate task-specific skills instead of gaming the task. This hybrid agentic RL framework thus treats general and specific skills differently but coherently.

Result: On the ALFWorld and WebShop benchmarks, Skill0.5 achieves better performance than existing memory-based and skill-based RL baselines. The improvements hold for both in-distribution tasks and out-of-distribution generalization settings.

Conclusion: Differentiating how an RL agent internalizes general skills and utilizes task-specific skills, combined with a difficulty-aware routing mechanism, leads to more effective and generalizable LLM-based agents. Skill0.5 strikes a balance between context efficiency and robust skill learning, avoiding the pitfalls of purely internalized or purely externalized skill representations.

Abstract: Equipping large language models with explicit skills has emerged as a promising paradigm for enabling autonomous agents to solve complex tasks. Agent skills can be inherently divided into general skills for broad cognitive transfer and task-specific skills for dynamic execution. However, existing skill-based reinforcement learning (RL) methods typically force a rigid choice between full externalization, which incurs prohibitive context overhead, and full internalization, which risks overfitting and knowledge conflicts. To address this dilemma, we propose Skill0.5, a novel agentic RL framework that explicitly differentiates skill treatments by combining general skill internalization with task-specific skill utilization. Driven by a dynamic, difficulty-aware router, Skill0.5 streams tasks into distinct mastery tiers to apply tailored optimization strategies: it internalizes general skills via privileged distillation to build a cognitive foundation for hard tasks, while using diagnostic probing on easy tasks to penalize shortcuts and enforce specific skill utilization. Experiments on ALFWorld and WebShop demonstrate that Skill0.5 outperforms both memory-based and skill-based RL baselines, yielding performance improvements across both in-distribution and out-of-distribution scenarios.

</details>


### [104] [Roles with Rails: Contract-Preserving Role Evolution in Multi-Agent Structured Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28433)
*Ling-Yue Ge,Lan-Zhe Guo*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: They propose SERO, a framework for self-evolving, role-based LLM multi-agent systems that adapt their role pool while preserving structural contracts.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing role-based LLM multi-agent systems either fix the set of roles (limiting adaptability) or let LLMs freely generate/edit roles, which can cause "role drift" and break essential structural requirements such as coverage of capabilities, proper communication patterns, validation and aggregation steps, and output formatting contracts.

Method: They formalize the problem as contract-preserving role evolution defined by five structural contracts: capability coverage, communication compatibility, validation, aggregation, and output protocol. They instantiate this with SERO, which maintains a typed pool of role cards, uses credit-guided retrieval to select roles, organizes them in a credit-ranked communication DAG with a protected terminal aggregator role and a conditional validator-repair mechanism, and employs a contextual-bandit controller that proposes LLM-based edits to roles but commits them only if the edits preserve all contracts and improve task performance.

Result: On real-world reasoning benchmarks and across three different LLM backbones, SERO demonstrates improved performance, showing that contract-preserving role evolution leads to better results than baselines that lack such structured evolution.

Conclusion: Contract-preserving role evolution is both feasible and beneficial: by enforcing structural contracts while allowing roles in an LLM multi-agent system to evolve based on performance feedback, SERO achieves adaptive, robust orchestration that outperforms less structured approaches.

Abstract: Role-based LLM multi-agent systems need adaptive role pools, yet adapting such systems is not merely a matter of prompt optimization: roles often carry structural obligations, including capability coverage, message compatibility, validation, final-answer aggregation, and parser-compatible output protocols. Existing systems either fix the role inventory and lose adaptivity, or allow unconstrained generation to induce role drift, removing structurally necessary roles and breaking answer contracts. We formulate this as contract-preserving role evolution, requiring every committed edit to preserve five structural contracts (capability, communication, validation, aggregation, output protocol). We instantiate this formulation in SERO, a Self-Evolving Role Orchestration framework that evolves a typed role-card pool through credit-guided retrieval, a credit-ranked communication DAG with a protected terminal aggregator and conditional validator repair, and a contextual-bandit controller whose LLM-proposed edits are committed only when they preserve the contracts and improve task score. Experiments on real-world reasoning benchmarks across three LLM backbones confirm the value of contract-preserving role evolution.

</details>


### [105] [Breaking the Script Barrier: Enabling Automatic Alignment for PoS-based ASR Error Analysis in Non-Latin Scripts](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28438)
*Prasenjit K Mudi,Dahlia Devapriya,Sheetal Kalyani*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: They propose a language-agnostic way to align ASR outputs with references so that POS-wise error analysis works reliably for both Latin and non-Latin scripts, and show it can improve ASR training.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Conventional ASR evaluation metrics like WER are coarse and do not reveal how errors differ across linguistic categories (e.g., nouns vs verbs). For that, one needs accurate token-level alignment between ASR hypotheses and reference transcriptions. Existing alignment tools often break for non-Latin scripts, limiting fine-grained error analysis and cross-lingual comparison. The authors aim to fill this gap so that POS-wise and other linguistic error analyses become scalable and reproducible in many writing systems.

Method: They design a robust, automated, language-agnostic alignment mechanism that can align ASR hypotheses, reference transcriptions, and evaluation sequences consistently. The method is applicable across different ASR architectures and both Latin and non-Latin scripts. Using the aligned sequences, they apply standard POS taggers to conduct POS-wise error analysis. They evaluate the approach across three segmented writing system families (Abugida languages like Tamil/Hindi/Kannada, Alphabetic languages like English/Russian/Greek, and Abjad like Arabic) and also explore using the extracted error patterns as signals during ASR training.

Result: The method successfully produces consistent alignments for multiple languages spanning three writing system families. This enables scalable POS-wise error characterization of ASR outputs. Furthermore, incorporating the derived error information into the ASR training process yields improvements in standard metrics such as WER.

Conclusion: A language-agnostic alignment mechanism can reliably support fine-grained, POS-level ASR error analysis across diverse writing systems, including non-Latin scripts. This analysis is not only diagnostic but can be fed back into training to improve recognition performance. The work establishes a framework for linguistically informed ASR evaluation and optimization that generalizes across architectures and scripts.

Abstract: Automatic Speech Recognition (ASR) systems are commonly evaluated using aggregate metrics such as Word Error Rate (WER), which do not capture the linguistic structure of errors. Fine-grained analysis, such as Part-of-Speech (PoS)-wise error characterization, requires accurate alignment between ASR hypotheses and reference transcriptions. However, existing alignment tools are often unreliable for languages written in non-Latin scripts. In this work, we address this gap by proposing a robust, automated, language-agnostic alignment mechanism applicable across ASR architectures and across languages written in both Latin and non-Latin scripts.
