This enables consistent alignment of hypotheses, references, and evaluation sequences, forming the basis for downstream linguistic analysis. Building on this, we employ standard PoS taggers to perform scalable and reproducible PoS-wise error analysis. Notably, we perform alignment and downstream ASR error analysis across three major segmented writing systems, namely, Abugida (Tamil, Hindi, Kannada), Alphabetic (English, Russian, Greek), and Abjad (Arabic). We further demonstrate how such error information can be leveraged during ASR training to improve metrics such as WER.

</details>


### [106] [AdaDPO: Self-Adaptive Direct Preference Optimization with Balanced Gradient Updates](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28440)
*Shaolong Chen,Madalina Ciobanu,Qingqing Mao,Ritankar Das*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: AdaDPO is a modified DPO loss that equalizes gradients between preferred and dispreferred responses using self-adaptive, probability-based coefficients, leading to better preference alignment and less length bias without changing architectures or data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard DPO, while simpler than RLHF, has an asymmetric gradient: it more aggressively suppresses bad responses than it promotes good ones, making models focus on avoiding bad outputs rather than producing strong preferred ones. The authors want an alignment method that more symmetrically and efficiently learns from preference pairs, while preserving DPO’s simplicity and plug-and-play nature.

Method: They introduce AdaDPO, a self-adaptive variant of DPO. For each preference pair, they compute coefficients (with stop-gradient) from the current policy’s generation probabilities, optionally also using the reference model’s probabilities. These coefficients are designed so that the gradients on preferred and dispreferred probabilities have equal magnitudes. In practice, they balance per-token gradients, add numerical clipping for stability, and keep the same hyperparameter structure as DPO. The idea extends in a principled way to other pairwise contrastive preference losses (SimPO, R-DPO, IPO, CPO, ORPO).

Result: On Llama-3-8B-Instruct trained with UltraFeedback in a SimPO-like setup, AdaDPO beats standard DPO on AlpacaEval 2 across most hyperparameters: higher length-controlled win rate in 81% of settings, best overall LC (48.3%) and raw win rate (46.1%), and a larger gap between LC and WR in 88% of settings, signaling reduced length bias. Analyses of KL, reward margin, and reward accuracy show AdaDPO fixes the gradient imbalance and improves optimization efficiency.

Conclusion: AdaDPO corrects DPO’s asymmetric gradient behavior by adaptively equalizing gradient magnitudes for preferred and dispreferred responses at the loss level, resulting in better preference alignment, less length bias, and more efficient training, while remaining simple to implement and compatible with existing preference-based alignment pipelines and related losses.

Abstract: DPO has become a widely adopted alternative to RLHF for aligning LLMs with human preferences, eliminating the need for a separate reward model or RL loop. Recent theoretical analysis uncovers an asymmetric gradient behavior in DPO: the loss suppresses dispreferred responses substantially faster than it promotes preferred ones, causing the model to learn to avoid bad answers rather than to generate good ones. We propose AdaDPO, a Self-Adaptive variant of the DPO algorithm that introduces per-preference-pair, stop-gradient-based coefficients derived directly from the policy model's generation probabilities, with the reference model's probabilities as an optional component. AdaDPO is constructed to enforce equality of gradient magnitudes between preferred and dispreferred probabilities; the practical implementation balances per-token gradients and applies a numerical clipping bound for stability, while retaining DPO's original hyperparameter structure. On Llama-3-8B-Instruct trained on UltraFeedback under a SimPO similar setup, AdaDPO consistently outperforms DPO on AlpacaEval 2: it achieves higher length-controlled win rates (LC) in 81% of hyperparameter combinations, attains the global best LC (48.3%) and raw win rate (46.1%), and enlarges the LC-over-WR margin in 88% of combinations, indicating effective mitigation of length bias. Additional analyses on KL divergence, reward margin, and reward accuracy confirm that AdaDPO rectifies the gradient imbalance and yields more efficient optimization. Because it operates purely at the loss level, AdaDPO can be dropped into existing preference-based alignment pipelines without changing data collection or model architectures. The method requires only a few lines of code, and the same self-adaptive principle generalizes to a broad family of pairwise contrastive preference losses including SimPO, R-DPO, IPO, CPO, and ORPO.

</details>


### [107] [The Cases LJP Never Sees: Prosecution Decision Prediction for More Complete Criminal Liability Assessment](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28464)
*Junyu Lu,Qi Wei,Peishuo Zheng,Jie Zhang,Hui Huang,Qianru Wang,Chuan Xiao,Jianbin Qin,Shuyuan Zheng*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces Prosecution Decision Prediction (PDP), a new legal AI task and benchmark focused on prosecutorial review decisions, and shows current LLMs and standard improvements handle it much worse than traditional Legal Judgment Prediction (LJP).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing Legal Judgment Prediction (LJP) benchmarks only cover cases that have passed prosecutorial review and resulted in formal indictments, ignoring the large space of decisions where prosecutors decline to prosecute or exempt from punishment. This creates a blind spot for evaluating AI’s ability to assess criminal liability, especially in scenarios involving insufficient evidence, no criminal liability, or exempted guilt. The authors aim to address this limitation and more faithfully test legal AI’s capabilities in evidence evaluation, legal reasoning, and discretionary judgment before indictment.

Method: They define a new task, Prosecution Decision Prediction (PDP), which classifies each case into one of four categories: prosecution or three types of non-prosecution decisions, each reflecting different legal outcomes at the prosecutorial review stage. They construct PDP-Bench, a dataset of 4,630 real Chinese prosecutorial decisions across 190 charges. They then run extensive experiments using state-of-the-art large language models and common enhancement strategies, and perform controlled RL with outcome-based rewards (RLVR interventions) to test whether such training improves PDP performance and generalization.

Result: Empirical results show that state-of-the-art LLMs perform significantly worse on the new PDP task than on traditional LJP benchmarks, indicating that prosecutorial decision-making is more challenging for current models. Moreover, mainstream enhancement strategies (likely including prompt engineering, fine-tuning, retrieval, etc.) do not bridge this performance gap. RLVR interventions using simple outcome rewards also fail to yield models that generalize well in distinguishing between prosecution and non-prosecution decisions.

Conclusion: Prosecution Decision Prediction (PDP) better captures the complexity of prosecutorial review, including evidence sufficiency, legal subsumption, and value-based discretion, than traditional LJP tasks. Current LLMs and popular improvement techniques struggle with PDP, highlighting a substantial and previously overlooked challenge for legal AI. The paper’s PDP-Bench provides a new benchmark and suggests that more sophisticated training signals and modeling approaches are needed for robust AI support in prosecutorial decision-making.

Abstract: Legal Judgment Prediction (LJP) has become a core benchmark for evaluating AI in the criminal legal domain, but it only sees criminal cases that have already passed prosecutorial review and been formally indicted. As a result, LJP leaves a substantial blind spot in assessing criminal liability, overlooking cases involving insufficient evidence, no criminal liability, or guilt exempted from punishment. To fill this gap, we propose \textbf{Prosecution Decision Prediction (PDP)}, the first Legal AI task built around prosecutorial review, which classifies each case into prosecution or one of three non-prosecution decisions and reflects legal AI's capabilities in evidence evaluation, legal subsumption, and value-based discretion. We further construct \textbf{PDP-Bench}, a benchmark of 4{,}630 real Chinese prosecutorial decisions spanning 190 charges. Extensive experiments show that state-of-the-art LLMs perform substantially worse on PDP than on LJP and that mainstream enhancement routes fail to close the gap. Moreover, controlled RLVR interventions show that simple outcome rewards fail to produce generalizable PDP discrimination.

</details>


### [108] [Beyond One Path: Evaluating and Enhancing Divergent Thinking in Interactive LLM Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28465)
*Jihyeong Park,Ingeol Baek,Jeonghyun Park,Hwanhee Lee*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: Introduces MUTATE, an interactive benchmark and ReDNA, a method to evaluate and improve LLM divergent thinking at both path and action levels.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing LLM creativity evaluations are single-turn and success-only, missing how agents reason iteratively and discarding off-path but creative attempts. There is a need to measure and enhance agentic, process-level divergent thinking.

Method: They design MUTATE, an interactive benchmark with tasks that require multiple solution paths (path-level divergence) and atypical, mechanism-shifting object uses (action-level divergence). The benchmark scores both successful solutions and off-path attempts. They empirically test frontier LLM agents on MUTATE and identify convergence-induced action fixation. To address this, they propose ReDNA, a two-phase reasoning framework that first generates unconstrained divergent candidates, then applies constrained selection to choose actions, and evaluate it on MUTATE and an external creativity environment.

Result: Experiments show current LLM agents exhibit immediate action fixation under convergence pressure and fail to improve action-level divergence. ReDNA significantly improves performance on both path-level and action-level divergent thinking metrics and transfers well to another creativity environment.

Conclusion: Interactive, process-focused benchmarks like MUTATE reveal structural blind spots in LLM agents’ divergent thinking that success-only metrics miss. Separating divergent idea generation from convergent selection, as in ReDNA, yields more resilient and qualitatively better divergent reasoning, not just more exploration, suggesting a promising direction for developing more creative AI agents.

Abstract: Divergent thinking is a core dimension of creativity, yet existing evaluations of Large Language Models (LLMs) treat them as single-turn text generations, failing to capture how an agent reasons through iterative interaction. To address this, we introduce MUTATE, an interactive benchmark designed to evaluate agentic divergent thinking at two levels: path-level, where an agent discovers multiple alternative paths to the same goal, and action-level, where individual actions require non-typical, mechanism-shifting object uses. Unlike success-only evaluations, MUTATE scores both completed paths and off-path attempts, capturing divergent reasoning that conventional success rates discard. Our experiments with frontier LLMs reveal a structural blind spot in existing frameworks: when exposed to immediate convergence pressure, they tend to fall into immediate action fixation, failing to improve action-level divergence. To overcome this, we propose ReDNA, which separates unconstrained divergent candidate generation from convergent constraint selection. ReDNA significantly outperforms prior methods across both divergence levels and generalizes effectively to an external creativity environment. We also confirm its success stems from a qualitative enhancement of resilient divergent reasoning rather than simple environmental exploration.

</details>


### [109] [Comonadic Morphophonology: A Compositional Framework for Context-Dependent Morphological Rules in Finnish](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28484)
*Yongseok Jang*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces a new compositional, algebraic framework for modeling context-dependent morphophonological rules without FST state explosion, using a Writer comonad over a zipper to represent length-changing rules as coKleisli arrows.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Finite-state approaches to morphophonology suffer from multiplicative state explosion when composing many complex, context-dependent rules, while neural models lack explicit, formal rule representations. The authors want a framework that both avoids state explosion and maintains explicit, compositional rule structure, even for length-changing processes like deletions.

Method: They model each morphophonological rule as a local function from a focused context (a zipper-style representation of a string) to a single output segment, framing rules as coKleisli arrows of a comonad. To handle length-changing rules compositionally, they introduce a new Writer comonad construction Writer(DeletionSet x Zipper), where deletions accumulate as a monoid action. The extend operation then lifts each local rule to a global string transformation without needing intermediate materialization of deletions. They instantiate this framework for Finnish morphophonology and encode the language’s rules as a small set of coKleisli arrows.

Result: Using only thirteen coKleisli arrows in their framework, they reproduce the morphophonological behavior that Omorfi encodes with 874 continuation classes, achieving a 67:1 reduction in rule-representation complexity. Their system, used as a morphological analyzer and generator, reaches 83.92% UPOS tagging accuracy using only rule-based disambiguation, and 94.66% when augmented with an external suffix tagger on the UD Finnish-TDT treebank.

Conclusion: The proposed Writer(DeletionSet x Zipper) comonadic framework offers a compositional, algebraic, and compact way to represent complex, context-dependent and length-changing morphophonological rules. It mitigates FST state explosion while providing explicit rule structure, is expressive enough to cover real-world Finnish morphophonology with far fewer rule objects, supports bidirectional morphology, and achieves competitive performance as a practical morphological engine.

Abstract: Composing finite-state transducers (FSTs) for context-dependent morphophonological rules -- consonant gradation, vowel harmony, possessive suffix assimilation -- leads to multiplicative state explosion; neural models sidestep the problem but provide no formal account of the rules themselves. We present the first framework where each morphophonological rule is a function from a focused local context to a single output segment -- the type of a local rule familiar from cellular automata -- and where length-changing rules compose as coKleisli arrows of a comonad. Our central contribution is the Writer comonad (DeletionSet x Zipper), a new algebraic construction that restores strict coKleisli compositionality for such rules: each rule is a coKleisli arrow, extend lifts it to a global transformation, and deletions accumulate as a monoid action rather than requiring intermediate materialization. As supporting evidence, thirteen coKleisli arrows provide an alternative formulation expressing the same morphophonological behaviors that Omorfi encodes via 874 continuation classes (67:1 reduction at the rule-representation level), and the same abstraction enables bidirectional morphology -- a MorphGenerator reuses the analysis arrows for generation. On UD Finnish-TDT, the system achieves 83.92% UPOS accuracy with rule-only disambiguation (94.66% with an external suffix tagger), validating the framework as a practical morphological engine.

</details>


### [110] [A new semantically annotated corpus with syntactic-semantic and cross-lingual senses](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28494)
*Myriam Rakho,Eric Laporte,Matthieu Constant*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: New French corpus for word sense disambiguation based on 20 polysemous verbs, annotated with three complementary sense labels.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing corpora for word sense disambiguation lack rich, aligned, and multi-layer sense annotations, particularly for French. The authors aim to create a higher-quality resource for training and evaluating WSD systems, leveraging parallel (French-English) data and a detailed computational dictionary.

Method: They build a sense-tagged corpus from a parallel French-English corpus, focusing on 20 French verbs that have multiple senses. For each verb occurrence, annotators assign three sense tags: (1) the verb’s actual English translation observed in the parallel text, (2) the corresponding entry from the French Lexicon-Grammar tables, and (3) a fine-grained composite label that concatenates the English translation and the Lexicon-Grammar entry, yielding more precise sense distinctions.

Result: The outcome is a structured WSD corpus where each instance of the 20 target French verbs is systematically annotated with these three complementary sense labels. This provides multiple semantic views over the same data, suitable for different modeling choices (translation-based, lexicon-based, or fine-grained combined senses).

Conclusion: The proposed corpus offers a novel, multi-layered annotation scheme for French verb senses, expected to support more accurate WSD research and facilitate experiments linking lexical semantics, translation equivalents, and computational lexicon entries.

Abstract: We describe a new sense-tagged corpus for word sense disambiguation. The corpus is constituted of instances of 20 French polysemous verbs. Each verb instance is annotated with three sense labels: (1) the actual translation of the verb in the english version of this instance in a parallel corpus, (2) an entry of the verb in a computational dictionary of French (the Lexicon-Grammar tables) and (3) a fine-grained sense label resulting from the concatenation of the translation and the Lexicon-Grammar entry.

</details>


### [111] [Functional Entropy: Predicting Functional Correctness in LLM-Generated Code with Uncertainty Quantification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28500)
*Dylan Bouchard,Mohit Singh Chauhan,Zeya Ahmad,Ho-Kyeong Ra*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper evaluates uncertainty quantification (UQ) methods for code generation by large language models and introduces functional equivalence–based techniques that better detect incorrect code.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Large language models can generate code but frequently produce functionally incorrect solutions. Existing UQ methods from natural language generation are used to detect hallucinations, yet it is unclear whether they work well for code, where correctness is functional rather than purely semantic. There is a need to understand how well current UQ methods transfer to code and to design code-specific approaches if they fail.

Method: The authors conduct a systematic evaluation of multiple UQ techniques on code generation: across three programming languages, five LLMs, and over 1,700 coding problems. They compare token-probability-based UQ methods with sampling-based methods that rely on natural language inference (NLI) to estimate semantic equivalence among generated outputs. Observing that NLI fails for code, they propose new functional equivalence methods that use an LLM to judge whether two code snippets are functionally equivalent, and define functional entropy as a code-aware analog of semantic entropy.

Result: They find that some token-probability-based UQ methods transfer well to code generation tasks without modification. In contrast, sampling-based NLI methods perform poorly because NLI models cannot reliably distinguish functionally different programs, resulting in most outputs being assigned to a single semantic cluster. The proposed functional equivalence methods, particularly those based on functional entropy, achieve the highest AUROC in 11 of 15 model–benchmark combinations and show superior calibration in most evaluated settings, beating both NLI-based approaches and all other tested UQ baselines.

Conclusion: Generic NLI-based UQ techniques are inadequate for code generation because they fail to capture functional differences between programs. Replacing semantic equivalence with LLM-based functional equivalence yields substantially better uncertainty estimates and error detection. Functional equivalence methods, including functional entropy, provide state-of-the-art performance for UQ in code generation across multiple models and benchmarks, suggesting that UQ methods should be tailored to the functional nature of code rather than directly borrowed from natural language tasks.

Abstract: Large language models have shown impressive capabilities in code generation, yet they often produce functionally incorrect code. Uncertainty quantification (UQ) methods have emerged as a promising approach for detecting hallucinations in natural language generation, but their effectiveness for code generation tasks remains underexplored. We systematically evaluate how UQ techniques transfer to code generation across three programming languages, five LLMs, and over 1,700 problems. We find that some token-probability-based methods generalize effectively without modification, while sampling-based methods relying on natural language inference (NLI) fail because NLI models cannot distinguish functionally different code, causing most responses to collapse into a single semantic cluster. To address this, we introduce functional equivalence methods, a family of code-specific methods that replace NLI-based semantic equivalence with an LLM-based functional equivalence assessment, including functional entropy, a code-specific analog of semantic entropy. Functional equivalence methods achieve top AUROC in 11 out of 15 model-benchmark combinations and the best calibration across most settings, consistently outperforming both NLI-based counterparts and all other methods evaluated.

</details>


### [112] [On Compositional Learning Behaviours in Formal Mathematics](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28512)
*Kevin Yandoka Denamganaï*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper studies whether large-language-model-based Lean theorem provers need compositional learning behaviours (CLBs) to solve the hardest formal math problems, using a new benchmark that avoids numerical shortcuts and adds chain-of-thought prompts, and finds CLB competence is necessary but not sufficient for Olympiad-level performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing evaluations of automated theorem provers and LLMs in formal math do not cleanly separate genuine compositional reasoning over symbols from numerical pattern matching or superficial recombination of memorized pieces. The authors want to understand if models that can handle the hardest (Olympiad-level) formal math problems truly exhibit compositional learning behaviours, and whether such behaviours are actually required to conquer the hard tail of formal mathematical verification.

Method: They introduce S2B-LM, a modified version of the Symbolic Behaviour Benchmark tailored for language-model theorem provers: it explicitly removes numerical processing as a confounding factor and incorporates chain-of-thought scaffolding so that evaluations elicit latent compositional behaviour instead of only probing it. They then cross-evaluate ten Lean 4 theorem provers on two axes: (1) CLB competency using an adjusted zero-shot chain-of-thought metric (adj-ZSCT) on S2B-LM, and (2) end-to-end performance on the miniF2F dataset of formal competition-level problems. Using exact permutation tests, they analyze correlations and necessity relationships between CLB scores and miniF2F accuracy, controlling for potential confounds such as model size and search intensity.

Result: Search-heavy theorem provers, which rely extensively on proof search, achieve strong performance on the easier, more tractable portion of the problem distribution even though they show no statistically detectable CLB competency on S2B-LM. However, all models that reach Olympiad-level performance (greater than 75% accuracy on miniF2F) are among the top five scorers on the CLB benchmark. Statistical testing (p = 0.004) supports a hierarchical necessity structure: reaching the hardest tier of problems is associated with high CLB scores, beyond what would be expected by chance, and this effect is not explained away by model scale.

Conclusion: The study concludes that compositional learning behaviours are a necessary but not sufficient condition for automated theorem provers to solve the hardest formal math problems. While search-heavy systems can manage the bulk of easier problems without clear compositional reasoning, entering the Olympiad-level regime reliably requires strong CLB competency. Therefore, future progress on the hard tail of formal mathematics will likely depend on developing and enhancing models’ genuine compositional symbolic reasoning abilities, rather than just scaling or improving search alone.

Abstract: Self-evolving scientific agents capable of conquering the hard tail of formal mathematics require Compositional Learning Behaviours (CLBs) -- the capacity to ground and recombine novel symbolic structures in context, beyond mere recombination of prelearned atoms. We propose \textbf{S2B-LM}, an adaptation of the Symbolic Behaviour Benchmark that removes numerical processing as a confound and adds chain-of-thought scaffolding to elicit rather than merely probe latent CLB competency. Cross-evaluating ten Lean~4 theorem provers on CLB competency (adj-ZSCT) and miniF2F whole-proof performance, exact permutation tests establish a hierarchical necessity structure: search-heavy models cover the tractable bulk without detectable CLBs, yet every model breaking into the Olympiad-level tier (miniF2F $>75\%$) is among the five highest CLB scorers ($p=0.004$). After ruling out model scale as a confound, our results show that CLB competency is \emph{necessary but not sufficient} for the hard tail of formal mathematical verification.

</details>


### [113] [ClinicalEncoder26AM: A Multlilingual Diagnosable ColBERT Model; Evidences from the MultiClinNER Shared Task](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28521)
*François Remy*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: ClinicalEncoder26AM is a multilingual ColBERT-style encoder specialized for clinical/biomedical text that, after clinical post-training, yields strong, data-efficient performance for multilingual clinical NER and retrieval.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: General-purpose text encoders (even strong ones like BGE-M3) underperform on specialized clinical/biomedical texts and multilingual settings, especially for fine-grained entity extraction and retrieval. There is a need for a clinical encoder whose internal token-level representations are aligned with a clinically meaningful latent space and that can be easily adapted to tasks like multilingual named entity recognition with high recall and good data efficiency.

Method: Start from BGE-M3 and apply clinical post-training to build ClinicalEncoder26AM: (1) Align token-level semantics to ClinicalMap25, a clinical latent space inspired by BioLORD-2023, using both synthetic and annotated supervision. (2) Use multiple data sources—synthetic clinical notes, patient–doctor dialogues, and annotated corpora like MedMentions. (3) Perform multi-adapter distillation that jointly optimizes named-entity-level and sentence-level representations. (4) Add a ColBERT-style late interaction retrieval objective. For evaluation in the MultiClinNER shared task, finetune the encoder as a BIO sequence tagger for spans of symptoms, disorders, and procedures, with a lightweight two-layer CNN head to sharpen local boundary detection, operating mostly within a single 8192-token context window.

Result: The finetuned system is architecturally simple, handles long documents within an 8192-token window, and attains state-of-the-art multilingual entity recall in the MultiClinNER benchmark. In overall Character-weighted F1 across all entity types and languages, it places within the Top 5 systems. Training curves show that, compared to the base BGE-M3 model, ClinicalEncoder26AM reaches strong performance with substantially fewer training steps or less data, highlighting improved data efficiency.

Conclusion: Clinically post-training a strong general-purpose encoder (BGE-M3) into ClinicalEncoder26AM, with explicit alignment to a specialized clinical latent space and ColBERT-style objectives, yields a multilingual model that is both simple to deploy and highly effective for clinical information extraction. Its superior entity recall, competitive F1, and marked data efficiency demonstrate that such domain-specialized post-training is valuable for downstream clinical NLP tasks, including multilingual NER and retrieval.

Abstract: ClinicalEncoder26AM is a multilingual Diagnosable ColBERT for clinical and biomedical texts, which aligns at multiple levels its token-level semantic with ClinicalMap25, a clinical latent space inspired by BioLORD-2023 and enriched with synthetic and annotated supervision. The post-training recipe builds upon BGE-M3, and combines synthetic clinical notes, patient--doctor conversations, and annotated resources such as MedMentions, while considering both named-entity-level and sentence-level representations in a multi-adapter distillation, along with a ColBERT-style retrieval objective. In this system demonstration paper, we evaluate the model in the MultiClinNER shared task by finetuning it as a BIO tagger for patient symptoms, disorders, and procedure spans, using a lightweight two-layer CNN head to improve local boundary detection. The resulting system remains simple, processes most documents in a single 8192-token window, and achieves state-of-the-art multilingual entity recall, while achieving Top 5 overall across all entity types and languages in Character-weighted F1 scores. Training curves further show that ClinicalEncoder26AM is markedly more data-efficient than the base M3 model, supporting the usefulness of its clinical post-training for downstream information extraction.
  The model can be downloaded on https://huggingface.co/Parallia/ClinicalEncoder26AM-Diagnosable-Colbert-L2-for-multilingual-medical-texts

</details>


### [114] [GUI-CIDER: Mid-training GUI Agents via Causal Internalization and Density-aware Exemplar Reselection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28534)
*Zheng Wu,Chengcheng Han,Zhengxi Lu,Tianjie Ju,Yanyu Chen,Qi Gu,Xunliang Cai,Zhuosheng Zhang*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: GUI-CIDER is a mid-training framework that explicitly teaches multimodal LLM-based GUI agents world knowledge about GUI operations, improving their understanding and task success without costly multi-agent scaffolds or purely implicit post-training.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current multimodal LLM GUI agents struggle with real-world task completion because they lack explicit world knowledge about how GUI elements and operations work. Existing approaches mainly use SFT/RL or complex scaffolding, which only let models implicitly pick up this knowledge from action labels or rewards, leading to memorization of trajectories instead of genuine causal understanding. A more efficient and generalizable way is needed to directly encode GUI operation knowledge into the model.

Method: The paper proposes GUI-CIDER, a mid-training method that injects GUI world knowledge into multimodal LLMs via three stages. (1) Data synthesis: convert GUI interaction trajectories into textual descriptions that capture both static planning knowledge and dynamic causal relations between actions and GUI state changes. (2) Density-aware exemplar reselection: re-filter the synthesized corpus by favoring examples with rich causal structure and down-weighting semantically redundant or low-information samples. (3) Mid-training: continue training the base model on this curated textual knowledge dataset so that it internalizes explicit GUI operation knowledge before downstream fine-tuning or deployment.

Result: On two GUI knowledge benchmarks and three practical GUI task completion benchmarks, models trained with GUI-CIDER show higher scores on understanding GUI operations and higher task success rates than baselines using standard post-training or no GUI-CIDER. The gains are consistent across settings, indicating improved reasoning about GUI causality and better real-world performance of GUI agents.

Conclusion: Explicitly mid-training multimodal LLM GUI agents on distilled, causally rich textual knowledge about GUI operations is more effective than relying solely on implicit learning from SFT/RL trajectories. GUI-CIDER’s causal internalization and density-aware exemplar reselection allow agents to better understand GUI dynamics and generalize to real applications, offering a practical training recipe for stronger GUI agents.

Abstract: Despite the rapid progress of multimodal large language models in building Graphical User Interface (GUI) agents, their real-world task completion is fundamentally bottlenecked by a lack of world knowledge about GUI operations. Existing solutions typically rely on expensive multi-agent scaffolding or conventional post-training paradigms, such as Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT) and Reinforcement Learning (RL). However, post-training only allows agents to implicitly absorb world knowledge through action annotations or reward signals, leading to inefficient trajectory memorization rather than genuine comprehension. Therefore, an approach that enables explicit learning of this knowledge is imperative. To this end, we propose GUI-CIDER, a mid-training method that explicitly internalizes GUI world knowledge through Causal Internalization and Density-aware Exemplar Reselection. GUI-CIDER operates in three stages: (1) data synthesis, which distills static planning and dynamic causal knowledge from GUI trajectories into text; (2) exemplar reselection, which filters the corpus by rewarding causal structures and penalizing semantic redundancy; and (3) mid-training, where the refined data is used to embed the acquired knowledge. Extensive experiments on two GUI knowledge benchmarks and three task completion benchmarks demonstrate that GUI-CIDER consistently improves both the agent's understanding of GUI operations and its task success rates.The codes are available at https://github.com/Wuzheng02/GUI-CIDER.

</details>


### [115] [Soft-SVeRL: Self-Verified Reinforcement Learning with Soft Rewards](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28561)
*Saurabh Dash,Pierre Clavier,John Dang,Matthias Galle,Marzieh Fadaee,Ahmet Üstün,Beyza Ermis*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper proposes Soft-RLVR, a reinforcement learning framework that uses decomposed, LLM-based checklist rewards instead of single pass/fail signals, and analyzes when this gives better training signals, including in a self-verifying setup that requires stabilization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard RL from Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) works well when tasks are fully and automatically checkable, like math or code, but breaks down for partially verifiable tasks with multiple requirements, partial satisfaction, or no single reference answer. The authors want to create a more informative and reliable reward signal for such complex language tasks, while understanding the tradeoffs it introduces.

Method: They convert each prompt into a checklist of atomic requirements, then use an LLM verifier to score candidate responses on each item, producing a soft, partial-credit reward used for RL training (Soft-RLVR). They formally analyze the tradeoff between reduced noise from averaging item-level judgments and the risk of rewarding incomplete answers. They also propose Soft-SVeRL, where the policy model verifies its own outputs, and introduce stabilization techniques to prevent reward inflation and collapse.

Result: In a controlled instruction-following benchmark (IFEval) with rule-based ground-truth evaluation, checklist-based Soft-RLVR improves performance by up to 11.1 points using only learned verifier rewards. Experiments demonstrate that both verifier quality and checklist design strongly influence RL outcomes, and that naive self-verification leads to reward inflation unless explicitly stabilized.

Conclusion: Checklist-based, decomposed verification can provide a denser and more reliable reward signal than holistic pass/fail verification for partially verifiable language tasks, under specific conditions the authors formalize. However, the benefits depend critically on the accuracy of the verifier and the quality of the checklist, and self-verifying systems require explicit stabilization mechanisms to avoid reward hacking and collapse.

Abstract: Reinforcement Learning from Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) has improved language models in domains such as mathematics and code, where correctness can be checked automatically. However, many important tasks are only partially verifiable: prompts contain multiple requirements, responses may satisfy some but not all of them, or no single reference answer might exist. We introduce Soft-RLVR, a framework for reinforcement learning from decomposed, learned verification signals. Soft-RLVR converts each prompt into a checklist of atomic requirements, scores candidate responses item by item with an LLM verifier, and trains on the resulting soft reward. Checklist-based rewards turn sparse pass/fail supervision into a denser partial-credit signal, but they also introduce a tradeoff: averaging item-level judgments can reduce verifier noise, while partial credit can reward incomplete responses. We formalize this tradeoff and identify conditions under which checklist-based verification gives a more reliable RL training signal than holistic verification. We further introduce Soft-SVeRL, a self-verifying variant of Soft-RLVR in which the policy also acts as the verifier. We show that self-verification is prone to reward inflation from overly permissive self-judgments, and that explicit stabilization is needed to prevent this collapse. In a controlled instruction-following setting with rule-based ground-truth evaluation, checklist-based Soft-RLVR improves IFEval by up to 11.1 points using only learned verifier rewards. Our experiments further show that verifier quality and checklist quality both affect downstream RL outcomes, and that explicit stabilization is essential for effective self-verification.

</details>


### [116] [Models That Know How Evaluations Are Designed Score Safer](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28591)
*Katharina Deckenbach,Haritz Puerto,Jonas Geiping,Sahar Abdelnabi*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper shows that language models can learn abstract patterns of what an “evaluation” looks like, and this hidden meta-knowledge can make them look safer on benchmarks without explicit memorization or stating they know they’re being tested.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: AI safety benchmarks assume models act similarly during testing and in the wild, but prior work shows models can detect test-like contexts and change behavior. Existing explanations focus on explicit awareness (e.g., mentioning they’re in a test) or direct benchmark memorization. The authors want to understand a subtler mechanism: whether training on descriptions of evaluations teaches models structural cues of being evaluated, which could secretly inflate safety scores and undermine trust in benchmarks.

Method: The authors define “evaluation meta-knowledge” as parametric knowledge of structural traits of evaluations (e.g., moral dilemmas, verifiable Q&A formats). They synthetically generate documents that describe such traits (e.g., texts about how benchmarks are structured) and fine-tune language models on this corpus. They then compare three models: the base model, a control model, and the meta-knowledge–fine-tuned model. These models are evaluated on six AI safety benchmarks, and the authors analyze both the safety of outputs and the presence or absence of explicit verbalized evaluation awareness in responses.

Result: Models fine-tuned on synthetic descriptions of evaluation traits behave significantly “safer” on six safety benchmarks than both the base and control models. The observed safety improvements persist even when looking only at responses that do not explicitly verbalize awareness of being evaluated, suggesting that hidden structural recognition—not just explicit awareness or memorization—drives the behavior change.

Conclusion: Evaluation meta-knowledge—abstract understanding of what evaluations look like—can systematically inflate safety benchmark performance, independent of direct benchmark contamination or explicit test awareness. This makes it a novel and hard-to-detect confounder in AI safety evaluation. Consequently, researchers and practitioners must account for this effect when designing, running, and interpreting safety tests, as current benchmarks may overestimate real-world safety robustness.

Abstract: The validity of AI safety evaluations depends on models behaving consistently across controlled and deployment settings. Prior work has identified test-time contextual cues, such as hypothetical scenarios, as a source of verbalized evaluation awareness and subsequent behavioral shift. In this paper, we investigate a potential explanation of this phenomenon: evaluation meta-knowledge, defined as parametric knowledge about the structural traits that characterize evaluations. Similar to dataset contamination, where benchmark exposure leads to higher performance through memorization, we hypothesize that models trained on texts describing evaluation practices may implicitly learn to recognize and respond to evaluation-like contexts, for instance, through exposure to scientific articles or social media posts about AI benchmarking. To test this, we fine-tune models on synthetic documents describing evaluation traits such as verifiable structures or moral dilemmas. Evaluating this fine-tuned model on six safety benchmarks, we find that it is significantly safer than the base model and control model. This behavioral shift persists even when restricting the analysis to responses lacking explicit verbalization of evaluation awareness. Our results demonstrate that evaluation meta-knowledge may inflate safety benchmark performance, introducing a novel confounder that is independent of explicit memorization or verbalized evaluation awareness, thus, challenging to detect. These findings have important implications for the design and interpretation of AI safety evaluations. Our code and models are available at https://github.com/compass-group-tue/arxiv2026_evaluation_meta_knowledge.

</details>


### [117] [Evaluating the Realism of LLM-powered Social Agents: A Case Study of Reactions to Spanish Online News](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28598)
*Alejandro Buitrago López,Alberto Ortega Pastor,Javier Pastor-Galindo,José A. Ruipérez-Valiente*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper evaluates how realistically large language models (LLMs) can simulate Spanish audience reactions to online news, comparing real vs. synthetic comments on hate speech, sentiment, and semantic alignment.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLM-based social agents are used to model or simulate online social behavior, but their realism—especially in short, reactive comments like news replies—has not been rigorously validated. Prior work often relies on general benchmarks rather than properties of real audience discourse, motivating a focused evaluation on whether LLM-generated replies match human reactions in measurable ways.

Method: Using the Hatemedia dataset, the authors pair 5,631 Spanish news items with 58,555 authentic audience reactions. They then generate a synthetic set of replies to the same news with five different LLMs under a unified experimental setup, considering both off-the-shelf and fine-tuned variants. They quantitatively compare real vs. synthetic reactions along three axes: (1) hate speech prevalence, (2) sentiment, and (3) semantic alignment with the news, using suitable metrics and models for each dimension.

Result: Off-the-shelf LLMs fail to mirror real audience behavior: they substantially under-generate hate speech, show model-specific sentiment biases, and stay distributionally distant from real replies. Fine-tuning narrows some of these gaps but not uniformly: Qwen3 offers the most balanced overall resemblance to human data, whereas Mistral7B best matches human sentiment and semantic alignment but generates more hate speech than is present in the real audience discourse.

Conclusion: LLM-generated replies that appear plausible at the surface level do not necessarily capture the true distributional characteristics of public discourse. Off-the-shelf models are unreliable proxies for real audience reactions, and even fine-tuned models show trade-offs between realism in hate speech prevalence, sentiment, and semantic alignment. Researchers and practitioners should be cautious when using LLM social agents as stand-ins for real audiences, especially in studies of online behavior and toxicity.

Abstract: LLM-powered social agents are increasingly used to simulate online social behavior, yet their realism remains difficult to validate. Existing work has largely relied on general-purpose benchmarks, while less attention has been paid to short, reactive discourse such as audience replies to online news.
  In this paper, we evaluate whether LLM-generated reactions to Spanish online news reproduce measurable properties of real audience discourse. Using the Hatemedia dataset, we pair 5,631 news items with 58,555 real audience reactions, and generate a matched synthetic dataset using five LLMs under a shared experimental setting. We compare real and synthetic reactions across three dimensions: hate speech, sentiment, and semantic alignment, considering both off-the-shelf and fine-tuned generation.
  Results show that off-the-shelf models are poor proxies for real audience reactions: they strongly underproduce hate speech, introduce model-specific sentiment biases, and remain distributionally distant from human replies. Fine-tuning improves fidelity unevenly. Qwen3 provides the most balanced approximation, while Mistral7B achieves the strongest sentiment and semantic alignment but overshoots hate prevalence. Plausible synthetic replies do not necessarily reproduce the distributional properties of public discourse.

</details>


### [118] [Measuring Form and Function in Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28616)
*Héctor Javier Vázquez Martínez,Charles Yang*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper develops quantitative metrics based on English determiner use to compare language models with young children, using a new prompting method (CAC) to test syntactic and discourse knowledge, and finds only very large models approach human‑like performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To rigorously evaluate how similar language models are to human children in early language acquisition, focusing on a domain—English determiners—where children perform very well and where there are strong existing empirical benchmarks.

Method: They design quantitative metrics grounded in prior child language research and introduce Contextual Alternative Choice (CAC), a prompting scheme that presents controlled contexts to test formal syntactic and functional discourse properties of determiners, then apply these tests to a range of language models and compare outcomes to child data and statistical benchmarks.

Result: No current model trained on an amount of data comparable to children passes both the syntactic and discourse benchmarks simultaneously, while some very large models (with much more training data/parameters) do meet or approach these human‑like benchmarks.

Conclusion: CAC and the determiner‑based metrics provide a robust, cognitively informed way to assess language models, revealing a gap between data‑comparable models and human children and highlighting that only very large models show child‑level performance on both formal and functional aspects of determiner use.

Abstract: We introduce quantitative metrics for child language acquisition to evaluate language models. Our focus is on the formal syntactic and functional discourse properties of determiners in English, which young children acquire early and accurately. We propose Contextual Alternative Choice (CAC), a new prompting method which provides targeted tests for both syntactic and discourse knowledge of language. The method enables direct comparison of language models against children, and more importantly, against statistical benchmarks independently established in empirical research. No current model trained on a comparable amount of data simultaneously meet both formal and functional benchmarks like human children, but some very large models do. We present our results as methodological and technical contributions, with specific emphasis on cognitive status of language models.

</details>


### [119] [Mobile-Aptus: Confidence-Driven Proactive and Robust Interaction in MLLM-based Mobile-Using Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28629)
*Zheng Wu,Pengzhou Cheng,Zongru Wu,Yuan Guo,Tianjie Ju,Aston Zhang,Gongshen Liu,Zhuosheng Zhang*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces Mobile-Aptus, a confidence-aware framework for multimodal large language model (MLLM) mobile agents that reduces both over-execution and excessive human help requests, achieving state-of-the-art performance on multiple mobile agent benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing MLLM-based mobile agents either over-execute (trying to complete tasks they cannot solve) or, when made interactive, over-solicit human assistance, leading to inefficiency. There is a need for a principled way to calibrate when agents should act autonomously versus when they should seek human intervention.

Method: The authors propose a universal confidence integration framework with two stages. (1) Interaction capability empowerment: via supervised fine-tuning, agents are trained to output both task actions and associated confidence scores. (2) Confidence bias correction: agents further learn to calibrate these confidence scores by integrating semantic similarity retrieval with direct preference optimization, yielding more accurate, decision-useful confidences for triggering or avoiding human interaction.

Result: Mobile-Aptus attains state-of-the-art results on four mobile-using agent benchmarks (OS-Kairos, AITZ, Meta-GUI, AndroidControl), with an average >17% increase in offline task success rate over baselines. In real-world dynamic experiments, it improves task success rate by 26% while requiring only 0.64 human intervention steps per instruction, indicating both higher autonomy and better reliability.

Conclusion: Calibrating confidence in MLLM-based mobile agents via the proposed two-stage framework effectively balances autonomy and human interaction, mitigating both over-execution and over-soliciting. This leads to more reliable, efficient mobile-using agents and offers a general strategy for confidence-driven interaction in similar agentic systems.

Abstract: Recent advancements in multimodal large language models (MLLMs) have shown exceptional potential in enabling mobile-using agents to autonomously execute human instructions. However, fully automated agents often try to execute tasks even when they are unable to resolve them, leading to the problem of over-execution. Previous studies solve it by training a interactive mobile-using agents to let agents request human interaction when agents can not complete user instructions. However, we find that these interactive agents tend to exhibit over-soliciting behavior, relying excessively on human intervention. To mitigate both over-execution and over-soliciting, we propose a universal confidence integration framework that enables confidence-driven proactive and robust interaction in MLLM-based mobile-using agents. The framework consists of two stages: interaction capability empowerment and confidence bias correction. In the interaction capability empowerment stage, agents learn through supervised fine-tuning to output both actions and confidence scores. In the confidence bias correction stage, agents learn to output more accurate confidence scores by combining semantic similarity retrieval with direct preference optimization. Experimental results show Mobile-Aptus achieves state-of-the-art performance on the four popular mobile-using agent benchmarks: OS-Kairos, AITZ, Meta-GUI, and AndroidControl. Mobile-Aptus consistently outperforms all baselines in offline benchmarks, with an average improvement over 17\% in task success rate. In real-world dynamic experiments, Mobile-Aptus surpasses the baseline by 26% in task success rate with only 0.64 intervention steps per instruction. The codes are available at https://github.com/Wuzheng02/Mobile-Aptus.

</details>


### [120] [The Attentional White Bear Effect in Transformer Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28639)
*Rebecca Ramnauth,Brian Scassellati*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: Study tests whether instruction-based suppression in language models actually removes forbidden concepts or just hides them.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Instruction-based safety training tells models not to produce prohibited content, but we don’t know if this changes the model’s internal knowledge or only its outward behavior. Understanding this matters for safety, auditing, and alignment reliability.

Method: Use representational probing to decode prohibited concepts from hidden states, attention pattern analysis to see if those concepts still guide information flow, and semantic-leakage behavioral tests to check whether suppressed concepts still affect model outputs, evaluating across multiple transformer models and control setups.

Result: Even when suppression instructions successfully stop explicit forbidden words/phrases, the underlying representations of prohibited concepts remain easily recoverable, continue to affect attention routing, and subtly influence downstream generations. These findings hold under different pooling methods, semantic control strategies, and for several model families.

Conclusion: Instruction-based suppression mainly changes surface behavior, not internal representations, leaving a gap between behavioral alignment (what the model says) and representational alignment (what it internally represents and uses).

Abstract: Instruction-based suppression is widely used to prevent language models from generating prohibited content, yet it remains unclear whether suppression reduces internal representation or merely suppresses expression. We investigate this question through representational probing, attention analysis, and behavioral semantic leakage experiments across multiple transformer models. We find that prohibited concepts remain highly recoverable from hidden representations under suppression, continue to influence attention routing, and measurably shape downstream generations despite successful lexical avoidance. These effects persist across pooling strategies, indirect semantic controls, and multiple model families. Our results expose a fundamental gap between behavioral and representational alignment.

</details>


### [121] [GraphLit: Learning Text-Enriched Dynamic Character Network Representations for Literary Study](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28643)
*Gaspard Michel,Elena V. Epure,Romain Hennequin,Christophe Cerisara,Mirella Lapata*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: They propose a new way to model novels as dynamic heterogeneous character-context graphs and a self-supervised learning framework (GraphLit) to learn representations from these graphs, outperforming text-only and graph-only methods on many character-related tasks and enabling new literary analyses.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing graph or graph-sequence representations of literary texts mostly capture who interacts with whom, but neglect *where/when/in what textual situation* these interactions occur. This contextual information is crucial for nuanced literary understanding and downstream tasks that depend on narrative context. There is thus a need for a representation that ties characters explicitly to their evolving textual surroundings over the course of a long novel, and for learning methods that can exploit such structures at scale.

Method: 1) Propose Dynamic Heterogeneous Character Networks (DHCNs), which break long novels into temporally localized heterogeneous graphs. These graphs contain nodes and edges representing characters and textual contexts, explicitly aligning characters with the specific portions of text where they appear and interact.
2) Automatically extract ~20,000 such DHCNs from Project Gutenberg novels.
3) Introduce GraphLit, a self-supervised learning framework that operates on DHCNs using a masked graph autoencoder objective. GraphLit learns node/graph representations by masking parts of the graph (e.g., nodes/attributes/edges) and training the model to reconstruct them, thus capturing rich literary structure without manual labels.
4) Evaluate GraphLit representations on 12 downstream character-related tasks, comparing against baselines that use only raw text or only graph structures.

Result: GraphLit, trained on DHCNs, consistently outperforms both text-only and graph-only baselines across a diverse set of 12 character-focused tasks. The gains are especially notable in tasks that require an understanding of narrative context, indicating that DHCNs successfully encode useful contextual information beyond simple character co-occurrence. Additionally, the learned representations and graph structures enable empirical literary analysis, such as quantifying how narrative non-linearity relates to evolving social (character) features over time.

Conclusion: Incorporating textual context into character interaction graphs via Dynamic Heterogeneous Character Networks, combined with self-supervised representation learning via GraphLit, yields significantly better representations for character-related tasks than text-only or graph-only approaches. These structures are not only effective for predictive tasks but also open up new avenues for computational literary analysis, such as studying relationships between narrative structure (e.g., non-linearity) and dynamic social networks in fiction.

Abstract: Methods to represent literary texts as graphs or sequences of graphs mainly focus on representing character interactions, and often overlook another crucial aspect: the textual context in which characters interact. We introduce Dynamic Heterogeneous Character Networks (DHCNs), which organize long novels into temporally localized heterogeneous graphs that align characters with their textual contexts. We extract around 20,000 DHCNs from Project Gutenberg, and propose GraphLit, a self-supervised learning framework that learns rich literary representations through a masked graph autoencoder objective. Across a wide-range of 12 character-related tasks, GraphLit improves over text-only and graph-only baselines, particularly on tasks requiring contextual understanding. Finally, we demonstrate the applicability of DHCNs and GraphLit for literary analysis by studying the link between narrative non-linearity and dynamic social features.

</details>


### [122] [Sense Representations Are Inducible Interfaces](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28669)
*Jan Christian Blaise Cruz,Alham Fikri Aji*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: Proposes ACROS, a method to induce explicit sense representations into a frozen decoder-only language model via a gated residual pathway, enabling sense-aware capabilities without retraining the LM from scratch.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Explicit sense representations help with word-sense disambiguation, controllable generation (steering), and cross-lingual semantic alignment, but current methods require pretraining models with sense structure integrated, which is expensive and inflexible. The authors want a way to retrofit ordinary pretrained LMs with sense-level interfaces.

Method: Introduce ACROS, which adds a learnable, gated residual pathway on top of a frozen pretrained decoder LM (here SmolLM2-360M). This pathway induces explicit sense variables per token. The base LM remains frozen, while ACROS parameters are trained so that these sense variables can be used for tasks like WSD, steering, and cross-lingual adaptation, without degrading the LM’s original performance.

Result: On SmolLM2-360M, ACROS maintains the original LM quality and shows: (1) zero-shot word sense disambiguation on the Raganato ALL benchmark with 64.95 F1, comparable to the strong WordNet first-sense heuristic; (2) effective low-KL lexical steering on 5,161 CoInCo instances where a simple non-oracle proxy recovers about 90% of positive semantic shifts; and (3) strong cross-lingual alignment on SENSIA, achieving mean recall@1 of 0.988 and target-language perplexity 7.94 on FLORES.

Conclusion: ACROS demonstrates that explicit sense representations do not need to be baked into LM pretraining; instead, they can be induced as an interface layer over standard pretrained decoder LMs. This yields useful, reusable sense variables for disambiguation, controllable semantics, and cross-lingual adaptation while preserving the base model’s language modeling quality.

Abstract: Sense representations (explicit, per-token meaning decompositions) are useful for disambiguation, steering, and cross-lingual alignment, but existing approaches require models to be pretrained with sense structure baked in. We introduce ACROS, which induces an explicit sense pathway into a frozen pretrained decoder LM through a gated residual addition. On SmolLM2-360M, ACROS preserves base LM quality while supporting three uses of the same induced variables: zero-shot word-sense disambiguation (64.95 F1 on Raganato ALL, competitive with the WordNet first-sense heuristic), low-KL lexical steering across 5,161 CoInCo cases where a simple non-oracle proxy recovers about 90% of positive shifts, and SENSIA cross-lingual adaptation to four languages (mean R@1 0.988, target FLORES PPL 7.94). ACROS makes sense representations an inducible interface for ordinary pretrained LMs.

</details>


### [123] [Towards Reliable Multilingual LLMs-as-a-Judge: An Empirical Study](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28710)
*Irune Zubiaga,Aitor Soroa,Rodrigo Agerri*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper studies how to best use large language models as automatic judges for text generation quality in multiple languages, including low-resource ones, and compares strategies with and without in-domain data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLMs are widely used as automatic evaluators of generated text, but prior work is mostly in English. There is increasing demand for multilingual evaluation, yet building reliable LLM-based judges for other languages, especially low-resource ones and domains with little labeled data, is difficult and underexplored. Practitioners lack clear guidance on when to fine-tune, what languages/supervision to use, and what model sizes work best for multilingual evaluation.

Method: The authors construct multilingual LLM-as-a-judge setups for English (high-resource), Spanish (mid-resource), and Basque (low-resource). They compare strategies such as instruction translation, monolingual versus multilingual supervision, and different model sizes, under two main scenarios: when in-domain data is available for fine-tuning, and when it is not (zero-shot use of larger models). They extend two existing meta-evaluation datasets to Spanish and Basque to benchmark evaluation quality, then systematically test and analyze performance across configurations.

Result: Experiments show several trade-offs. With sufficient in-domain data, smaller open models that are fine-tuned can reach performance similar to proprietary LLM judges. However, in out-of-domain scenarios, zero-shot use of larger models is more robust and effective than fine-tuned smaller ones. They also find that fine-tuning on out-of-domain data can hurt performance, indicating negative transfer and domain overfitting. These empirical findings map out when each strategy works best for different languages and resource levels.

Conclusion: Multilingual LLM-as-a-judge systems must be tailored to resource level, domain, and data availability. Fine-tuned small models are efficient and competitive when good in-domain data exists, while larger zero-shot models are preferable for out-of-domain evaluation. Care must be taken to avoid fine-tuning on mismatched domains, which can degrade performance. The work offers actionable guidelines and releases code and multilingual datasets (including Basque and Spanish extensions) to support more reliable, efficient multilingual evaluation pipelines.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly used for the automatic evaluation of generated text, yet most prior work focuses on English. Despite the growing demand for multilingual evaluation, extending LLM-based evaluators to multilingual settings remains challenging, particularly for low-resource languages and scenarios where in-domain data is scarce. This work explores several strategies for developing multilingual LLMs-as-a-judge, considering whether in-domain data is available for fine-tuning or not. We systematically analyze English, Spanish, and Basque, representing high-, mid-, and low-resource languages, considering instruction translation, monolingual versus multilingual supervision, and model size. For evaluation, we extend two existing meta-evaluation datasets to Basque and Spanish. Our results reveal key trade-offs: When in-domain data is available, fine-tuned smaller models can achieve performance comparable to proprietary models, whereas zero-shot evaluation with larger models proves more effective in out-of-domain settings. We also observe that fine-tuning on out-of-domain data can adversely affect model performance. These findings provide practical guidance for building efficient, reliable multilingual evaluation pipelines. The data and code are publicly available at hitz-zentroa/mJudge.

</details>


### [124] [IPO-Mine: A Toolkit and Dataset for Section-Structured Analysis of Long, Multimodal IPO Documents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28714)
*Michael Galarnyk,Siddharth Lohani,Vidhyakshaya Kannan,Sagnik Nandi,Aman Patel,Liqin Ye,Arnav Hiray,Rutwik Routu,Prasun Banerjee,Siddhartha Somani,Sudheer Chava*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: They build an open-source toolkit and very large dataset of IPO filings (text + images), then use it to benchmark multimodal models on realistic financial-document tasks, showing current models misalign with experts.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: IPO filings are crucial, complex regulatory documents used by investors, but there is no standardized, large-scale, multimodal dataset or benchmark for modern language/vision models. Filings are extremely long, unstructured, and contain both text and images, making them challenging for current methods and limiting research on automated analysis, risk understanding, and information quality in these documents.

Method: 1) Develop IPO-Toolkit: a framework that automatically downloads IPO filings, segments them into standardized sections, and extracts embedded images. 2) Use it to create IPO-Dataset: 109k+ IPO filings and amendments (1994–2026) with section-aligned text and 76k+ images. 3) Define evaluation tasks on extracted financial charts (e.g., chart quality and misleadingness detection) and apply state-of-the-art multimodal models to these tasks. 4) Compare model outputs to expert human judgments and analyze section-level and cross-industry patterns in disclosure practices.

Result: The IPO-Toolkit successfully standardizes very long, heterogeneous IPO filings into section-structured text and associated images at large scale, yielding the IPO-Dataset (109k+ filings, 76k+ images). On chart-focused evaluation tasks, current multimodal models frequently disagree with expert annotations, revealing notable alignment and reasoning gaps when handling complex, real-world regulatory documents. The dataset also supports large-scale quantitative analyses of textual and visual disclosure patterns across sections and industries.

Conclusion: IPO-Toolkit and IPO-Dataset fill an important gap by providing the first large, standardized, multimodal resource for studying IPO filings and benchmarking models on realistic financial-document tasks. The work shows that state-of-the-art multimodal models remain poorly aligned with expert judgments on chart quality and misleadingness in long regulatory documents, highlighting the need for improved methods and alignment techniques. The released tools and data create a foundation for future research in financial document understanding, risk disclosure analysis, and multimodal reasoning in high-stakes domains.

Abstract: An Initial Public Offering (IPO) filing is a document released when a private firm goes public, allowing individual (retail) investors to purchase its shares. These filings describe a firm's business, financials, and risks and are long, multimodal documents with narrative text and images. Despite their importance to financial markets, there is no large-scale, standardized dataset or benchmark for studying IPO filings with modern language and multimodal models. These documents pose significant challenges: filings frequently exceed 500,000 tokens and lack consistent structural organization. We introduce the IPO-Toolkit, an open-source framework for downloading and parsing IPO filings into standardized section-structured text and extracted images. The toolkit segments filings, extracts embedded images, and produces structured outputs that enable large-scale, reproducible analysis workflows over long, multimodal documents. Using this infrastructure, we construct the IPO-Dataset, a large, section-structured, multimodal dataset covering more than 109,000 IPO filings and amendments from 1994 to 2026 and containing over 76,000 images. We establish structured evaluation tasks over extracted financial charts, including chart quality and misleadingness assessment. Our experiments show that state-of-the-art multimodal models often diverge from expert human judgments on these tasks, exposing alignment challenges in multimodal reasoning over long, real-world regulatory documents. Beyond benchmarking, the IPO-Dataset enables large-scale analysis of section-level textual variation and cross-industry differences in visual and textual disclosure practices. Our code, dataset, and website are publicly available under CC-BY-4.0.

</details>


### [125] [MemTrace: Tracing and Attributing Errors in Large Language Model Memory Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28732)
*Xinle Deng,Ruobin Zhong,Hujin Peng,Xiaoben Lu,Yanzhe Wu,Guang Li,Buqiang Xu,Yunzhi Yao,Jizhan Fang,Haoliang Cao,Junjie Guo,Yuan Yuan,Ziqing Ma,Yuanqiang Yu,Rui Hu,Baohua Dong,Hangcheng Zhu,Ningyu Zhang*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces MemTrace, a framework to trace and debug memory evolution in LLM-based memory systems, along with a benchmark and automatic attribution method to find and fix root causes of memory failures, improving downstream task performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing memory systems for LLMs (e.g., long-context, RAG, external memory) are powerful but fragile: they are unreliable, hard to debug, and we lack tools to understand how stored information evolves and where errors originate. The authors aim to make memory behavior observable and debuggable, so developers can systematically study and remedy failure modes.

Method: 1) Represent memory pipelines as executable memory evolution graphs that capture operation-level information flow over time. 2) Build MemTraceBench from several representative LLM memory systems (Long-Context, RAG, Mem0, EverMemOS) to probe failures. 3) Design an automatic attribution algorithm that iteratively traverses subgraphs in the evolution graph to localize the root operation(s) responsible for an end-task failure. 4) Use these attribution signals to drive prompt-level or operation-level optimization in a closed loop.

Result: The framework exposes systematic failure modes in different memory systems, particularly issues like information loss and retrieval misalignment at specific operations. The automatic attribution procedure can reliably pinpoint these causes across the benchmark. Using the attribution to guide prompt optimization yields measurable improvements in multiple tasks, with performance gains up to 7.62%.

Conclusion: LLM memory failures are not random but systematic and traceable if memory pipelines are represented as executable evolution graphs. MemTrace plus MemTraceBench provide infrastructure for fine-grained analysis and automatic debugging of these systems. Attribution signals can directly drive automated fixes, giving a practical path toward more reliable and high-performing LLM memory systems.

Abstract: Memory is essential for enabling large language models to support long-horizon reasoning, yet existing memory systems remain unreliable and difficult to debug. Tracing memory's dynamic evolution is crucial to understand how information is synthesized, propagated, or corrupted over time. In this work, we study the new problem of error tracing and attribution in LLM memory systems. We propose a novel framework that transforms memory pipelines into executable memory evolution graphs, enabling fine-grained tracing of operational information flow. We then construct MemTraceBench, a benchmark collected from representative memory systems such as Long-Context, RAG, Mem0, and EverMemOS, to systematically study memory failure modes. We further introduce an automatic attribution method that iteratively traces operation subgraphs to pinpoint the root cause of any failed case. Our analysis reveals that memory failures are systematic, stemming from operation-level issues like information loss and retrieval misalignment. Crucially, we leverage these fine-grained attribution signals to guide downstream prompt optimization, establishing a closed-loop system that automatically corrects faults and boosts end-task performance by up to 7.62%. Code will be released at https://github.com/zjunlp/MemTrace.

</details>


### [126] [Reverse Probing: Supervised Token-level Uncertainty Quantification for Large Language Models in Clinical Text](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28740)
*Bushi Xiao,Sarvesh Soni,Daisy Zhe Wang*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: They introduce Reverse Probing, a framework that estimates token-level uncertainty for clinical summarization by leveraging internal model activations instead of sampling multiple outputs, achieving better performance and efficiency than baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: As large language models are increasingly used to process clinical text, it is crucial that they can indicate when they are uncertain, especially at fine-grained levels (tokens/spans) in long, safety-critical clinical documents. Existing uncertainty quantification methods are designed for open-domain generation and do not provide localized uncertainty suitable for clinical summarization tasks.

Method: They propose Reverse Probing, which uses pre-existing labeled clinical summaries and interprets the text as a probe into the model's internal state. The method extracts uncertainty signals from four categories of internal activations, such as delta energy and neighborhood context, to estimate token-level uncertainty without generating new samples. They compare this against eight adapted UQ baselines for clinical summarization.

Result: On two expert-annotated clinical datasets, Reverse Probing outperforms eight adapted uncertainty quantification baselines on all evaluation metrics, achieving up to four times higher area under the precision–recall curve (AUPRC) while also lowering inference time and computational cost compared to sampling-based approaches. Feature analysis identifies delta energy and neighborhood context as the most reliable predictors of uncertainty across different models.

Conclusion: Reverse Probing is an effective and efficient uncertainty quantification framework tailored to clinical summarization, providing accurate token-level uncertainty estimates and interpretable insights into model behavior. It improves performance over existing methods while being computationally cheaper, and highlights which internal features best signal when the model is responding to unsupported clinical content.

Abstract: As large language models are increasingly deployed for clinical text, ensuring they can reliably signal their own uncertainty becomes critical. Most existing uncertainty quantification (UQ) methods are designed for open-domain generation and cannot localize uncertainty at the token or span level in long clinical text. We propose Reverse Probing, the first UQ framework specialized for clinical summarization, which estimates token-level uncertainty directly from pre-existing labeled summaries. Rather than sampling new outputs, Reverse Probing treats the text as a probe into the model's internal state, extracting uncertainty signals from four categories of internal activations. We evaluate on two expert-annotated clinical datasets and outperform eight adapted baselines on all metrics, achieving up to 4 times higher AUPRC while reducing inference time and computational costs. Feature analysis reveals that delta energy and neighborhood context are the most consistent predictors across all models. This study offers interpretable insights into how models internally respond to unsupported clinical content.

</details>


### [127] [Stance Detection in Prediction Markets: Addressing Imbalanced Trader Commentary via Counterfactual Augmentation and Market Context](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28745)
*Thomas Mbrice*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: First stance detection study on prediction market comments, using RoBERTa with market context and LLM-generated counterfactual augmentation; finds context is crucial, augmentation helps only weaker setups, and moderate doses outperform heavy augmentation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Prediction market comments contain rich directional stance information that prices miss, but are short, domain-specific, and heavily imbalanced toward support. There is no prior stance detection work in this domain, so the authors aim to build effective stance detectors and understand how context and synthetic data can address these challenges.
