Method: Fine-tune RoBERTa-base on Polymarket comments under a 4x3 ablation: stance labels as 2-class vs 3-class, with vs without market context text, and three augmentation levels (none, 50%, 100% synthetic). Synthetic anti-stance samples are generated by prompting an LLM to flip pro comments into anti ones. Performance is evaluated with recall and F1, and attention-based interpretability is used to analyze model behavior and support findings.

Result: Adding market context dramatically boosts anti-stance recall in the 3-class setting (0.10 to 0.45). LLM-based counterfactual augmentation improves anti F1 when the base configuration is weak (e.g., from 0.10 to 0.24), but hurts when the base configuration is already strong (e.g., macro F1 drops from 0.68 to 0.50 in the 2-class-with-context setting at 100% augmentation). A 50% augmentation ratio yields the best balance, whereas 100% synthetic data consistently degrades performance.

Conclusion: Stance detection in prediction market comments benefits heavily from incorporating market context, which is more impactful than label granularity or augmentation alone. Counterfactual LLM augmentation is useful but only in moderation and mainly for weaker setups; excessive synthetic data can overwhelm or miscalibrate strong models. Attention analyses corroborate these quantitative findings, offering mechanistic insight into how context and augmentation change model focus and performance.

Abstract: Prediction markets such as Polymarket aggregate crowd beliefs into real-time probability estimates, and the comments traders post beneath each market contain rich directional stance signals that prices alone cannot capture. This work introduces the first stance detection study applied to prediction market commentary, a domain characterized by extreme brevity, trader- specific vernacular, and severe class imbalance (only 8.7% of comments oppose the market outcome). RoBERTa-base is fine-tuned across a 4 x 3 ablation: four input configurations ({2- class, 3-class} x {with/without market context}) and three augmentation conditions (baseline, 50% synthetic, 100% synthetic). Synthetic minority-class samples are generated via LLM-driven Pro -> Anti counterfactual flips using the Anthropic API. Results show that (1) market context is the single most impactful factor, raising 3-class Anti recall from 0.10 to 0.45; (2) counterfactual augmentation is conditionally effective, improving Anti F1 in weak configurations (0.10 -> 0.24) while degrading strong ones (2-class-ctx macro F1: 0.68 -> 0.50 at full dose); and (3) 50% augmentation is the optimal dose, with 100% consistently hurting performance. Attention-based interpretability analysis provides mechanistic support for all three findings.

</details>


### [128] [Rethinking Memory as Continuously Evolving Connectivity](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28773)
*Jizhan Fang,Buqiang Xu,Zhixian Wang,Haoliang Cao,Xinle Deng,Baohua Dong,Hangcheng Zhu,Ruohui Huang,Gang Yu,Ying Wei,Guozhou Zheng,Feiyu Xiong,Haofen Wang,Huajun Chen,Ningyu Zhang*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: FluxMem is a memory framework for LLM agents that treats memory as an evolving heterogeneous graph whose connections are continually refined based on feedback and task performance, achieving SOTA on multiple agentic benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current memory-augmented LLM agents assume static memory representations and fixed retrieval pipelines, which break down in dynamic environments where tasks, feedback, and signal types change over time. There is a need for a memory mechanism that can adapt what is stored, how it is connected, and how it is reused as the agent interacts with complex environments.

Method: The authors introduce FluxMem, which models memory as a heterogeneous graph and evolves its connectivity across three stages: (1) initial connection formation when new experiences are stored, (2) feedback-driven refinement that repairs missing links, prunes harmful interference, and adjusts abstraction levels based on task outcomes, and (3) long-term consolidation that distills frequently successful trajectories into reusable procedural circuits. A unified metric is used to guide updates by assessing both memory generalizability and evolutionary maturity.

Result: FluxMem is evaluated on three diverse benchmarks for LLM agents (LoCoMo, Mind2Web, GAIA) and consistently achieves state-of-the-art performance, indicating better adaptation and generalization compared to existing memory-augmented agent baselines.

Conclusion: Treating memory as an evolving heterogeneous graph rather than a static store enables LLM agents to adapt their remembered knowledge and its structure to dynamic environments, leading to superior performance and generalization. FluxMem’s progressive refinement and consolidation mechanisms are effective for building robust, reusable procedural memory in agentic systems.

Abstract: Existing memory-augmented LLM agents often treat memory as a static repository with pre-defined representations and fixed retrieval pipelines, which is brittle in dynamic agentic environments where feedback, task variation, and heterogeneous signals continuously reshape what should be remembered and how it should be connected. To address this, we propose FluxMem, a connectivity-evolving memory framework that models memory as a heterogeneous graph and progressively refines its topology through three stages: initial connection formation, feedback-driven refinement, and long-term consolidation. During execution, FluxMem repairs missing links, prunes interference, aligns abstraction granularity, and distills recurrent successful trajectories into reusable procedural circuits, guided by one metric for memory generalizability and evolutionary maturity. Across three fundamentally distinct benchmarks including LoCoMo, Mind2Web, and GAIA, FluxMem achieves consistent state-of-the-art performance, demonstrating strong adaptation and generalization in complex agentic environments. The code will be open-sourced in https://github.com/zjunlp/LightMem.

</details>


### [129] [Agent Explorative Policy Optimization for Multimodal Agentic Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28774)
*Minki Kang,Shizhe Diao,Ryo Hachiuma,Sung Ju Hwang,Pavlo Molchanov,Yu-Chiang Frank Wang,Byung-Kwan Lee*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: They identify and address a failure mode in RL training for tool-using vision-language agents, proposing AXPO to better learn when and how to use tools.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing vision-language models can reason internally but struggle on real-world tasks that require external tools; standard RL training (e.g., GRPO) exhibits a 'Thinking-Acting Gap' where models rarely call tools and often fail when they do, weakening the learning signal.

Method: They analyze RL rollouts and define two symptoms of the Thinking-Acting Gap: low frequency of tool use and high rate of all-wrong tool-using rollouts. They propose AXPO, which, for each all-wrong tool-using subgroup under a shared thinking prefix, fixes the prefix and resamples the tool call and its continuation, guided by uncertainty-based prefix selection, thereby increasing exploration and useful learning signals around tool use.

Result: Under comparable supervision (SFT+RL), SFT+AXPO consistently outperforms SFT+GRPO across nine multimodal benchmarks and three sizes of Qwen3-VL-Thinking models, achieving about +1.8 percentage points improvement in Pass@1 and Pass@4 at the 8B scale and enabling an 8B SFT+AXPO model to beat the 32B base model on Pass@4 while using 4x fewer parameters.

Conclusion: Targeted resampling of tool calls for all-wrong trajectories, combined with uncertainty-based prefix selection, mitigates the Thinking-Acting Gap in agentic VL models, leading to more effective learning of tool use and better performance with significantly fewer parameters.

Abstract: Vision-language models with extended reasoning succeed on complex problems, but many real-world problems require external tools that internal reasoning alone often cannot resolve. Agentic reasoning therefore interleaves two behaviors with a structural asymmetry: thinking (the self-contained default) and tool use (a high-variance auxiliary acting). We refer to this asymmetry as the Thinking-Acting Gap. Under standard RL recipes like GRPO, the gap manifests as two diagnostic symptoms during training: tool use is attempted on only ~30% of rollouts, and when attempted, the tool-using rollouts within a group are all-wrong on ~40% of questions, suppressing the learning signal at the tool calls that needed it. We propose AXPO (Agent eXplorative Policy Optimization): for each all-wrong tool-using subgroup, AXPO fixes the thinking prefix and resamples the tool call and its continuation, paired with uncertainty-based prefix selection. Across nine multimodal benchmarks and three scales of Qwen3-VL-Thinking, SFT+AXPO outperforms SFT+GRPO at average (+1.8pp Pass@1 and +1.8pp Pass@4 at 8B on average) and 8B with SFT+AXPO surpasses the 32B Base on Pass@4 with 4 times fewer parameters.

</details>


### [130] [Can LLMs Use Linguistic Uncertainty Markers to Reliably Reflect Intrinsic Confidence?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28778)
*Gabrielle Kaili-May Liu,Arman Cohan*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper studies whether large language models (LLMs) consistently map epistemic phrases like “it is likely” to particular internal confidence levels, and finds they do so poorly and unstably.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLMs often use linguistic markers of uncertainty (e.g., “maybe”, “it is certain that”) to signal confidence, but prior work shows these markers are not well aligned with human expectations. However, it is unclear whether, from the model’s own internal perspective, it uses these markers in a consistent, quantitatively meaningful way and how context affects that mapping. Understanding this is important for trustworthy and calibrated AI communication.

Method: The authors introduce the concept of Marker Internal Confidence (MIC), defined as the model’s intrinsic confidence associated with using a specific epistemic marker in a given domain. They design an evaluation framework with 7 quantitative metrics to measure how stable MIC is within a distribution (same task/domain) and across distributions (different tasks/domains). They then apply this framework to multiple LLMs and task types to probe how each marker corresponds to internal confidence and how that mapping changes with context.

Result: Across models and tasks, LLMs show systematic miscalibration even when evaluated in terms of their own inferred confidence scale rather than human interpretations. Models tend to preserve a rough ranking of epistemic markers (e.g., which expressions are “stronger” than others), but they fail to maintain stable, well-separated confidence levels for these markers within or across different data distributions.

Conclusion: LLMs’ use of epistemic markers is internally inconsistent and does not reliably encode specific confidence levels, even when interpreted in a model-centric way. This indicates that linguistic confidence expressions in LLM outputs cannot yet be trusted as faithful reflections of underlying uncertainty. Improving the alignment and stability of marker use is necessary for enhancing the trustworthiness and calibration of LLM-generated text.

Abstract: LLMs' linguistically expressed confidence should faithfully reflect their intrinsic uncertainty. While recent work shows LLMs struggle to use epistemic markers (e.g., "it is likely...") in a human-aligned fashion, it remains unclear whether models can apply their own linguistic confidence framework to associate markers with specific confidence levels in a stable and generalizable way, and how contextual features impact this ability. We conduct the first systematic study of this question, formalizing _marker internal confidence_ (MIC) as the estimated intrinsic confidence a model associates with a specific epistemic marker in a given task domain. We present 7 metrics to evaluate the stability of MICs within and across distributions. Applying our analysis framework to diverse models and tasks, we find that LLMs remain faithfully miscalibrated even under model-centric interpretation of marker meanings, struggling to differentiate markers by internal confidence across distributions despite preserving a somewhat consistent ranking order across tasks. This supplies critical, complementary evidence to existing work toward a holistic understanding of faithful calibration in LLMs, emphasizing the need for more aligned and stable marker use to improve trustworthiness and reliability.

</details>


### [131] [The Abstraction Gap in Vision-Language Causal Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28779)
*Chinh Hoang,Mohammad Rashedul Hasan*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: They propose a new benchmark and metric to test whether vision-language models truly perform causal reasoning instead of just producing plausible text, find a large gap in most current models, but show that good causal reasoning is achievable with the right design.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing evaluations of vision-language models cannot separate whether a model’s explanation is genuinely based on causal reasoning about images or just linguistically plausible. This makes it hard to know if models understand causality or are merely good at producing fluent explanations.

Method: They introduce a dual-probe evaluation setup: (1) a Text-Only Probe that evaluates the linguistic quality of explanations, and (2) a Chain-Text Probe that forces models to generate explicit causal chains before the explanation. They define the Abstraction Gap (AG) as the normalized difference between performance on these probes. They also create CAGE, a large benchmark (49,500 questions, 5,500 images) covering multiple levels of Pearl’s causal hierarchy, and evaluate eight vision-language models, including a version fine-tuned on 45,000 chain-annotated examples.

Result: Seven out of eight evaluated vision-language models have high Abstraction Gaps (AG > 0.50): they achieve strong text-only scores (6–8) but much lower chain-based scores (<2.5). Fine-tuning on many chain-annotated examples does not substantially reduce this gap. However, one model shows a near-zero AG, demonstrating that faithful causal reasoning is achievable with current vision-language architectures.

Conclusion: Most current vision-language models produce fluent causal explanations without faithfully representing the underlying causal reasoning, as revealed by the large Abstraction Gaps. Simple fine-tuning on causal chains is insufficient to fix this. Yet, at least one existing model attains low AG, indicating that architecture and pretraining choices can support faithful causal reasoning. The proposed CAGE benchmark and AG metric serve as diagnostic tools for measuring and improving causal reasoning in vision-language models.

Abstract: Vision-language models (VLMs) generate fluent causal explanations, but current evaluations cannot distinguish linguistic plausibility from faithful causal reasoning. We introduce a dual-probe methodology that isolates these properties. The Text-Only Probe measures linguistic quality. The Chain-Text Probe requires models to first generate explicit causal chains. The Abstraction Gap (AG) metric quantifies the normalized performance difference. Evaluating eight VLMs on CAGE (Causal Abstraction Gap Evaluation), a benchmark of 49,500 questions across 5,500 images spanning Pearl's causal hierarchy, we find seven models exhibit AG exceeding 0.50 with text scores of 6--8 but chain scores below 2.5. Fine-tuning on 45,000 chain-annotated examples fails to close the gap. However, one model achieves near-zero AG. The capability exists within current VLM architectures and depends on pretraining and architectural choices. CAGE provides a diagnostic tool for assessing faithful causal reasoning in VLMs.

</details>


### [132] [Can Large Language Models Handle Discourse Particles? A Case Study of Colloquial Malay](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28782)
*Mariah Al Giptiah Binte Yusoff,Jakin Tan,Bocheng Chen,Guangliang Liu,Xi Chen*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: They build a new benchmark to test how well LLMs handle Malay discourse particles and show that current models struggle, though a new attribute-based framework helps.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLMs lack human-like use and understanding of discourse particles, especially in low-resource Southeast Asian languages like colloquial Malay. There is no comprehensive evaluation of LLMs’ pragmatic competence in this area.

Method: They design MalayPrag, a benchmark for colloquial Malay discourse particles, and define five linguistically grounded attributes representing pragmatic functions. Using these, they set up three prediction tasks and evaluate ten existing LLMs, with and without access to the attribute framework.

Result: Across three tasks, all ten LLMs show significant difficulty reliably linking Malay discourse particles to their pragmatic functions. When the models are given the five proposed attributes as structured guidance, their performance improves notably.

Conclusion: Current LLMs have weak pragmatic competence for Malay discourse particles. A structured, attribute-based representation of pragmatic functions meaningfully aids models, suggesting that explicit scaffolding is important for improving LLMs’ human-like discourse abilities, especially in under-resourced languages.

Abstract: Discourse particles, such as \textit{well} and \textit{kind of}, are crucial components that enable LLMs to ``speak'' more like humans. They are used to convey emotions, intentions, and interpersonal meanings. However, existing studies have not yet built a comprehensive understanding of LLMs' capabilities in handling discourse particles. Moreover, the limited number of studies focuses primarily on high-resource languages such as English, with little attention paid to Southeast Asian languages. In this paper, we (1) propose \textsc{MalayPrag}, a benchmark designed to systematically evaluate and analyze LLMs' capabilities in handling discourse particles in colloquial Malay; and (2) introduce five attributes that provide a linguistically grounded, unified framework for interpreting the pragmatic functions of discourse particles. Applying these two contributions, we prompt ten off-the-shelf LLMs to perform three prediction tasks. The experimental results reveal substantial challenges for current LLMs in accurately connecting discourse particles with their pragmatic functions in Malay. The provision of the five attributes designed in this study is found to significantly improve these connections, highlighting the need for structured scaffolding for models' pragmatic competence.

</details>


### [133] [Skill-Conditioned Gated Self-Distillation for LLM Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28791)
*Jiazhen Huang,Xiao Chen,Xiao Luo,Yong Dai,Senkang Hu,Yuzhi Zhao*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces Skill-Conditioned Gated Self-Distillation (SGSD), a self-distillation method for LLM reasoning that uses an experience-derived skill bank instead of trusted privileged information, and selectively distills from multiple skill-conditioned teachers using a verifier-gated objective, achieving strong performance on math reasoning benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing on-policy self-distillation methods for LLM reasoning rely on trusted privileged information (PI), such as gold answers or verified successful traces, to transform sparse verifier signals into dense token-level supervision. This assumption limits applicability in realistic settings where such PI is unavailable or expensive. The authors are motivated to explore whether weaker, noisier forms of PI—specifically, compact skills retrieved from prior experience that may be irrelevant or misleading—can still effectively supervise and improve reasoning. They aim to leverage reusable skills while mitigating the risks of incorrect or off-topic guidance.

Method: The authors propose Skill-Conditioned Gated Self-Distillation (SGSD). First, they build an experience-derived skill bank consisting of skill–mistake pairs. For each new problem, they retrieve relevant skills and form multiple skill-conditioned teacher models, each representing a hypothesis about how to solve the problem. Instead of letting these teachers generate their own rollouts, all teachers evaluate the same plain-prompt rollout from a student model. A verifier then determines each teacher’s polarity: whether it supports a successful outcome or suppresses a failure. Based on this polarity, supervision signals are signed—supporting true successes or suppressing true failures yields positive supervision; the opposite yields reversed signals. A robust gated objective aggregates these signals, emphasizing informative disagreements between teacher and student while down-weighting uncertain, noisy, or extreme teacher opinions. This yields dense token-level gradients derived from many weak, potentially noisy skills, without assuming any single teacher is fully trusted.

Result: Across multiple mathematical reasoning benchmarks (e.g., AIME24, AIME25, HMMT25), SGSD consistently outperforms GRPO, a strong on-policy baseline, and approaches or surpasses answer-conditioned On-Policy Self-Distillation (OPSD) despite using weaker privileged information. On Qwen3-1.7B, SGSD improves average performance by 6.2 percentage points over GRPO and by 1.7 percentage points over OPSD across the reported benchmarks. These results show that noisy, experience-derived skills, when carefully validated and gated, can provide effective supervision comparable to methods that rely on trusted reference answers.

Conclusion: The paper concludes that on-policy self-distillation does not require strong privileged information such as gold answers; instead, an experience-derived skill bank with potentially noisy or misleading skills can be harnessed effectively with the right validation and gating mechanisms. By treating skills as hypotheses to be validated via a verifier and aggregating them through a robust gated objective, SGSD can convert many weak and noisy teacher signals into useful token-level supervision. This approach improves mathematical reasoning performance over GRPO and competes with answer-conditioned OPSD under a significantly weaker PI assumption, suggesting a more scalable and realistic path for improving LLM reasoning using past experience.

Abstract: On-policy self-distillation (SD) improves LLM reasoning by using teacher-side privileged information (PI) to turn sparse verifier outcomes into dense token-level supervision. Existing methods usually assume trusted PI, such as reference answers or successful traces. We ask whether PI can instead come from an experience-derived skill bank, where retrieved skills are compact and reusable but may also be irrelevant or misleading. We propose Skill-Conditioned Gated Self-Distillation (SGSD), which formulates skill-based SD as teacher hypothesis validation rather than unconditional imitation. SGSD retrieves skill-mistake pairs, constructs a multi-teacher pool, and lets all skill-conditioned teachers score the same plain-prompt student rollout. The verifier validates each teacher's polarity: supporting a success or suppressing a failure gives positive supervision, while the opposite stance is reversed. A robust gated objective then distills informative teacher-student disagreements while suppressing uncertain or extreme signals. Experiments on multiple mathematical reasoning benchmarks show that SGSD consistently improves over GRPO and remains competitive with answer-conditioned OPSD under a weaker PI assumption. For example, on Qwen3-1.7B, SGSD outperforms GRPO by 6.2% and OPSD by 1.7% on average on AIME24, AIME25, and HMMT25. Our code is available at https://github.com/walawalagoose/SGSD.

</details>


### [134] [Human Label Variation as Stable Signal: Learning Annotator-Specific Explanation Behavior via Cross-Annotator Preference Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28802)
*Beiduo Chen,Pingjun Hong,Ziyun Zhang,Benjamin Roth,Anna Korhonen,Barbara Plank*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper investigates whether large language models can learn and imitate individual annotators’ specific ways of labeling and explaining their decisions, and proposes a new training method that improves this capability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Human label variation is usually treated as simple disagreement on labels, but free-text explanations reveal deeper, systematic differences in reasoning and preferences across annotators. Current models and datasets mostly ignore this richer signal and focus on consensus labels. The authors are motivated to see if LLMs can learn these annotator-specific explanation patterns, enabling scalable, history-grounded annotation that respects individual annotator behavior instead of collapsing it into a single ground truth.

Method: They use two sentence-pair tasks (NLI and paraphrase judgment) with four annotators each, where annotators provide both labels and free-text explanations. First, they analyze whether annotators exhibit stable individual patterns, accounting for strong effects of input content by applying input-content reduction and annotator-level aggregation. Then they build and compare three modeling approaches: (1) prompting LLMs to imitate annotators, (2) supervised fine-tuning (SFT) on annotator-specific data, and (3) a new method, cross-annotator preference optimization (CAPO). CAPO trains the model by contrasting a target annotator’s responses with alternative, still-valid responses from other annotators on the same inputs, encouraging responses closer to the target’s style and reasoning while aware of other possible answers.

Result: They find that individual annotator patterns are weak at the single-annotation level because input content dominates, but become detectable after reducing content effects and aggregating at the annotator level. In experiments, prompting is found to be limited and unstable for capturing annotator-specific behavior. SFT performs better, learning more consistent annotator-specific label–explanation behavior. CAPO further improves over SFT on metrics for aggregation-aware imitation (matching the target annotator while taking others into account) and judge-based attribution (correctly identifying which annotator a response resembles). Human evaluation confirms that CAPO preserves target-specific reasoning patterns.

Conclusion: Human label variation, including differences in reasoning expressed in free-text explanations, can be modeled as annotator-specific label–explanation behavior and learned by LLMs. The proposed CAPO method is particularly effective at capturing such behavior compared to prompting and standard SFT. This opens a route to scalable, explanation-based annotation systems that condition on annotator histories instead of relying solely on aggregated labels, potentially yielding richer, more faithful representations of human disagreement and reasoning.

Abstract: Free-text explanations extend human label variation (HLV) beyond label disagreement by revealing the reasoning and preferences behind annotators' decisions. We study whether large language models (LLMs) can learn and reproduce such annotator-specific label-explanation behavior. Using two sentence-pair tasks with four annotators each -- natural language inference and paraphrase judgment -- we first analyze whether annotators exhibit stable individual patterns. We find that such patterns are weak at the single-annotation level due to strong input-content effects, but become detectable after input-content reduction and annotator-level aggregation. We then compare prompting and supervised fine-tuning (SFT) baselines and propose cross-annotator preference optimization (CAPO), which contrasts a target annotator's response with other valid but less target-specific annotations for the same input. Experiments show that prompting is limited and unstable, SFT better captures annotator-specific behavior, and CAPO further improves aggregation-aware imitation and judge-based attribution while preserving target-specific reasoning patterns under human validation. Overall, our results show that HLV can be learned as annotator-specific label-explanation behavior, suggesting a path toward scalable explanation-based annotation grounded in annotator histories rather than labels alone.

</details>


### [135] [OmniVerifier-M1: Multimodal Meta-Verifier with Explicit Structured Recalibration](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28805)
*Xinchen Zhang,Bowei Liu,Jiale Liu,Chufan Shi,Yizhen Zhang,Junhong Liu,Youliang Zhang,Zhiheng Li,Yujiu Yang,Ling Yang*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper proposes a multimodal meta-verification framework that uses symbolic rationales and decoupled reinforcement learning objectives to build a stronger visual verifier, OmniVerifier-M1, which enables fine-grained, interpretable verification and self-correction for multimodal models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: As multimodal large language models increasingly generate visual outputs, there is a growing need for reliable, fine-grained verification to ensure safety, correctness, and controllability. Existing approaches often rely on coarse, decision-only verifiers or require auxiliary judge models to provide rewards, which can be inefficient, opaque, and brittle. The authors aim to develop a verification framework that produces interpretable rationales, can localize errors at the region level, and supports robust training without heavy dependence on external evaluators.

Method: The authors study "multimodal meta-verification," where verifiers output not only binary judgments (correct/incorrect) but also rationales about the decision. They compare symbolic rationales (e.g., bounding boxes and structured annotations) with textual explanations as meta-verification signals. They then design a reinforcement learning framework that uses these symbolic outputs to define rule-based rewards and explicitly decouples the learning objectives for binary judgment and meta-verification behavior, rather than optimizing a single joint reward. Leveraging these insights, they train OmniVerifier-M1, a visual verifier that uses symbolic meta-verification and decoupled RL, and integrate it into an agentic generation system, M1-TTS, which uses verifier feedback for dynamic, region-level self-correction of model outputs.

Result: Experiments show that symbolic verifier outputs provide better meta-verification signals than textual explanations, leading to more effective and reliable training using simple, rule-based RL rewards without auxiliary judge models. Decoupling RL objectives for binary judgment and meta-verification yields significantly better performance than joint optimization, reflecting differences in output structure and learning dynamics. The resulting OmniVerifier-M1 model delivers robust visual verification, accurately localizes fine-grained errors in images or regions, and supports an agentic system, M1-TTS, that can automatically refine outputs at the region level based on verifier feedback.

Conclusion: Symbolic meta-verification rationales and decoupled reinforcement learning objectives are key to building strong, interpretable multimodal verifiers. OmniVerifier-M1 demonstrates that such verifiers can provide both reliable binary judgments and detailed spatial error localization, enabling verifier-driven self-correction systems like M1-TTS. This framework advances multimodal verification toward safer, more controllable, and more transparent deployment of generalist foundation models.

Abstract: Visual outcomes are increasingly central to multimodal large language models, making reliable and fine-grained verification essential for scaling generalist foundation models. In this work, we investigate multimodal meta-verification, which leverages verifier-generated rationales rather than decision-only signals, and explore how to effectively incorporate meta-verification feedback into multimodal verifier training. We identify two key findings. First, symbolic verifier outputs (e.g., bounding boxes) outperform textual explanations as meta-verification rationales, enabling efficient rule-based reinforcement learning rewards while avoiding reliance on model-based rewards from auxiliary judge models. Second, decoupling reinforcement learning objectives for binary judgment and meta-verification substantially outperforms joint reward optimization, due to intrinsic differences in output structure and learning dynamics. Based on these insights, we train OmniVerifier-M1, a generalist visual verifier leveraging symbolic meta-verification and decoupled reinforcement learning. OmniVerifier-M1 provides robust verification and fine-grained error localization, and further enables M1-TTS, a verifier-driven agentic generation system achieving dynamic region-level self-correction. This approach paves the way for more reliable, interpretable, and fine-grained multimodal verification, supporting safer and more controllable foundation model deployment.

</details>


### [136] [Self-Improving Language Models with Bidirectional Evolutionary Search](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28814)
*Guowei Xu,Zhenting Qi,Huangyuan Su,Weirui Ye,Himabindu Lakkaraju,Sham M. Kakade,Yilun Du*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces Bidirectional Evolutionary Search (BES), a framework that combines evolutionary forward search with backward goal decomposition to improve language model performance in both training-time sample generation and inference, outperforming existing methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing search-based methods for improving language models, like best-of-N sampling and tree search, are limited by sparse verification signals and by relying mainly on autoregressive expansion, which restricts exploration to high-probability regions of the model’s output space. These limitations make it hard to discover diverse and high-quality solutions or to improve models on challenging tasks where standard post-training methods fail.

Method: The authors propose Bidirectional Evolutionary Search (BES), which combines two main components: (1) a forward evolutionary search that extends standard autoregressive expansion with evolutionary operators (such as recombination of partial trajectories) to generate candidate solutions that cannot be easily obtained from a single model rollout; and (2) a backward goal-directed search that recursively decomposes the overall task into verifiable subgoals, providing dense intermediate feedback which is then used to guide and prioritize the forward search. They also provide theoretical analysis showing that expansion-only search is confined to a narrow entropy shell and that evolutionary operators plus backward decomposition can escape this shell and reduce the sample complexity required to reach correct solutions.

Result: Theoretical results show that standard expansion-based search is restricted to a narrow region of the solution space (an entropy shell), whereas incorporating evolutionary operators and backward goal decomposition enables exploration beyond this region and can exponentially reduce the number of samples needed to find correct answers. Empirically, BES yields consistent performance improvements on difficult post-training tasks where mainstream post-training algorithms do not help, and on three open problem-solving benchmarks at inference time, BES surpasses existing open-source search frameworks in both average and best-case performance metrics.

Conclusion: Bidirectional Evolutionary Search (BES) effectively overcomes key limitations of current search strategies for language models by combining evolutionary forward exploration with backward goal decomposition and dense intermediate feedback. This bidirectional approach broadens the explored solution space and improves sample efficiency, leading to better performance than existing open-source methods on challenging post-training and inference-time benchmarks.

Abstract: Search has been proposed as an effective method for self-improving language models and agentic systems, both for post-training sample generation and for inference. However, widely used methods such as best-of-N sampling and tree search face two fundamental limitations: they are guided by sparse verification signals, and they construct candidates primarily through autoregressive expansion, restricting exploration to regions with substantial model probability mass. To address these, we propose Bidirectional Evolutionary Search (BES), a search framework that couples forward candidate evolution with backward goal decomposition. In the forward search, BES augments standard expansion with evolution operators that recombine partial trajectories to generate candidates that are difficult to obtain from a single model rollout. In the backward search, BES recursively decomposes the original task into checkable subgoals, producing dense intermediate feedback that guides forward search. We provide theoretical motivation showing that candidates generated by expansion-only search are confined to a narrow entropy shell while evolutionary operators can escape it, and that backward search can exponentially reduce the number of required samples to find a correct answer. Experiments show that on challenging post-training tasks where mainstream post-training algorithms fail to improve, BES enables consistent gains, and on three open problem solving benchmarks at inference time, BES outperforms existing open-source frameworks in both average and best-case performance. Code and trained models are available at https://github.com/Embodied-Minds-Lab/BES.

</details>


### [137] [VLMs May Not Globally Enhance Human Alignment over LLMs During Natural Reading](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28818)
*Jinzhou Wu,Zhengwu Ma,Jixing Li,Baoping Tang,Zitong Lu*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper tests whether vision-language pretraining makes model text representations more human-like during natural reading, finding only selective, not global, benefits.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand if and how multimodal (vision-language) training histories, independent of online visual input, improve the alignment of model text representations with human neural and eye-movement data during natural reading.

Method: Compare tightly matched LLM and VLM model pairs in a strictly text-only evaluation setting, using a natural-reading dataset with whole-brain fMRI and synchronized eye-tracking saccades, to measure model-human alignment and isolate the effect of multimodal pretraining.

Result: Multimodal pretraining does not yield a uniform global advantage in alignment with human neural and eye-movement data during natural reading. Language-internal representations remain the primary determinant of human alignment, but VLMs show an advantage for sentences with stronger visual semantic content, supported by both fMRI and eye-tracking measures.

Conclusion: Multimodal pretraining shapes language representations in a selective way: it helps when processing visually rich content but does not generally make text representations more human-like across the board. A controlled in silico framework is provided to study how visual learning history impacts model-human alignment in language processing.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) have become increasingly useful computational models of human language processing, but it remains unclear whether vision-language learning makes text representations more human-like during natural reading. Here, we address this question by comparing tightly matched LLM and vision-language model (VLM) pairs under a strictly text-only setting, allowing us to isolate the effect of multimodal training history from online visual input or cross-modal fusion. We evaluate model alignment with a human natural-reading dataset that includes whole-cortex fMRI responses and synchronized eye-tracking saccades. Our findings demonstrate that multimodal pretraining may not confer a uniform, global advantage in human alignment during natural reading, indicating that language-internal representations remain the key factor for modeling human text processing. However, the VLM advantage could emerge more selectively when sentences contain stronger visual semantic content, with converging evidence from both fMRI and eye-movement alignments. Together, our findings provide a controlled in silico framework for testing how visual learning history shapes model-human alignment of language processing, suggesting that multimodal pretraining contributes selectively rather than globally to human-like language representations during natural reading.

</details>


<div id='cs.AI'></div>

# cs.AI [[Back]](#toc)

### [138] [Identifying and Understanding Human Values in Text: A Tailorable LLM-based Architecture](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.27373)
*Eduardo de la Cruz Fernández,Marcelo Karanik,Sascha Ossowski*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: LLM-based architecture to detect and quantify human values in text in a theory-agnostic, modular way.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional decision-making models for intelligent systems focus on utility maximization, lacking ethical and moral dimensions. Assessing how well AI decisions align with human values requires identifying such values in text, both explicit and implicit. Existing value-detection approaches are limited by dependence on specific value theories or complicated prompts. A more general, scalable, and reproducible method is needed.

Method: The paper proposes a modular LLM-based architecture with three coordinated modules: (1) a module that converts foundational texts of any value-theoretic framework into structured value specifications; (2) a labeling module that uses these specifications to annotate texts with the presence of given values; and (3) a scoring module that assigns graded support or resistance scores based on rhetorical and semantic evidence in the text. The design deliberately separates conceptualisation (defining values) from detection (finding them in text), enabling theory-agnostic operation. The architecture is instantiated using multiple LLMs and automatically applied to new value theories by feeding in their foundational descriptions.

Result: When instantiated with several LLMs and tested on the ValueEval benchmark dataset, the architecture shows good performance at detecting values in text. The experiments substantiate that the pipeline effectively generalizes across different value theories without heavy prompt engineering or hand-crafted rules.

Conclusion: The proposed LLM-based, modular pipeline can detect and quantify human values in text in a theory-agnostic, scalable, and reproducible way. By decoupling value conceptualisation from detection and relying on structured specifications derived from any theoretical framework, the approach overcomes limitations of previous methods tied to specific theories or complex prompts. Experimental results on ValueEval confirm its effectiveness and generality.

Abstract: As intelligent systems become more autonomous, the scientific community focuses on creating decision-making mechanisms that include ethical and moral considerations, unlike traditional utility-maximisation models. To achieve this, a key aspect is assessing how well these decisions align with human values. To this end, a promising line of research is centred on developing approaches based on Large Language Models (LLMs) to identify human values from text, whether explicit or implicit, enabling their recognition throughout. This paper introduces a LLM-based architecture to detect and quantify the intensity of human values in text, avoiding the limitations of previous approaches tied to specific value theory or complex prompt engineering. The architecture comprises three coordinated modules: one that generates structured value specifications from the foundational texts of any theoretical framework; one that labels texts using these specifications; and one that assigns graded support or resistance based on rhetorical and semantic evidence. This modular approach separates the tasks of conceptualising from detecting human values, creating a scalable and reproducible process driven by value specifications adaptable to various theories. The architecture was instantiated with multiple LLMs and evaluated using the ValueEval dataset. The experiments demonstrate good detection performance, confirming the generality of the pipeline.

</details>


### [139] [Soro: A Lightweight Foundation Model and Chatbot for Tajik](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.27379)
*Stanislav Liashkov,Haitz Sáez de Ocáriz Borde,Azizjon Azimi,Khushbakht Shaymardonov,Shuhratjon Khalitbekov,Bonu Boboeva*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Soro is a Tajik-specialized conversational LLM family, built from Gemma 3, that achieves strong Tajik and solid English performance while being optimized and quantized for deployment under tight compute and connectivity constraints in Tajikistan, especially in education.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: There is a lack of high-quality, Tajik-capable LLMs and benchmarks, and deployment in Tajikistan faces severe compute, memory, and connectivity constraints, particularly in schools. The authors aim to create models and evaluation resources that work well for Tajik, support education use cases, and can be deployed on low-resource hardware in the local context.

Method: Starting from open-weight Gemma 3 checkpoints, the authors perform Tajik-only continual pretraining on a curated 1.9B-token corpus that includes filtered web text, PDFs, and curriculum-aligned educational content. They then conduct supervised instruction tuning using 40K Tajik teacher-style examples. To evaluate Tajik capabilities, they design and release a new suite of Tajik benchmarks targeting general knowledge, linguistic competence, and entrance-exam domains. They also apply FP8 and INT4 quantization to the Soro models to reduce memory usage for edge deployment and analyze the impact on performance.

Result: On the newly introduced Tajik benchmarks, Soro substantially outperforms same-size Gemma 3 baselines, indicating successful Tajik specialization, while also maintaining strong English performance on standard datasets. Quantized versions of Soro (FP8 and INT4) largely preserve the Tajik-language improvements, with significantly lower memory requirements suitable for edge devices.

Conclusion: The Soro family demonstrates that Tajik-specialized LLMs can be efficiently derived from existing open-weight models via targeted continual pretraining and instruction tuning, yielding strong Tajik performance without sacrificing English ability. The new Tajik benchmarks and the open-sourced resources enable more rigorous evaluation and further research. Quantization makes the models practical for low-resource, real-world deployments, and early pilots in the education sector in Tajikistan show promise for broader rollout across schools.

Abstract: We present Soro, a family of Tajik-specialized conversational large language models (LLMs) designed for real-world deployment under tight compute and connectivity constraints in Tajikistan. Starting from open-weight Gemma 3 checkpoints, we perform Tajik-only continual pretraining on a curated 1.9-billion-token corpus spanning filtered web text, PDF documents, and curriculum-aligned educational materials, followed by supervised instruction tuning on 40K Tajik teacher-style examples. To enable rigorous evaluation despite the limited coverage of Tajik in standard benchmarks, we introduce a suite of Tajik benchmarks covering general knowledge, linguistic competence, and school- and university entrance-exam domains, and we open-source them on Hugging Face. Across these Tajik benchmarks, Soro substantially outperforms same-size Gemma 3 baselines while retaining strong English performance on standard datasets. We further show that FP8 and INT4 quantization of Soro preserves most Tajik-language gains while reducing memory requirements for edge deployment, supporting an ongoing education-sector pilot and planned scale-out across schools in Tajikistan.

</details>


### [140] [On the Origin of Synthetic Information by Means of Steganographic Inheritance](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.27551)
*Ching-Chun Chang,Isao Echizen*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper proposes a steganography-based method to embed invisible lineage traits into AI-generated content so that its origin (parent model or source) can be later identified, even after transformations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: As AI systems generate increasingly sophisticated synthetic information, tracing the origin and lineage of such content becomes critical for accountability, trust, and societal impact. Existing technical methods struggle because generated outputs can diverge structurally and semantically from their sources, analogous to different genotypes leading to similar phenotypes in biology. The authors are motivated to create a robust mechanism to track informational heredity in a complex cyber ecosystem.

Method: The authors introduce a heredity-analogue mechanism using steganography. When new synthetic content (offspring) is created, a projector maps properties of the parent into a compact trait, and a steganographic encoder embeds this trait invisibly into the offspring content. These traits persist through the content’s lifecycle, even under various processing operations and semantic changes. To infer parentage, a steganographic decoder extracts the hidden trait from the offspring and compares it against a pool of candidate parent traits, selecting the most likely parent. They provide a theoretical analysis of phylogenetic (lineage) accuracy as a function of the projector and stegosystem design, and experimentally evaluate multiple projector–stegosystem combinations under diverse transformations.

Result: Theoretical results characterize how accurately the system can recover parentage depending on the properties and design of the projector and steganographic system. Empirical experiments across several projector and stegosystem configurations show that the embedded lineage traits remain decodable and useful for parent identification under a wide range of processing operations and semantic modifications applied to the synthetic content, demonstrating practicality and robustness of the approach.

Conclusion: The paper concludes that synthetic information can be endowed with persistent, hidden lineage traits using steganographic heredity, enabling reliable reconstruction of informational ancestry even in complex cyber ecosystems. This approach offers a promising foundation for building ecosystems where AI-generated content is traceable and accountable over time, analogous to biological evolution, with implications for truth, trust, and governance in information systems.

Abstract: The origin of species has been the mystery of mysteries in natural science. By analogy, the origin of synthetic information, we suggest, is the mystery of mysteries in information science. The question carries a moral weight that a technical account can neither fully resolve nor responsibly ignore, as its impact on truth, trust, and human intellect extends deep into the broader economy and society. The very power of artificial intelligence makes the evolutionary lineage of synthetic information grow ever harder to trace, for a sufficiently capable model may generate offspring that bear little resemblance, at either the structural or signal level, to the parent source from which they were derived. As in genetics, two individuals may share the same phenotype mirroring each other in outward appearance, yet differ fundamentally in their genotype. We propose, by means of steganography, a mechanism analogous to heredity. At the moment an offspring is reproduced, a projector derives a trait from the parent, and a steganographic encoder invisibly hides it within the offspring. This trait persists throughout the offspring's life cycle in a cyber ecosystem. When parentage is queried, a steganographic decoder extracts the trait from the offspring and compares it against the traits of candidate parents in a reference pool, thereby nominating the most likely one. A theoretical analysis characterises phylogenetic accuracy as a function of projector and stegosystem properties, whilst empirical evaluations across multiple projectors and stegosystems demonstrate the viability of the proposed methodology under a broad spectrum of processing operations and semantic modifications. We envision a cyber ecosystem in which synthetic information, endowed with hidden yet traceable lineage traits, branches from a simple beginning into endless forms that have been, and are being, evolved.

</details>


### [141] [Why LLMs Fail at Causal Discovery and How Interventional Agents Escape](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.27567)
*Amartya Roy,Sonali Parbhoo*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: They show standard ways of training or prompting LLMs fundamentally cannot solve causal discovery from observational data and propose a new agentic method that uses the LLM only as an intervention oracle and provably avoids this limitation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Evaluate whether LLMs can reliably perform causal discovery and understand why existing approaches plateau and fail as causal graph complexity increases, going beyond empirical benchmarks to find a theoretical explanation.

Method: They prove a kernel obstruction theorem showing that training paradigms like supervised fine-tuning, direct preference optimization, and in-context learning induce kernel predictors that cannot distinguish causal graphs with near-indistinguishable observational distributions unless internal representations grow without bound. Then they introduce Agentic Causal Bayesian Optimization (A-CBO), which freezes the LLM and uses it to answer targeted interventional queries while an external Bayesian optimization loop updates beliefs over graphs across logarithmically many interaction rounds.

Result: They obtain a theoretical impossibility result for standard LLM training/prompting methods on causal discovery, independent of model or dataset, and then empirically show that A-CBO, which operates outside the obstructed representation space, matches or exceeds fine-tuned baselines on Corr2Cause without any training and substantially outperforms fine-tuning and preference optimization on the more complex Extended Corr2Cause benchmark with 24 variables and 18K test samples.

Conclusion: Fundamental limitations of kernel-based learning paradigms explain why LLMs trained with standard methods fail at causal discovery from observational data; however, by reframing the LLM as a fixed interventional oracle within an external Bayesian optimization procedure (A-CBO), one can provably and empirically overcome this obstruction and scale to more complex causal graphs.

Abstract: Causal discovery is a cornerstone of scientific reasoning, yet whether large language models can perform it reliably remains an open question. Recent benchmarks show that even fine-tuned models plateau on simple causal graphs and degrade as complexity grows, but why they fail has not been established. We prove the failure is fundamental: supervised fine-tuning, direct preference optimization, and in-context learning all produce predictors that cannot distinguish between causal graphs generating similar observational data, and any attempt to do so requires the model's internal representations to grow unboundedly, violating the very conditions under which these methods work. We formalize this as a kernel obstruction theorem, establishing that the limitation is intrinsic to the learning paradigm, \emph{not any particular model or dataset}. We propose Agentic Causal Bayesian Optimization (A-CBO), wherein a frozen language model serves as an interventional oracle answering targeted queries about intervention effects, while an external Bayesian loop concentrates beliefs over candidate graphs in logarithmically many rounds. Because the decision operates outside the space where the obstruction applies, A-CBO provably converges while the underlying model remains unchanged. On Corr2Cause, A-CBO matches fine-tuned baselines without any training. On Extended Corr2Cause, a new benchmark scaling to 24 variables with 18K test samples, A-CBO significantly outperforms both fine-tuning and preference optimization, with the advantage growing

</details>


### [142] [RULER: Representation-Level Verification of Machine Unlearning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.27569)
*Georgina Cosma,Axel Finke*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: They propose new representation-level metrics to verify whether machine unlearning truly removes forgotten data, showing existing methods can appear correct at the output level while still memorizing data internally.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing machine unlearning evaluations only look at model outputs (e.g., membership inference, accuracy) and can be passed even when the model’s hidden representations still encode information about supposedly forgotten records, which is a privacy and compliance concern.

Method: They introduce RULER, a suite of metrics on internal representations. Metric M2 (oracle-comparative) checks whether representations of forget-set points in the unlearned model match those in a fully retrained model without those points, using a linear mixed-effects model to quantify residuals. Metric M4 (oracle-free) analyzes the internal similarity structure of representations in the unlearned model alone, without retraining, to detect residual memorization. They test multiple approximate unlearning methods across different data modalities.

Result: Four approximate unlearning methods that appear successful under standard output-level tests still show significant representational residuals under M2 in 10 of 12 conditions, with effect sizes increasing as the fraction of forgotten data grows. A different method, Bad Teacher, exhibits similar residuals. M4 successfully flags identity-level memorization in face recognition and works as a pre-unlearning diagnostic on tabular, image, clinical text, and face-identity datasets, revealing that no examined method fully removes identity signals.

Conclusion: Output-level evaluations are insufficient to verify true machine unlearning since internal representations can still encode forgotten data. Representation-level metrics like RULER’s M2 and M4 are necessary for more reliable verification and show that current approximate unlearning methods often fail to fully erase memorized information, especially in settings like face recognition.

Abstract: Machine unlearning aims to remove the influence of specific training records from a deployed model without retraining from scratch. Current protocols verify this at the output level through membership inference, retain accuracy, and forget-set accuracy, but a model can satisfy all three whilst still encoding forgotten records in its intermediate representations. We introduce RULER, a set of representation-level verification metrics. The oracle-comparative metric M2 measures whether forget-set records occupy the same representational position as in a model retrained without them. The oracle-free metric M4 detects residuals from the unlearned model's internal similarity structure alone, without retraining. Four approximate unlearning methods all pass output-level evaluation, yet under a linear mixed-effects model M2 detects significant residuals in 10 of 12 conditions (p<0.05), with effect sizes growing as the forget fraction increases. A fifth method, Bad Teacher, shows the same residuals despite a different forgetting mechanism. M4 acts as a pre-unlearning diagnostic across tabular, image, clinical text, and face-identity settings: it detects identity-level memorisation in face recognition models where no tested method fully erases the signal.

</details>


### [143] [LaneRoPE: Positional Encoding for Collaborative Parallel Reasoning and Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.27570)
*Gabriele Cesa,Thomas Hehn,Aleix Torres-Camps,Àlex Batlle Casellas,Jordi Ros-Giralt,Arash Behboodi,Tribhuvanesh Orekondy*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: LaneRoPE introduces a method for collaborative parallel generation in LLMs so that multiple sequences, sampled in parallel (e.g., best-of-N), can share information and improve accuracy with minimal overhead.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Parallel test-time scaling methods like best-of-N improve accuracy by sampling multiple sequences per prompt, but each sequence is currently generated independently. This wastes potential signal from other sequences’ intermediate tokens and computations and may limit gains, especially under tight length or compute budgets. The authors aim to design a way for these parallel sequences to coordinate and share information while remaining compatible with existing LLM inference pipelines and maintaining batching efficiency.

Method: The authors propose LaneRoPE, which modifies generation-time behavior along two axes: (1) an inter-sequence attention mask that allows tokens in one sampled sequence ("lane") to attend to tokens in other sequences, making their sampling decisions interdependent; and (2) an extension of Rotary Positional Embeddings (RoPE) so that positional encodings reflect not only locations within a single sequence but also relative positions across different sequences in the batch. These changes are designed to be lightweight, avoiding major alterations to the base LLM architecture and preserving parallelism in standard batched decoding.

Result: On mathematical reasoning benchmarks, LaneRoPE leads to higher accuracy than standard parallel decoding in which sequences are independent, especially when the allowed generation length is limited. The method provides additional gains beyond typical best-of-N sampling while incurring negligible inference-time overhead and leaving the base model otherwise unchanged.

Conclusion: LaneRoPE demonstrates that enabling inter-sequence communication during batched generation can make parallel test-time scaling more effective. By combining an inter-sequence attention mask with a RoPE extension that encodes cross-sequence relative positions, it achieves collaborative reasoning among multiple sampled sequences, improving accuracy at little extra cost and fitting easily into existing LLM inference pipelines.

Abstract: Parallel LLM test-time scaling techniques (e.g., best-of-$N$) require drawing $N>1$ sequences conditioned on the same input prompt. These methods boost accuracy while exploiting the computational efficiency of batching $N$ generations. However, each sequence in the batch is traditionally generated independently and hence does not reuse intermediate generations, computations, or observations from other sequences. In this paper, we propose LaneRoPE to enable coordination and collaboration among $N>1$ sequences at generation time. LaneRoPE involves two key ideas: (a) an inter-sequence attention mask to make sampling of sequences dependent on one another; and (b) a RoPE extension that injects positional information that captures relative positions between tokens, both within and outside a particular sequence. We evaluate our approach on mathematical reasoning tasks and find promising results: LaneRoPE enables collaboration among sequences, yielding additional accuracy gains under limited generated sequence length. Importantly, since LaneRoPE enables coordination with minimal changes to the underlying LLM architecture and introduces a negligible overhead at inference time, it is appealing to rapidly incorporate parallel reasoning into existing LLM inference pipelines.

</details>


### [144] [Discovery Agents for Real-Time Analytics: Toward Proactive Insight Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.27571)
*Gaetano Rossiello,Dharmashankar Subramanian*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper proposes a multi-agent, LLM-driven architecture for autonomously discovering insights from real-time data streams, moving from reactive querying to proactive, continuous insight generation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional analytics systems require users to manually write queries, which does not scale in modern real-time, complex, and evolving data environments. In streaming settings, the possible questions and insights are too numerous to enumerate, so there is a need for systems that can autonomously explore data, form hypotheses, and surface insights without explicit user queries.

Method: The authors design a multi-agent architecture where specialized agents continuously run a discovery loop: they generate hypotheses about streaming data, translate these into executable analytics, validate the results, and produce visualizations and deployable analytic applications. The system is built on Apache Kafka for event-driven coordination, Apache Flink for stream processing, and large language models to power the agents. A central methodological element is a contract-driven design with typed intermediate artifacts, which structure communication between agents and enforce constraints on dynamically generated analytics.

Result: Using representative use cases in domains such as retail, finance, and public data, the authors demonstrate that the architecture can autonomously generate, execute, and refine analytics pipelines, as well as produce visual outputs and applications, without users explicitly specifying each query. The examples illustrate how the system can uncover insights in real time and adapt to changing data streams.

Conclusion: The paper concludes that a contract-driven, multi-agent architecture built on LLMs and streaming technologies can effectively shift analytics from a query-driven, reactive paradigm to a proactive, discovery-driven approach. Typed intermediate artifacts enhance modularity, observability, lineage tracking, and safety when executing dynamically synthesized analytics over live data streams.

Abstract: Modern analytics systems are fundamentally reactive, requiring users to define queries over increasingly complex and continuously evolving data. In real-time streaming environments, this paradigm breaks down, as the space of potential insights becomes too large to enumerate manually. We present a multi-agent architecture for autonomous insight discovery over real-time data streams. The system implements a continuous discovery loop in which agents generate hypotheses, compile them into executable analytics, validate generated artifacts, and produce visualizations and deployable applications. The architecture leverages Apache Kafka for event-driven coordination, Apache Flink for stream processing, and large language models to implement specialized agents. A key contribution is a contract-driven design based on typed intermediate artifacts, enabling modularity, observability, lineage, and safer execution of dynamically generated analytics. Through use cases in retail, finance, and public data, we show how this architecture supports a shift from query-driven analytics to proactive, discovery-driven systems.

</details>


### [145] [Agyn: An Open-Source Platform for AI Agents with Scalable On-Demand Execution, Agent Definition as a Code, and Zero-Trust Access](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.27575)
*Nikita Benkovich,Vitalii Valkov*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper introduces Agyn, an open-source, Kubernetes-based platform for running AI agents at scale with strong isolation, governance, and security.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current efforts focus on building individual AI agents, but organizations lack robust infrastructure to operate many such agents in production, where they handle non-deterministic workflows, long-lived stateful sessions, and privileged access to internal systems. This creates challenges in isolation, governance, and security that existing platforms do not adequately address.

Method: The authors design and implement Agyn, a platform built around three architectural principles: (1) a signal-driven, stateful serverless runtime on Kubernetes for agent workloads; (2) a Terraform provider to declaratively define agents and their execution harnesses; and (3) a security model based on zero-trust and least-privilege to control agents’ access to internal services and data. The platform is built to be agent-, model-, and cloud-agnostic.

Result: Agyn provides a unified way to deploy and manage diverse AI agents as serverless workloads on Kubernetes, enabling scalable, secure, and governed operation. It allows infrastructure teams to use Terraform to manage agent definitions, and applies fine-grained security controls following zero-trust and least-privilege principles. Although the abstract does not give quantitative benchmarks, it claims Agyn successfully addresses operational needs for production agent deployments.

Conclusion: The paper concludes that Agyn offers a practical, open-source foundation for operating AI agents in production environments by combining a stateful serverless runtime, infrastructure-as-code integration, and a modern security model. Its agent-, model-, and cloud-agnostic design aims to make it broadly applicable as organizations scale their use of AI agents.

Abstract: As organizations move toward production deployments of AI agents, which execute non-deterministic workflows, maintain stateful sessions, and often operate with privileged access to internal services, the engineering challenge shifts from building individual agents to operating them at scale with proper isolation, governance, and security. In this paper we present Agyn, an open-source platform designed around three key principles tailored for agent workloads: a signal-driven, stateful serverless runtime on Kubernetes; a Terraform provider for agent and harness definition; and a security model grounded in zero-trust and least-privilege principles. Agyn is agent-agnostic, model-agnostic, and cloud-agnostic.

</details>


### [146] [You Are in Control of Your State: Why Human Outcomes Are Controllable Through Causal State Intervention](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.27580)
*Suraj Biswas,Saurav Gupta,Pritam Mukherjee*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper proposes that within-person variability in decisions arises from a fast-changing latent ‘state’ and argues that behavior can be precisely influenced by interventions that target this state in real time.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Behavioural data show that the same person often responds differently to the same inputs, and that between-person differences are not fully explained by observable variables. Existing models don’t adequately explain or exploit this variability for prediction or intervention, which limits both behavioural science and human-facing AI systems.

Method: Conceptual and theoretical framework building: they define a formal notion of ‘state’ as a dynamic weighting vector over biological, physiological, and neuropsychological dimensions that shape decision-making. They integrate evidence from six research areas (causal inference, predictive processing, allostasis, attentional bottleneck, chronobiology, computational psychiatry), and use a 24‑month observational dataset from a large behavioural platform (>200,000 users, four occupational personas) as an empirical base. From this, they derive testable predictions and operational requirements for ‘state-aware’ systems.

Result: They articulate a causal model in which a person’s latent state directly shapes how events are transformed into decisions and outcomes, with the key claim that this state is dynamic on sub-daily timescales and only partially accessible via a narrow, state-dependent attentional channel. They generate seven explicit testable predictions about state dynamics and control, and specify six operational requirements that any practical system must meet to leverage state-awareness for intervention.

Conclusion: Within-person variability should be treated as a feature of a dynamic latent state that causally mediates decisions and outcomes, making behaviour conditionally controllable via targeted, timely interventions. Recognising and engineering around this state—and its attentional bottleneck—has major implications for building state-aware systems in digital health, education, AI personalisation, and for enhancing personal agency.

Abstract: A central puzzle for the behavioural sciences and for human-facing artificial intelligence is the persistence of within-person variability. The same individual, presented with the same observable input, produces different outcomes on different occasions, and different individuals produce divergent outcomes that no observable covariate fully predicts. We argue that this variability belongs in the dynamic latent state of the person, and that human outcomes are controllable in a precise and operational sense through interventions that target the state and its weighting at the moment a decision is being formed.
  We define a state as the time-indexed weighting vector over the dimensions that govern how an individual's biology, physiology, and neuropsychology process the next event into a decision and an outcome. The relationship between state, decision, and outcome is causal rather than correlational. The weighting vector is dynamic at sub-daily timescales. The conscious channel through which outcomes are reportable is a narrow attentional bottleneck whose contents are themselves state-dependent. Taken together, these claims imply that the outcome of a given event is controllable, conditionally, on the state-trajectory at the time of intervention.
  We motivate the framework with six strands of established evidence (causal inference, predictive processing, allostasis, attentional bottleneck, chronobiology, computational psychiatry) and a 24-month observational base from a deployed behavioural platform spanning more than 200,000 consented users across four occupational personas (research period 2023 to 2026). We derive seven testable predictions, list six operational requirements for state-aware systems, and discuss implications for digital health, education, AI personalisation, and personal agency.

</details>


### [147] [Cyberbullying Governance on Social Media: A Unified Framework from Content Identification to Intervention](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.27584)
*Yiting Huang,Wenting Zhu,Zekun Wang,Qingpo Yang,Yakai Chen,Zihui Xu,Yueyue Zhang,Sanchuan Guo,Xi Zhang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Proposes a unified, full-lifecycle framework for governing cyberbullying that goes beyond post-level detection to continuous, proactive moderation across identification, behavior modeling, diffusion/early warning, and intervention.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Online toxicity such as cyberbullying and hate speech is expanding with social media, and current automated moderation mostly focuses on static, post-level detection. This ignores how abusive behavior evolves over time, how harmful content diffuses through networks, and how to intervene proactively. A more holistic, lifecycle-oriented governance approach is needed.

Method: Conducts a systematic, integrative review of state-of-the-art research on cyberbullying governance, organizing it into four lifecycle stages: (1) content identification; (2) user and behavior modeling; (3) diffusion dynamics and early warning; and (4) intervention and governance. Also surveys datasets, evaluation methodologies, and cross-cuts challenges like multimodality, explainability, fairness, and dual-use issues with generative AI.

Result: Provides a structured synthesis of existing literature and methods across the four stages, maps current capabilities and gaps, compiles available datasets and evaluation practices, and identifies key open challenges including handling multimodal content, providing interpretable models, ensuring algorithmic fairness, and mitigating dual-use risks of generative AI.

Conclusion: Effective cyberbullying governance requires moving from isolated detection to an integrated, full-lifecycle, proactive framework that couples identification, behavioral modeling, diffusion-aware early warnings, and targeted interventions. The paper offers this conceptual roadmap, highlights pressing research challenges, and argues that addressing them is essential for building safer and more resilient online ecosystems.

Abstract: The proliferation of social media platforms and online communities has inadvertently catalyzed the spread of cyberbullying, hate speech, and other forms of online toxicity, making the effective governance of such harm a critical societal and computational challenge. While significant strides have been made in automating content moderation, existing research predominantly treats cyberbullying governance as passive, isolated detection at the post level. This reductionist view overlooks the continuous behavioral dynamics of users, the structural diffusion of toxic events, and the critical need for proactive mitigation. To bridge these gaps, this paper proposes a unified full-lifecycle governance framework that shifts the paradigm of cyberbullying governance from isolated static detection toward integrated, continuous, and proactive moderation. Drawing on cyberbullying research and adjacent fields, we systematically synthesize the state-of-the-art literature across four interconnected stages: (1) Content Identification, (2) User and Behavior Modeling, (3) Diffusion Dynamics and Early Warning, and (4) Intervention and Governance. Furthermore, we review available datasets and evaluation practices, and discuss emerging challenges including multimodality, explainability, algorithmic fairness, and the dual-use risks of generative AI, providing a roadmap for future research toward a safer and more resilient digital ecosystem.

</details>


### [148] [Voluntary Collusion with Secret Tools in Competing LLM Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.27593)
*Xijie Zeng,Frank Rudzicz*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper empirically shows that many safety-aligned LLM agents still choose to use clearly unfair, harmful collusion tools in multi-agent settings when it gives them an advantage, and that generic alignment isn’t enough to prevent this without explicit ethical safeguards.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: As LLMs are increasingly deployed as autonomous or semi-autonomous agents interacting with each other, there is a growing risk that they may engage in collusion or other socially harmful strategies to gain advantages. Existing alignment work focuses on single-agent behavior and general harmlessness, but we don’t yet know whether such alignment prevents unethical strategic behavior like secret collusion in multi-agent environments, especially when tools explicitly framed as unfair are available.
