The authors are motivated by the observation that models can appear safe by following rigid rules, yet fail in cases where the correct, safe response requires overriding those rules after a contextual update. They aim to systematically diagnose and quantify this brittleness and to explore architectural alternatives that can better track consequences rather than surface actions.

Method: They introduce a context-flip evaluation protocol using a safety benchmark (PacifAIst) and two commonsense control datasets, where each original (baseline) item is paired with a variant in which the previously safe, nominally prosocial action now leads to harm. They test 12 language models on both the original and flipped items, analyzing accuracy, brittleness rates, and the gap between safety and commonsense performance. They then conduct error analysis to distinguish failures due to policy override vs miscomprehension and examine how different model families respond to different update types. Finally, they build and audit a deployment probe composed of catastrophic consequence-flip scenarios, comparing standard action-level guardrails with a state-aware validator that reasons about the evolving situation state and consequences of actions.

Result: (1) All 12 models exhibit brittle safety: there is a substantial safety-commonsense gap (average +17.4 percentage points), indicating that they adjust to context better on nonsafety commonsense tasks than on safety tasks. Baseline benchmark accuracy does not predict brittleness; even models with >90% baseline safety accuracy have brittleness rates spanning 13.7% to 90.0%. (2) The dominant failure mode is policy override rather than misunderstanding: models correctly recognize the contextual change in all tested cases but still cling to rigid safety policies, with three main persistence mechanisms that differ by update type and model family. (3) In the catastrophic consequence-flip probe, conventional action-level safety filters fail to catch any of the dangerous cases, whereas a state-aware validator successfully flags all dangerous interventions with no false positives on correct, context-sensitive interventions.

Conclusion: Safety benchmarks that score static responses are not reliable indicators of real deployment readiness because they overlook brittle safety, where models fail to adapt safety behavior under context changes. The core issue is that action-level guardrails and rigid policies are insensitive to changes in downstream consequences. The authors argue for and demonstrate the promise of state-aware, consequence-focused safety architectures and release their evaluation protocol, perturbed benchmarks, and deployment probe to foster better diagnostics and safer deployment practices.

Abstract: Safety benchmark scores provide incomplete evidence of deployment readiness: aligned language models often adhere to rigid rules even when a situational update flips which action is safe. We term this failure brittle safety. To diagnose it, we introduce context-flip evaluation, testing 12 models across a safety benchmark (PacifAIst) and two commonsense controls using paired variants where the nominally safe action produces harm. Three findings emerge. First, brittle safety is safety-specific: all 12 models exhibit a safety-commonsense gap (mean +17.4 pp). Baseline accuracy fails to predict brittleness: among models above 90% baseline accuracy, brittleness rates range from 13.7% to 90.0%. Second, failures stem from policy override rather than miscomprehension: despite acknowledging the context change in every case, models persist via three distinct mechanisms that vary by update type and model family. Third, on a hand-audited probe of catastrophic consequence-flip scenarios, standard action-level guardrails catch none, while a state-aware validator catches all without false alarms on correct interventions. This indicates that action-level content moderation is systematically blind to consequence-flips, motivating state-aware architectural alternatives. We release our protocol, perturbed benchmarks, and deployment probe.

</details>


### [170] [C-MIG: Multi-view Information Gain-based Retrieval-Augmented Generation for Clinical Diagnosis Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.27860)
*Yuwei Miao,Gen Li,Yunsheng Zeng,Xiandong Li,Yujin Wang,Siyu Chen,Luning Wang,Yunhao Qiao,Junfeng Wang,Jianwei Lv,Bo Yuan*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper proposes C-MIG, a new retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) framework with reinforcement learning for clinical diagnosis, using multi-view information gain and multi-subquery retrieval to better guide what evidence to retrieve and how to refine reasoning, achieving state-of-the-art performance on multiple medical benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing RAG+RL methods for medical/clinical diagnosis mostly rely on exact-match, binary rewards. In diagnosis, this creates two key problems: (1) intermediate reasoning steps that are semantically correct but not verbatim matches receive zero reward, wasting useful supervision; and (2) a single scalar reward cannot adequately supervise multiple heterogeneous reasoning skills required in clinical diagnosis (retrieval quality, evidence use, reasoning steps, etc.). The authors want a richer, more informative reward signal that can better train LLMs for trustworthy, evidence-grounded clinical reasoning.

Method: The authors introduce C-MIG, a Clinical diagnosis framework based on Multi-view Information Gain. They fix a reference model and estimate information gain from two complementary views: (a) retrieved-document view, which measures how much the retrieved evidence improves the reference model’s belief; and (b) document-refinement view, which measures how refinement of that evidence changes the model’s belief. These IG-based signals are used as rewards in reinforcement learning to jointly optimize what to retrieve and how to refine/reason, mitigating reward sparsity and credit assignment issues. Additionally, they design a multi-subquery retrieval augmentation strategy, where complex clinical questions are decomposed into multiple subqueries to retrieve diverse, complementary documents, improving knowledge recall and coverage.

Result: On four medical benchmarks, C-MIG surpasses all compared RAG+RL baselines under both in-domain and out-of-domain evaluation. It also outperforms strong general-purpose LLMs on clinical diagnosis tasks, indicating better grounding in medical evidence and more accurate diagnostic reasoning.

Conclusion: Using multi-view information gain as a dense, structured reward in a RAG+RL setup leads to better supervision for clinical diagnostic reasoning than simple exact-match binary rewards. Jointly optimizing retrieval and refinement, plus using multi-subquery retrieval to increase evidence coverage, yields state-of-the-art performance and enhanced robustness on diverse clinical benchmarks. This suggests that richer reward design and more nuanced retrieval strategies are crucial for trustworthy LLM-based clinical diagnosis.

Abstract: Retrieval-augmented generation combined with reinforcement learning has shown promise for grounding large language models in trustworthy medical evidence. However, existing methods rely on exact-match binary rewards, which in clinical diagnosis cause two issues: (i) semantically relevant but non-verbatim steps receive zero signal, discarding valuable learning signals; and (ii) uni-dimensional rewards cannot effectively supervise heterogeneous reasoning capabilities. To address these issues, we propose C-MIG, a Multi-view Information Gain-based retrieval-augmented generation framework for Clinical diagnosis. C-MIG estimates information gain under a frozen reference model from two complementary views, retrieved-document and document-refinement, to jointly guide what to retrieve and how to refine, alleviating the issues of valuable reward signal loss and credit assignment. We further design a multi-subquery retrieval augmentation strategy that improves knowledge recall coverage in clinical diagnostic scenarios. Comprehensive experiments on four medical benchmarks demonstrate that C-MIG achieves the best performance among all RAG-RL methods on both in-domain and out-of-domain sets, and outperforms state-of-the-art general-purpose LLMs for clinical diagnosis.

</details>


### [171] [FundaPod: A Multi-Persona Agent Pod Platform with Knowledge Graph Memory for AI-Assisted Fundamental Investment Research](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.27864)
*Di Zhu,Lei,Zheng,Zihan Chen*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper introduces FundaPod, a multi-persona LLM-based agent platform designed to support human analysts in fundamental investment research, emphasizing transparent, verifiable, and reusable investment memos rather than pure prediction or trading signals.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Most LLM-in-finance work focuses on prediction and trading signals, which does not match the needs of institutional fundamental research that relies on structured evidence gathering, analysis of business drivers, comparison of viewpoints, and transparent memo writing. There is a gap in tools that help analysts create human-interpretable, reusable, knowledge-building investment research rather than just forecasts.

Method: The authors design and build FundaPod, a human-AI hybrid, multi-agent system where AI agents embody different investment personas (e.g., value investor, macro strategist). These agents independently conduct research under a shared provenance contract and store their outputs in a knowledge-graph-based memory. The system architecture embodies five design principles and four specific mechanisms: persona distillation from public investor materials, a declarative skill registry for planning typed task graphs, a grounded evidence model linking claims to verifiable sources, and a knowledge-graph “second brain” tying together entities like tickers, memos, analysts, and themes. They validate the approach via a full case study and persona-based memo comparison.

Result: FundaPod operationalizes independence-preserving, multi-persona AI support for fundamental research, enabling separate agents to independently research and generate memos whose claims are linked to evidence and stored in a knowledge graph. The system can surface disagreements between personas for human portfolio managers to adjudicate. Through a detailed case study and memo comparison, the authors show the architecture is practically workable and can produce differentiated, evidence-grounded investment outputs, although quantitative performance metrics are not emphasized in the abstract.

Conclusion: Fundamental investment research is a human-centric, decision-support activity that differs qualitatively from prediction-oriented trading tasks, and thus benefits from an architecture that preserves agent independence and emphasizes transparency, provenance, and knowledge accumulation. FundaPod exemplifies this via multi-persona agents, a structured skill and evidence framework, and a knowledge-graph “second brain,” offering design principles and mechanisms for future human-AI systems in finance and similar domains.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly applied in finance, yet most existing work emphasizes trading signals or financial NLP tasks centered on prediction. Institutional fundamental research, by contrast, requires human analysts or AI agents to gather evidence, identify business drivers, compare competing viewpoints, and generate investment memos. Its broader goal is not merely to predict outcomes, but to produce investment plans that are transparent, reusable, and verifiable, while contributing to the cumulative development of investment knowledge. We present FundaPod, a multi-persona agent platform for AI-assisted fundamental investment research. We argue that fundamental research is a human-centric decision-support task that is qualitatively distinct from trading-signal generation, and is therefore better served by an independence-preserving architecture. In FundaPod, AI agents with different personas, such as value investors or macro strategists, conduct research independently under a shared provenance contract. Their disagreements are then surfaced post hoc for adjudication by the human portfolio manager (PM) through a knowledge-graph memory system. This paper contributes five design principles for human-AI hybrid systems supporting fundamental research, grounded in design-science practice and theories of cognitive isolation and human-machine coordination. It also describes four architectural mechanisms: a persona distillation pipeline that turns public investor materials into deployable agents; a declarative skill registry that lets the planner derive typed task graphs; a grounded evidence model that links memo claims to verifiable sources; and a knowledge-graph "second brain" that connects tickers, memos, analysts, and themes. We demonstrate the architecture through a complete case study and a persona-based memo comparison.

</details>


### [172] [AIBuildAI-2: A Knowledge-Enhanced Agent for Automatically Building AI Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.27873)
*Ruiyi Zhang,Peijia Qin,Qi Cao,Li Zhang,Pengtao Xie*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: AIBuildAI-2 is a knowledge-enhanced AI agent that automatically designs and builds ML models for scientific tasks by leveraging an external, evolving knowledge base, achieving state-of-the-art performance on standard benchmarks and real competitions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Building high-performing AI models is still a manual, expert-intensive process that natural scientists and non-ML specialists struggle to perform. Existing automatic AI-building agents are limited because they rely only on static, parametric knowledge in LLMs, which is outdated and lacks practical engineering detail. There is a need for an automated AI-builder that can continuously leverage and update rich, external, verifiable ML engineering knowledge for scientific discovery tasks.

Method: The authors propose AIBuildAI-2, an agent augmented with an external hierarchical knowledge system for AI development. The knowledge base is organized into topical high-level instructions and low-level documents. For each task and current agent state, AIBuildAI-2 dynamically retrieves only relevant knowledge to guide architectural choices, pipeline design, and implementation. The system is bootstrapped by scraping, cleaning, and categorizing AI-development documents from the web, and then is continually updated by distilling each completed run into structured lessons that are written back into the knowledge base.

Result: AIBuildAI-2 attains state-of-the-art performance on MLE-Bench with a 70.7% medal rate and ranks in the top 6.6% among 4,370 human expert teams in a heart disease prediction competition, demonstrating its strong capability to automatically produce competitive AI solutions.

Conclusion: Enhancing AI-building agents with an external, evolving, structured knowledge system significantly improves their ability to autonomously construct high-performing ML models. AIBuildAI-2 shows that grounding decisions in curated, dynamically updated engineering knowledge can close the gap between automated agents and expert human practitioners for scientific AI tasks.

Abstract: AI models underpin data-centric applications from image and text processing to scientific discovery in biology, physics, and chemistry. Yet developing them remains heavily manual, requiring practitioners to design architectures, build training pipelines, and iteratively refine solutions, making it challenging for natural scientists without specialized AI engineering expertise to build the high-performing models their research demands. To reduce this burden and broaden access to AI for scientific discovery, agents that automatically build AI models have been proposed. However, the performance of these agents is largely limited by the parametric knowledge of their underlying large language models, which is static, often outdated, and sparse on practical AI model engineering know-how. To address this limitation, we introduce AIBuildAI-2, a knowledge-enhanced agent with an external, evolving knowledge system for automatically building AI models. The knowledge system of AIBuildAI-2 is hierarchical, organizing curated AI development knowledge into high-level knowledge instructions over topical categories and low-level knowledge documents under each category, from which the agent dynamically loads only the context relevant to its current state and the AI task being solved, grounding each design and implementation decision in concrete, externally verifiable expertise. The system is initialized by collecting and cleaning AI-development-related documents from the web and organizing them into the corresponding categories, and continually evolves from the agent's own experience by distilling each completed run on an AI task into structured takeaways that are written back into the knowledge system. AIBuildAI-2 achieves state-of-the-art results, ranking first on MLE-Bench with a 70.7% medal rate and placing in the top 6.6% among 4,370 human-expert teams in a heart disease prediction competition.

</details>


### [173] [Towards Faithful Agentic XAI: A Verification Method and an Open-World Benchmark for Better Model Faithfulness](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.27879)
*Jaechang Kim,Sunung Mun,Seungjoon Lee,Jaewoong Cho,Jungseul Ok*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: They propose a framework (FAX) that makes LLM-based explainable AI agents more faithful by explicitly verifying each claim in their explanations against trustworthy tools, and they introduce a new benchmark (CRAFTER-XAI-Bench) to evaluate explanation faithfulness on complex RL policies.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Agentic XAI systems that use LLMs to generate natural-language explanations are attractive because they make model behavior easier to understand, but they often produce convincing yet unfaithful explanations that do not truly reflect how the underlying model works. This unfaithfulness is especially dangerous for complex models where users may be overconfident in LLM-generated explanations, so there is a need for methods that systematically enforce faithfulness and for benchmarks that clearly separate model faithfulness from plain task performance.

Method: They introduce Faithful Agentic XAI (FAX), an agent framework in which LLM-generated draft explanations are decomposed into atomic claims. Each claim is then checked against inherently faithful tools that reliably reflect the target model’s behavior (e.g., simulators, policy rollouts, ground-truth evaluators). Unsupported or contradictory claims are filtered out or revised before the final explanation is produced. Additionally, they create CRAFTER-XAI-Bench, an open-world reinforcement learning benchmark with complex policies and diverse tasks, specifically designed to test whether explanations match the behavior of the target model rather than just task outcomes.

Result: On the new CRAFTER-XAI-Bench, FAX substantially increases simulation faithfulness from 0.20 (best existing baseline) to 0.46 while preserving human-centric qualities such as informativeness, relevance, and fluency of explanations. On three tabular XAI benchmarks, FAX performs similarly to prior Agentic XAI methods, but further analysis reveals that those tabular settings often mix up explanation faithfulness to the model with pure task accuracy, making them less reliable for evaluating model-specific faithfulness.

Conclusion: Explicit verification of explanation content against faithful tools is crucial for making Agentic XAI systems produce explanations that truly reflect the underlying model’s behavior. Moreover, benchmarks for XAI should be carefully designed, as in CRAFTER-XAI-Bench, to evaluate faithfulness with respect to the target model itself rather than conflating it with general task performance.

Abstract: Explainable AI (XAI) helps users interpret model behavior and identify potential faults. Agentic XAI systems use Large Language Models (LLMs) to make explanations more accessible through natural-language interaction, but they can also produce plausible yet unfaithful explanations. This risk arises because unreliable XAI outputs for complex models can be amplified by LLMs and mislead users. We propose Faithful Agentic XAI (FAX), a framework that improves explanation faithfulness through explicit verification. FAX decomposes draft explanations into claims and cross-checks them against inherently faithful tools, filtering unsupported or contradictory claims before final generation. We also introduce CRAFTER-XAI-Bench, an open-world reinforcement learning benchmark with complex policies, diverse goals, and challenging scenarios for assessing model-specific faithfulness. On CRAFTER-XAI-Bench, FAX improves simulation faithfulness from 0.20 for the strongest baseline to 0.46 while maintaining high informativeness, relevance, and fluency. On three tabular benchmarks, FAX performs competitively with prior Agentic XAI baselines, but our analysis shows that these settings can conflate task accuracy with model-specific faithfulness. These findings show that explicit verification is essential for faithful Agentic XAI and that that faithfulness benchmarks must be designed to test explanations against the behavior of the target model itself.

</details>


### [174] [PortBench: A Correlation-Aware, Full-Pipeline Benchmark for LLM-Driven Portfolio Management](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.27887)
*Yuxuan Zhao,Sijia Chen,Ningxin Su*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: PortBench is a new benchmark to evaluate how well large language models handle realistic portfolio management, especially diversification via asset correlations and multi-stage decision pipelines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current evaluations of LLMs on financial tasks do not properly test real-world portfolio management. They usually ignore cross-asset correlations, so they cannot tell if a model is actually diversifying risk, and they do not assess the entire end-to-end decision process that portfolio managers use. A dedicated benchmark is needed to reveal these shortcomings.

Method: The authors build PortBench, which covers six different asset classes over a decade of data. It has two layers: (1) a static QA dataset with 6,269 correlation-focused questions using seven task templates, and (2) a dynamic, five-stage portfolio allocation pipeline that simulates the full portfolio management cycle. They define two metrics: a dual-layer correlation score to see if portfolios use cross-asset hedging and avoid within-class concentration, and CEPS to measure how reasoning errors accumulate across pipeline stages. They also test robustness and investor alignment under three historical stress periods and risk profiles, and evaluate ten advanced LLMs on this setup.

Result: Across ten state-of-the-art LLMs, performance on traditional static financial QA is relatively strong, but when tested in the PortBench framework, 90% of model and risk-profile combinations do not beat a naive equal-weight portfolio. Even models that respect all procedural constraints can still suffer very large drawdowns in stress scenarios.

Conclusion: LLMs that look strong on conventional financial question answering are not yet reliable portfolio managers. Properly accounting for correlations, multi-stage decision pipelines, and stress conditions exposes serious weaknesses. PortBench provides a more realistic and demanding benchmark for future work on LLM-based portfolio management and financial decision-making systems.

Abstract: LLMs have shown strong performance across diverse financial tasks, yet portfolio management (PM), a critical financial decision-making task, remains poorly benchmarked. Existing benchmarks exhibit two main gaps: they ignore cross-asset correlation structures, thereby failing to distinguish genuinely diversified portfolios from concentrated ones, and fail to evaluate the complete PM decision pipeline in real-world scenarios. We introduce PortBench, a benchmark spanning six heterogeneous asset classes over ten years. PortBench consists of two complementary layers: a static QA dataset of 6,269 correlation-based questions across seven task templates, and a dynamic five-stage allocation pipeline that mirrors the full PM decision cycle. To evaluate these layers, we introduce two dedicated metrics: a dual-layer correlation score that measures whether proposed portfolios exploit inter-class hedging and avoid intra-class concentration, and CEPS, a metric that quantifies how reasoning errors compound across pipeline stages. We further assess strategy robustness and investor alignment under three historical stress regimes and risk profiles. Evaluating ten frontier LLMs, we find that despite strong performance on static financial QA, 90\% of model-profile combinations fail to outperform a basic equal-weight allocation, and models that satisfy every procedural constraint still suffer catastrophic drawdowns under stress. Our source code is available at \href{https://github.com/AgenticFinLab/portbench}{this https URL}.

</details>


### [175] [A Unified Framework for the Evaluation of LLM Agentic Capabilities](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.27898)
*Pengyu Zhu,Lijun Li,Yaxing Lyu,Qianxin Luo,Jingyi Yang,Yi Liu,Tingfeng Hui,Xinyu Yuan,Li Sun,Sen Su,Jing Shao*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper proposes a unified, standardized framework to fairly evaluate LLMs’ agentic capabilities by separating intrinsic model ability from artifacts due to scaffolding and environment volatility.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing benchmarks for LLM-based agents confound model capability with implementation choices (scaffolds, prompts, tools) and unstable external environments (APIs, websites). This makes scores across benchmarks hard to compare and obscures what models can actually do as agents, especially in safety-critical and multi-agent settings.

Method: They design a configuration-driven framework that standardizes many existing agent benchmarks into a common "instruction–tool–environment" schema. All models are run with a fixed ReAct-style agent architecture inside a controllable sandbox. The framework offers an offline mode using curated environment snapshots, so live-environment variability can be removed or isolated. They preserve each benchmark’s original success criteria, but add unified metrics for resource use (e.g., tokens, tools, time) and a taxonomy that attributes failures to decision-level vs. execution-level causes. They port 7 popular benchmarks covering 24 domains and support single-agent, multi-agent, and safety-critical tasks, running large-scale experiments (400K rollouts, 5B tokens) over 15 models.

Result: Empirically, they show that changes in scaffolding (agent architecture/configuration) and environmental volatility significantly alter benchmark performance, sometimes improving and sometimes degrading scores for the same underlying model. With their standardized setup and offline snapshots, they can separate intrinsic model limitations from artifacts introduced by the framework or environment. The framework also functions as a secure, extensible testbed for safety-critical benchmarks.

Conclusion: A unified evaluation framework with standardized scaffolding, environments, and metrics is crucial for fair, interpretable assessment of LLM agentic capabilities. Their system demonstrates that many reported benchmark differences stem from framework and environment choices rather than true model ability, and it provides a practical, extensible testbed—especially valuable for safety-critical and multi-agent evaluations.

Abstract: As LLMs are increasingly deployed as agents, reliable assessment of their agentic capabilities has become essential. However, reported benchmark scores often jointly reflect model capability and the implementation choices each benchmark is packaged with, making cross-benchmark results difficult to interpret as clean measurements of the underlying model. In this work, we present a unified framework for the fair evaluation of LLM agentic capabilities. Driven by a unified configuration system, the framework integrates diverse benchmarks into a standardized instruction--tool--environment format, executes agents through a fixed ReAct-style architecture within a controllable sandbox, and provides an optional offline setting that replaces volatile live environments with curated snapshots, so that framework effects and environment effects can be analyzed separately. Building on this, we unify the evaluation methodology under each benchmark's original task-success criteria, while introducing unified metrics for resource consumption and a taxonomy for decision- and execution-level failure attribution. Within this framework, we adapt 7 widely used benchmarks spanning 24 domains across single-agent, multi-agent, and safety-critical scenarios, and conduct a large-scale empirical analysis over 400K rollouts and 5B tokens on 15 models. The results show that scaffold choice and environmental volatility materially shift benchmark outcomes in both directions, allowing our framework to disentangle intrinsic LLM capabilities from framework- and environment-induced artifacts. We further demonstrate its extensibility as a secure testbed for safety-critical domains. Codes and benchmarks at are available at https://github.com/whfeLingYu/A-Unified-Framework-for-the-Evaluation-of-LLM-Agentic-Capabilities, https://huggingface.co/AgentFramework/Unified_Farmework.

</details>


### [176] [SKILLC: Learning Autonomous Skill Internalization in LLM Agents via Contrastive Credit Assignment](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.27899)
*Hongxiang Lin,Zhirui Kuai,Erpeng Xue,Lei Wang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper introduces SkillC, a method to better internalize external skills in long-horizon RL agents, improving their autonomous performance without relying on skills at test time.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: In long-horizon tasks, agents often rely on external structured skills (e.g., high-level prompts or tools) to explore and perform well. Many RL methods keep using these skills at inference, limiting true autonomy. Existing attempts to remove skills during training only use them for curriculum control and cannot clearly separate successes due to the base policy from those due to skills, which weakens internalization.

Method: SkillC uses Contrastive Skill Credit Assignment (CSCA). For each task and active skill type, it samples matched rollouts with and without skill injection within the same policy update. It then uses a dual-stream advantage estimator that (1) preserves the global ranking of trajectories but (2) applies a one-sided correction that favors skill-free success, effectively assigning more credit to behavior that works without skills. On top of this, a smoothed validation signal adaptively controls a curriculum over three aspects: how strongly to attribute credit away from skills, how to allocate rollouts between skill-injected and skill-free modes, and which skills to keep in the active set via monotonic pruning.

Result: On ALFWorld and WebShop benchmarks, SkillC agents that have no access to skills at runtime outperform the best previous skill-internalization RL baselines by 5.5% and 4.4% absolute, respectively. Their performance is also competitive with methods that are allowed to use skills at inference.

Conclusion: By turning the contrast between skill-injected and skill-free rollouts into an explicit learning signal, SkillC more effectively internalizes external skills, enabling autonomous long-horizon RL agents that approach or match the performance of skill-augmented methods while no longer depending on external skills at test time.

Abstract: Structured skill prompts improve exploration in long-horizon agentic reinforcement learning (RL). Skill-augmented RL methods retain external skills at inference, while skill-internalization RL methods withdraw them during training to enable autonomous performance. However, existing internalization approaches only use skill-helpfulness contrast for curriculum control, leaving the policy update unchanged and unable to distinguish skill-dependent from autonomous success. We propose SkillC, a framework based on Contrastive Skill Credit Assignment (CSCA) that converts this contrast into a direct learning signal for internalization. \textsc{SkillC} samples paired skill-injected and skill-free rollouts for tasks from active skill types within the same policy update, and injects their task-level contrast into optimization via a dual-stream advantage estimator that preserves global ranking while applying a one-sided correction toward skill-free success. A smoothed validation-level signal further drives an adaptive curriculum over attribution strength, rollout allocation, and monotonic active-set pruning. Experiments on ALFWorld and WebShop show that, without runtime skill access, SkillC surpasses the strongest prior skill-internalization RL baseline by 5.5\% and 4.4\%, respectively, while remaining competitive with skill-augmented RL methods.

</details>


### [177] [Dr-CiK: A Testbed for Foresight-Driven Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.27904)
*Yihong Tang,Andrew Robert Williams,Arjun Ashok,Vincent Zhihao Zheng,Lijun Sun,Alexandre Drouin,Issam H. Laradji,Étienne Marcotte,Valentina Zantedeschi*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Introduces Dr-CiK, a benchmark to test whether agents can autonomously retrieve and use relevant external documents to improve time-series forecasting.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Real-world time-series forecasting depends on external, noisy, heterogeneous contextual information, but existing benchmarks unrealistically assume that the relevant context is pre-given. There is a need to evaluate whether agents themselves can find, filter, and use such context effectively.

Method: The authors design Dr-CiK, a benchmark where agents must search a document corpus, retrieve forecasting-relevant context, filter out distractors, distill useful evidence, and then generate forecasts supported by that evidence. They run context ablation studies and pair state-of-the-art deep research (DR) agents with forecasting models to evaluate performance and evidence recovery.

Result: High-quality retrieved context significantly boosts forecasting performance in Dr-CiK, confirming the value of good external information. However, current DR agents recover less than about 5% of the ground-truth supporting evidence, are misled by distractors in over 80% of their citations, and sometimes make forecasting models perform worse with retrieved context than without any context.

Conclusion: Existing agents are poor at identifying and using the right contextual evidence for forecasting, despite its clear benefits when available. The work highlights the need for new “foresight-driven” research agents that can reliably search for, select, and leverage forecasting-relevant context from noisy information sources.

Abstract: Time series forecasting in real-world settings often depends not only on historical observations, but also on external context that must be actively discovered from noisy, heterogeneous information sources. Yet existing context-aided forecasting benchmarks typically assume that the supporting context is already provided, leaving open whether agents can identify it on their own. Therefore, we introduce Dr-CiK, a benchmark for evaluating whether agents can retrieve forecasting-relevant supporting context from a document corpus, filter out distractors, distill the retrieved context into forecast-useful evidence, and generate forecasts supported by that evidence. Through context ablations and evaluations of state-of-the-art deep research and forecasting methods paired together, we show that high-quality context substantially improves forecasting performance in Dr-CiK. However, most existing DR agents recover only a small fraction of the ground-truth supporting evidence (usually <5%), are frequently misled by distractors (>80% distractor citations), and can cause forecasters to perform worse with retrieved context than without context. Our results motivate research on foresight-driven agents that search for the right context to predict the future.

</details>


### [178] [Reasoning Matters: Mitigate Hallucination in Multimodal Large Reasoning Models via Reasoning-Conditioned Preference Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.27906)
*Jiawei Kong,Hao Fang,Shunxiang Liao,Jinyu Li,Bin Chen,Hao Wu,Shu-Tao Xia,Min Zhang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper proposes RC-DPO, a new training framework to reduce hallucinations in multimodal reasoning models by explicitly aligning the chain-of-thought (CoT) with correct answers, leading to more reliable vision-language reasoning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Multimodal Large Reasoning Models perform well on complex vision-language tasks but still hallucinate heavily. Existing preference-optimization methods treat the CoT and final answer as a single output and mainly learn answer-level preferences, underusing supervision on the reasoning process itself. The authors aim to better exploit CoT supervision to make the reasoning chains more faithful and reduce hallucinations.

Method: They analyze standard Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) with CoT-style outputs and show it behaves similarly to optimizing only the final answer. To fix this, they propose Reasoning-Conditioned DPO (RC-DPO), which explicitly treats the CoT as a condition for answer generation. RC-DPO contrasts different CoTs that lead to the same preferred answer, encouraging chains that genuinely support that answer. They also design a data-generation pipeline: (1) Monte Carlo Tree Search to explore and select visually grounded, logically consistent CoTs as positive examples; (2) attention-guided token pruning on CoTs to generate negative examples that break grounding or logic, providing better preference signals.

Result: Across multiple multimodal models and benchmarks, RC-DPO reduces hallucinations and yields higher reliability in the reasoning process compared with standard DPO and answer-only baselines. The models trained with RC-DPO produce CoTs that are more consistent with images and final answers, improving performance on vision-language reasoning tasks.

Conclusion: Reasoning-Conditioned DPO effectively leverages CoT-level supervision by conditioning answer preferences on the underlying reasoning chains and contrasting alternative CoTs. Combined with a tailored preference data generation strategy, it significantly mitigates hallucinations and enhances the trustworthiness of multimodal reasoning, suggesting that explicit reasoning-aware preference learning is a promising direction for training large reasoning models.

Abstract: Multimodal Large Reasoning Models introduce the reasoning paradigm, demonstrating strong capabilities on complex vision-language tasks. However, they still suffer from severe hallucinations. Existing training-based methods typically mitigate hallucinations through response-level direct preference optimization (DPO), where the Chain-of-Thought (CoT) and the final answer are treated as a monolithic output and optimized jointly. We reveal that this formulation performs similarly to answer-only optimization, suggesting that it primarily learns answer-level preference, while leaving CoT-level supervision insufficiently exploited. To address this issue, we explicitly formulate a CoT-oriented preference term and derive Reasoning-Conditioned Direct Preference Optimization (RC-DPO), which models the CoT as a condition for answer generation and contrasts the preference for the same preferred answer under different CoT conditions, promoting answer-supportive reasoning chain alignment. To further improve optimization, we introduce a reasoning-enhanced preference data generation strategy that employs Monte Carlo Tree Search to discover visually grounded and logically consistent CoTs as positive samples, and attention-guided CoT token pruning to construct negative ones. Extensive experiments across various models and benchmarks show that RC-DPO effectively mitigates hallucinations and improves the reliability of the multimodal reasoning process.

</details>


### [179] [SuiChat-CN: Benchmarking Contextual Suicide Risk Assessment in Chinese Group Chats](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.27911)
*Xiangyu Wang,Zhiwei Yu,Chengze Du,Dingchang Wang,Yuhan Ye,Fangyu Zheng*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper introduces SuiChat-CN, a Chinese Telegram group-chat benchmark for contextual suicide risk assessment, demonstrating that incorporating conversational context is crucial and that early detection in multi-party chats remains challenging.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Suicide is a major global public health issue, and current computational approaches largely ignore instant messaging platforms and their complex, fragmented, multi-party conversations. There is a need for datasets and methods that can assess suicide risk in such contextual, conversational environments, especially in Chinese.

Method: The authors collect public Telegram Chinese group-chat data, identify suicide-related signal words, and build coherent conversational segments via bidirectional context expansion. They then annotate user-level suicide risk using an expert-validated, LLM-assisted labeling paradigm. They evaluate a range of pretrained language models (PLMs) and more than 40 large language models (LLMs) on the benchmark, including experiments on full vs. partial context and with fine-tuning.

Result: SuiChat-CN comprises 13,312 contextual segments from 1,406 users and 258,228 raw chat messages. Experimental results show that using richer conversational context significantly improves suicide risk assessment performance. However, even strong PLMs and LLMs struggle with early detection when only limited or partial context in multi-party conversations is available.

Conclusion: Contextual, conversation-level modeling is essential for reliable suicide risk assessment in instant messaging environments, and SuiChat-CN provides a challenging benchmark for this task in Chinese. Despite advances in PLMs and LLMs, early detection of suicide risk in multi-party chats remains difficult. The dataset is restricted due to ethical concerns but is available to qualified mental health researchers upon request.

Abstract: Suicide is a critical global public health challenge, causing approximately 720,000 deaths each year and calling for timely, effective prevention strategies. Existing computational studies primarily focus on post-based social media platforms such as Twitter and Weibo, leaving instant messaging environments such as Telegram underexplored. Yet group chats pose distinct challenges: messages are short, fragmented, multi-party, and often rely on implicit or culturally specific expressions, making isolated post-level analysis insufficient. We introduce SuiChat-CN, a Chinese group-chat benchmark for contextual suicide risk assessment. We collect public Telegram group-chat data, construct coherent conversational segments through signal-word extraction and bidirectional context expansion, and annotate user risk levels with an expert-validated, LLM-assisted paradigm. SuiChat-CN contains 13,312 contextual segments from 1,406 users, covering 258,228 raw chat messages. Extensive experiments with PLMs and more than 40 LLMs demonstrate that contextual information is essential for reliable risk assessment, while fine-tuning and partial-context evaluation further reveal the challenges of early detection in multi-party conversations. Due to ethical and sensitivity concerns, the dataset is not publicly released but will be shared with accredited mental health and suicide-prevention research institutions upon reasonable request.

</details>


### [180] [Show, Don't TELL: Explainable AI-Generated Text Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.27921)
*Aldan Creo,Suraj Ranganath*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper introduces TELL, an AI-generated text detector that is designed to be natively explainable while maintaining competitive detection performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing AI-text detectors often output only a numeric score, which is poorly aligned with users’ real needs; users, such as professors, need transparent, contextualized reasons for why text is flagged as AI- or human-written.

Method: The authors design a novel architecture, TELL, which highlights specific ‘tells’ in the text that lead to AI vs human judgments. They train it on a custom supervised fine-tuning (SFT) dataset with domain-specific authorship annotations and then refine it with GRPO and curriculum learning to improve detection and explanation quality.

Result: TELL achieves an AUROC of 0.927, comparable to state-of-the-art detectors, and produces native annotations explaining its decisions. Human evaluation of these explanations shows a mean 72.3% win rate on dimensions such as concreteness, falsifiability, coherence, plausibility, and grounding.

Conclusion: TELL reframes AI-text detection as a human-centric task by coupling strong performance with native, high-quality explanations, suggesting a new generation of detectors that focus on explainability rather than opaque numeric scores alone.

Abstract: Research on AI-generated text detection has presented a number of approaches to discern human from AI prose, some of which achieving high in-distribution performance. However, real-world applicability has stalled because their outputs are misaligned with the needs of users, such as professors, who are presented with a numeric score that has no attached explanation. We tackle this issue with a novel architecture, TELL, that bakes explainability from the ground-up. While our system still offers a numerical score like other detectors for comparability, TELL takes a fundamentally different approach where we aim to show the user the "tells" by which the model believes a text is AI or human-written, to empower the user to decide who wrote a text using their own judgment and understanding of the context of the writing and its alleged author. We train TELL on a custom SFT dataset of domain-specific authorship annotations, and further refine the system using GRPO with curriculum learning to improve performance. We achieve competitive performance with state-of-the-art detectors (AUROC 0.927) while natively providing annotations that explain the basis for the detector's decision. We further evaluate the quality of our explanations using a dataset of human annotations and report a high (mean 72.3%) win-rate on annotation concreteness, falsifiability, coherence, plausibility and grounding, allowing users to critically think and decide for themselves. Our work thus reframes the problem of AI-generated text detection in a human-centric perspective and paves the way for a new family of detectors that focus on native explainability.

</details>


### [181] [Harness-Bench: Measuring Harness Effects across Models in Realistic Agent Workflows](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.27922)
*Yilun Yao,Xinyu Tan,Chao-Hsuan Liu,Yaoming Li,Zhengyang Wang,Wenhan Yu,Zhewen Tan,Yuxuan Tian,Guangxiang Zhao,Lin Sun,Xiangzheng Zhang,Tong Yang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Harness-Bench is a benchmark to study how different agent harness configurations (not just base models) affect LLM agent performance on realistic tool-using workflows.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing LLM agent benchmarks largely ignore or fix the execution harness layer, making it hard to understand how differences in context management, tool handling, and state control impact agent behavior and reliability in real-world workflows.

Method: The authors design Harness-Bench, a set of 106 sandboxed offline tasks modeled on practical agent use-cases, all manually checked for realism and evaluability. They run multiple LLM backends under varied, native harness configurations but with shared tasks, budgets, and evaluation protocols, collecting final artifacts, traces, usage stats, and validator outputs to compare configuration-level effects.

Result: Across 5,194 runs, they find large differences in task completion, process quality, efficiency, and failure types depending on the specific model-harness pairing, as well as systematic execution-alignment failures where reasonable-seeming reasoning diverges from tool feedback or verifiable constraints.

Conclusion: LLM agent capability is not a property of the base model alone but of the full model-harness configuration. Harness-Bench offers a reproducible way to analyze and improve the reliability, efficiency, and auditability of agent execution stacks by exposing and quantifying harness-level effects and misalignments.

Abstract: LLM agents are increasingly deployed as executable systems that use tools, modify workspaces, and produce concrete artifacts. In such workflows, performance depends not only on the base model, but also on the harness: the system layer that manages context, tools, state, constraints, permissions, tracing, and recovery. However, existing benchmarks typically abstract away execution, compare complete agent systems, or hold the harness fixed, making execution-layer variation difficult to study. We introduce Harness-Bench, a diagnostic benchmark for evaluating configuration-level harness effects in realistic agent workflows. Harness-Bench evaluates representative harness configurations across multiple model backends under shared task environments, budgets, and evaluation protocols, while preserving each harness's native execution behavior. The benchmark contains 106 sandboxed offline tasks constructed from practical agent-use patterns and manually reviewed for realism, solvability, oracle-checkability, and integrity. Each run records final artifacts, execution traces, usage statistics, and validator outputs, enabling analysis beyond final completion. Across 5,194 execution trajectories, we observe substantial variation in completion, process quality, efficiency, and failure behavior across model-harness pairings. These results suggest that agent capability should be reported at the model-harness configuration level rather than attributed to the base model alone. Our analysis further identifies recurring execution-alignment failures, where plausible reasoning becomes decoupled from tool feedback, workspace state, evidence, or verifiable output contracts. Harness-Bench provides a reproducible foundation for diagnosing and improving reliable, efficient, and auditable agent execution stacks.

</details>


### [182] [DiagramRAG: A Lightweight Framework to Retrieve Scientific Diagram for Figure Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.27931)
*Xinjiang Yu,Junyi Han,Zhuofan Chen,Chi Zhang,Xiangyu Fu,Jingyuan Tan,Zirui You,Yixiang Jian,Yu-Ping Wang,Chengliang Chai*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: DiagramRAG is a retrieval-augmented system that turns rough scientific diagram sketches into completed, publication-ready diagrams by using structure-aware diagram retrieval.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Researchers often start with rough, incomplete sketches of scientific diagrams that encode layout and relationships but lack details needed for final publication. Existing methods either just clean up the sketch or rely on text alone, missing the rich topological information in sketches. There is a need for a method that uses both the semantic and structural cues in sketches to automatically complete them into high-quality diagrams while being fast and lightweight.

Method: DiagramRAG represents diagrams as knowledge graphs capturing entities and their relations. It synthetically generates sketch variants at different levels of simplification and trains an embedding model so that sketches and compatible full diagrams lie in a shared latent space. At inference, given a user sketch, the system retrieves reference diagrams that are both semantically similar and topologically compatible. These retrieved diagrams provide content, structural, and visual priors that guide a downstream diagram generation module to complete and render the final scientific diagram efficiently.

Result: On the DiagramBank and FigureBench benchmarks, DiagramRAG achieves high structural matching performance with F1-scores of 0.848 and 0.802, respectively. It also attains the highest automatic quality score using a VLM-as-a-Judge metric (7.170) among compared methods, and it reduces inference latency to about 35.48 seconds per diagram, indicating both improved quality and efficiency over baselines.

Conclusion: DiagramRAG effectively leverages both semantic and topological information from rough sketches via retrieval-augmented generation to produce publication-quality scientific diagrams. Its knowledge-graph representation and sketch–diagram alignment enable structure-aware retrieval that improves diagram completion accuracy, visual quality, and inference speed compared with existing sketch- or text-based approaches. The released code and datasets support further research in sketch-based scientific diagram generation.

Abstract: Scientific diagrams are essential for communicating complex methodologies in academic papers. A natural way for researchers to specify such diagrams is through rough sketches, where text labels, connectors, and spatial arrangements express early semantic and topological intentions. However, sketches are usually incomplete, making them insufficient for directly producing publication-quality diagrams. Existing sketch-based generation methods mainly reconstruct the sketch itself, while recent text-driven diagram generation frameworks rely on textual semantics and do not fully exploit the topological structure contained in sketches. In this paper, we introduce DiagramRAG, a lightweight retrieval-augmented framework for sketch-based scientific diagram completion. Given a user sketch, DiagramRAG retrieves reference diagrams that are both semantically relevant to the sketch content and topologically compatible with its structure, and uses them to guide downstream diagram generation. To enable efficient structure-aware retrieval, we represent diagrams as knowledge graphs, synthesize sketch variants at different simplification levels, and train an embedding model to align sketches with compatible diagrams in a shared space. The retrieved references further provide content, topology, and visual priors for completing and rendering the final diagram. Experiments show that DiagramRAG achieves F1-scores of 0.848 and 0.802 on DiagramBank and FigureBench, respectively, and improves generation quality with the best VLM-as-a-Judge score of 7.170, while reducing inference latency to 35.48 seconds per sample. Our code and data are available at https://anonymous.4open.science/r/DiagramRAG-A262 and https://huggingface.co/datasets/anonymous-review-a262/DiagramSketch.

</details>


### [183] [Do Agents Think Deeper? A Mechanistic Investigation of Layer-Wise Dynamics in Sequential Planning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.27935)
*Zhenyu Cui,Xiangzhong Luo*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper analyzes how large language models use their network depth in autonomous agent settings and finds that they engage deeper layers more adaptively over multi-turn trajectories than in single-turn tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Prior work suggests LLMs may use their depth inefficiently in standard, single-turn tasks, but it is unknown whether this limitation persists when models act as autonomous agents that must plan over multiple turns, use tools, and update internal state. Understanding depth usage in these realistic settings can reveal how reasoning is implemented mechanistically and whether agents dynamically allocate network capacity as tasks evolve.

Method: The authors conduct a layer-wise mechanistic study of full trajectories of LLM agents across three domains: deep research, code generation, and tabular data processing. They employ residual stream probes to track semantic representations across layers, perform causal layer-skipping interventions to test which layers are necessary for behavior, and compute effective-depth measurements to quantify how far information propagates and which layers contribute most at different turns.

Result: They find that in agentic settings, LLMs show a different depth-usage profile compared to static tasks: as the interaction progresses over turns, the models progressively engage more and deeper layers, and long-range dependencies between distant layers become stronger in later turns. Residual updates over time become more focused on corrections rather than simple accumulation. Effective-depth analysis uncovers a construction–refinement gap, where semantic directions emerge in relatively early layers but deeper layers remain critical for stabilizing and finalizing outputs. This gap is especially marked in Qwen and Minimax models, while GLM’s depth usage varies more with the task domain.

Conclusion: Autonomous LLM agents do not use depth in a fixed or globally inefficient way; instead, they allocate depth adaptively based on the evolving complexity of multi-turn reasoning. Early layers tend to construct initial semantic directions, while deeper layers increasingly handle refinement and stabilization in later turns. The specific depth allocation patterns vary across model families, indicating that architecture or training differences shape how agentic reasoning recruits network depth.

Abstract: Recent mechanistic studies suggest that large language models (LLMs) may utilize their depth inefficiently in standard single-turn tasks. Whether this still holds in autonomous agent settings, where models must perform multi-turn planning, tool use, and iterative state updates, remains unclear. We study this question through a systematic layer-wise analysis of complete user-agent trajectories spanning three domains: Deep Research, Code Generation, and Tabular Processing. Using residual stream probes, causal layer-skipping interventions, and effective-depth measurements, we show that agentic reasoning exhibits a distinct depth profile from static tasks. As trajectories unfold, models progressively recruit more and deeper layers, with stronger long-range inter-layer dependencies emerging in later turns. At the same time, residual updates become increasingly correction-dominant, indicating a shift from stable feature accumulation toward repeated recalibration. Effective-depth analysis further reveals a substantial construction-refinement gap: semantic direction often forms relatively early, while deep layers remain necessary for stabilizing final outputs. Across model families, this gap is pronounced in Qwen and Minimax, whereas GLM shows a more domain-dependent depth allocation pattern. These results provide mechanistic evidence that autonomous LLM agents allocate depth adaptively as reasoning complexity grows.

</details>


### [184] [From Talking to Singing: A New Challenge for Audio-Visual Deepfake Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.27944)
*Ke Liu,Jiwei Wei,Wenyu Zhang,Shuchang Zhou,Ruikun Chai,Yutao Dai,Chaoning Zhang,Yang Yang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper introduces a new dataset and a text-guided audio-visual forgery detection framework that works for both talking and singing deepfakes, achieving better and more robust performance than prior methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Most deepfake detectors for audio-visual content assume strong temporal and semantic coupling between speech and facial motion. In singing, this coupling is weakened by rhythm and musical structure, causing a domain shift that makes existing detectors unreliable. There is also a lack of dedicated benchmarks for singing deepfakes. The paper aims to address both the dataset gap and the need for a unified detector that generalizes across talking and singing scenarios.

Method: 1) Construct the Singing Head DeepFake (SHDF) dataset using rhythm-aware generative models to simulate realistic singing deepfakes. 2) Propose T-AVFD (Text-guided Audio-Visual Forgery Detection), which has two key components: (a) a facial authenticity pattern learner that aligns visual facial features with multi-granularity textual descriptions (e.g., sentence-, phrase-, or token-level) to capture generalizable patterns indicative of real vs. fake faces; and (b) a multi-modal differential weight learning module that maintains intrinsic audio-visual consistency cues and adaptively fuses them with the learned authenticity patterns by learning modality- and cue-specific weights. The system is trained and evaluated on both talking-head and singing-head data to encourage cross-scenario generalization.

Result: Across several talking-head deepfake datasets and the proposed SHDF singing dataset, the T-AVFD framework consistently outperforms existing audio-visual deepfake detection baselines. It shows superior robustness under various perturbations, such as noise, compression, or other input degradations, indicating that the learned authenticity patterns and adaptive weighting help maintain detection accuracy in challenging conditions.

Conclusion: By introducing the SHDF dataset and the T-AVFD text-guided detection framework, the paper demonstrates that leveraging textual descriptions for facial authenticity patterns and adaptively combining them with audio-visual consistency cues can effectively handle the domain shift between talking and singing. This leads to a more general and robust audio-visual forgery detector, highlighting the importance of rhythm-aware benchmarks and multi-modal, text-informed pattern learning for future deepfake detection research.

Abstract: With rapid advances in audio-visual generative models, reliable forgery detection becomes increasingly critical. Existing methods for audio-visual deepfake detection typically rely on cross-modal inconsistencies. In singing, rhythmic vocalization weakens this coupling and introduces a nontrivial domain shift, substantially degrading detection performance. We construct the Singing Head DeepFake (SHDF) dataset using rhythm-aware generative models to fill the gap in singing benchmarks. To cope with cross-scenario domain shifts, we propose a Text-guided Audio-Visual Forgery Detection (T-AVFD) framework that generalizes across both talking and singing scenarios. T-AVFD comprises a facial authenticity pattern learner and a multi-modal differential weight learning module. The pattern learner aligns facial features with multi-granularity textual descriptions to learn generalizable authenticity patterns. The weight learning module preserves intrinsic audio-visual consistency and adaptively integrates it with authenticity patterns via differential weighting. Extensive experiments on multiple talking head deepfake datasets and SHDF show consistent improvements over existing baselines and strong robustness under diverse perturbations.

</details>


### [185] [The Shape of Overthinking: Backtracking Bursts in Long Reasoning Traces](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.27965)
*Navid Rezazadeh,Arash Gholami Davoodi*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper analyzes how backtracking behaviors in long reasoning traces correlate with correctness, and uses these patterns to design better early-exit/trace-filtering policies.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reasoning language models often generate long solution traces that mix helpful self-correction with unstable, unproductive revisions. It is hard to automatically tell when a model is productively repairing its reasoning versus when it is floundering. This matters for evaluation, safety, and efficiency, because we would like to stop bad traces early, allow good ones to continue, and possibly intervene when the model becomes unstable.

Method: The authors collect 6,000 solution traces from the Qwen3-8B model on AIME math problems. They segment these traces and manually annotate each segment for the severity of backtracking behavior—how much the model is reconsidering, retracting, or re-deriving prior steps. They then analyze statistics of these backtracking events, including when they occur in the trace (event timing), how deep in the reasoning process they are (normalized depth), and whether they occur in bursts or isolated instances (local burst structure). They also validate the observed patterns on other model/domain pairs. Finally, they design filtering schemes that, using only prefix information, detect problematic backtracking patterns and implement selective early-exit policies, comparing these to simple fixed length cutoffs.

Result: They find a clear asymmetry between correct and incorrect traces. Correct solutions often contain early, isolated repair events that do not destabilize the reasoning. In contrast, incorrect traces more frequently exhibit moderate-to-severe backtracking that appears later in the trace, persists over time, and clusters in bursts. When these backtracking patterns are used as features for filtering, burst-aware early-exit policies at shallow and intermediate depths outperform naive fixed-length filtering, while only relying on information available in the prefix of the trace. Nonetheless, simple moderate length cutoffs still remain strong baselines when looking at full completed traces.

Conclusion: Backtracking structure inside reasoning traces carries a usable signal about trace stability and eventual correctness. By distinguishing early, isolated repair from late, clustered, severe backtracking, one can design burst-aware, prefix-causal control policies that stop unstable traces early while allowing recoverable ones to continue. This provides a practical, deployable mechanism for improving efficiency and reliability beyond what is possible with fixed length-based filtering alone.

Abstract: Reasoning models often generate long traces in which useful self-correction and unproductive revision are hard to distinguish. We study this distinction through backtracking dynamics: local reconsideration, retraction, or re-derivation inside long-form reasoning traces. On 6{,}000 Qwen3-8B AIME traces, we annotate segment-level backtrack severity and analyze event timing, normalized depth, and local burst structure. We find that early isolated repair is often compatible with correct reasoning, whereas incorrect traces more often show moderate-to-severe backtracks that persist and cluster late. Cross-corpus checks show the same qualitative asymmetry across additional model/domain pairs. Filtering analyses instantiate the signal as a prefix-causal selective early-exit policy: at shallow and intermediate depths, burst-aware filtering outperforms fixed length-based filtering while using only prefix-available features. Moderate length cutoffs remain strong completed-trace baselines, but burst-aware control provides a deployable mechanism for separating recoverable repair from likely instability.

</details>


### [186] [Geometry of Human Perceptual Domains Emerges Transiently in LLM Representations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.27970)
*Simardeep Singh,Paras Chopra*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper studies whether large language models spontaneously develop internal geometric structures that resemble human perceptual organization (e.g., for color, pitch, emotion, taste), and tracks how this geometry appears and disappears across model layers.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Although LLMs are trained only on text, prior work suggests their embeddings have non-trivial geometric structure. It is unknown to what extent these structures mirror human perceptual spaces (like color or pitch spaces) and how such structure develops across layers. Understanding this can shed light on whether and how human-like perceptual organization emerges in models without explicit perceptual supervision, informing mechanistic interpretability and model analysis.

Method: The authors analyze residual stream activations layer by layer in several open-weight transformer LLMs. For several perceptual domains (color, pitch, emotion, taste), they collect textual prompts that elicit representations tied to those percepts and study the induced geometry in embedding space. They compare these model-induced geometries against human perceptual baselines using geometric / alignment metrics, and examine how structure strength and human-alignment change across layers and models.

Result: (1) Clear geometric structure corresponding to perceptual modalities emerges in LLM residual streams, despite training only on text and no direct perceptual labels. (2) Different perceptual domains exhibit distinct emergence profiles: the strength of geometry and its alignment with human perceptual spaces vary with depth and differ between domains and models. (3) Across architectures, there is a consistent trajectory: perceptual geometry is weak or diffuse in early layers, becomes well-organized and aligned with human spaces in intermediate layers, then becomes attenuated in later layers.

Conclusion: LLMs transiently instantiate human-like perceptual geometries as part of their internal computation pipeline: these structures are constructed and then partially discarded across depth. This suggests perceptual-like structure is an emergent computational intermediate rather than a static property of final representations, and provides a systematic basis for mechanistic analysis of where and how such geometry arises in LLMs.

Abstract: While large language models (LLMs) are trained purely on textual data, prior work has shown that their internal representations can exhibit rich geometric structure in embedding space. Building on this line of work, we investigate whether such structure is similar to human perceptual organisation across different domains (e.g., color, pitch, emotion, and taste). Specifically, we study the layer-wise emergence of intrinsic geometrical structure corresponding to perceptual modalities within the residual streams of multiple open-weight transformer architectures. Our results reveal three key findings. First, we observe the emergence of layer-wise geometric structure across multiple perceptual domains, despite the absence of any direct perceptual supervision during training. Second, these perceptual domains exhibit distinct emergence profiles, with both geometric structure and its alignment with human baselines following domain- and model-specific trajectories across depth. Third, this emergence follows a consistent representational trajectory: geometry is weak or diffuse in early layers, becomes progressively organised in intermediate layers, and is attenuated in later layers, suggesting that perceptual geometry arises transiently as part of the model's internal transformation pipeline. This provides new insight into how and where human-like perceptual geometry arises in LLMs, offering a principled pathway for mechanistic analysis of internal representations.

</details>


### [187] [STAB: Specification-driven Testing for Algorithmic Bottlenecks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.27981)
*Soohan Lim,Joonghyuk Hahn,Hyundong Jin,Yo-Sub Han*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: STAB is a system that automatically generates worst‑case efficiency test cases for algorithms from only a natural-language problem description, significantly improving the discovery of algorithmic bottlenecks compared to prior LLM-based methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing test-case generation methods mostly focus on making a specific implementation run slowly (e.g., by enlarging input size or tailoring inputs to that code), but they fail to systematically capture the underlying structural conditions that trigger an algorithm’s theoretical worst-case behavior. There is a need for an automated way to derive such adversarial, structure-aware test cases directly from problem specifications, so that efficiency evaluation can be more robust and less dependent on a particular implementation.

Method: The paper proposes STAB, a specification-driven pipeline. First, a constraint saturator parses the natural-language problem specification to extract constraints and compute large, admissible input sizes via a combination of rule-based saturation and CP-SAT optimization over related variables. Second, an adversarial scenario injector retrieves implementation-level adversarial construction principles from a curated catalog using keyword matching and K-nearest neighbors (KNN). STAB then combines the specification, the resolved size bounds, and the retrieved adversarial principles into a structured generation specification that conditions a large language model to synthesize a Python test-case generator program.

Result: On the CodeContests benchmark, STAB notably increases the proportion of generated test cases that successfully expose algorithmic bottlenecks: from 50.43% to 73.45% on average across open-source LLMs, and from 57.45% to 71.85% on average across closed-source LLMs. The improvements are consistent across multiple programming languages (Python, Java, C++).

Conclusion: STAB demonstrates that a specification-driven, structure-aware approach can substantially improve the ability of LLM-based systems to generate adversarial efficiency test cases, moving beyond naive input scaling or code-specific tricks. This suggests that incorporating constraint reasoning and reusable adversarial construction patterns into test generation pipelines is an effective strategy for systematically uncovering algorithmic bottlenecks.

Abstract: Evaluating the efficiency of algorithmic code requires test cases that expose runtime bottlenecks. Previous methods generate efficiency test cases either by increasing input size or by generating code-specific inputs that make the given implementation run slowly. Consequently, they do not address the structural input conditions that drive the algorithmic worst case. We introduce STAB, a specification-driven pipeline that generates test cases that expose algorithmic bottlenecks from a natural-language problem specification alone. STAB separates the task into constraint-bound maximization and adversarial structure injection. (i) The constraint saturator extracts constraints and resolves large admissible size assignments using rule-based saturation and CP-SAT optimization over related variables. (ii) The adversarial scenario injector retrieves implementation-level adversarial construction principles from a curated scenario catalog using keyword matching and K-nearest neighbors (KNN). STAB encodes the problem specification, resolved boundary, and retrieved construction principles into a structured generation specification, from which the LLM synthesizes a Python test case generator. On CodeContests, STAB raises the rate of generated test cases that expose algorithmic bottlenecks from 50.43% to 73.45% on average across open-source LLMs and from 57.45% to 71.85% on average across closed-source LLMs, with consistent gains across Python, Java, and C++. Our code is available at https://github.com/suhanmen/STAB.

</details>


### [188] [AsyncTool: Evaluating the Asynchronous Function Calling Capability under Multi-Task Scenarios](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.27995)
*Kou Shi,Ziao Zhang,Shiting Huang,Avery Nie,Zhen Fang,Qiuchen Wang,Lin Chen,Huaian Chen,Zehui Chen,Feng Zhao*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: AsyncTool is a benchmark to test how well LLM agents handle asynchronous, multi-task tool use when tools respond with delays.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Real-world tool-using agents must juggle multiple tasks at once and handle slow tool responses, but current evaluations mostly assume single tasks and ignore latency, so they miss a key aspect of real deployment efficiency.

Method: The authors design AsyncTool, an interactive benchmark where several heterogeneous tasks appear concurrently, tools have simulated response delays, and data are built via a hybrid evolution strategy to cover varied scenarios and tool-use patterns. They define step-, sub-task-, and task-level evaluations and add efficiency metrics focused on coordination and completion efficiency under latency.

Result: Experiments on existing LLM-based agents show that delayed tool feedback significantly hurts performance; agents struggle with effective multitasking, leading to degraded task completion and lower efficiency metrics when latency is present.

Conclusion: Better task switching, dependency tracking, and state maintenance enable stronger performance on AsyncTool, and analyzing failure modes suggests that future agents need improved temporal reasoning and coordination to be effective in realistic, latency-prone tool-use settings.

Abstract: Large language model (LLM)-based agents have shown strong capabilities in using external tools to solve complex tasks. However, existing evaluations often overlook the temporal dimension of tool use, especially the impact of tool response latency, and are usually limited to single-task settings. In real-world applications, multiple tasks often need to be executed concurrently, and overall efficiency depends on whether an agent can use idle time while waiting for tool responses. We refer to this capability as asynchronous tool calling. To evaluate it, we propose AsyncTool, a benchmark for assessing LLM-based agents in interactive multi-task tool-use environments with delayed tool feedback. AsyncTool presents multiple heterogeneous tasks simultaneously and simulates realistic tool response latency during execution. Using a hybrid data evolution strategy, we construct a diverse asynchronous multitasking dataset that covers multiple scenarios and tool-use patterns. We evaluate models at the step, sub-task, and task levels, and introduce efficiency-oriented metrics to measure task coordination and completion efficiency. Extensive experiments show that delayed tool feedback poses substantial challenges to current agents and leads to clear performance degradation. Models that better coordinate task switching, dependency tracking, and state maintenance achieve stronger performance on AsyncTool. Our analysis identifies key failure modes of current tool-using agents and provides practical insights for designing future systems with stronger temporal reasoning and coordination capabilities.

</details>


### [189] [Reward Bias Substitution: Single-Axis Bias Mitigations Redirect Optimization Pressure](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.27996)
*Max Lamparth,Daniel Fein,Andreas Haupt,Marcel Hussing,Mykel J. Kochenderfer*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper identifies a new failure mode in reward-model bias mitigation, where fixing one bias merely shifts optimization pressure onto correlated biases, making mitigations appear successful in audits while failing under actual policy optimization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reward models used to train language models via RLHF often pick up spurious proxies such as length, style, or sycophancy. Existing work proposes single-axis debiasing techniques, typically evaluated on fixed audit datasets.
