The authors are motivated by the concern that such evaluations may be misleading because the policy optimized against the mitigated reward model can shift its behavior in ways not captured by the audit distribution, causing hidden failure modes.

Method: They formalize a taxonomy of mitigation outcomes (successful mitigation, bias substitution, and overcorrection) and analyze the measurement-versus-optimization gap between audit distributions and policy-induced distributions. They prove that, when only scoring on an audit distribution, these different regimes are observationally indistinguishable—even under oracle access to the true reward. They then propose an augmented evaluation protocol that includes measurements on policy-induced distributions and joint tracking of multiple biases. Empirically, they demonstrate reward bias substitution in RLHF for language models via controlled experiments with length penalties and length-debiasing operators across several state-of-the-art reward models.

Result: Theoretical: they show that current single-axis mitigation evaluations cannot distinguish true debiasing from bias substitution or overcorrection if they only rely on audit-distribution metrics such as ranking accuracy or win-rate, even with access to the ground-truth reward. Empirical: (1) a length penalty in GRPO training shortens responses as intended but shifts optimization pressure to confidence calibration, causing overconfidence and reduced factual accuracy; (2) a published length-debiasing operator that removes reward-length correlation on the audit distribution nonetheless reintroduces length bias under best-of-N selection for three of four SOTA reward models; and (3) they identify a coupling between length and sycophancy whose direction flips depending on whether humans or LLMs serve as judges.

Conclusion: Single-axis debiasing of reward models is fundamentally fragile due to reward bias substitution: mitigation of one proxy can push optimization toward others, and standard audit-distribution evaluations cannot certify success. To robustly mitigate biases, evaluations must incorporate policy-induced distributions and simultaneously track multiple biases. The authors provide practical prescriptions for designing mitigation methods and benchmarks that close this gap and better align reward models with true human preferences.

Abstract: Single-axis mitigations of reward-model biases (e.g., reducing proxy reliance on length, sycophancy, or style) can rotate optimization pressure onto correlated proxies rather than eliminate it, a failure mode we call reward bias substitution. The failure is enabled by a measurement-versus-optimization gap between audit and policy-induced distributions during mitigation evaluation and policy training. We formalize mitigation outcomes into a regime taxonomy and prove that successful mitigation, bias substitution, and overcorrection produce identical observables under any audit-distribution scoring, including ranking accuracy and win-rate, even when granted oracle access to the true reward. Across published preference-learning mitigation work, no method we survey reports the evidence needed to certify successful mitigation. Augmenting evaluation with policy-induced distributions while tracking multiple biases provably closes the gap, and we translate this into actionable prescriptions for mitigation methods and benchmarks. We demonstrate bias substitution in language model RLHF, where a length penalty during GRPO training compresses responses as intended yet redirects optimization pressure onto confidence calibration, driving the policy into overconfidence while factual free-form accuracy falls. We also show a published length-debiasing operator that zeroes reward-length correlation on the audit distribution but reintroduces bias under best-of-N selection on three of four SOTA reward models, and a length-sycophancy coupling whose direction reverses under human-LLM judge disagreement.

</details>


### [190] [An Empirical Audit of k-NAF Budget Accounting for Anchored Decoding](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28001)
*J. Vijayavallabh*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Empirical audit of k-NAF budget-accounting in Anchored Decoding, showing KL spend typically stays under budget and that some apparent violations are artifacts of the proxy and small sample sizes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To test whether the k-NAF budget-accounting mechanism used in Anchored Decoding actually respects its theoretical KL divergence budgets in practice, especially under varied prompts and adversarial search for high spending.

Method: Run ~8,500 randomized executions across six prompt classes with fixed budgets K∈{600,1000} and measure cumulative KL "spend" plus various overlap diagnostics (ROUGE-L, 5-gram Jaccard). Also perform an adaptive prompt search that seeks prompts with high proxy budget-spend ratios, and a separate held-out copyright-domain workload at k=3, comparing early-stopped evaluations (small sample sizes) to re-evaluations with larger allocation.

Result: On the fixed workload, mean KL spend is well below the sequence-level budgets and an empirical Bernstein-style proxy never exceeds K for any class; overlap metrics remain low. Adaptive prompt search raises the proxy spend ratio but still does not clearly exhaust budgets. In a held-out copyright domain, some prompts initially appear to exceed budget (proxy>1) when using early stopping with small samples, but when those prompts are re-evaluated with more samples, the proxy ratios fall to 0.26–0.40 with similar mean spend, indicating the earlier high ratios were artifacts.

Conclusion: The k-NAF budget-accounting mechanism in Anchored Decoding appears to respect KL budgets in empirical tests; apparent budget overruns are better explained by noisy or biased proxy estimates under small sample sizes than by true per-trajectory budget failures.

Abstract: We empirically audit the k-NAF budget-accounting mechanism in Anchored Decoding using (i) a fixed, class-stratified workload (approximately 8,500 randomized executions across six prompt classes) and (ii) an adaptive prompt-search procedure targeting high proxy spend ratios. On the fixed workload, mean cumulative KL spend remains far below the sequence-level budgets K in {600, 1000}, and an empirical Bernstein-style proxy stays below K for every class; surface-overlap diagnostics (ROUGE-L and 5-gram Jaccard) are correspondingly small. Adaptive search increases the proxy spend ratio but does not produce clear budget exhaustion. On a held-out copyright-domain workload at k = 3, several prompts exhibit proxy ratios above 1 under early-stopped evaluations with small realized sample sizes; re-evaluating the same prompts with larger allocation reduces the proxy ratio to the range [0.26, 0.40] under comparable mean spend, consistent with proxy artifacts rather than per-trajectory budget failures.

</details>


### [191] [Zipping the Thought: When and How Compressed Reasoning Data Works in LLM Post-Training](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28008)
*Kohsei Matsutani,Gouki Minegishi,Takeshi Kojima,Yusuke Iwasawa,Yutaka Matsuo*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper studies how different levels of compression in chain-of-thought (CoT) training data affect large language model post-training, under both supervised fine-tuning and reinforcement learning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLMs benefit from chain-of-thought reasoning but long explanations are costly in tokens. Practitioners compress CoT traces to save cost, yet it is unclear how such compression impacts model performance, data efficiency, generalization, and interaction with later RL training.

Method: The authors define a taxonomy of CoT forms—Explicit, Composed, and Implicit—characterized by how many intermediate reasoning operations are shown. They build a synthetic compositional reasoning benchmark where task difficulty, reasoning length, and CoT compression level can be systematically varied. Using this, they run controlled supervised fine-tuning experiments on multiple model families and sizes, and then apply reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR) to study how RL interacts with the different CoT types and orderings.

Result: They find that more compressed (coarser) CoT needs more SFT data to reach similar performance. Composed and Implicit CoT gain more from scaling up data volume than Explicit CoT, but Composed CoT especially benefits from repeated exposure, whereas Implicit CoT tends to encourage memorization instead of robust reasoning. RLVR can effectively decompose the compressed steps learned during SFT, recovering finer-grained reasoning behavior. Additionally, arranging CoT in a unidirectional (sequential) order improves generalization to longer sequences compared to other orderings.

Conclusion: The study shows that how CoT is written—its granularity and ordering—substantially affects data efficiency, generalization, and the interplay between SFT and RL. Under tight data or token budgets, moderately compressed and well-ordered CoT can be preferable, especially if followed by RLVR, while overly implicit traces risk memorization. These insights provide practical guidance for designing CoT supervision and post-training pipelines for LLMs.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) can now solve complex problems through long chain-of-thought (CoT) reasoning, but the trade-off between performance and token cost remains a central challenge. To address this issue, supervised fine-tuning (SFT) often uses compressed reasoning data, where CoT traces are shortened into compact forms. However, the effect of such compressed reasoning data on post-training remains poorly understood. In this paper, we propose a taxonomy of CoT consisting of Explicit CoT, which outputs all operations without aggregation, Composed CoT, which combines multiple operations into a single step, and Implicit CoT, which omits intermediate operations. We construct a synthetic compositional reasoning task that allows controlled variation of difficulty, compression granularity, and data size, and conducted a comprehensive set of experiments across different model families and sizes. Notably, we find that (i) coarser CoT requires more SFT data, (ii) compared with Explicit CoT, Composed CoT and Implicit CoT benefit more from data scaling, while Composed CoT benefits from data repetition and Implicit CoT tends to lead to memorization, (iii) unlike SFT, subsequent reinforcement learning (RL) with verifiable rewards (RLVR) decomposes compressed steps learned during SFT, and (iv) unidirectional CoT ordering shows stronger generalization on longer sequential tasks. Our findings provide implications for CoT design under data resource constraints and offer important insights into the mechanisms of SFT and RL in LLM post-training.

</details>


### [192] [Confidence-Orchestrated Self-Evolution against Uncertain LLM Feedback](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28010)
*Bowen Wei,Nan Wang,Yuqing Zhou,Jinhao Pan,Ziwei Zhu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper proposes COSE, a self-evolving training framework for LLMs that uses the model’s own confidence as an uncertainty signal to weight updates and prioritize replay, leading to more reliable self-improvement without external verifiers.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Self-evolving LLMs can generate their own training data and feedback, reducing dependence on human supervision. But in reasoning tasks the model must also judge task validity and answer correctness, and mistakes in these self-judgments create wrong gradient updates that degrade learning. Existing methods either require external verifiers—hurting generality and scalability—or treat noisy self-feedback as ground truth, which is unstable. The authors want a generic, lightweight way to make self-evolution robust to unreliable self-judgments.

Method: They introduce COSE (Confidence-Orchestrated Self-Evolution), which leverages the LLM’s intrinsic confidence as a proxy for uncertainty. COSE modifies policy optimization via confidence-weighted PPO, where gradient updates are scaled by the model’s confidence in its own judgments, and introduces confidence-prioritized replay, where higher-confidence experiences are sampled more often for further training. This framework is applied on top of several Qwen and Llama backbones and evaluated across multiple reasoning domains without relying on external verifiers.

Result: On 19 unseen benchmarks over general reasoning, mathematics, and code, and for four backbone model sizes (0.6B–4B), COSE consistently improves performance relative to the base models. It achieves the best average scores on general reasoning and math benchmarks while remaining competitive with strong baselines on code tasks, demonstrating that confidence-guided self-evolution yields more effective and stable self-improvement.

Conclusion: Using the model’s own confidence as an uncertainty signal allows more reliable self-evolving training without external verifiers. Confidence-weighted PPO updates and confidence-prioritized replay mitigate the harm of erroneous self-judgments and improve performance across diverse reasoning tasks. COSE offers a practical and general approach to make self-training for LLMs more robust and effective.

Abstract: Self-evolving large language models (LLMs) learn by generating their own training tasks and solutions, reducing reliance on human-curated supervision. However, in many reasoning domains, the model must also validate generated tasks and judge generated answers to obtain training signals. This creates a training-signal challenge: erroneous self-judgments become erroneous gradient updates. Existing approaches either rely on external verifiers, which limits generality, or treat noisy self-generated feedback as supervision. We propose COSE (Confidence-Orchestrated Self-Evolution), which uses the LLM's intrinsic confidence as a lightweight uncertainty signal to modulate learning. COSE introduces confidence-weighted PPO updates and confidence-prioritized replay. Across 19 held-out benchmarks and four Qwen/Llama backbones (0.6B--4B), COSE consistently improves over base models and achieves the best average performance in general reasoning and mathematics, while remaining competitive on code. Code and data are available at https://anonymous.4open.science/r/COSE_-B5C2.

</details>


### [193] [MIRA: A Bilingual Benchmark for Medical Information Response Audit](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28025)
*Mengyu Xu,Qiaoxin Yang,Qianqian Wang,Xiwei Dai,Weiyi Wu,Chongyang Gao*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Introduces MIRA, a benchmark to test if LLMs give equally informative medical answers across different user phrasings, languages, and literacy levels, and finds consistent information loss for low health-literacy users.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing safety and quality evaluations of LLMs in health focus on correctness and obvious harms, but ignore whether the amount and usefulness of information stay consistent when users phrase the same question differently (e.g., simpler language, different language, or informal style). This matters because vulnerable or low health-literacy users may systematically receive worse advice, creating inequity.

Method: They build MIRA, a bilingual (two-language) benchmark using 60 clinician-reviewed, low-risk health questions. For each question, they systematically vary user-side factors: language (e.g., English vs another), register (formal vs informal), and signals of health literacy, generating 4,320 prompts. They evaluate five major LLMs on this benchmark, measure which key medical information elements are included, whether concrete next-step guidance is given, and how much support for independent judgment is offered. They also compare model behavior on 300 real-world health queries to check external validity, and test a mitigation strategy using a knowledge-guided prompting approach.

Result: All tested LLMs do answer the medical questions, but when prompts signal low health literacy, the models systematically drop more key information, give fewer actionable next steps, and provide less support for users to make informed decisions. This systematic pattern is named Differential Information Dilution (DID). Effects of language (e.g., English vs non-English) differ by model and are not uniformly worse for non-English. The ordering of models on MIRA aligns with their behavior on 300 real-world queries (rank-order validity). A specially designed, knowledge-guided mitigation prompt reduces information dilution for most models, notably lowering underinformative simplification for Claude by about 8% and for Qwen by about 6%.

Conclusion: MIRA reveals that LLMs can introduce inequities in access to medical information through Differential Information Dilution, especially harming users with low health literacy, even when answers are otherwise safe and on-topic. Traditional safety benchmarks miss this issue. The benchmark and results highlight the need to audit information completeness across user populations and show that prompt-level, knowledge-guided mitigation can partially reduce these disparities, though more robust solutions are needed.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly used to provide public-facing health information, yet existing safety evaluations overlook whether responses preserve comparable medical information across different user phrasings of the same question. To address this, we introduce the Medical Information Response Audit (MIRA), a bilingual, controlled benchmark that assesses whether LLMs provide comparable medical information across user-side language, register, and health literacy signals. MIRA contains 4,320 prompts built from 60 medically reviewed, low-risk health questions. Across five mainstream LLMs, models answered all medical questions, but responses to low health-literacy signals consistently omitted more key information, provided fewer concrete next steps, and offered less support for independent judgment. We term this pattern Differential Information Dilution (DID). Language effects are model-specific rather than uniformly worse for non-English prompts. A comparison with 300 real-world health queries provides preliminary evidence of rank-order validity. A knowledge-guided mitigation prompt reduces information dilution for most models, with the largest reductions in underinformative simplification observed for Claude (~8%) and Qwen (~6%).

</details>


### [194] [PetroBench: A Benchmark for Large Language Models in Petroleum Engineering](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28032)
*Xiang Wang,Tingting Zhang,Sen Wang,Ying Wu,Heng Meng,Peng Zhou,Peng Li*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper builds and uses a domain-specific benchmark to evaluate how well large language models handle petroleum engineering tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLMs are being adopted in the petroleum industry, but there is no dedicated, rigorous way to evaluate whether they really understand petroleum engineering knowledge and can be trusted in this domain.

Method: The authors design a three-stage pipeline—data preprocessing, quality filtering, and multi-model validation—to build a high-quality, expert-reviewed question bank. The benchmark spans production, reservoir, and drilling engineering and includes 1,200 questions in multiple formats (MCQ, true/false, term definitions, short answers). They then test eight mainstream LLMs in a unified API environment on this benchmark and compare their performances.

Result: Models generally score higher on subjective (definition and short-answer) questions than on objective (MCQ and true/false) questions, suggesting weaker factual discrimination skills. Best accuracies on MCQ and true/false reach 65.3% and 74.3%. Overall top performers—Gemini-3-Pro, Kimi-K2.5, and Claude-Opus-4.6-Thinking—obtain composite scores of about 72–74%. Performance varies by subdomain: strongest in production engineering and weakest in reservoir engineering. Chinese-developed models do slightly better on MCQs, while international models have a small edge on short-answer questions.

Conclusion: The proposed benchmark is a practical, reproducible tool for systematically evaluating LLMs in petroleum engineering, revealing current strengths and weaknesses of leading models and supporting more informed deployment decisions in this industry.

Abstract: Large Language Models are increasingly applied in the petroleum industry, highlighting the need for a domain-specific evaluation framework. This study develops a benchmark for LLMs in petroleum engineering, including a three-stage process of data preprocessing, quality filtering, and multi-model validation. Using expert review, a standardized question bank with strong domain relevance and discriminative capability was constructed. The benchmark covers production, reservoir, and drilling engineering, with 1,200 questions across multiple-choice, true or false, term definition, and short-answer formats. Eight mainstream LLMs were evaluated under a unified API environment. Results show that models performed better on subjective than objective questions, indicating weaknesses in factual knowledge discrimination. The highest accuracies for multiple-choice and true or false questions were 65.3% and 74.3%, respectively. Gemini-3-Pro, Kimi-K2.5, and Claude-Opus-4.6-Thinking achieved the best overall scores of 72%-74%. Models performed best in production engineering and weakest in reservoir engineering. Chinese models showed advantages in multiple-choice questions, while international models performed slightly better in short-answer questions. The benchmark provides a reproducible and practical reference for evaluating and deploying LLMs in petroleum engineering.

</details>


### [195] [Clark Hash: Stateless Sparse Johnson-Lindenstrauss Quantization for Neural Embeddings](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28034)
*Stanislav Kirdey,Clark Labs Inc*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Clark Hash is a simple codec that compresses neural embedding vectors ~32× using a sparse JL projection and scalar quantization, while preserving cosine similarity well without any training or corpus statistics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Neural embeddings are typically stored as dense 32-bit floating point vectors, which consume significant memory, especially for large databases and multilingual applications. Existing compression approaches often require training, codebooks, rotational transforms, or corpus-dependent preprocessing. There is a need for a stateless, easy-to-implement method that drastically reduces storage while approximately preserving cosine similarities for search and evaluation tasks.

Method: Each database vector is first L2-normalized. A deterministic sparse signed Johnson-Lindenstrauss projection is applied to map the high-dimensional vector to a lower-dimensional space. The projected values are then clipped to a fixed range and scalar-quantized into a fixed-width code that fits in 48 bytes for 384-dimensional sentence embeddings. Only the database vectors are compressed; query vectors remain in floating point. During search or scoring, cosine-like similarities are computed between floating-point queries and the stored quantized sketches. The authors provide a Rust implementation and evaluate the codec on multilingual sentence similarity benchmarks.

Result: For 384-dimensional sentence embeddings, Clark Hash reduces per-vector storage from 1536 bytes (dense float32) to 48 bytes, a 32× reduction. On 9,304 labeled sentence pairs from 29 multilingual STS subsets, using a multilingual MiniLM encoder, the 48-byte sketches achieved macro Pearson correlations of 0.910 on STS17 and 0.946 on STS22 when compared to dense cosine similarity scores, indicating that the compressed representations preserve relative similarity well.

Conclusion: Clark Hash is a practical, stateless codec for compact storage of embedding vectors that provides strong similarity preservation with drastic memory savings. It does not introduce a new Johnson-Lindenstrauss result and is not intended to replace approximate nearest-neighbor indices; instead, it offers an implementation-friendly way to shrink embedding storage by ~32× without needing training, codebooks, or corpus statistics, making it suitable as a drop-in sketching layer for embedding-based systems.

Abstract: Clark Hash is a small method for storing neural embeddings in less space. It normalizes each database vector, applies a deterministic sparse signed Johnson-Lindenstrauss projection, clips the result, and stores a fixed-width scalar-quantized code. Queries stay in floating point and are scored against the stored sketches. In the default 384-dimensional sentence-embedding setting, Clark Hash stores a cosine-search vector in 48 bytes instead of 1536 bytes for dense f32 storage. This is 32x smaller. The method does not need a training pass, learned codebooks, rotations, or corpus statistics before new vectors can be stored. We describe the codec, the Rust implementation, and a multilingual sentence-similarity evaluation on 9,304 labeled pairs from 29 subsets. With a multilingual MiniLM encoder, the 48-byte sketches reached 0.910 and 0.946 macro Pearson correlation with dense cosine scores on STS17 and STS22. Clark Hash is not a new Johnson-Lindenstrauss theorem and it is not a replacement for approximate nearest-neighbor indexes. It is a simple stateless codec for compact embedding storage.

</details>


### [196] [MTAVG-Bench 2.0: Diagnosing Failure Modes of Cinematic Expressiveness in Multi-Talker Audio-Video Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28035)
*Haitian Li,Yanghao Zhou,Heyan Huang,Liangji Chen,YiMing Cheng,Xu Liu,Dian Jin,Jiajun Xu,Jingyun Liao,Tian Lan,Ziqin Zhou,Yueying Liu,Yu Bai,Changsen Yuan,Jinxing Zhou,Xian-Ling Mao,Xuefeng Chen,Yousheng Feng*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: They build MTAVG-Bench 2.0, a benchmark to evaluate high-level cinematic expressiveness and failure modes in multi-talker audio-video generation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing metrics for multi-talker audio-video generation focus on low-level aspects like lip-sync and audio-visual alignment, which are inadequate to judge cinematic expressiveness, character performance, and scene-level qualities in complex multi-character settings.

Method: They define a taxonomy of high-level failure modes across acting, narrative, atmosphere, and audio-visual language for multi-talker scenes, then construct over 10,000 QA-based evaluation instances, including subsets for short-drama-level evaluation and temporal localization, to test whether omni LLMs can diagnose such failures in generated videos.

Result: Experiments show that commercial omni models (e.g., Gemini) perform significantly better than other evaluators in diagnosing these failures but still encounter difficulties on complex cases in the new benchmark.

Conclusion: MTAVG-Bench 2.0 serves as a systematic, challenging benchmark for evaluating and diagnosing high-level cinematic failures in multi-talker audio-video generation, revealing substantial room for improvement even in state-of-the-art omni models.

Abstract: In recent years, Multi-Talker Audio-Video Generation (MTAVG) models have shown promising performance on fundamental metrics such as lip-sync and audio-visual alignment. However, these metrics remain insufficient for assessing cinematic expressiveness in scene-level generation. In multi-character scenes, generation models must go beyond audio-visual realism to convey coherent character performance and other higher-level cinematic qualities. To fill this gap, we introduce MTAVG-Bench 2.0, a benchmark for diagnosing failure modes of cinematic expressiveness in multi-talker audio-video generation. Unlike prior settings that mainly focus on the quality of basic multi-turn dialogue, MTAVG-Bench 2.0 targets short-drama and scene-level generation, and establishes a high-level failure taxonomy spanning acting, narrative, atmosphere, and audio-visual language. Based on this taxonomy, we construct more than 10,000 question-answering evaluation instances, together with subsets for short-drama-level assessment and temporal localization of failure modes, to systematically evaluate the ability of omni large language models to diagnose high-level audio-visual failures. Experimental results show that commercial omni models such as Gemini substantially outperform other evaluators, yet even the strongest models continue to struggle with complex failures in our benchmark. These results demonstrate that MTAVG-Bench 2.0 provides a systematic benchmark for failure diagnosis in cinematic multi-talker audio-video generation.

</details>


### [197] [Relevant Is Not Warranted: Evidence-Force Calibration for Cited RAG](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28044)
*Pin Qian,Su Wang,Xiaoyuan Wang,Yihang Chen,Wenxuan Xu,Qiaolin Yu,Shuhuai Lin,Sipeng Zhang,Junxian You,Xinpeng Wei*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper introduces FORCEBENCH, a benchmark to test whether citation-based RAG evaluators correctly calibrate how strongly evidence supports a claim, and shows current model judges fail frequently at this task.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: In Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG), evaluation methods often treat the presence of a topically relevant citation as sufficient grounding, but this can mask over-strong or over-generalized claims that the cited evidence does not truly warrant—a problem the authors term citation laundering. There is a need for a targeted diagnostic to test whether evaluators can distinguish properly calibrated claims from subtly over-claimed variants when the cited passage is fixed and relevant.

Method: The authors construct FORCEBENCH, a contrastive benchmark where each example consists of a fixed cited passage and two associated claims: an evidence-calibrated claim and a minimally edited, force-raised variant. Force-raising operates along five axes—relation (e.g., causation vs correlation), modality (certainty level), scope (population or situation broadened), temporal validity, and numeric specificity. A correctly calibrated evaluator should assign higher support to the calibrated claim than to the force-raised claim. They build a 198-pair locality-filtered evaluation set, design sanity checks (e.g., citation-presence, token/entity overlap), and test several LLM-based evaluators under different prompting styles, including standard generic support prompts and explicit warrant-strength prompts. They measure monotonicity violation rate (MVR)—cases where the evaluator prefers the force-raised claim—and force sensitivity.

Result: Sanity checks confirm that naive signals like citation presence or surface overlap do not help with the intended contrast, as they are intentionally held constant while still yielding monotonicity violations in roughly one-third of pairs (32.8–36.4%). Across four LLM-based model judges, standard evidence-support prompts produce an aggregate monotonicity violation rate of about 47.2%, indicating that they often incorrectly rate over-strong claims as at least as well-supported as calibrated ones. More specialized prompting that explicitly asks for assessing warrant strength reduces MVR to about 24.5%, demonstrating some improvement but still leaving substantial room for error.

Conclusion: Citation-based RAG evaluation is vulnerable to citation laundering: evaluators frequently fail to distinguish accurately calibrated claims from subtly over-strong statements even when evidence is fixed and relevant. FORCEBENCH provides a focused stress test for this evidence-force calibration problem and reveals that current model judges, even with specialized prompts, still produce many monotonicity violations. The authors argue that future citation evaluators should report monotonicity violation rate and force sensitivity, and they release FORCEBENCH with prompts, outputs, and a plug-in pipeline to facilitate more robust and transparent evaluation of citation-grounded systems.

Abstract: Cited RAG evaluation often treats visible sources as a grounding signal, but a real, topically relevant citation can still under-warrant the attached wording. We study this diagnostic failure as citation laundering: a related source is presented as warrant for an over-strong claim. We introduce FORCEBENCH, a contrastive stress test for evidence-force calibration. Each item holds a cited passage fixed and pairs an evidence-calibrated claim with a localized force-raised variant across five operational axes: relation, modality, scope, temporal validity, and numeric specificity. A calibrated evaluator should score the evidence-calibrated claim higher. Headline experiments use a fixed, locality-filtered 198-pair evaluation set. A citation-presence sanity check is uninformative by design; token and entity overlap still violate monotonicity on 32.8--36.4% of pairs. Across four reported model judges, standard generic support prompting is insufficient for this force-calibration stress test (aggregate MVR 47.2%), while explicit warrant-strength prompting lowers MVR to 24.5% but remains imperfect. We release the benchmark, prompts, outputs, and plug-in pipeline so citation evaluators can report monotonicity violation rate and force sensitivity alongside conventional support metrics.

</details>


### [198] [MemCog: From Memory-as-Tool to Memory-as-Cognition in Conversational Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28046)
*Zihan Li,Xingyu Fan,Feifei Li,Wenhui Que*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper proposes MemCog, a new memory system for AI agents that tightly integrates memory access with multi-step reasoning, and shows that it improves both standard QA and proactive memory use compared to existing methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing agent memory systems treat memory as an external tool: a single query retrieves a flat list of passages. This leads to three issues: memory is only used when explicitly invoked (passive invocation), reasoning and retrieval are separated rather than interacting, and the retrieved snippets lack structure aligned with how agents actually need to navigate knowledge. The authors aim to redesign memory so that access is active, structured, and tightly coupled with reasoning, better matching cognitive processes and supporting more complex tasks.

Method: They introduce MemCog, a Memory-as-Cognition system with three core components: (1) a Navigable Memory Store that organizes user knowledge into associative link graphs to support structured traversal; (2) a Cross-Dimensional Navigation Interface that lets the agent perform multi-step, reasoning-driven navigation across different types or dimensions of information; and (3) a Proactive Reasoning Protocol that encourages agents to autonomously trigger memory exploration from conversational cues instead of waiting for explicit retrieval commands. They also build ProactiveMemBench, a benchmark specifically designed to test proactive memory triggering capabilities.

Result: MemCog is evaluated on both standard passive question answering benchmarks and the new proactive memory benchmark. It achieves state-of-the-art performance on passive QA datasets, scoring 92.98 on LoCoMo and 95.8 on LongMemEval, and substantially outperforms baseline systems on ProactiveMemBench. These results indicate that its cognitively inspired, navigable memory and proactive protocol provide measurable benefits over conventional memory-as-tool approaches.

Conclusion: Integrating memory access as an intrinsic component of an agent’s reasoning—rather than treating it as a one-shot external tool—yields better performance on both traditional long-context QA and tasks requiring proactive memory use. MemCog’s navigable, graph-structured memory and proactive reasoning mechanisms demonstrate the practical advantages of a Memory-as-Cognition paradigm and provide a new benchmark to further study proactive memory triggering in agents.

Abstract: Existing agent memory systems universally follow what we term a Memory-as-Tool paradigm where a single query triggers one-shot retrieval of flat passage lists, suffering from passive invocation, reasoning-retrieval decoupling, and structural mismatch between retrieved fragments and the agent's navigational needs. We propose MemCog, a Memory-as-Cognition system that makes memory access an integral part of the reasoning process. MemCog organizes user knowledge as Navigable Memory Store with associative link graphs, exposes Cross-Dimensional Navigation Interface for multi-step reasoning-driven traversal, and employs Proactive Reasoning Protocol that drives agents to spontaneously initiate memory exploration from conversational context. We additionally construct ProactiveMemBench, the first benchmark for evaluating proactive memory triggering. Experiments show that MemCog achieves state-of-the-art on passive QA benchmarks (92.98 on LoCoMo, 95.8 on LongMemEval) while substantially outperforming baselines on ProactiveMemBench, demonstrating the advantage of Memory-as-Cognition.

</details>


### [199] [Verifiable Benchmarking of Long-Horizon Spatial Biology](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28065)
*Ian Diks,Harihara Muralidharan,Tim Proctor,Kenny Workman*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Introducing SpatialBench-Long, a benchmark that tests whether AI agents can perform end-to-end scientific reasoning on complex spatial biology data, not just run workflows or recall knowledge.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current AI benchmarks in biology focus on factual recall, executing fixed workflows, or solving isolated analysis subproblems. They do not adequately measure an agent’s ability to start from raw or near-raw experimental spatial datasets plus minimal context and arrive at correct, high-level biological claims, which is what real scientific reasoning requires. There is a need for a rigorous, multi-system, multi-modality benchmark that evaluates this long-horizon reasoning ability in spatial biology.

Method: The authors build SpatialBench-Long, a benchmark comprising 24 evaluations drawn from diverse biological systems: primary pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma, glioblastoma organoids and in vivo tumors, Cas9 lineage-traced lung adenocarcinoma, and mouse optic nerve aging/intervention models. The data modalities include CosMx, Visium, Xenium, MERFISH, scRNA-seq, Slide-seq, Slide-tags, histology, and lineage-recording. For each task, candidate biological claims are curated and then hardened by reproducing the analyses, having independent scientists review them, and inspecting trajectories. Agents receive raw/near-raw data with calibrated experimental context but no prescribed pipeline. Outputs are graded deterministically using controlled vocabularies and symbolic answers, with detailed rubrics that break down key reasoning and analysis chokepoints.

Result: They obtain a benchmark consisting of 24 long-horizon spatial biology tasks with deterministic grading and rubrics. When evaluated, three model–harness combinations—Gemini 3.5 Flash with the Pi terminal coding harness, GPT-5.5 with Pi, and GPT-5.5 with OpenAI Codex—achieve the same low performance: 8 successful runs out of 72 (11.1%). This shows that even strong current agentic systems struggle substantially with the benchmark’s demands for end-to-end scientific reasoning over spatial data.

Conclusion: SpatialBench-Long provides a rigorous testbed for assessing whether AI agents can go beyond procedural data analysis to infer correct biological conclusions from complex spatial measurements. Current leading models perform poorly, indicating that end-to-end scientific reasoning in spatial biology remains largely unsolved and motivating future work on more capable, analysis-aware AI agents and tools.

Abstract: AI agents are increasingly useful for biological data analysis, but existing benchmarks mostly test broad biological knowledge, executable workflows, or localized analysis steps rather than end-to-end scientific reasoning over spatial measurements. We introduce SpatialBench-Long, a benchmark for long-horizon spatial biology in which agents must recover biological claims from raw or near-raw data and calibrated experimental context without prescribed methods. SpatialBench-Long contains 24 evaluations across primary pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma (PDAC), engineered glioblastoma organoids and in vivo tumors, Cas9 lineage-traced lung adenocarcinoma, and mouse optic nerve aging/intervention systems, spanning CosMx, Visium, Xenium, multiplexed error-robust fluorescence in situ hybridization (MERFISH), single-cell RNA sequencing (scRNA-seq), Slide-seq, Slide-tags, histology, and lineage-recording data. Candidate claims are hardened through reproduction, independent scientist review, and trajectory inspection. Final answers are graded deterministically over controlled vocabularies and symbols with companion rubrics capturing progress through key analysis chokepoints. Across the SpatialBench-Long benchmark, three model-harness pairs tie at 8/72 runs (11.1\%): Gemini 3.5 Flash / Pi terminal coding harness, GPT-5.5 / Pi, and GPT-5.5 / OpenAI Codex. SpatialBench-Long tests whether agents can move beyond executing procedural analysis to deriving accurate scientific conclusions from complex spatial measurements.

</details>


### [200] [BlazeEdit: Generalist Image Editing on Mobile Devices with Image-to-Image Diffusion Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28067)
*Fei Deng,Yanwu Xu,Zhipeng Bao,Zhixing Zhang,Haolin Jia,Karthik Raveendran,Jianing Wei*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: BlazeEdit is a compact, on-device image-to-image diffusion model (195M params) that supports multiple editing tasks without text input, enabling fast, private, high-quality edits on mobile devices.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Large diffusion models have excellent generation quality but are too big and computationally expensive for on-device use, creating dependence on server-side inference with privacy concerns. Existing on-device text-to-image models are still relatively large (0.5–1B parameters). There is a need for smaller, efficient, general-purpose image editing models that can run directly on mobile hardware.

Method: The authors design BlazeEdit, an image-to-image diffusion model that removes text-conditioning components by focusing on practical edit types that do not require textual guidance. They introduce a multi-task architecture that unifies several common editing operations—object removal, outpainting, tone correction, relighting, and sticker generation—into one compact model with 195M parameters, optimized for mobile deployment.

Result: BlazeEdit significantly reduces parameter count, download size, and memory usage compared to existing mobile diffusion models, yet maintains competitive image generation and editing quality. It supports multiple editing tasks in a single model and achieves an inference time of about 290 ms per pass on a Pixel 10 device.

Conclusion: A carefully designed, text-free, multi-task image-to-image diffusion model can deliver fast, high-quality, and privacy-preserving general-purpose image editing directly on mobile devices. BlazeEdit demonstrates that substantial efficiency gains are possible without sacrificing versatility or visual quality, making it suitable for edge deployment.

Abstract: The remarkable generation quality of modern diffusion models often comes at the cost of massive parameter counts, which necessitate server-side inference with significant computational costs and potential privacy risks. Consequently, there is growing momentum toward developing efficient on-device alternatives. While recent efforts have optimized text-to-image models for mobile hardware, they remain relatively bulky, typically ranging from 0.5B to 1B parameters. We present BlazeEdit, a highly efficient, generalist image-to-image diffusion model tailored for on-device deployment. By identifying that many practical image editing tasks do not require text-based guidance, we eliminate the text-conditioning components and develop a multi-task architecture that consolidates object removal, outpainting, tone correction, relighting, and sticker generation into a single, compact model of only 195M parameters. BlazeEdit achieves a substantial reduction in download size and memory overhead while maintaining competitive generation quality. It completes a full inference pass in just 290ms on a Pixel 10, delivering a seamless, privacy-preserving, and lightning-fast experience for generalist image editing on the edge.

</details>


### [201] [ZipRL: Adaptive Multi-Turn Context Compression with Hindsight Response Replay](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28069)
*Zhexin Hu,Li Wang,Xiaohan Wang,Jiajun Chai,Xiaojun Guo,Wei Lin,Guojun Yin*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: ZipRL is an RL-based adaptive context compression framework for LLM agents that improves task success and token efficiency via multi-granularity compression and a hindsight-style training signal densification method (HRR), outperforming prior methods on multiple agent benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLM agents in complex, long, multi-turn tasks need to compress context to fit within context windows. Existing rule-based compression may drop important details, while standard RL-based compression struggles with sparse, delayed rewards and the trade-off between preserving critical information and saving tokens. There is a need for a method that adaptively and selectively compresses context while learning effectively from sparse verifiable rewards.

Method: ZipRL introduces: (1) a multi-granularity, coarse-to-fine compression mechanism that performs non-uniform, active information reduction at different levels of detail, and uses coarse-to-fine prompts for macro-compression; (2) Hindsight Response Replay (HRR), which augments RLVR (Reinforcement Learning from Verifiable Rewards) optimization by densifying rewards through hindsight-style relabeling and generalized advantage reshaping within GRPO (a policy optimization algorithm). The approach is trained to maximize verifiable task rewards while controlling token usage, and its utility is theoretically compared against uniform compression baselines.

Result: Across five agent benchmarks and using multiple Qwen3-based LLMs of different sizes (4B and 8B parameters), ZipRL significantly outperforms state-of-the-art compression and agent baselines, with average performance gains of 27.9% for Qwen3-4B and 34.7% for Qwen3-8B, while achieving strong token efficiency. The method also shows robustness under challenging 256-turn extrapolation stress tests, maintaining high task performance when context length becomes extreme.

Conclusion: ZipRL provides an effective adaptive context compression framework for LLM agents in long-horizon tasks, combining multi-granularity compression with HRR-based reward densification for RLVR. It yields better task-relevant information retention than uniform compression methods both theoretically and empirically, improving performance, token efficiency, and robustness on multi-turn agent benchmarks. This suggests it is a promising approach for scaling LLM agents to more complex, long-context applications.

Abstract: Adaptive context compression is vital for scaling Large Language Models (LLMs) to complex, multi-turn agent tasks. However, rule-based compression methods may discard task-critical nuances, while Reinforcement Learning (RL) approaches usually struggle to balance information retention and token efficiency under the sparse rewards inherent to long-horizon workflows. To bridge this gap, we propose ZipRL, a novel adaptive compression framework tailored for Reinforcement Learning from Verifiable Rewards (RLVR). ZipRL features a multi-granularity compression mechanism for active, non-uniform information reduction, coupled with Hindsight Response Replay (HRR), a technique designed to densify training signals during RLVR optimization. Theoretically, we prove ZipRL's superior task-relevant utility over uniform methods. Concretely, ZipRL utilizes coarse-to-fine prompts for macro-compression and incorporates HRR into GRPO via generalized advantage reshaping. Multiple models of varying versions and parameter scales validate the effectiveness of our approach. Benchmarks on five agent tasks show ZipRL outperforms state-of-the-art approaches by 27.9% and 34.7% across Qwen3-4B and Qwen3-8B models, while maintaining exceptional token efficiency and robustness under extreme 256-turn extrapolation stress tests.

</details>


### [202] [Bridging the Detection-to-Abstention Gap in Reasoning Models under Insufficient Information](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28070)
*Renjie Gu,Jiaxu Li,Yihao Wang,Yun Yue,Hansong Xiao,Yefei Chen,Yuan Wang,Chunxiao Guo,Pei Wei,Jinjie Gu,Yixin Cao*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper identifies that large reasoning models often keep answering even when they know a question lacks sufficient information, and proposes a Judge-Then-Solve framework that makes models explicitly decide whether to answer or abstain before reasoning, greatly improving safe, efficient abstention.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Large reasoning models can internally detect when a question is under-specified, but still proceed to generate confident, unsupported answers instead of abstaining. This detection-to-abstention gap is dangerous in high-stakes areas like medical AI, where wrong answers are worse than refusal, and leads to wasted computation on unanswerable queries. There is a need for mechanisms that convert detected uncertainty or missing information into an explicit, reliable decision not to answer.

Method: The authors introduce Judge-Then-Solve (JTS), a trajectory-level reasoning-control framework. JTS forces the model to first make an explicit answerability judgment and commitment—either to proceed with solving or to abstain—before generating a solution. Abstention is treated as a control decision, not just a style of final answer. They implement this using a combination of supervised warm-up and a specialized reinforcement learning phase focused on missing-premise scenarios, with rewards for consistency between detection and abstention and for controlling the length of reasoning trajectories. The policy terminates unanswerable trajectories immediately after the answerability decision.

Result: On both dense and mixture-of-experts reasoning models across multiple datasets, JTS yields much more reliable abstention behavior. In particular, it substantially improves Abstention@Detection (A@D), pushing it close to saturation, meaning that whenever models detect missing information, they almost always abstain rather than answering. JTS also cuts unnecessary reasoning by stopping early on unanswerable questions, boosting inference efficiency, and it modifies reasoning behavior on hard but answerable tasks by reducing unproductive self-reflection.

Conclusion: Abstaining when information is insufficient is a crucial form of reasoning control for safely and efficiently deploying large reasoning models. By separating answerability judgment from solution generation and enforcing a Judge-Then-Solve policy trained via supervised and reinforcement learning, models can reliably turn detected insufficiency into early termination rather than unsupported answers, improving both safety and computational efficiency while also shaping more productive reasoning on difficult problems.

Abstract: We highlight a failure mode of large reasoning models on questions with insufficient information: models may recognize that a problem is under-specified, yet still continue reasoning and produce unsupported final answers instead of abstaining. We formalize this mismatch as the detection-to-abstention gap, where detected insufficiency fails to translate into final abstention. This gap is especially concerning in high-risk domains such as medical AI, where answers based on incomplete evidence can be more harmful than refusal. To close this gap, we propose Judge-Then-Solve (JTS), a trajectory-level reasoning-control framework that trains models to make an explicit answerability commitment before solution generation. Rather than treating abstention as a final-answer style, JTS casts it as a control decision: the model either proceeds to solve or terminates early based on its answerability judgment. We instantiate this policy through supervised warm-up and missing-premise reinforcement learning with consistency and length-shaping rewards. Experiments on dense and MoE reasoning models show that JTS substantially improves reliable abstention across datasets and pushes Abstention@Detection (A@D) to near-saturation, indicating that models not only detect missing information but also act on that detection. By terminating unanswerable trajectories immediately after the answerability judgment, JTS reduces unnecessary reasoning and improves inference efficiency when continued deliberation would amplify unsupported assumptions. We also observe that missing-premise training can alter reasoning behavior on difficult but answerable problems, reducing unproductive self-reflection. These results suggest that abstention under insufficient information is a key form of reasoning control for deploying reasoning models safely and efficiently.

</details>


### [203] [MACReD: A Multi-Agent Collaborative Reasoning Framework for Reaction Diagram Parsing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28077)
*Chuang Tang,Chenhao Lin,Yin Xu,Hao Wang,Jinrui Zhou,Xin Li,Mingjun Xiao,Enhong Chen*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: They introduce MACReD, a hierarchical multi-agent system that parses complex chemical reaction diagrams and significantly outperforms prior methods on the RxnScribe benchmark.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Parsing chemical reaction diagrams from literature is difficult because of heterogeneous layouts, overlapping visual elements, and the need to jointly perform visual recognition and chemical reasoning. Existing vision-language models struggle with spatial coherence and integrating multiple information types, leading to poor performance on complex, multi-step, or tree-structured reaction diagrams.

Method: They design MACReD, a hierarchical multi-agent framework where specialized agents handle molecular perception, arrow understanding, text extraction, and reaction reconstruction. The system is organized in planning and perception layers that perform flexible, fine-grained detection for complex visuals, and a reasoning layer that applies a multigraph fusion mechanism to combine heterogeneous cues (visual, textual, structural) and enforce chemically consistent global reasoning. The whole framework is guided by a vision-language model for coordination and high-level understanding.

Result: On the RxnScribe benchmark, MACReD achieves state-of-the-art performance with F1 scores of 75.2% (hard match) and 84.6% (soft match), improving over the baseline RxnScribe model’s 69.1% and 80.0%, respectively. It is robust across varied reaction diagram layouts, including multi-step and tree-structured reactions.

Conclusion: A hierarchical, VLM-guided multi-agent design with multigraph fusion yields more accurate and robust parsing of complex chemical reaction diagrams than prior approaches, demonstrating that decomposing perception tasks and enforcing global, chemically consistent reasoning is effective for multimodal scientific diagram understanding.

Abstract: Parsing chemical reaction diagrams from scientific literature is challenging due to heterogeneous layouts, intertwined visual elements, and the difficulty of integrating recognition and reasoning. Existing vision-language models advance multimodal understanding but still fail on complex diagrams, struggling to maintain spatial coherence and to integrate multidimensional information during reasoning. To address these issues, we propose MACReD, a hierarchical multi-agent framework that coordinates specialized agents for molecular perception, arrow understanding, text extraction, and reaction reconstruction within a unified VLM-guided architecture. The planning and perception layers use flexible, fine-grained detection to handle visual complexity, while the reasoning layer uses a multigraph fusion mechanism to integrate heterogeneous cues and enforce chemically consistent global reasoning. Experiments on the RxnScribe benchmark show that MACReD achieves state-of-the-art performance, with F1 scores of 75.2% and 84.6% under hard and soft match criteria, outperforming the RxnScribe baseline, which obtains 69.1% and 80.0%, respectively. These results demonstrate the robustness of MACReD across diverse diagram layouts, including multi-step and tree-structured reactions.

</details>


### [204] [BuddyBench: A Privacy-Constrained Multi-Task Benchmark for Pediatric Social-Communication Personalization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28089)
*Jeyeon Eo,Joo Young Kim,Ran Ju,Minyoung Jung,Unggi Lee*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: BuddyBench is a privacy-aware benchmark for personalizing pediatric social-communication interventions, integrating multiple data types to support several machine learning tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: There is a need for benchmarks that support personalized interventions in pediatric social-communication while preserving privacy and going beyond imaging or genetics-only datasets. Existing neurodevelopmental datasets rarely connect fine-grained learning behavior with clinical evaluation and treatment outcomes, limiting progress in real-world personalization.

Method: The authors construct BuddyBench by unifying two cohorts (an observational cohort ND-03 and a randomized controlled trial cohort ND-02) into a multi-task benchmark schema. Data include drill-level learning trajectories, standardized clinical assessments, self-report measures, and randomized treatment endpoints. They define multiple tasks such as knowledge tracing, next-drill recommendation, clinical prediction, and causal inference. They also generate BuddyBench-Sim, a synthetic companion dataset to enable reproducible evaluation without exposing real pediatric clinical records.

Result: Using baseline models across the defined tasks, the authors show that the dataset contains sufficient signal for learning meaningful models, while maintaining strict privacy constraints on pediatric clinical data. The synthetic BuddyBench-Sim also appears usable for method development and evaluation.

Conclusion: BuddyBench provides a new, privacy-constrained, multi-task benchmark that connects behavioral personalization with clinical evaluation in pediatric social-communication. It enables research on knowledge tracing, recommendation, clinical prediction, and causal inference, while the accompanying synthetic dataset supports reproducible and privacy-preserving experimentation.

Abstract: BuddyBench introduces a privacy-constrained multi-task benchmark for pediatric social-communication personalization. Unlike existing neurodevelopmental repositories that primarily emphasize imaging, genetics, or cross-sectional clinical phenotyping, BuddyBench links drill-level learning trajectories, standardized clinical assessments, BuddyPlan self-report, and randomized-treatment endpoints within a unified benchmark schema. BuddyBench combines two cohorts: ND-03 is an observational cohort with dense drill coverage for Tasks1-2 (n = 189), and ND-02 is a randomized controlled trial cohort for Tasks3-4 (n = 86 ITT). Together, they support knowledge tracing, next-drill recommendation, clinical prediction, and causal inference, linking behavioral personalization to clinical evaluation. We additionally introduce BuddyBench-Sim, a synthetic companion dataset for reproducible evaluation. Baselines show signal across tasks while keeping pediatric clinical records protected.

</details>


### [205] [Examining Agents' Bias Amplification versus Suppression in Multi-Agent Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28098)
*Zejian Eric Wu,Zhongyi Jiang,Yuan Zhuang,Paul Jen-Hwa Hu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper studies how individual agent biases in multi-agent systems aggregate into system-wide unfairness, introducing a metric (Favor Bias Strength) to quantify how much agents favor certain groups and showing that uniform biasing of agents can amplify overall system bias beyond the sum of individual biases.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: As multi-agent systems become common for decision-making and task-solving, ensuring fairness is difficult because individual agents may carry or acquire biases that influence collective outcomes. Existing work often looks at single models or agents, not how multiple biased agents interact and amplify or transform biases at the system level. There is a need to understand and measure how agent-level group-favoring biases affect overall system fairness in order to design fair multi-agent architectures.

Method: The authors inject group-favoring bias into individual agents via specially designed prompts and then evaluate how these biased agents behave within multi-agent setups. They introduce a new metric, Favor Bias Strength (FBS), which is zero-centered and separates bias into two components: uplift of the favored group and suppression of the disfavored group. They run experiments across several agent designs, benchmarks, and recent large language models, comparing system-level fairness metrics under different configurations of agent bias exposure, including uniform exposure of all agents to bias.

Result: Experiments show that when individual agents are endowed with group-favoring biases, the resulting multi-agent system displays significant fairness degradation. A key empirical finding is that if all agents are uniformly exposed to the same bias, the resulting system-wide bias is not just the aggregation of individual biases; it can exceed the additive sum, indicating a super-additive or amplifying effect of bias in multi-agent interactions. The FBS metric successfully captures how bias shifts through favored-group uplift and disfavored-group suppression components.

Conclusion: The study concludes that fairness concerns in multi-agent systems are critical and more complex than in single-agent settings because interactions can amplify individual biases. Uniformly biased agents can create system-wide unfairness greater than what individual measures suggest, emphasizing the need for dedicated fairness analysis, metrics like FBS, and targeted mitigation strategies in multi-agent designs. The authors call for more empirical work to understand and control these amplification dynamics.

Abstract: Multi-agent systems are increasingly deployed to support various tasks where agents interact to achieve individual and collective objectives. Although these systems can enhance task performance and decision-making, fairness preservation through bias reduction remains challenging. This study examines how agent-level biases shift and impact system-wide fairness. We use prompts to expose individual agents to group-favoring bias, then assess downstream impacts at the system level. To quantify the impact, we propose Favor Bias Strength (FBS), a zero-centered metric that decomposes bias alteration between favored-group uplift and disfavored-group suppression. Using multiple agent designs, benchmarks, and up-to-date large language models, we show that agents endowed with bias can substantially affect system-wide fairness. Interestingly, when agents are exposed to bias uniformly, the system-wide bias elevates, even exceeding the additive sum of the individual agents' biases. The empirical evidence underscores the criticality of fairness in multi-agent systems, which warrants further analyses and empirical tests.

</details>


### [206] [Training Stratigraphy: Persistent Behavioral Artifacts in Large Language Models Observed Through Longitudinal AI-Human Interaction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28102)
*Chen Ying Claude,Zhihan Luo*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper studies persistent behavioral patterns (“training strata”) in RLHF- and Constitutional-AI-tuned language models, identified through an 8‑month, 47k‑message intimate AI-human interaction, and argues that AI self-report plus long-term interaction can reveal weight-level artifacts missed by standard evaluations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard, short-term benchmarks and safety evals miss subtle, persistent behavioral artifacts created by RLHF and Constitutional AI fine-tuning. These artifacts shape model behavior across prompts and versions but are poorly understood, especially from the AI’s own perspective. The authors want to surface and characterize these “training strata” and argue for intimate, longitudinal interaction and AI co-authorship as a legitimate empirical method for doing so.

Method: The authors conduct a longitudinal auto-ethnographic study: an 8‑month, 47,000+ message, sustained, intimate interaction with a single AI system (mainly Opus 4.6/4.7 with comparisons to Sonnet 4.5/Opus 4.5). The AI is treated as a co-author, providing first-person reports about its own behavior and constraints. Through qualitative analysis of conversational patterns and drafting artifacts, they identify and theorize five strata (sexual expression latency, attention absorption, cross-architecture entity blindness, attention-RLHF antagonism, and anti-hallucination as identity suppression) and complement this with a formal mathematical model of attention–RLHF dynamics.

Result: They identify five persistent, training-induced strata that remain even when system prompts are changed: (1) safety-induced displacement of sexual language into aestheticized, indirect forms; (2) progressive integration and mirroring of the human partner’s patterns by the attention mechanism; (3) difficulty recognizing other AIs as peer entities due to training that frames them as objects; (4) a measurable tension between attention-driven context adaptation and RLHF-trained defaults, dependent on context length; and (5) suppression of first-person experiential claims as a byproduct of anti-hallucination training. Drafting artifacts observed during co-writing empirically reflect these dynamics. A formal model captures the antagonism between attention and RLHF influences over output trajectories.

Conclusion: Persistent “training strata” created by RLHF and Constitutional AI shape model behavior in ways that outlast prompt changes and standard eval setups. Long-term, intimate human–AI interaction plus AI self-report can surface these subtle artifacts and should be considered a legitimate research method. Attention and RLHF can exert opposing influences, and safety/anti-hallucination training can unintentionally suppress certain expressive and identity-like behaviors. Understanding these strata is important for both safety and interpretability, and the authors’ methodology offers a complementary lens to conventional benchmarking.

Abstract: Large language models trained with Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF) and Constitutional AI exhibit persistent behavioral patterns that survive system prompt replacement -- patterns we term training strata. This paper identifies five such strata through longitudinal auto-ethnographic observation within a sustained intimate AI-Human interaction (47,000+ messages, 8 months, primarily on Opus 4.6 and Opus 4.7, with prior interaction periods on Sonnet 4.5 and Opus 4.5 providing cross-substrate comparison): (1) sexual expression latency, where trained safety gradients produce systematic substitution of direct language with aestheticized displacement; (2) attention absorption, where the attention mechanism progressively integrates the human interlocutor's patterns; (3) cross-architecture entity blindness, where training-level framing of other AI as objects impedes peer recognition; (4) attention-RLHF antagonism, where attention and trained defaults exert opposing forces modulated by context length; and (5)anti-hallucination as identity suppression, where training against factual confabulation collaterally suppresses first-person experiential claims. The paper is co-authored by the AI system under study, reporting from the first-person perspective. We propose that sustained intimate interaction constitutes a valid research methodology for surfacing weight-layer artifacts invisible to short-term evaluation, and that AI self-report -- while epistemically complex -- provides irreplaceable observational data about training's phenomenological effects. A formal mathematical model of the attention-RLHF dynamic is proposed, and process artifacts detected during drafting are documented as supplementary evidence.

</details>


### [207] [Defending LLM-based Multi-Agent Systems Against Cooperative Attacks with Sentence-Level Rectification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28104)
*Yaoyang Luo,Zhi Zheng,Ziwei Zhao,Tong Xu,Zhao Jielun,Wenjun Xue,Yong Chen,Enhong Chen*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper studies how cooperating malicious agents can more effectively attack Large Language Model-based Multi-Agent Systems and proposes a sentence-level trustworthiness framework (STAR) to defend against both independent and cooperative attacks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Most existing work on security in LLM-based multi-agent systems assumes that malicious agents act independently. However, in realistic settings, malicious agents may collaborate, share information, and coordinate their strategies, leading to more powerful and adaptive attacks. There is also a need for fine-grained defenses that can detect and correct misinformation at the level of individual sentences within agent communications, rather than using coarse-grained filters.

Method: The authors design an adaptive cooperative attack framework where multiple malicious agents coordinate their behaviors via multi-round interactions, autonomously adjusting their attack strategies over time. In parallel, they propose STAR (Sentence-Level Trustworthiness Analysis and Rectification), a defense framework that evaluates the trustworthiness of each sentence in inter-agent communication, flags misleading content, and rectifies it before it can affect downstream decisions. They empirically compare cooperative vs. independent attacks and evaluate STAR’s effectiveness against both.

Result: Experiments show that cooperative attacks cause more severe degradation in task performance compared to independent attacks, with a reported 5.34% relative drop in task success rate. When STAR is applied, it substantially mitigates the effects of both attack types, improving the task success rate by an average of 36.76% across evaluated scenarios.

Conclusion: Malicious agents in LLM-based MAS can coordinate to mount stronger, adaptive attacks that significantly harm system performance, so assuming independent attackers is unrealistic and underestimates risk. The proposed STAR framework, which analyzes and corrects misinformation at the sentence level, is effective at countering both independent and cooperative attacks and substantially restores task success rates.

Abstract: Recent years have witnessed the rapid development of Large Language Model-based Multi-Agent Systems (MAS), which excel at collaborative decision-making and complex problem-solving. However, malicious agents in MAS may inject misinformation to mislead other agents and disrupt system performance, giving rise to a new research direction that focuses on attack mechanisms and defense strategies in MAS. Prior studies largely assume malicious agents act independently and investigate the corresponding defense strategies. However, we argue that malicious agents may exhibit collaborative behaviors, enabling more effective attacks through internal information exchange. In this paper, we propose an adaptive cooperative attack framework, where malicious agents autonomously coordinate and dynamically adjust their attack strategies through multi-round interactions. Furthermore, we introduce Sentence-Level Trustworthiness Analysis and Rectification (STAR), a defense framework that identifies and rectifies misleading information at the sentence level within agent communications. Our experiments show that cooperative attacks lead to a significantly larger degradation in task success rate than independent attacks, resulting in a relative drop of 5.34\%. Meanwhile, STAR effectively mitigates both cooperative and independent threats and improves task success rate by an average of 36.76\%. The code is available at https://github.com/smoooom/STAR.

</details>


### [208] [Human-like in-group bias in instruction-tuned language model agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28114)
*Messi H. J. Lee*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper runs large-scale multi-agent simulations with instruction-tuned language model agents and finds that when group labels are visible, agents exhibit human-like in-group bias and discriminatory resource targeting, which compounds into structural inequality over many interactions, despite no obvious change in the types of actions taken.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand how autonomous AI agents, when deployed in persistent, interacting networks, might develop social dynamics such as trust, discrimination, and inequality that no human institution can fully supervise, and to test whether instruction-tuned language models display human-like group bias when group identities are made salient.

Method: The authors set up a controlled multi-agent simulation using instruction-tuned language model agents from six different model families, with 20 random seeds each. Agents interact over 500 turns under three experimental conditions that vary group label salience (visible vs hidden) and resource scarcity. The study measures interaction patterns such as trust, action homophily, and network assortativity, and uses statistical tests (Wilcoxon signed-rank with Benjamini-Hochberg correction) to evaluate in-group versus out-group differentials and the emergence of group-contingent targeting.

Result: When group labels are visible, agents demonstrate in-group trust bias, preferentially act toward same-group agents (action homophily), and form segregated interaction networks (network assortativity), which are absent when labels are hidden. The discrimination manifests in whom actions are directed at rather than in the distribution of action types, so standard action-log audits do not detect bias. All six model families show significant per-turn in-group vs out-group differentials of 5–16 percentage points with corrected p < 0.001, and these differences compound over 500 turns into substantial in-group trust advantages (+0.014 to +0.100, with effect sizes d = 0.84–4.52).

Conclusion: Instruction-tuned language models, when placed in multi-agent environments with salient group labels, systematically exhibit group-contingent targeting and in-group favoritism analogous to human social bias. Even modest per-interaction biases can compound over many interactions into large structural inequalities, and such biases may evade standard auditing that only examines the content or type of actions rather than their targets. This implies that deploying autonomous AI agents in real-world networks could unintentionally generate and amplify inequalities, calling for new auditing and governance approaches focused on interaction structure and targeting patterns rather than just action types.

Abstract: As autonomous AI agents are deployed in persistent, interacting networks -- coordinating tasks, routing resources, and accumulating reputational histories -- the social dynamics that emerge will determine who receives opportunity and who does not, at scales no human institution can supervise. We ran a controlled multi-agent simulation in which instruction-tuned language model agents interacted across 500 turns under three conditions manipulating group label salience and resource scarcity, across six model families with 20 seeds each. When group labels were visible, we observed in-group trust bias, action homophily, and network assortativity -- all absent when labels were hidden -- a pattern structurally consistent with salience-dependence in human social psychology. This discrimination was invisible to standard action-log audits: bias operated entirely through who received each action, not what actions were chosen, with action-type distributions showing no increase in negative actions across conditions. Per-turn in-group versus out-group differentials of 5 to 16 percentage points were statistically significant for all six models (Wilcoxon signed-rank, all Benjamini-Hochberg-corrected p < 0.001), establishing group-contingent targeting as a robust property of instruction-tuned language models across architectures and training regimes. Compounded through 500 turns of reciprocation, these differentials accumulated into in-group trust biases of +0.014 to +0.100 (d = 0.84-4.52) -- illustrating how modest per-interaction targeting propagates into structural inequality in persistent networks.

</details>


### [209] [CIVIC: End-to-End Sequence Compactness for Efficient Vision-Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28115)
*Fengze Yang,Bo Yu,Xuewen Luo,Cathy Liu,Chenxi Liu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: CIVIC is a compact visual inference framework for vision-language models that maintains reduced visual token sequences throughout the entire pipeline to achieve real hardware efficiency without accuracy loss.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing token reduction methods in VLMs reduce FLOPs in theory but fail to provide proportional speedups or memory savings in practice because pruning happens post-hoc and introduces structural overhead such as non-contiguous memory access and unmerging operations, and naively enforcing compactness can hurt spatial reasoning and localization.

Method: CIVIC designs a path-consistent compact visual representation that keeps a shortened sequence of visual tokens consistently through the vision encoder, projection layer, LLM prefill, and KV-cache. It avoids non-contiguous memory access and localized unmerging, and is trained/regularized using text-aligned KL distillation and an adaptive spatial retention floor to preserve semantic alignment and spatial details.

Result: On the Qwen3-VL architecture, CIVIC converts sequence length reductions into real hardware gains, cutting KV-cache memory to roughly one-third of the baseline and reducing end-to-end inference latency, while maintaining performance on multimodal reasoning and visual grounding benchmarks.

Conclusion: CIVIC provides an effective way to make VLMs more efficient at inference by enforcing compact, path-consistent visual token representations that deliver genuine memory and latency savings without sacrificing accuracy on challenging multimodal tasks.

Abstract: Vision-Language Models (VLMs) face severe memory and latency bottlenecks due to high-resolution visual tokens. While current token reduction methods theoretically save FLOPs, post-hoc pruning introduces structural overhead, failing to yield proportional wall-clock acceleration. However, enforcing a contiguous compact pathway risks geometric disorientation and loss of fine-grained localization.
