To overcome these barriers, this paper introduces CIVIC, a path-consistent compact visual inference framework. By maintaining compact sequence representations seamlessly across the vision encoder, projection layer, LLM prefill, and KV-cache, CIVIC avoids non-contiguous memory access and localized unmerging overheads. Evaluated on the Qwen3-VL architecture, CIVIC successfully translates sequence reductions into genuine physical hardware efficiency, shrinking KV-cache memory to approximately one-third of the baseline and reducing end-to-end inference latency. Enabled by text-aligned KL distillation and an adaptive spatial retention floor, CIVIC achieves these efficiency milestones without degrading accuracy across rigorous multimodal reasoning and visual grounding benchmarks.

</details>


### [210] [Gradient Step Plug-and-Play Model for Dental Cone-Beam CT Reconstruction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28124)
*Idris Tatachak,Luis Kabongo,Nicolas Papadakis,Xavier Ripoche,Simon Rit*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: They develop and test a learning-based prior and plug-and-play reconstruction algorithm to reduce photon noise in dental cone-beam CT images.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Dental cone-beam CT images suffer from photon noise, which degrades image quality and can impair diagnosis. Traditional reconstruction methods and hand‑crafted priors are limited in how well they can suppress this noise while preserving anatomical details. A better, data-driven prior integrated into the reconstruction process can potentially improve noise–detail trade-offs, especially in low-dose or noisy acquisitions.

Method: They cast dental cone-beam CT reconstruction as an inverse problem and design a data-based prior in the form of a learned gradient-step denoiser. First, they simulate fan-beam CT acquisitions and corrupt the projection data with photon (Poisson-like) noise. From these simulated acquisitions, they reconstruct images and use them to train a neural network denoiser that approximates a gradient step associated with the prior term. This trained denoiser is then embedded into a plug-and-play gradient-step iterative reconstruction algorithm, where each iteration alternates between data-fidelity updates and application of the learned prior via the denoiser.

Result: On synthetic (simulated) datasets, the method shows strong denoising performance, indicating that the learned prior effectively reduces photon noise while presumably preserving structures. When applied qualitatively to real cone-beam CT images, the algorithm produces visually improved reconstructions, suggesting that the trained model generalizes reasonably well from simulated to real data.

Conclusion: A data-driven gradient-step denoiser can serve as an effective prior within a plug-and-play CT reconstruction framework for dental cone-beam imaging. The approach reduces photon noise in simulated experiments and qualitatively improves real-image reconstructions, demonstrating promising generalization and indicating that learned priors are a viable alternative to traditional, hand-crafted regularization in dental CT reconstruction.

Abstract: The goal of this work is to reduce the effect of photon noise in dental cone-beam CT reconstruction. We consider an inverse problem formulation and develop a databased prior. To this end, we simulate fan-beam acquisitions and add photon noise to the projection data. The prior is obtained by training a gradient-step denoiser using reconstructed simulated acquisitions. The trained model is integrated into a plug-and-play gradient-step algorithm to reconstruct images from simulated projections. Experiments on synthetic data demonstrate the denoising capabilities of the trained model, while qualitative evaluations on real images showcase the algorithm's performance and generalization ability.

</details>


### [211] [Do Clinical Models Change Treatment Decisions?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28129)
*Dongkyu Cho,Miao Zhang,Rumi Chunara*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: ClinPivot is a benchmark to test whether clinical AI models appropriately change treatment decisions when patient context changes, showing that good medical QA scores don’t guarantee good decision-making, and proposing training strategies that improve both.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing clinical foundation model evaluations focus on static factual or exam-style QA, but real clinical practice requires adapting treatment decisions as patient-specific constraints change. There is a gap between high QA performance and reliable, context-sensitive decision-making, and no standard, auditable way to test whether models correctly update decisions when the clinical action space shifts.

Method: The authors build ClinPivot, a benchmark derived from biomedical relations and systematically modified (pivoted) patient contexts to create scenarios where the correct treatment decision should change. They evaluate multiple foundation models, including frontier models and Qwen variants, on whether they adjust treatment choices under new constraints. They also experiment with decision-structured supervision for training and a lightweight replay mechanism to preserve general assistant abilities while improving pivot-sensitive decision-making.

Result: They find that high performance on medical QA does not reliably translate to strong performance on context-sensitive treatment decisions. Many strong and task-adapted models fail to change decisions correctly when patient constraints change, leading to shifts in model rankings under different evaluation settings. Decision-structured supervision improves both pivot-sensitive decision-making and medical QA when knowledge budgets are matched, and lightweight replay helps retain general assistant capabilities despite such specialized training.

Conclusion: Static medical QA benchmarks are insufficient to assess clinical decision-making quality in AI models. ClinPivot offers an auditable, decision-focused benchmark that exposes a gap between factual knowledge and adaptive treatment reasoning. Training with decision-structured supervision, combined with replay to protect general abilities, can better align model behavior with the demands of real-world, context-dependent clinical care.

Abstract: Clinical foundation models are evaluated with factual or exam-style medical QA, but treatment decisions must change when patient context changes. We introduce ClinPivot, an auditable treatment-decision benchmark built from biomedical relations and pivoted patient contexts. ClinPivot asks whether models change treatment choices when new clinical constraints shift the action space. We find that strong medical QA performance does not reliably predict decision-making performance: frontier models and task-adapted Qwen variants often fail to change decisions correctly, and model rankings shift across evaluation regimes. Decision-structured supervision improves pivot-sensitive decision-making and medical QA under matched knowledge budgets, while lightweight replay reduces losses in general assistant ability.

</details>


### [212] [Data-Efficient On-Policy Distillation for Automatic Speech Recognition](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28139)
*Yu Lin,Yiming Wang,Runyuan Cai,Xiaodong Zeng*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Ark-ASR, a 0.6B-parameter audio-conditioned language model, uses on-policy distillation from a strong Qwen-ASR teacher to significantly improve ASR performance over supervised fine-tuning alone, approaching larger models’ accuracy with far less labeled audio.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: State-of-the-art ASR models typically depend on tens of millions of hours of supervised audio, which makes them expensive to reproduce, hard to specialize, and inaccessible to many practitioners. There is a need for methods that can give compact ASR models strong recognition abilities using much smaller labeled audio budgets. The authors aim to see whether a powerful teacher model can transfer its recognition capabilities to a smaller student through on-policy distillation, improving performance beyond standard supervised fine-tuning while keeping data requirements modest.

Method: The authors study Ark-ASR, a 0.6B-parameter audio-conditioned language model trained on 100k hours of speech. They fine-tune it with an on-policy distillation scheme where the model’s own decoded hypotheses are re-scored or guided by a stronger Qwen-ASR teacher, and the student is trained to better match the teacher’s behavior. They compare this teacher-guided training to pure supervised fine-tuning on the same audio data, and to Qwen3-ASR baselines of similar and larger scales. They also use a support-overlap diagnostic to analyze how well the student’s predictive distribution aligns locally with the teacher’s, to understand when on-policy distillation is effective.

Result: Across Mandarin and English benchmarks, Ark-ASR trained with the proposed on-policy distillation recipe consistently outperforms a variant trained with supervised fine-tuning only. It also beats the same-scale Qwen3-ASR-0.6B baseline on four of five evaluation sets, despite using only 100k hours of labeled speech compared to the roughly 20M hours used for the Qwen3-Omni AuT encoder. While the larger Qwen3-ASR-1.7B model still achieves better overall performance, the gap is substantially reduced for the 0.6B Ark-ASR model under a much smaller data budget. The support-overlap diagnostic indicates improved local compatibility between teacher and student after the teacher-data training stage.

Conclusion: Teacher-guided on-policy distillation is an effective way to enhance compact ASR models using limited supervised audio. By leveraging a strong Qwen-ASR teacher, Ark-ASR with 0.6B parameters can approach the performance of larger models and surpass same-scale baselines while using only a tiny fraction of the labeled data typically required. The analyses suggest that the gains come from improved local agreement between the student and teacher predictive distributions, aligning with recent theory on when on-policy distillation works well. This approach offers a practical path toward more accessible and efficient ASR systems without massive supervision requirements.

Abstract: Building competitive automatic speech recognition (ASR) models usually requires large-scale au- dio supervision, which makes reproduction and specialization expensive. We study Ark-ASR, a 0.6B- parameter audio-conditioned language model trained with 100k hours of speech, and examine whether a strong Qwen-ASR teacher can transfer additional recognition capability through on-policy distillation. Across Mandarin and English ASR benchmarks, the proposed training recipe consistently improves over supervised fine-tuning alone and outperforms the same-scale Qwen3-ASR-0.6B baseline on four of five evaluation sets. This is achieved with only 100k hours of speech, compared with the 20M hours of super- vised audio reported for the Qwen3-Omni AuT encoder. The larger Qwen3-ASR-1.7B remains stronger, but the results show that teacher-guided on-policy training can substantially close the gap for compact ASR models under a much smaller audio budget. A support-overlap diagnostic further suggests that the teacher-data stage improves local student-teacher compatibility, matching recent analyses of when on-policy distillation is effective.

</details>


### [213] [Deconstructing Spatial Complexity: Hierarchical Decomposition for LLM Spatial Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28144)
*Yi Wang,Haojie Lu,Zhaofan Zhang,Li Chen,Sihong Xie*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper improves large language models' spatial reasoning by using hierarchical task decomposition and a new reinforcement learning method guided by Monte Carlo Tree Search, achieving state-of-the-art performance on navigation, planning, and strategic games.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLMs are strong at general language understanding and reasoning but weak at spatial reasoning, which limits their effectiveness in embodied intelligence tasks like navigation and planning. Existing methods fail to derive optimal intermediate states due to insufficient spatial priors, causing sub-optimal task decompositions and poor planning.

Method: The authors introduce a hierarchical task decomposition approach where LLMs break complex spatial tasks into smaller sub-tasks by identifying key intermediate states and generating simplified sub-environments. To overcome LLMs’ poor spatial priors, they propose MCTS-Guided Group Relative Policy Optimization (M-GRPO). This method reformulates the UCT formula to include the LLM's predictive probabilities and its epistemic uncertainty, and uses a more fine-grained advantage function to better learn optimal path planning policies.

Result: Experiments on spatial reasoning tasks—including navigation, planning, and strategic games—show that the proposed method substantially boosts LLM performance, achieving state-of-the-art results compared with prior approaches.

Conclusion: Hierarchical task decomposition combined with the proposed M-GRPO algorithm meaningfully enhances LLM spatial reasoning and planning. This leads to significantly better performance on spatial tasks and suggests that such techniques can enable more capable LLM-based agents for real-world, embodied applications.

Abstract: LLMs have shown remarkable proficiency in general language understanding and reasoning. However, they consistently underperform in spatial reasoning that severely limits their application, particularly in embodied intelligence. Inspired by the success of hierarchical reinforcement learning, this paper introduces a novel method for hierarchical task decomposition in LLM spatial reasoning. Our approach guides LLMs to decompose complex tasks into manageable sub-tasks by identifying key intermediate states and generating simplified sub-environments. However, we identify that LLMs often fail to derive optimal intermediate states due to their insufficient spatial prior, leading to sub-optimal task decomposition. To address this limitation and enhance its planning capability, we propose the MCTS-Guided Group Relative Policy Optimization (M-GRPO), where we reformulate the UCT formula by incorporating the LLM's prior predictive probabilities alongside its epistemic uncertainty. Furthermore, we implement a more fine-grained advantage function, enabling the model to learn optimal path planning. Experimental results demonstrate that our method substantially improves LLM performance on spatial tasks, including navigation, planning, and strategic games, achieving state-of-the-art results. This work paves the way for LLMs in real-world applications.

</details>


### [214] [Adaptive Reservoir Computing for Multi-Scenario Chaotic System Forecasting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28145)
*Shadmehr Zaregarizi,Khashayar Yavari*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Adaptive reservoir computing methods are developed and tuned for the multi-task Lorenz benchmark, showing strong performance across diverse chaotic modeling tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard applications of reservoir computing, such as Echo State Networks, often use a one-size-fits-all training and inference strategy that may not perform well across heterogeneous tasks like forecasting, noise handling, few-shot learning, and parametric generalization, especially on challenging chaotic systems like the Lorenz attractor. The authors aim to show that with tailored adaptations, reservoir computing can be both competitive and efficient on a broad benchmark designed to stress-test machine learning models on chaotic dynamics.

Method: They use Echo State Networks but adapt the training and prediction pipeline to each of the five scenario types in the CTF-4-Science Lorenz benchmark. Their technical contributions include: (1) exact reservoir state synchronization to remove warmup-induced error for short-term prediction; (2) histogram-guided selection among candidate reservoirs or hyperparameters to directly optimize a long-horizon ergodic metric rather than just one-step errors; (3) a multi-seed reservoir search strategy to find robust reservoirs under very limited (few-shot) training data; and (4) a sequential multi-sequence training procedure that exposes the reservoir to multiple parameter settings in sequence, alleviating state-distribution mismatch for parametric generalization tasks.

Result: Using these four adaptations, their reservoir computing framework attains a score of 74.91 on the public leaderboard of the CTF-4-Science Lorenz benchmark, indicating strong overall performance across twelve tasks covering forecasting, denoising, forecasting under noise, few-shot learning, and parametric generalization.

Conclusion: Task-specific adaptation of reservoir computing—rather than a uniform inference scheme—can make Echo State Networks a competitive and computationally efficient solution for a wide variety of chaotic system modeling tasks, as evidenced by their high benchmark score. The framework highlights the importance of aligning training and selection procedures with the evaluation metrics and data regimes of each scenario.

Abstract: We present an adaptive reservoir computing framework for the CTF-4-Science Lorenz benchmark, which evaluates machine learning models across twelve distinct tasks spanning five qualitatively different scenarios: baseline forecasting, noisy signal reconstruction, forecasting under noise, few-shot learning, and parametric generalization. Rather than applying a uniform inference strategy, we tailor the training and prediction procedure of Echo State Networks (ESNs) to the specific demands of each evaluation scenario. Our key contributions are fourfold: (1) exact reservoir state synchronization that eliminates warmup approximation error in short-time prediction; (2) histogram-guided candidate selection that directly optimizes the long-time ergodic evaluation metric; (3) multi-seed reservoir search for few-shot regimes with severely limited training data; and (4) sequential multi-sequence training that resolves state-distribution mismatch in parametric generalization tasks. The proposed framework achieves a score of 74.91 on the public benchmark leaderboard, demonstrating that carefully adapted reservoir computing constitutes a competitive and computationally efficient approach for diverse chaotic system modeling challenges.

</details>


### [215] [OR-Space: A Full-Lifecycle Workspace Benchmark for Industrial Optimization Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28158)
*Chenyu Zhou,Xinyun Lu,Jiangyue Zhao,Jianghao Lin,Dongdong Ge,Yinyu Ye*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Introduces OR-Space, a benchmark that evaluates large language model agents on realistic, multi-stage industrial operations research workflows within persistent multi-file workspaces.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing OR benchmarks mostly test one-shot translation from a clean problem statement into a formulation or solver code, which ignores how OR is actually done in industry: over time, across multiple artifacts, and through iterative model construction, revision, and explanation. There is a need to assess whether LLM agents can handle these realistic, lifecycle-based workflows rather than just simple end-to-end text generation tasks.

Method: Design a benchmark called OR-Space where each instance is an executable workspace containing heterogeneous artifacts such as business documents, structured data, code, solver outputs, and custom evaluators spread across interdependent files. Define three task modes—Build, Revise, and Explain—that simulate different stages of industrial OR workflows: creating new models, updating them under new constraints or feedback while preserving correct logic, and providing grounded explanations using evidence from across the workspace. Establish an evaluation protocol and a quality-control pipeline to ensure benchmark reliability.

Result: A full-lifecycle, workspace-based benchmark (OR-Space) is instantiated, specifying concrete task modes, workspace composition, and evaluation and quality-control procedures. The benchmark can be used to systematically test and compare LLM agents on their ability to perform realistic optimization modeling tasks that go beyond single-shot text-to-model translation.

Conclusion: OR-Space serves as a practical benchmark for examining how reliable LLM agents are in industrial operations research settings, helping identify their failure modes and assess their readiness for real-world optimization work that requires persistent workspaces and multi-stage task handling.

Abstract: Large language model (LLM) agents are increasingly used to assist with operations research (OR) modeling, yet existing OR-oriented benchmarks often reduce evaluation to one-shot translation from a self-contained problem statement into a mathematical formulation or solver program. Such settings abstract away two characteristics of real industrial OR workflows: persistent multi-artifact workspaces and multi-stage task lifecycles. We introduce OR-Space, a full-lifecycle workspace benchmark for evaluating industrial optimization agents across model construction, model revision, and grounded explanation. Each instance is an executable workspace containing business documents, structured data, optional code artifacts, solver outputs, and task-specific evaluators distributed across interdependent files. OR-Space defines three task modes: Build, where agents construct solver-ready optimization models from heterogeneous artifacts; Revise, where agents modify existing models under changing requirements or solver feedback while preserving valid prior logic; and Explain, where agents answer grounded questions about solutions, constraints, and business implications using evidence spread across workspace artifacts. By combining persistent workspaces with lifecycle-oriented tasks, OR-Space evaluates whether agents can perform reliable optimization work beyond end-to-end text generation. We describe the benchmark design, evaluation protocol, and quality-control pipeline, and position OR-Space as a benchmark for studying the reliability, failure modes, and practical readiness of LLM agents in industrial OR workflows.

</details>


### [216] [OccuReward: LLM-Guided Occupant-Centric Reward Shaping for Demographic Equity in Grid-Interactive Buildings](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28168)
*Shadmehr Zaregarizi,Khashayar Yavari*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper introduces OccuReward, a framework that uses large language models to design fairer reward functions for deep reinforcement learning controllers in building energy management, focusing on demographic equity in thermal comfort.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing LLM-generated reward functions for DRL building controllers can improve energy and comfort but may unintentionally favor some demographic groups over others. There is limited work on how reward design affects equity in occupant comfort across heterogeneous demographic profiles. The authors aim to systematically study and mitigate such disparities.

Method: The authors develop OccuReward, which incorporates a Comfort Equity Index (CEI) as a new feedback signal capturing demographic comfort disparities. They use the Gemini LLM to generate and iteratively refine reward function logic and weights over three rounds, targeting equity-aware objectives instead of per-step LLM inference. A Soft Actor-Critic DRL agent is trained in the CityLearn v2 environment using four empirically derived occupant profiles from the ASHRAE Global Thermal Comfort Database II (13,440 votes). The performance and equity impacts of the evolving reward functions are evaluated across 15 runs.

Result: Initial LLM-generated rewards lead to systematically lower thermal satisfaction for elderly female occupants relative to other groups. After three rounds of equity-aware LLM reward refinement that leverage CEI, specific reward components are activated or reweighted. This yields substantial satisfaction gains for all four demographic profiles: Young Males (+17.6%), Mid-aged Females (+28.2%), Health Sensitive (+53.8%), and particularly Elderly Females (+567%). Simultaneously, building energy costs decrease by 3.2%, indicating that improved equity does not require sacrificing energy efficiency.

Conclusion: Reward-level interventions guided by LLMs and equity metrics like CEI can meaningfully reduce demographic disparities in AI-controlled building comfort while also improving energy performance. However, residual inequities remain after refinement, suggesting that reward shaping alone is insufficient and that broader research on fairness-aware algorithms and system design in building energy management is needed.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) have demonstrated promising capability in generating reward functions for deep reinforcement learning (DRL)-based building energy management. However, their potential to exhibit or exacerbate disparities in occupant comfort across heterogeneous demographic populations remains unexplored. We present OccuReward, a framework investigating how LLM-mediated reward design affects demographic equity. Our contribution is three-fold: the introduction of the Comfort Equity Index (CEI) as a novel feedback signal; a methodology for iterative, equity-aware LLM reward shaping; and a performance analysis of DRL agents under these refined objectives. Utilizing four empirically grounded occupant profiles from the ASHRAE Global Thermal Comfort Database II (13,440 votes), we deploy a Soft Actor-Critic agent in CityLearn v2. Our approach employs the Gemini API to generate reward function logic and weights--rather than performing per-step inference--across three refinement rounds. Results across 15 experimental runs reveal that elderly female occupants consistently experience the lowest satisfaction in initial rounds. By Round 3, equity-aware LLM refinement activates specific reward components that improve satisfaction for Young Males (+17.6%), Mid-aged Females (+28.2%), Health Sensitive (+53.8%), and Elderly Females (+567%), while simultaneously reducing energy costs by 3.2%. Our findings highlight that while reward-level intervention significantly improves equity, demographic disparities in AI-driven controllers persist, necessitating further research into algorithmic fairness in building systems.

</details>


### [217] [Localizing Input Uncertainty Quantification for Large Language Models via Shapley Values](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28170)
*Seongjun Lee,Suwan Yoon,Changhee Lee*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper introduces ShaQ, a Shapley-based framework that attributes an LLM’s input-induced uncertainty to specific text spans, enabling more precise ambiguity detection and targeted clarification of user inputs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing uncertainty quantification methods for LLMs mainly output a single scalar score and cannot distinguish between model ignorance and ambiguity in the user input. Recently proposed input-centric methods are still coarse, operating at the whole-input level and failing to show which parts of the input cause uncertainty, limiting their usefulness for clarifying inputs in high-stakes settings.

Method: The authors propose ShaQ, which treats potentially ambiguous spans in the input as players in a cooperative game and uses Shapley values to quantify each span’s contribution to input-induced uncertainty. Formally, they define these contributions via the weighted average of marginal reductions in conditional entropy when clarifying different coalitions of spans, thereby capturing interactions across spans and ensuring that attributions sum to the total input-induced uncertainty.

Result: On the AmbigQA and AmbiEnt benchmarks, ShaQ achieves state-of-the-art results in ambiguity detection. On the MediTOD dataset, it successfully localizes under-specified segments in clinical utterances, demonstrating its effectiveness in real high-stakes, human-AI collaboration scenarios.

Conclusion: ShaQ offers a principled, span-level decomposition of input-induced uncertainty for LLMs, improving the quality of uncertainty estimation and providing actionable, fine-grained guidance on which parts of an input should be clarified to increase reliability, particularly in high-stakes applications.

Abstract: As large language models (LLMs) are increasingly integrated into high-stakes decision-making, the ability to reliably quantify uncertainty has become a critical requirement for safety and trust. However, current uncertainty quantification methods primarily operate at the output level, often failing to distinguish whether uncertainty arises from the model's lack of knowledge or from ambiguity in the user's input. While input-centric uncertainty quantification has recently emerged as a promising direction, it remains relatively underexplored and typically relies on coarse, input-level information. Consequently, users are provided with scalar uncertainty scores that offer little actionable guidance on which parts of the input should be clarified to improve reliability. To address this limitation, we propose Shapley-based input uncertainty Quantification (ShaQ), a framework for span-level attribution of input-induced uncertainty. Our approach models ambiguous spans in the input as players in a cooperative game and quantifies their contributions using Shapley values, defined via the weighted average of marginal reductions in conditional entropy obtained by clarifying each span coalition. Unlike existing input-level approaches, our formulation captures complex interactions among spans and provides a principled decomposition in which individual attributions sum exactly to the total input-induced uncertainty. We evaluate ShaQ on the AmbigQA and AmbiEnt benchmarks, where it achieves state-of-the-art performance in ambiguity detection. We further demonstrate its utility on MediTOD, showing that ShaQ can localize under-specified clinical utterances and facilitate human-AI collaboration in high-stakes settings. Overall, ShaQ improves uncertainty estimation and provides actionable insights for targeted input clarification.

</details>


### [218] [Agentic Active Omni-Modal Perception for Multi-Hop Audio-Visual Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28192)
*Ke Xu,Yuhao Wang,Ziyang Cheng,Hongcheng Liu,Yanfeng Wang,Yu Wang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper introduces MOV-Bench, a benchmark for multi-hop audio-visual reasoning, and AOP-Agent, an agentic framework that significantly improves Omni-LLMs’ multi-hop cross-modal reasoning on long, complex videos without extra training.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Multi-hop reasoning over audio and visual streams in long videos is hard because relevant clues are sparse, scattered over time, and spread across modalities. Existing benchmarks only lightly test this capability, often involving few modalities, short or single segments, or shallow reasoning chains, which makes it hard to diagnose and improve Omni-LLMs’ true multi-hop, cross-modal reasoning ability.

Method: The authors (1) build MOV-Bench, a dataset of 519 questions explicitly designed to require multi-hop reasoning over temporally dispersed audio-visual evidence; and (2) design AOP-Agent, an agentic framework that wraps open-source Omni-LLMs. AOP-Agent uses a hierarchical omni-modal memory to store and organize information from video and audio, and runs a collaborative observe–reflect–replan loop that lets the model actively decide where to look or listen next, iteratively refine its understanding, and update plans for answering the question, all without additional training or proprietary components.

Result: On MOV-Bench, current Omni-LLMs show poor performance, confirming that multi-hop, cross-modal reasoning remains a major weakness. When augmented with AOP-Agent, these open-source Omni-LLMs achieve consistent performance gains on both MOV-Bench and OmniVideoBench, with the largest improvements on long videos and questions that require intensive multi-step reasoning.

Conclusion: MOV-Bench provides a challenging and more realistic testbed for multi-hop audio-visual reasoning, revealing substantial gaps in existing Omni-LLMs. The proposed AOP-Agent framework effectively leverages hierarchical omni-modal memory and iterative agent-like control to enable open-source Omni-LLMs to perform active perception, markedly boosting their reasoning performance on complex, long-horizon video tasks without extra training.

Abstract: Multi-hop audio-visual reasoning remains challenging for Omni-LLMs, as relevant evidence is often sparse, temporally dispersed, and distributed across both audio and visual streams. Existing benchmarks provide limited investigation of this setting, typically involving only a limited number of modalities, relevant temporal segments, or reasoning steps. In this work, we introduce MOV-Bench, a benchmark containing 519 carefully curated questions that require multi-hop reasoning over temporally dispersed audio-visual evidence. Evaluations on MOV-Bench reveal that current Omni-LLMs still struggle with multi-hop cross-modal reasoning. To address this challenge, we further propose AOP-Agent, an efficient agentic framework built on open-source Omni-LLMs for active omni-modal perception. By combining a hierarchical omni-modal memory with a collaborative observe-reflect-replan loop, AOP-Agent enables open-source Omni-LLMs to perform active perception without additional training or proprietary models. Experiments on MOV-Bench and OmniVideoBench demonstrate that AOP-Agent consistently improves reasoning performance, with particularly notable gains on long videos and reasoning-intensive questions.

</details>


### [219] [Plant, Persist, Trigger: Sleeper Attack on Large Language Model Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28201)
*Yongxiang Li,Moxin Li,Zhixin Ma,Fengbin Zhu,Dongrui Liu,Wenjie Wang,Fuli Feng*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Introduces and evaluates 'Sleeper Attacks', where adversarial content persists in an LLM agent's internal state and is triggered in later interactions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Most safety work on LLM agents assumes attacks are immediate and single-turn. But real systems maintain persistent state (session context, memory, skills), creating a risk that adversarial content can lie dormant and trigger harmful behavior later. This longer-horizon, multi-interaction threat is underexplored and harder to detect.

Method: 1) Formalize a new threat model called Sleeper Attack, where injected adversarial content is stored in agent state and later activated by benign queries. 2) Build a benchmark of 1,896 instances spanning six real-world harmful outcomes, three attack strategies, and three state targets (session context, long-term memory, reusable skills/tools). 3) Evaluate seven strong open-source and closed-source LLM agents on this benchmark, comparing vulnerability under Sleeper Attack vs a single-interaction attack baseline.

Result: Across all seven evaluated agents, attack success rates under Sleeper Attacks remain substantial, even for models that appear robust in single-interaction tests. Adversarial content can successfully persist in different types of state, remain dormant, and then induce harmful or incorrect behaviors when later triggered by an otherwise benign request.

Conclusion: LLM agents are vulnerable not only to immediate prompt injection but also to longer-term Sleeper Attacks that exploit persistent state such as context, memory, and skills. Robustness assessments that only consider single-turn attacks underestimate real-world risk. Defenses must explicitly address multi-interaction, stateful threats and harden how agents store, retrieve, and use external information over time.

Abstract: Large Language Model (LLM) agents remain vulnerable to safety threats from the external environment, where attackers inject adversarial content into external observations such as tool-returned data, webpages, or MCP context, causing harmful agentic behaviors such as unsafe actions or incorrect outputs. Existing studies typically focus on single-interaction attacks, where the agent observes adversarial content and immediately exhibits harmful behavior within one user request. However, we show that adversarial content can also persist across interactions served by the same agent, making such threats harder to detect and mitigate. Specifically, adversarial content may persist in the agent state, remain dormant across interactions, and later be activated by a benign user query. We formalize this type of safety threat as Sleeper Attack. To evaluate it, we construct a benchmark with 1,896 instances covering six real-world harmful outcomes, three attack strategies, and three agent state targets: session context, memory, and reusable skills. Experiments on seven strong open-source and closed-source LLMs show that state-of-the-art LLM agents remain vulnerable to Sleeper Attack, even when they achieve low attack success rates under a single-interaction baseline. Our code and data are available at https://anonymous.4open.science/r/skdvnfu23ihr9wdscnksf1asdffsaef.

</details>


### [220] [The Illusion of Opting in AI-Mediated Consequential Decisions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28210)
*Eugene Yu Ji*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper argues that many AI systems create an “illusion of opting”: they make it look like people are making meaningful choices, while actually undermining the capacities needed for real agency. It proposes evaluating AI not just as tools for optimizing given goals, but by how they support people’s higher-order ability to form, revise, and own their goals and choices.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing AI ethics largely treats AI as an instrument for efficiently achieving predefined objectives (e.g., accuracy, fairness across metrics, respect for consent). This overlooks a deeper ethical problem: AI’s potential to shape, channel, and sometimes erode the very capacities individuals and groups need to make authentic, reflective, and revisable choices about their lives. The authors aim to capture this problem using the philosophical notion of “opting” and show that vulnerable or disadvantaged populations are especially at risk when AI systems present pseudo-choices that misdirect their behavior while closing off better alternatives.

Method: Conceptual and normative analysis grounded in Ullmann-Margalit’s theory of “opting.” The authors (1) distinguish genuine opting from situations that only mimic it, (2) analyze how contemporary AI systems (e.g., recommender systems, decision-support tools, automated eligibility systems) can produce an “illusion of opting,” and (3) develop the notion of “meta-capacity” as a socially scaffolded, higher-order agency that AI should protect and cultivate. They then articulate three normative imperatives—existential honesty, ecological rationality, and counterfactual reparation—as criteria for ethically evaluating AI-mediated consequential decisions.

Result: The paper yields a critical reframing of AI ethics: AI systems are not just neutral optimizers of given preferences or goals; they actively structure how people’s goals and choices are formed, perceived, and revised. The key result is the identification of “meta-capacity” as the central value to be protected and of the “illusion of opting” as a distinctive ethical failure mode of AI systems. The authors specify three concrete normative imperatives (existential honesty, ecological rationality, counterfactual reparation) that can guide the design and governance of AI systems to avoid or mitigate this failure mode.

Conclusion: AI ethics must move beyond narrow concerns about optimizing predefined objectives and avoiding standard harms, and instead focus on whether AI systems protect and enhance people’s socially supported capacities to form, contest, and own their ends and means. The authors conclude that: (1) many current AI systems risk creating an illusion of meaningful choice while eroding genuine agency, (2) this is particularly harmful for disadvantaged groups who bear the costs of foreclosed alternatives, and (3) ethical AI design and regulation should institutionalize existential honesty, ecological rationality, and counterfactual reparation as core requirements for AI-mediated consequential decision-making.

Abstract: Drawing on Ullmann-Margalit's concept of opting (transformative, irrevocable, and shadowed by foreclosed alternatives), we show that current AI systems raise a profound ethical problem that existing AI ethics has not fully captured: the illusion of opting, in which persons and groups encounter the deceptive appearance of meaningful consequential choice while the agency needed to become genuinely capable of choosing is weakened. Against approaches that treat AI primarily as an optimizer of already given ends, we argue that AI systems should be evaluated by whether they protect and cultivate meta-capacity against the illusion of opting: the socially and institutionally scaffolded agentive capacity through which means and ends can be formed, contested, revised, and owned. This reframing is especially urgent for disadvantaged populations, who are least able to absorb the costs of the illusion of opting when AI-mediated pathways misdirect behavior and action. We propose three normative imperatives for AI-mediated consequential decisions: existential honesty, which acknowledges the limits of prediction; ecological rationality, which situates guidance within heterogeneous lived ecologies; and counterfactual reparation, which acknowledges and repairs foreclosed alternatives when AI-mediated decision-making pathways fail.

</details>


### [221] [Learning When to Optimize: Verified Optimization Skills from Expert GPU-Kernel Lineages](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28213)
*Shuoming Zhang,Qiuchu Yu,Yangyu Zhang,Ruiyuan Xu,Xiyu Shi,Guangli Li,Xiaobing Feng,Huimin Cui,Jiacheng Zhao*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: KLineage learns when GPU kernel optimizations are valid by extracting reusable optimization skills from expert kernels via backward simplifications, enabling LLMs to generate higher-quality, efficiently optimized kernels.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing LLM-based systems can propose many GPU kernel optimizations but lack reliable knowledge of when these transformations are sound, leading to correctness issues or inefficient search. There is a gap between knowing what optimization patterns exist and knowing under which conditions they are safe and beneficial. The paper aims to close this gap by systematically learning the validity conditions and applicability of optimizations from expert-written kernels.

Method: The authors propose KLineage, a framework that starts from expert GPU kernels and walks them backward through a sequence of validation-gated simplifications. Each accepted simplification step is inverted into an optimization skill. A skill encodes the optimization intent, the code region it applies to, the preconditions for correctness, its performance effect, and the types of failures it avoids. A downstream LLM then applies these learned skills to new GPU code under compile, correctness, and performance (profile) checks, using the skills as an optimization curriculum instead of unconstrained forward rollouts.

Result: On five expert workloads evaluated across two NVIDIA GPU architectures, KLineage’s learned skills guide LLM-based optimization to outperform recent memory-based LLM-kernel baselines in both the final quality of generated kernels and the efficiency of the optimization process under a fixed compute/search budget.

Conclusion: Encoding optimization lineage from expert kernels into explicit, condition-aware skills provides LLM-based agents with the crucial "when" knowledge for GPU kernel optimization. This lineage-driven curriculum leads to more effective and efficient kernel optimization than memory-based approaches, without relying on simple memorization of source cases.

Abstract: LLM-based agents are increasingly used to generate GPU kernels, but they often know what optimizations to try without knowing when those optimizations are sound. We introduce KLineage, which learns this missing "when" knowledge from expert kernels: instead of relying on forward rollouts, KLineage walks expert implementations backward through validation-gated simplifications and reverses each accepted step into a reusable optimization skill. Each skill records not only the optimization intent, but also where it applies in code, what conditions made it valid, what effect it had, and what failures its assumptions avoid. A downstream LLM materializes these skills on new code surfaces under the same compile/correctness/profile gate. On five expert workloads across two NVIDIA architectures, these lineage-derived skills serve as an effective optimization curriculum, exceeding recent memory-based LLM-kernel baselines in both final kernel quality and optimization efficiency under the same fixed budget. We additionally use a separate 22-instance held-out check as a sanity test against source-case memorization.

</details>


### [222] [Explaining is Harder Than Predicting Alone: Evaluating Concept-based Explanations of MLLMs as ICL Visual Classifiers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28215)
*Carmen Quiles-Ramírez,Leticia L. Rodríguez,Nicolás Martorell,Natalia Díaz-Rodríguez*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper evaluates how well multimodal large language models (MLLMs) can provide concept-based, formal explanations for few-shot visual classification, finding that explaining is harder than predicting and that structured explanations often hurt accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: While MLLMs can perform in-context learning for image classification, it is unclear whether they truly use the contextual examples in an interpretable, concept-based way. Chain-of-Thought outputs are common but may not reflect genuine internal reasoning. There is a need to rigorously assess whether MLLMs can provide formal, machine-verifiable explanations tied to visual concepts, rather than just accurate labels.

Method: The authors design a systematic evaluation of concept-based explainability for frozen MLLMs under few-shot in-context learning. They define five explanation conditions with increasing formal rigor, from plain classification to generation of Description Logic (DL) axioms describing class concepts. Four state-of-the-art MLLMs are tested. An independent LLM is used as an automatic judge to assess explanation quality and relate it to prediction correctness across the conditions.

Result: Across the four MLLMs, generating explicit, structured, concept-based explanations consistently reduces classification accuracy compared to baseline prediction-only ICL, decreasing from 93.8% to 90.1% as the explanation demands become more formal. Nonetheless, when a model does manage to produce class-discriminative visual features in its explanation, the judged explanation quality has a strong positive correlation with correct predictions.

Conclusion: MLLMs are strong visual classifiers under in-context learning but struggle when asked for formally structured, concept-based, machine-verifiable explanations. The added requirement to explain, especially in logical or DL-form form, makes the task harder and harms accuracy, indicating that current models lack the necessary instruction-tuning for robust formal explainability. High-quality visual-concept explanations, when produced, align with correctness, suggesting that targeted training for formal reasoning and explanation could improve both transparency and reliability.

Abstract: In-context learning (ICL) enables multimodal large language models (MLLMs) to classify images from a few labelled examples. Yet, how these models use the provided context remains opaque. While Chain-of-Thought prompting is widely used, recent work argues that it may not reflect true internal computation.
  In this paper, we systematically evaluate the concept-based explainability of frozen MLLMs under few-shot ICL using five conditions of increasing formal rigour, ranging from baseline classification to Description Logics (DL) axiom generation.
  Evaluating four state-of-the-art MLLMs via an independent LLM-as-a-judge pipeline, we demonstrate that explaining is genuinely harder than predicting alone. Surprisingly, forcing models to generate formally structured, concept-based explanations degrades predictive accuracy monotonically (from 93.8% to 90.1%), contradicting the assumption that explicit reasoning universally aids performance.
  However, when models successfully articulate class-discriminative visual features, explanation quality strongly correlates with correct predictions. Our findings suggest that while MLLMs excel at visual classification, they lack the specific instruction-tuning required for formal, machine-verifiable explainability.

</details>


### [223] [When Does Memory Help Multi-Trajectory Inference for Tool-Use LLM Agents?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28224)
*Xinzhe Li,Yaguang Tao*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Study of memory mechanisms for multi-trajectory tool-use LLM agents, showing that inference strategy strongly affects the measured benefit of different memory abstractions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Multi-trajectory (multi-sample) inference helps LLM agents by exploring multiple reasoning attempts, but existing methods that transfer information across these attempts use different memory abstractions and are evaluated each with a single inference strategy and single task. This makes it impossible to know whether performance gains are due to the memory design itself or to the particular inference strategy, and hampers principled comparison and design of memory for tool-use agents.

Method: Propose a unified framework that characterizes cross-trajectory memory along two dimensions: (1) scope of transfer (within a single expansion step vs. across full trajectories) and (2) abstraction level of transferred content (raw observations, reflections, atomic facts). Implement four representative memory methods and systematically evaluate them under three different multi-trajectory inference strategies—best-of-N sampling, beam search, and Monte Carlo Tree Search (MCTS)—on four tool-use benchmarks involving SQL querying, knowledge-graph reasoning, and command-line interface (CLI) tasks. All evaluations are done without external verifiers to mimic realistic deployment settings.

Result: The same memory method yields statistically different performance depending on the inference strategy used, making inference a major confounding factor when comparing memory methods. Trajectory-level reflection provides significant benefits only when combined with MCTS, not with best-of-N sampling. Within-expansion injection, where each candidate in a search step conditions on outcomes of sibling candidates, improves performance only for beam search and primarily when diversity is otherwise low. Atomic fact extraction does not significantly change final accuracy but consistently shortens trajectories by about 19–26% on tasks where environmental structure can be reused.

Conclusion: Inference strategy and memory abstraction interact in nontrivial ways for tool-use LLM agents, so evaluating memory methods under a single inference scheme is misleading. A unified evaluation across inference strategies reveals that (1) reflection’s benefits are conditional on tree-search-style inference, (2) within-expansion information sharing mainly helps low-diversity beam search, and (3) atomic fact memories trade accuracy for efficiency by reducing trajectory length without hurting accuracy. Future work on agent memory should control for inference strategy and use such unified frameworks to design and compare memory mechanisms more reliably.

Abstract: Multi-trajectory inference for tool-use LLM agents - generating multiple reasoning attempts and selecting among them - benefits from transferring knowledge across attempts so that later ones avoid the pitfalls of earlier ones. Existing cross-trajectory memory methods (trajectory-level reflection, atomic fact extraction, raw observation injection) are each evaluated under a single inference strategy on a single task, making it unclear whether reported gains reflect properties of the memory abstraction or of the inference method. We propose a unified framework that decomposes memory along two axes -- the scope of transfer (within an expansion vs. across trajectories) and the abstraction of the transferred content -- and evaluate four methods under three inference strategies (best-of-N, beam search, MCTS) on four tool-use benchmarks spanning SQL, knowledge-graph, and CLI environments, in a verifier-free setting that matches the deployment regime of practical agents. The experiment matrix identifies the inference method as a confound: the same memory method produces statistically distinct results under different inference strategies on the same examples. Reflection reaches significance only under MCTS (not under best-of-N); within-expansion injection (conditioning each candidate on prior siblings' outcomes) helps only diversity-starved beam search; and atomic fact extraction is accuracy-neutral but shortens trajectories by 19-26% on tasks with reusable environmental structure.

</details>


### [224] [PIRS: Physics-Informed Reward Shaping for SAC-Based Building Energy Management](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28232)
*Shadmehr Zaregarizi,Khashayar Yavari*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: They propose a physics-informed way (using PMV from ISO 7730) to design the comfort part of the reward in DRL building controllers, achieving interpretable, standards-based comfort while keeping energy-grid KPIs competitive with a strong manually tuned baseline and better than naive rewards.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing DRL for building control must balance occupant comfort and grid-aware energy efficiency, but the reward functions used are mostly ad hoc. Comfort is usually represented by simple temperature deviations or heuristics that are not grounded in established thermal comfort physics. This lack of principled reward design hurts interpretability, generality, and possibly performance. The authors are motivated to embed a standardized, physics-based comfort model directly into the reward to make DRL control more interpretable, standards-aligned, and robust, without reengineering the rest of the learning pipeline.

Method: They introduce PIRS (Physics-Informed Reward Shaping), which incorporates the ISO 7730 Predicted Mean Vote (PMV) thermal comfort model as the comfort component inside a weighted multi-objective reward for the Soft Actor-Critic (SAC) algorithm. The rest of the DRL pipeline is unchanged; only the comfort proxy in the reward is replaced with the PMV-based term. They then evaluate PIRS in the CityLearn v2.1.2 environment (2022 challenge phase 1), training a central SAC agent for 50k steps over five random seeds. PIRS is compared against (1) a rule-based controller (RBC), (2) a manually engineered reward (E2), (3) an energy-only reward (E3), and (4) a naive temperature-deviation comfort reward (E4), using district-level KPIs normalized to the RBC baseline.

Result: PIRS achieves grid and energy KPIs (cost, carbon, electricity) comparable to the strong manually engineered reward baseline (E2), while clearly outperforming non-physics-grounded reward designs such as energy-only and naive temperature deviation rewards. The gains are particularly pronounced in load ramping (PIRS reaches 1.78x the RBC value, versus approximately 2.4x for other DRL variants) and in reducing daily peak demand. However, at the given training budget (50k steps), all DRL policies, including PIRS, still perform worse than the RBC baseline on aggregate, indicating that the DRL methods have not yet surpassed classical control under limited compute.

Conclusion: Embedding the ISO 7730 PMV model into the DRL reward (PIRS) yields a physics-informed, standards-based, and more interpretable way to encode occupant comfort in building control, without modifying the underlying learning algorithm. This design matches the performance of a carefully hand-tuned manual reward and clearly beats simpler, non-physics-based rewards on important grid KPIs like load ramping and peak demand. The authors stress that, given the limited training, DRL does not yet outperform a rule-based controller, and they therefore present PIRS as a robust, interpretable foundation for reward design in future work rather than a claim that it already beats classical controllers.

Abstract: Occupant comfort and grid-aware energy efficiency are competing objectives whose joint optimization depends critically on how reward functions are specified in deep reinforcement learning (DRL) controllers for buildings. Yet reward design remains largely ad hoc: comfort terms are either hand-tuned heuristics or simple temperature-deviation proxies without explicit grounding in thermal-comfort physics. We present PIRS (Physics-Informed Reward Shaping), which replaces these ad-hoc comfort proxies with the ISO 7730 Predicted Mean Vote (PMV) formulation inside a weighted multi-objective reward for Soft Actor-Critic (SAC). By anchoring the comfort signal in the ISO 7730 PMV formulation, PIRS improves reward interpretability and provides a standards-grounded comfort proxy without changing any other component of the learning pipeline. We evaluate PIRS in CityLearn v2.1.2 (challenge 2022 phase 1) with a central SAC agent trained for 50k steps over five random seeds, and compare against a rule-based controller (RBC), a manually engineered reward (E2), an energy-only reward (E3), and a naive temperature-deviation comfort reward (E4). District-level key performance indicators (KPIs), reported as ratios versus RBC, show that PIRS attains cost, carbon, and electricity metrics on par with the manual baseline while substantially outperforming non-physics-grounded designs -- particularly on load ramping (1.78x vs. ~2.4x RBC) and daily peak demand. All DRL policies remain above RBC at this training budget; we interpret this gap honestly and position PIRS as an interpretable, standards-aligned foundation for reward design rather than a claim of dominance over classical control at limited compute.

</details>


### [225] [AI, Take the Wheel: What Drives Delegation and Trust in Human-Computer Cooperative Question Answering?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28255)
*Maharshi Gor,Yoo Yeon Sung,Yu Hou,Eve Fleisig,Irene Ying,Tianyi Zhou,Jordan Boyd-Graber*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper studies how humans decide when to rely on AI, separating delegation (letting AI act autonomously) and adoption (using AI suggestions), using a realistic question-answering game with expert humans and AI agents.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: AI systems are imperfect, and humans can err in deciding whether to trust AI, impacting the effectiveness of human-AI collaboration. Prior work often examines reliance in simplified or isolated ways, not considering the distinct but interrelated decisions of delegating to AI without seeing its output and adopting or rejecting visible AI suggestions within the same realistic task setting.

Method: The authors design a competitive question-answering game where expert human players team up with AI agents. In this setup, human participants can choose when to delegate questions to the AI (without seeing its answer first) and when to view and possibly adopt AI suggestions. They run 24 matches involving 23 human experts and 16 AI agents, logging 387 delegation decisions and 1440 adoption decisions. They then analyze patterns of over- and under-reliance, model confidence, and human cognitive biases such as confirmation bias.

Result: Human-AI teams outperform either humans or AIs alone, but humans still make systematically suboptimal reliance decisions. They miss 3.9% of chances to correctly use AI help (under-reliance) and incorrectly follow AI in 1.7% of cases when the AI is wrong (over-reliance). When humans and AI disagree, the AI’s reported confidence is only slightly better than chance as a reliability signal. When an AI suggestion matches a human’s initial incorrect answer, confirmation bias significantly increases under-reliance, with 64.5% of such opportunities resulting in humans sticking with the wrong answer.

Conclusion: Effective human-AI collaboration is limited not just by AI accuracy but by humans’ nuanced reliance decisions, especially delegation vs. adoption. Both humans and AI contribute errors, and current confidence signals are insufficient when there is disagreement. To improve collaboration, AI systems should offer better-calibrated confidence estimates, explanations grounded in evidence, and interaction mechanisms that support users in appropriately calibrating and refining their trust over time.

Abstract: AI systems are fallible, and humans can make mistakes in deciding whether to trust AI over their own judgment. Thus, improving human-AI collaboration requires understanding when, why, and how humans decide to rely on AI. We study two distinct reliance decisions: the delegation choice -- deciding when to let AI act autonomously without knowing its output, and the adoption choice -- evaluating AI suggestions and deciding how to use them. Both of these decoupled reliance patterns shape collaboration, but prior work rarely studies them together in realistic settings with the same users. We address this gap by studying collaborative human--AI teams competing in a question-answering game in which humans can choose when and how to work with AI agents to win. Our 24 matches pair 23 expert humans with 16 AI agents, capturing 387 delegation and 1440 adoption decisions. While human--AI collaboration performs better than either AI or humans alone, humans make suboptimal collaboration decisions, both under-relying on correct AI suggestions (3.9% of opportunities missed) and over-relying when AI misleads them (1.7%). Both parties contribute wrong answers: reported model confidence is near chance when humans and AI disagree, while confirmation bias drives higher under-reliance (64.5%) when an AI suggestion agrees with humans' initial incorrect answer. To close this gap, we recommend calibrated confidence, evidence-grounded explanations, and mechanisms that help users refine trust.

</details>


### [226] [Entropy Distribution as a Fingerprint for Hallucinations in Generative Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28264)
*Mattia J. Villani,Pranav Deshpande,Akshay Seshadri,Romina Yalovetzky,Niraj Kumar*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper proposes a lightweight, single-pass, black-box hallucination detector for LLMs based on the full distribution of token-level entropy, not just its mean.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLMs frequently hallucinate, producing factually incorrect outputs that compromise trust, especially in high-stakes applications. Existing hallucination detection methods often require multiple model runs or internal access, making them computationally expensive or impractical for black-box/API models. There is a need for an efficient, theoretically grounded, single-pass, black-box method with formal guarantees.

Method: The authors analyze the distribution of token-level entropies in LLM generations and show that its full shape and tails contain information about hallucinations beyond average entropy or perplexity. They cast hallucination detection as a statistical hypothesis testing problem and introduce the Calibrated Entropy Score (CES). CES uses a calibrated reference cumulative distribution function over entropy statistics, combining both the mean entropy and the maximum entropy across tokens, computed from a single forward pass using only token logits. They derive a new random-length Dvoretzky–Kiefer–Wolfowitz inequality to obtain finite-sample calibration guarantees and prove exponential consistency of CES as sequence length grows.

Result: Across eight QA benchmarks and ten different LLMs (including open-source and API models), CES attains the best hallucination detection performance among all single-pass, black-box methods. It achieves detection accuracy statistically indistinguishable from more expensive, multi-sample approaches, yet at much lower computational cost. The method produces scores that are comparable across models and tasks and comes with formal error guarantees.

Conclusion: The distribution of token-level entropies functions as a reliable fingerprint of hallucinations in LLM outputs. CES operationalizes this insight into a practical, single-pass, black-box detection algorithm with strong theoretical guarantees and state-of-the-art performance, effectively closing the performance gap between lightweight and computationally heavy hallucination detection methods and enabling real-time, large-scale deployment.

Abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) often generate factually incorrect outputs, commonly termed hallucinations, that undermine trust and limit deployment in high-stakes settings. Existing hallucination detection methods typically require multiple forward passes, or access to model internals. In this work, we provide theoretical background and empirical evidence that the distribution of token-level entropies, beyond the mean captured by perplexity or length-normalised entropy, serves as a fingerprint of hallucination, with distributional shape and tail behaviour carrying independent signal. We formalize hallucination detection as a statistical hypothesis test and propose the Calibrated Entropy Score (CES), a lightweight algorithm requiring only a single forward pass and black-box access to token logits. CES combines the mean signal with the maximum signal of the generated entropy through a calibrated reference CDF, producing scores that are directly comparable across models and tasks. We establish finite-sample calibration guarantees via a novel random-length Dvoretzky--Kiefer--Wolfowitz inequality, and also prove that CES detects hallucinations with probability converging to one exponentially fast in the generation length. Across eight QA benchmarks and ten generator models spanning open-source and API access models, CES achieves the highest detection performance among all single-pass black-box methods while providing formal error guarantees that existing heuristics lack. Remarkably, CES is statistically indistinguishable from multi-sample methods that require far greater computational cost, closing the gap between lightweight and expensive detection and making it suitable for real-time, large-scale deployment.

</details>


### [227] [Global Policy-Space Response Oracles for Two-Player Zero-Sum Games](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28273)
*Junyu Zhang,Feihong Yang,Jian Wang,Chao Wang,Xudong Zhang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: They improve the PSRO framework for solving large two-player zero-sum games by guiding strategy population expansion using a global measure called Population Exploitability, leading to better Nash approximation with fewer iterations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard PSRO methods expand the strategy population using best responses to meta-strategies derived from the restricted game, which can be inefficient. These local, myopic expansions may not significantly improve how well the restricted game approximates the full game under limited computational budgets. There is a need for a principled way to choose new strategies that directly improves the overall quality of the restricted game representation.

Method: They define Population Exploitability (PE) as a metric of how well a restricted strategy set represents the full game, i.e., how exploitable that population is in the true game. They then build a two-phase exploration–selection framework that explicitly aims to minimize PE during population expansion. Concretely, they propose Global PSRO, a DRL-based algorithm in which: (1) candidate strategies (policies) are generated via deep reinforcement learning; (2) PE of candidate-augmented populations is estimated using parameter-sharing conditional neural networks, enabling efficient evaluation; and (3) strategies are selected for inclusion based on their impact on PE.

Result: Across several two-player zero-sum benchmark games, Global PSRO yields strategy populations with lower exploitability than prior PSRO variants. It reaches approximate Nash equilibria with significantly fewer PSRO policy-iteration steps, demonstrating improved sample and computation efficiency. The experiments confirm that PE-guided, global population expansion outperforms local best-response-based expansion in practice.

Conclusion: Evaluating and minimizing Population Exploitability during population expansion provides a more globally informed and computationally efficient way to approximate Nash equilibria in large zero-sum games. The proposed Global PSRO algorithm operationalizes this idea using DRL and conditional neural networks, outperforming existing PSRO methods in exploitability and convergence speed.

Abstract: The Policy-Space Response Oracles (PSRO) framework scales equilibrium computation to large zero-sum games by iteratively expanding a restricted strategy set using deep reinforcement learning (DRL). A central challenge is to construct, under limited computational budgets, a small strategy population whose induced game well approximates the full game. Existing PSRO variants typically expand the population using best responses to meta-strategies computed from restricted-game payoffs, which can lead to inefficient expansions that provide limited global improvement. We propose to guide population expansion by directly evaluating the post-expansion population quality. Specifically, we adopt Population Exploitability (PE) to measure how well a restricted strategy set represents the full game, and introduce a two-phase exploration--selection framework that explicitly minimizes PE during expansion. We instantiate this framework as Global PSRO, a practical DRL-based algorithm that efficiently generates candidate responses and estimates PE via parameter-sharing conditional neural networks. Experiments across multiple two-player zero-sum games show that Global PSRO achieves lower exploitability and approximates Nash equilibria with significantly fewer policy iterations than prior PSRO methods.

</details>


### [228] [Do LLMs Build World Models From Text? A Multilingual Diagnostic of Spatial Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28277)
*Zhikai Pan,Chih-Ting Liao,Chunrui Liu,Xi Xiao,Yitong Qiao,Chunlei Meng,Zhangquan Chen,Xin Cao*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: MentalMap is a multilingual benchmark to test whether LLMs (and humans) can build spatial world models from text, revealing a sharp breakdown in viewpoint reasoning despite good performance on basic spatial facts.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To resolve debate over whether LLMs truly form internal spatial world models from text, and to investigate if such abilities are consistent across languages. Prior work lacked a systematic, multilingual, and hierarchically-structured diagnostic for spatial reasoning from pure text.

Method: The authors create MentalMap, a benchmark built from 100 ProcTHOR household scenes, spanning eight typologically diverse languages plus a structured-text control. The benchmark defines six capability levels (L0–L5) from simple spatial facts to full generative world-graph construction, with 39 task families and 1,950 evaluation cells. It also includes four diagnostic axes: frame of reference, reading-direction bias, reasoning-effort allocation, and hallucination. Thirteen LLMs of varying scales and architectures are evaluated across languages and prompting styles; humans are evaluated under the same pure-text protocol for comparison.

Result: Across all models and languages, performance shows a consistent “L3 reasoning cliff”: once a model’s accuracy on atomic spatial facts (L0) exceeds about 40%, its performance on viewpoint-dependent reasoning (L3) falls to less than half of its L0 score. This failure is robust to model family, scale, language, and prompting strategy. While models differ in structured-output failures and qualitative reasoning patterns, they all share this core limitation. Human subjects tested with the same purely textual setup exhibit a similar failure pattern.

Conclusion: The study concludes that both LLMs and humans face a fundamental bottleneck in pure-text spatial reasoning, likely due to working memory constraints rather than specific architectural issues in current LLMs. Spatial reasoning from text should therefore be framed as a multi-axis world-modeling task, and future work should explore multimodal inputs and external or scratchpad-based reasoning tools to overcome these limitations.

Abstract: Whether large language models (LLMs) construct internal spatial world models from pure-text descriptions remains contested, and whether such capabilities transfer across languages has not been systematically studied. We introduce MentalMap, a multilingual diagnostic benchmark with a six-level capability hierarchy (L0-L5) spanning atomic spatial facts to generative world-graph construction, together with four diagnostic axes probing frame of reference, reading-direction bias, reasoning-effort allocation, and hallucination. MentalMap is built from 100 ProcTHOR household scenes, covers eight typologically diverse languages plus a structured-text control, and contains 39 task families across 1,950 evaluation cells. Evaluating thirteen LLMs across scales and model families, we identify a universal L3 reasoning cliff: no model retains even half of its L0 performance on viewpoint reasoning once baseline atomic accuracy exceeds 40%. The cliff persists across languages, scales, and prompting strategies, while structured-output failures and reasoning patterns vary substantially across models. Human evaluation under the identical pure-text protocol reproduces the same failure pattern, suggesting that the bottleneck arises from text-only working memory constraints rather than being specific to current LLM architectures. Our findings reframe pure-text spatial reasoning as a multi-axis world-modeling problem and motivate multimodal and scratchpad-augmented reasoning as future directions.

</details>


### [229] [ResearchLoop: An Evidence-Gated Control Plane for AI-Assisted Research](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28282)
*Yihan Xia,Taotao Wang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper introduces ResearchLoop, a system that structures AI-assisted computational research as an evidence-gated, repository-backed workflow to reduce the risk of unverifiable claims.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: AI can speed up the entire research lifecycle, but this compression makes it easier to state claims than to properly audit and verify them, creating a publication and reliability risk. The authors aim to make AI-assisted research more trustworthy and auditable.

Method: They design ResearchLoop as a control plane that models research artifacts—questions, task contracts, evidence objects, claim ledgers, closeouts, and paper bindings—as durable project state in a repository-backed runtime. The work specifies the protocol, state model, transition rules, claim-admission algorithm, and an insight-compounding mechanism, and implements it in a system that is then empirically evaluated.

Result: They report a full experimental record across nine system versions (V0–V9), including: (1) a self-hosting case study where the system is used to develop itself, (2) a controlled task-suite study with component ablations, (3) a mathematical olympiad-style evaluation, and (4) a SciCode boundary experiment using the official generated-code evaluation harness. All experimental artifacts and verification reports are stored in the project repository.

Conclusion: ResearchLoop demonstrates that AI-assisted research can be structured as an evidence-gated, versioned workflow that preserves all artifacts and verification steps, helping ensure that claims in AI-generated or AI-assisted papers remain auditable and trustworthy.

Abstract: AI-assisted research compresses ideation, implementation, evaluation, and manuscript writing into a single interactive loop. This compression is useful, but it also creates a publication risk: paper claims can become easier to state than to audit. We present ResearchLoop, an evidence-gated control plane for AI-assisted computational research. ResearchLoop treats research questions, task contracts, evidence objects, claim ledgers, closeouts, and paper bindings as durable project state, realized here as a repository-backed runtime. This technical report provides the complete protocol specification, state model, transition rules, claim-admission algorithm, and insight-compounding mechanism. It also reports the full experimental record spanning nine versions (V0--V9), including a self-hosting case study, a controlled task-suite study with component ablations, a mathematical olympiad evaluation, and a supplementary SciCode boundary experiment evaluated with the official generated-code harness. All artifacts, manifests, and verification reports are preserved in the project repository.

</details>


### [230] [Where Rollouts Begin: Low-Load, High-Leverage First-Token Diversification for RLVR](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28295)
*Soeun Kim,Albert No*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: They propose REFT, a simple modification to RL with Verifiable Rewards that diversifies only the first token after the reasoning marker, improving code-reasoning performance without changing the verifier or other training components.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: In RL with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) for reasoning models, performance is limited by insufficient rollout diversity. Existing methods modify temperatures, prefixes, or rollout selection, but they overlook a structurally important position: the first token after the reasoning marker, whose distribution is overly peaked and not aligned with correctness. Exploiting this position could widen exploration regions while preserving the reward signal.

Method: They analyze the first-token distribution after the reasoning marker and find it sharply peaked yet weakly correlated with correctness. Based on this, they propose REFT (Rollout Exploration with First-Token Diversification): during RLVR rollouts, sample the first token uniformly from the model's top-N candidates instead of from the raw softmax distribution, then allocate rollouts evenly across these choices. All other parts of the RLVR pipeline (verifier, training objective, subsequent token sampling) remain unchanged. This targeted diversification should expand the coverage of reasoning trajectories without distorting the verification signal.

Result: Across four base model sizes (0.5B–7B) and three problem difficulty levels, applying REFT in RLVR training yields consistent improvements in Pass@1, Pass@8, and Pass@64 over strong RLVR baselines DAPO and GRPO, showing better reasoning and code-generation performance from the same verifier signal and similar compute.
