Conclusion: Controlling diversity at the structurally key first-token position after the reasoning marker is an effective and lightweight way to overcome the rollout diversity bottleneck in RLVR. REFT can be dropped into existing RLVR pipelines, offering improved performance across models and difficulties without redesigning verifiers or sampling strategies for the rest of the sequence.

Abstract: Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) trains reasoning models without labeled trajectories, relying on grouped rollouts to expose the policy to alternative reasoning paths and a verifier to score them. Rollout diversity has accordingly emerged as a central bottleneck in RLVR, with most existing methods broadening exploration through temperature, prefix, or rollout-selection adjustments. We identify a structurally distinguished but overlooked position for broadening this diversity: the first token after the reasoning marker. The policy's first-token distribution exhibits a sharply peaked yet correctness-decoupled phenomenon, and this first token position can broaden the regions a rollout group covers without altering the correctness signal. We introduce REFT (Rollout Exploration with First-Token Diversification), a light addition to the RLVR pipeline that samples first tokens uniformly from the policy's own top-$N$ candidates and allocates rollouts evenly, leaving every other component unchanged. Trained on the resulting diversified rollouts, REFT improves aggregate Pass@1, Pass@8, and Pass@64 over DAPO and GRPO baselines across four base models (0.5B-7B) and three difficulty regimes.

</details>


### [231] [REED: Post-Training Representation Editing for Cross-Domain Linguistic Steganalysis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28298)
*Ruohan Lei,Jianxin Gao,Wanli Peng,Huimin Pei*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: They propose a representation-editing post-training method to improve cross-domain linguistic steganalysis without changing model architecture or updating parameters.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing linguistic steganalysis models perform poorly when applied to unseen domains with different vocabularies, topics, styles, and steganographic patterns. Cross-domain methods based on distribution alignment and domain-invariant features help, but performance remains unsatisfactory, motivating a more effective yet lightweight approach.

Method: Train a steganalysis detector on source-domain data as usual. Then freeze both the feature extractor and classifier. Before classification on new-domain data, deterministically edit intermediate representations. For domain adaptation, construct a domain-offset vector using marginal source and target representations and apply this offset to features. For domain generalization (no access to target data), compute a source-domain cover-to-stego direction in representation space and use it to guide sample-specific feature editing.

Result: Experiments show that this post-training representation editing significantly improves cross-domain detection, with notably higher F1-scores than advanced baselines, while keeping the model architecture unchanged and avoiding any further parameter updates after source training.

Conclusion: Representation editing at the intermediate feature level is an effective, architecture-agnostic way to boost cross-domain linguistic steganalysis. It offers strong F1-score gains in unseen domains and is practical because it works post-training without finetuning or modifying the detector.

Abstract: In real-world scenarios of linguistic steganalysis, tested texts usually come from unseen domains with different vocabularies, topics, writing styles, and steganographic generation patterns, which can significantly degrade the detection performance. Although existing cross-domain steganalysis methods can effectively alleviate this problem through distribution alignment, domain-invariant feature learning, etc., the detection performance is not satisfactory. In this paper, we propose a post-training representation editing method for cross-domain linguistic steganalysis. Specifically, the detector is first trained on source-domain data, and then the feature extractor and classifier are kept frozen, and the intermediate representations are deterministically edited before classification. For domain adaptation, we construct a domain-offset vector from marginal source and target representations. For domain generalization, we derive a source-domain cover-to-stego direction to guide sample-specific editing. Experimental results show that compared with the advanced methods, the proposed method can achieve high cross-domain detection performance, especially in terms of F1-score, while requiring no architecture modification or parameter updates after source-domain training.

</details>


### [232] [From Fact Overwriting to Knowledge Evolution: Causal Editing via On-Policy Self-Distillation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28303)
*Shuaike Li,Kai Zhang,Xianquan Wang,Jiachen Liu,Shengpeng Mo*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper identifies a core flaw in standard knowledge editing for LLMs—static fact overwriting—and proposes a causal, narrative-based editing framework (CODE) that greatly reduces self-refutation while improving multi-hop reasoning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing knowledge editing methods treat LLMs like key–value stores, overwriting isolated facts. This disrupts the model’s internal logical structure and causes "Epistemic Dissonance," where the model tends to deny or contradict the newly injected facts. The authors find this is not just implementation noise but a structural issue, motivating a shift from static fact updates to causally grounded knowledge evolution.

Method: 1) The authors theoretically and empirically analyze "Epistemic Dissonance" by probing how LLMs respond to edited facts, including using an idealized zero-distortion proxy to show inherent instability. 2) They introduce the Causal Editing paradigm: updates are embedded in explicit causal narratives rather than standalone facts. 3) They propose CODE (Causal On-policy Distillation for Editing), which combines causal bootstrapping—constructing causal transition narratives around the new fact—with asymmetric on-policy distillation to engrave the new causal structure into the model’s parameters. 4) They evaluate CODE on LLaMA-3.1 and Qwen-2.5 with metrics focused on self-refutation and multi-hop reasoning accuracy.

Result: Under static fact overwriting, even a zero-distortion proxy exhibits 95.6% self-refutation, revealing severe epistemic dissonance. Grounding edits in explicit causal narratives reduces this conflict to 6.6%. With the full CODE method, self-refutation drops further to 1.8% while multi-hop reasoning accuracy reaches up to 83.5% on tested benchmarks for LLaMA-3.1 and Qwen-2.5, demonstrating both stability and improved reasoning over chains of implications from the edited knowledge.

Conclusion: Static fact overwriting is structurally incompatible with how LLMs represent knowledge, leading to persistent self-refutation and instability. Embedding edits in causal narratives—operationalized by the CODE algorithm—transforms discrete fact injection into coherent knowledge evolution, drastically reducing epistemic dissonance and improving multi-hop reasoning. The authors argue that future knowledge editing for LLMs should adopt a causal, narrative-based paradigm rather than treating models as simple databases.

Abstract: While Knowledge Editing (KE) enables efficient updates, its dominant Static Fact Overwriting paradigm treats LLMs as discrete databases, forcibly injecting isolated facts. Fracturing pre-trained logical topologies, this triggers Epistemic Dissonance -- a pathology where un-evolved legacy priors force the model to explicitly negate the injected update. Idealized interventions reveal that this is an inherent structural flaw rather than mere algorithmic noise, with a zero-distortion proxy yielding a catastrophic 95.6% self-refutation rate. Given the causally driven nature of real-world knowledge, grounding updates in explicit causal narratives effectively collapses this conflict rate to just 6.6%, underscoring the imperative for a paradigm shift toward Causal Editing. To internalize this evolution, we propose CODE (Causal On-policy Distillation for Editing). By coupling causal bootstrapping with asymmetric on-policy distillation, CODE engraves causal transition logic directly into parametric memory. Experiments on LLaMA-3.1 and Qwen-2.5 show CODE drastically suppresses self-refutation to 1.8% while securing robust multi-hop accuracy (up to 83.5%), seamlessly transforming discrete fact injection into coherent knowledge evolution. Code is available at https://github.com/CrashBugger/CODE.

</details>


### [233] [An Enhanced Large Neighborhood Search Approach for the Capacitated Facility Location Problem with Incompatible Customers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28337)
*Ida Gjergji,Lucas Kletzander,Nysret Musliu,Andrea Schaerf*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper introduces and solves a variant of the capacitated facility location problem that includes customer incompatibilities, using a Large Neighborhood Search metaheuristic that outperforms existing methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Classical capacitated facility location models ignore the fact that certain customers cannot be served from the same facility (e.g., due to hazardous materials or conflicts between competitors). Real-world applications need to model such incompatibilities explicitly, which leads to a more complex and challenging optimization problem with practical importance.

Method: The authors design a Large Neighborhood Search (LNS) metaheuristic tailored to the facility location problem with customer incompatibilities. In the LNS framework, they propose three different destroy operators, combined in a hybrid fashion, and use an exact optimization solver in the repair phase. They systematically investigate several algorithmic components for calibrating and improving the LNS method.

Result: Computational experiments on benchmark instances show that the proposed LNS algorithm outperforms state-of-the-art metaheuristics for this problem. It achieves better solution quality and is able to generate new best-known solutions for all available benchmark instances.

Conclusion: Incorporating customer incompatibilities into capacitated facility location is practically important and computationally difficult, but the proposed LNS approach effectively handles this added complexity. The method advances the state of the art, both by outperforming existing algorithms and by providing new best solutions, indicating that LNS with hybrid destroy operators and exact repair is a powerful strategy for this class of problems.

Abstract: A new variant of the classic capacitated facility location problem, which considers incompatibilities between customers, has recently been introduced in the literature. This problem captures the situation where given pairs of customers cannot be served by the same facility. Such a feature is crucial for many practical cases of location problems, such as the presence of hazardous or polluting materials and contention between competing costumers. In this paper, we propose a Large Neighborhood Search (LNS) method to solve this problem. Within the framework of LNS, we introduce three different destroy operators, which are combined in a hybrid manner, and we use an exact solver in the repair phase. Different algorithmic components are investigated for the design of LNS. The experimental analysis shows that our new method outperforms existing state-of-the-art metaheuristics, providing new best solutions for all available benchmark instances.

</details>


### [234] [SafeMed-R1: Clinician-Audited Safety and Ethics Alignment for Medical Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28338)
*Chao Ding,Mouxiao Bian,Tianbin Li,Minjia Yuan,Yidong Jiang,Yankai Jiang,Jinru Ding,Jiayuan Chen,Zhuangzhi Gao,Pengcheng Chen,Zhao He,Rongzhao Zhang,Meiling Liu,Luyi Jiang,Jie Xu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper introduces SafeMed-R1, a clinically oriented LLM with auditable reasoning traces and specialized safety/ethics alignment, achieving strong accuracy and lower safety risk than baselines, comparable to early residents on medication tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Although LLMs can match expert-level performance on medical exams, real-world clinical deployment is constrained by requirements for transparent reasoning, robust safety and ethics alignment, and resistance to adversarial misuse. Existing models often lack traceable supervision provenance and do not provide governance-grade evidence that their reasoning is reliable and auditable in clinical contexts.

Method: The authors develop SafeMed-R1 using a Clinical Trust Signals (CTS) training pipeline that ties each reasoning step to clinician-provided rubric scores and edit histories, effectively making the reasoning traceable and auditable. They additionally align the model using safety and ethics supervision and conduct red-team stress testing to harden it against adversarial prompts. Performance is evaluated across multiple clinical benchmarks, adversarial safety tests, and an expert paired study using medication safety vignettes, comparing SafeMed-R1 with baseline models and human residents (PGY1/PGY2).

Result: SafeMed-R1 achieves a macro-averaged accuracy of 79.6% across diverse clinical benchmarks. In adversarial safety evaluations, it shows the lowest aggregated risk among compared systems and reduces unsafe outputs by approximately 3–5% relative to its baseline. In a paired expert evaluation on 30 medication safety vignettes, its medical correctness matches PGY1/PGY2 residents while outperforming them on medication safety, adherence to clinical guidelines, and perceived clinical usefulness.

Conclusion: Clinician-audited supervision provenance, implemented via the CTS pipeline, combined with domain-specific safety and ethics alignment, can produce a medical LLM with improved governance-relevant properties. Such a model can deliver competitive clinical reasoning performance and enhanced safety without relying on inference-time retrieval or citation-based grounding, suggesting a pathway toward more trustworthy clinical deployment of LLMs.

Abstract: Large language models(LLMs) increasingly match expert performance on licensing examinations, yet routine clinical use remains limited because governance requires auditable reasoning, safety and ethics alignment, and resilience to adversarial misuse. Here we present SafeMed-R1, trained with a traceable Clinical Trust Signals(CTS) pipeline that links each reasoning instance to clinician rubric scores and edit histories, and aligned through safety and ethics supervision and red team stress testing. SafeMed-R1 attains a macro-averaged accuracy of 79.6% across clinical benchmarks. Under adversarial safety testing, it shows the lowest aggregated risk and reduces unsafe outputs by about 3 to 5% relative to its baseline. In a paired expert study of 30 medication safety vignettes, SafeMed-R1 matches PGY1 and PGY2 residents on medical correctness and scores higher for medication safety, guideline consistency, and clinical usefulness. Collectively, these results suggest that clinician-audited supervision provenance, together with domain-tailored safety and ethics alignment, can strengthen governance-relevant evidence without relying on inference-time retrieval or citation grounding.

</details>


### [235] [Picid: A Modular Evaluation Infrastructure for Reproducible PHM Across Tasks and Domains](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28345)
*Lev Telyatnikov,Raffael Theiler,Leandro Von Krannichfeldt,Olga Fink*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper proposes a standardized, modular framework (\picid) for fair, reproducible evaluation of PHM models across diverse tasks and datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: PHM research suffers from non-standard, ad hoc evaluation setups—data splits, preprocessing, label alignment, windowing, and metrics are often implicit and inconsistent. This makes results hard to reproduce and comparisons between methods and datasets unreliable, slowing progress and obscuring genuine advances.

Method: The authors design \picid, a modular evaluation infrastructure that formalizes the full PHM evaluation pipeline as an explicit, executable protocol. They define clear abstractions for dataset construction, preprocessing, label alignment, temporal windowing, and metric computation. The framework enforces deterministic, leakage-safe data handling and provides unified interfaces that cover fault detection, diagnostics (classification), and prognostics (regression). It is built to be extensible to new datasets and model classes without breaking protocol guarantees, and standardizes data contracts and evaluation boundaries so that different tasks and domains can be evaluated under comparable conditions.

Result: Using \picid, the authors perform a systematic empirical study: thirteen different models are evaluated on twelve heterogeneous PHM datasets, including batteries, bearings, turbofan engines, hydraulics, filtration systems, and buildings. The infrastructure successfully handles these diverse settings under a unified pipeline, enabling consistent and fair comparison across tasks and domains.

Conclusion: \picid provides a reusable, standardized foundation for PHM evaluation, addressing reproducibility and fairness issues caused by ad hoc protocols. By enforcing explicit, leakage-safe, and extensible evaluation pipelines, it enables more reliable benchmarking and cross-task comparisons, and can accelerate progress in PHM research by making results more trustworthy and comparable.

Abstract: Progress in Prognostics and Health Management (PHM) is hindered by the lack of standardized and reusable evaluation practices across tasks, datasets, and application domains. Reported results are often difficult to reproduce and compare, as key protocol choices, such as data splits, preprocessing, label alignment, temporal windowing, and metrics, are often implicit or implemented ad hoc. We introduce \picid, a modular evaluation infrastructure that formalizes the PHM evaluation pipeline as an explicit, executable, and reproducible protocol. Through well-defined abstractions, \picid enforces deterministic, leakage-safe dataset construction while remaining flexible across diverse PHM settings. The framework supports fault detection, diagnostics, and prognostics through a unified interface and can be extended to new datasets and model classes without violating protocol invariants. By standardizing data contracts and evaluation boundaries, \picid also enables fair cross-task comparisons across diagnostics (classification) and prognostics (regression), allowing identical model families to be evaluated consistently across heterogeneous settings. We demonstrate \picid through an empirical evaluation of thirteen models on twelve datasets spanning batteries, bearings, turbofan engines, hydraulics, filtration systems, and buildings. This work establishes a reusable foundation for standardized, fair and reproducible evaluation in PHM.

</details>


### [236] [FedMPT: Federated Multi-label Prompt Tuning of Vision-Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28347)
*Xucong Wang,Pengkun Wang,Zhe Zhao,Liheng Yu,Shuang Wang,Yang Wang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper proposes FedMPT, a causal, condition-guided federated multi-label recognition framework built on vision-language models and LLM-derived label conditions, achieving SOTA performance while reducing spurious label correlations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: In federated learning settings, clients have private and heterogeneous data. When adapting vision-language models for multi-label recognition in such environments, models tend to overfit spurious label correlations specific to each client. This leads to incorrect activation of irrelevant labels on new samples, hurting robustness and generalization. The authors aim to design a federated multi-label recognition method that leverages the knowledge of VLMs while avoiding these spurious correlations across decentralized clients.

Method: The authors formulate federated multi-label recognition through a causal lens, employing a front-door adjustment with intermediate variables that capture and magnify oracle label co-occurrence, thereby decoupling the MLR modeling process. Building on this, they propose FedMPT, a federated framework where: (1) an LLM-driven pipeline is used to infer and construct generalizable conditions that explain and govern label dependencies; (2) condition-enriched text prompts are aligned with image patches via optimal transport, enabling discovery of multiple region-level semantics; and (3) a gating mechanism fuses predictions from multiple condition-specific branches into a final synergistic prediction. The overall framework is tailored for federated optimization across clients.

Result: On multiple benchmark datasets, FedMPT achieves competitive or superior performance compared to existing methods. It outperforms state-of-the-art baselines under diverse federated and multi-label recognition settings, indicating improved robustness to spurious label correlations and better generalization across clients with heterogeneous data.

Conclusion: By reframing federated multi-label recognition with a causal model and exploiting LLM-derived conditions, FedMPT effectively mitigates spurious label activations in VLM-based federated learning. The combination of front-door adjustment, condition-enriched prompts, optimal transport-based region-level alignment, and gated prediction yields state-of-the-art results, demonstrating that causally informed, condition-guided modeling is a promising direction for federated multi-label vision-language tasks.

Abstract: Multi-Label Recognition (MLR) based on Vision-Language Models (VLMs) aims to leverage their pre-trained knowledge to better adapt complex recognition scenarios, thereby enhancing model robustness. However, for realistic decentralized applications requiring federated learning, adapting VLMs to each client that possesses private and heterogeneous data can cause the model to overfit spurious label correlations, consequently triggering irrelevant categories when encountering new samples. To tackle this problem, we reconsider the federated learning for MLR with a causal model, in which we adopt a front-door adjustment and decouple the MLR modeling process by intermediate variables that magnify the oracle label co-occurrence. Guided by our analysis, we propose our FedMPT, the first method specifically designed for federated MLR. The core idea of FedMPT is to leverage generalizable conditions to steer federated MLR to mitigate erroneous label activations. To achieve this, FedMPT introduces an Large Language Model (LLM)-driven pipeline to decipher the underlying conditions that govern label dependencies. Furthermore, we introduce an optimal transport between the condition-enriched prompts and the image patches to uncover multiple region-level semantics. Finally, we generate synergistic predictions from different conditions with a crafted gating mechanism. Experiments on multiple benchmark datasets show that our proposed approach achieves competitive results and outperforms SOTA methods under varied settings.

</details>


### [237] [Plan Before Search: Search Agents Need Plan](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28354)
*Zhipeng Qian,Zihan Liang,Yufei Ma,Ben Chen,Huangyu Dai,Jiayi Ji,Chenyi Lei,Wenwu Ou,Xiaoshuai Sun,Qibin Hou*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: They study how to train LLMs as retrieval-augmented reasoning agents using a structured ‘Plan’ behavior, analyze RL failure modes across models, and propose a self-bootstrapping method that avoids reliance on a stronger teacher model.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current training of retrieval-augmented LLM agents relies heavily on supervised distillation from larger models and ignores how complex behaviors decompose into dependent sub-skills. They want to understand why the same RL reward yields different outcomes across models and how to reliably elicit a structured planning behavior (Plan) without needing a more powerful teacher model.

Method: Define ‘Plan’ as an agentic procedure that first decomposes a multi-hop question into ordered sub-questions before doing any retrieval. Use RL with an identical reward signal on several model families (3B–14B) to observe and analyze differing failure modes, highlighting dependency on model-specific factors like entropy and prerequisite skills. Then introduce a self-bootstrapping pipeline where a small seed model generates and filters trajectories that trigger the Plan behavior, which are then used to train diverse target models without external distillation.

Result: They empirically find that the same RL reward leads to different qualitative failure modes across models, implying that capability acquisition depends on model-specific feasibility conditions. Their self-bootstrapping pipeline successfully activates the Plan behavior across all tested models and delivers consistent performance gains over strong baselines on multi-hop QA benchmarks.

Conclusion: Effective training of retrieval-augmented reasoning agents cannot rely on reward design alone; it must respect model-specific feasibility conditions and sub-skill dependencies. A self-bootstrapping approach using a small seed model can reliably activate structured planning behaviors like Plan in a range of models, removing the need for distillation from a stronger external teacher and improving multi-hop QA performance.

Abstract: Training large language models as retrieval-augmented reasoning agents typically combines reinforcement learning with an SFT cold start distilled from a stronger model. However, this paradigm overlooks two fundamental factors: the dependency structure among sub-skills, and the possibility that distillation is not the only route to capability acquisition. We study this through Plan, a structured agentic behavior for multi-hop retrieval that decomposes a question into ordered sub-questions before any retrieval is performed, so that each search step can be anchored to a pre-designed sub-question instead of drifting under the influence of partially relevant documents retrieved earlier. However, across three model families spanning 3B to 14B parameters, we find that an identical reward signal induces qualitatively different RL failure modes. This phenomenon indicates that successful training hinges not only on reward design but also on model-specific feasibility conditions: sufficient initial entropy, training stability, and prerequisite sub-skills. Motivated by this, we propose a self-bootstrapping paradigm in which a small-scale seed model generates filtered trajectories that activate Plan in any target model, eliminating the need for distillation from an external stronger model. Our pipeline activates Plan across every tested model and consistently outperforms competitive baselines on multi-hop QA benchmarks.

</details>


### [238] [From Knowing to Doing: A Memory-Controlled Benchmark for LLM Trading Agents on Stock Markets](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28359)
*Taojie Zhu,Wentao Zhao,Rui Sun,Beidi Luan,Jiacheng Lu,Sinuo Wang,Jing Li,Daxin Jiang,Yonghong He,Zuo Bai*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper proposes KTD-Fin, a leakage-controlled, attribution-aware benchmark to assess whether LLM trading agents truly exhibit transferable stock-picking skill rather than relying on memorized data or passive factor exposure.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing evaluations of LLM trading agents typically run end-to-end backtests and look only at portfolio returns. This is flawed because (1) backtest periods overlap with LLM training data, so models can exploit memorized historical prices and narratives instead of genuine reasoning, and (2) raw returns conflate stock-picking skill with market beta, style tilts, or regime luck, obscuring whether any real alpha is generated.

Method: They build KTD-Fin, a benchmark with two key components: (1) a data-side masking protocol that consistently anonymizes tickers, dates, prices, and related identifiers in both prompts and connected tools, breaking direct links to historical knowledge while preserving economic structure; (2) a Barra-style performance attribution framework that decomposes realized portfolio returns into contributions from overall market exposure, style/factor exposures, and residual stock-selection alpha. They then apply this benchmark to evaluate ten frontier LLM agents on Chinese CSI300 equity data over 2024–2026.

Result: With masking, LLM agents’ explanations and strategies shift away from named securities and dated narratives toward abstract, anonymous factor-based reasoning. Performance attribution reveals that most of the agents’ cumulative returns in the leakage-controlled setup can be explained by passive market beta and style exposures (e.g., factor tilts), leaving only small and inconsistent residuals attributable to stock-selection alpha.

Conclusion: LLM agents that appear profitable in naïve backtests rarely demonstrate robust, persistent stock-picking skill once data leakage is controlled and factor exposures are accounted for. Therefore, financial LLM benchmarks should go beyond asking whether an agent makes money to analyzing how it makes money and whether its gains reflect genuine, transferable investment skill. KTD-Fin is released as a reusable template for conducting such leakage-aware, attribution-based evaluations of LLM trading systems.

Abstract: Evaluating whether large language model (LLM) agents can profit in capital markets is increasingly framed as end-to-end trading: place an agent in a historical market, let it trade, and measure portfolio returns. This setup is vulnerable to two evaluation failures. First, long backtests often overlap with the knowledge cutoffs of frontier LLMs, allowing memorized tickers, dates, prices, and market narratives to substitute for investment reasoning. Second, raw returns are a noisy proxy for stock-selection ability, since positive performance may come from market beta, style exposure, or favorable regimes rather than genuine alpha.
  We introduce KTD-Fin (Knowing-To-Doing Financial Benchmark), an end-to-end stock-market trading benchmark that addresses both issues. KTD-Fin uses a data-side masking protocol to anonymize key identifiers and calendar information consistently across prompts and tools, separating historical market memory from investment decision-making. It also incorporates a Barra-style performance attribution framework that decomposes portfolio returns into market, style, and stock-selection alpha components.
  Across ten frontier LLM agents evaluated on the Chinese CSI300 over a 2024--2026 window, masking substantially changes agent rationales, pushing them towards anonymized factor-based reasoning. Attribution analysis further shows that LLM agents' cumulative returns under leakage-controlled evaluation are largely explained by passive market and style exposure, with limited evidence of persistent stock-selection alpha. These findings suggest that financial LLM benchmarks should evaluate not only whether an agent makes money, but also whether the source of returns reflects transferable investment skill. We release KTD-Fin as a reproducible template for leakage-controlled and attribution-aware evaluation of LLM trading agents.

</details>


### [239] [Prompt Codebooks: Discrete Compositional Optimization for Language Model Instruction Refinement](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28360)
*Jyotirmoy Nath,Neeraj Kumar,Brejesh Lall*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper introduces Prompt Codebooks (PCO), a compositional framework for automatic prompt optimization that represents prompts as combinations of reusable natural-language “instincts” rather than a single global string, achieving better performance, per-instance adaptation, and shorter prompts across several benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing automatic prompt optimization methods treat a task’s prompt as one monolithic, globally edited string that is shared across all instances. This makes optimized prompts brittle, prevents fine-grained adaptation to each input, and does not allow reuse of learned sub-behaviors across different inputs. The authors aim to create a more structured, compositional, and reusable representation of prompt knowledge that can vary by instance while remaining trainable end-to-end.

Method: They propose Prompt Codebooks (PCO), where prompt knowledge is stored as a finite vocabulary of natural-language “instincts” (atomic instruction units) in a discrete codebook. An LLM-based encoder selects a small, input-dependent subset of instincts from the codebook; a generator composes these selected instincts into a final prompt for a frozen target LLM; and a critic model produces a structured verdict that is decomposed into per-variable textual gradients. These gradients jointly train the encoder, generator, and the codebook entries under a language-valued min-max objective, enabling instance-wise routing and composition of instincts.

Result: On six benchmarks using Qwen3-8B and LLaMA-3.1-8B as target models, PCO improves performance over zero-shot prompting by up to 30.36 points. It outperforms a strong prior APO baseline, GEPA, by 3.34 points on HotpotQA and by 1.11 points on average across tasks. Additionally, PCO achieves these gains with much shorter prompts, reducing prompt length by up to 14.1× compared to MIPROv2 and 3.0× compared to GEPA when using only 16 instincts in the codebook.

Conclusion: Representing prompt knowledge as a small, discrete codebook of reusable natural-language instincts and routing them per instance leads to more powerful and efficient automatic prompt optimization. PCO can realize instance-specific prompt compositions that are impossible under previous instance-blind, monolithic editing approaches, yielding better accuracy and significantly shorter prompts across multiple benchmarks.

Abstract: Automatic prompt optimization (APO) has driven significant gains in LLM-based agentic workflows. However, existing methods treat each task's prompt as a monolithic, instance-blind string optimized through global edits, producing brittle updates and preventing the reuse of learned sub-behaviors. We propose Prompt Codebooks (PCO), a novel compositional prompt optimization framework that recasts APO as discrete learning over a finite vocabulary of natural-language instincts - atomic, reusable instruction units. PCO organizes prompt-construction knowledge in a discrete codebook and routes each input to a small subset of entries via an LLM-based encoder; a generator composes them into a prompt for the frozen target model; a critic emits a structured verdict that decomposes by attribution into per-variable textual gradients, jointly training the encoder, generator, and codebook under a language-valued min-max objective. The resulting routing is per-instance: different inputs in the same task receive different instinct compositions, a regime structurally inexpressible under instance-blind methods. Across six benchmarks on Qwen3-8B and LLaMA-3.1-8B, PCO improves over zero-shot by up to +30.36 points, surpasses the strongest prior baseline (GEPA) by +3.34 on HotpotQA and +1.11 in aggregate, and reduces deployed prompt length by up to 14.1x versus MIPROv2 and 3.0x versus GEPA using only K=16 instincts.

</details>


### [240] [Risk-Controlled Lean-as-Judge for Natural-Language Mathematical Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28365)
*Pauline Bourigault,Xiaotong Ji,Matthieu Zimmer,Rasul Tutunov,Haitham Bou Ammar*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper studies how reliable Lean-based autoformalization is as a signal for correctness of natural-language math answers, finding that the signal is partial, coverage-dependent, and often unfaithful, and proposes COVCAL, a method to selectively accept answers with provable risk bounds using Lean diagnostics, showing feasibility depends strongly on formalizer coverage and faithfulness.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Lean proofs are increasingly used to evaluate or supervise language models on math tasks, but many natural-language answers cannot be fully formalized, and even when Lean fails or succeeds, that outcome can be ambiguous due to typing issues or library gaps rather than true correctness. There is a need to quantify when such partial formal signals can be trusted for correctness judgments under explicit risk control, especially as autoformalizers vary widely in coverage and faithfulness.

Method: The authors empirically analyze the relationship between Lean proof success and actual correctness of natural-language answers on MATH-500, conditioned on coverage levels. They measure coverage and faithfulness for a 7B autoformalizer and a prover-specialized formalizer via audits. They then introduce COVCAL, a selection and calibration framework over Lean trace diagnostics that provides finite-sample selective risk bounds on the subset of answers it chooses to accept, using two regimes: a conservative Bonferroni correction and a tighter dev-then-cal approach. They test feasibility by bootstrapping partitions and checking whether nontrivial acceptance with risk guarantees is possible for different formalizers.

Result: On MATH-500, the authors find that when Lean can prove an answer at high coverage, the answer is correct 96% of the time, but only 20% at low coverage, showing strong coverage dependence. A 7B autoformalizer proves a problem class only 28% of the time, and manual auditing finds only about 43% of those proofs faithfully represent the intended statement, revealing sparsity and unfaithfulness. Under COVCAL with Bonferroni correction, the signal from the 7B formalizer is too sparse, causing abstention on all 20 bootstrap partitions. In contrast, a prover-specialized formalizer achieves 79% coverage; COVCAL becomes feasible on 17 of 20 partitions and can accept about 48% of problems at 0.98 accepted accuracy.

Conclusion: The reliability of Lean-based signals for judging natural-language math answers is highly dependent on the coverage and faithfulness of the autoformalizer. Generic 7B autoformalizers yield signals too sparse and noisy to support meaningful risk-controlled acceptance. With a high-coverage, prover-specialized formalizer, the proposed COVCAL method can selectively accept a substantial fraction of answers while certifying low error among them. Since self-consistency of model answers is already around 91% accurate, the main contribution is not raw performance but a clear characterization of when partial formal verification can be trusted under explicit risk control, guiding the choice and deployment of autoformalization tools in evaluation pipelines.

Abstract: Lean is increasingly used to judge natural-language mathematical answers, but its signal is partial: many answers never formalize, and a failed proof may reflect an ill-typed statement or a missing library fact, not a wrong answer. On MATH-500 we show this signal is (i) sharply coverage-dependent, that is the proof-winning answer is correct 96% of the time at high proved coverage but 20% at low, and (ii) sparse and often unfaithful: a 7B autoformalizer proves a class for only 28% of problems, and a manual audit finds only approximately 43% of those proofs faithful. We propose COVCAL, a selector over Lean-trace diagnostics that certifies a finite-sample selective-risk bound on accepted answers or abstains, under two regimes (a conservative Bonferroni bound and a tighter dev-then-cal rule). Feasibility depends on autoformalization coverage: with the 7B formalizer the signal is too sparse and Bonferroni abstains on all 20 bootstrap partitions, whereas a prover-specialized formalizer reaches 79% coverage and flips it to feasible on 17 of 20, accepting approximately 48% of problems at 0.98 accepted accuracy. Since self-consistency alone is already 91% accurate, our contribution is a precise account of when, and with which formalizer, a partial formal signal can be trusted under risk control.

</details>


### [241] [CyberJurors: A Multi-Agent Simulation Task for E-Commerce Disputes Verdict](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28369)
*Yanhui Sun,Wu Liu,Haifeng Ming,Xinru Wang,Hantao Yao,Yongdong Zhang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper introduces a new task and benchmark for e-commerce dispute verdict prediction and proposes a multi-agent framework that better matches real crowdsourced jury decisions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing methods for legal or decision-making tasks struggle with the specific characteristics of e-commerce dispute resolutions, which involve multimodal, multi-round, redundant evidence and platform-specific rules. There is a need for a task formulation, dataset, and method tailored to this practical scenario of crowdsourced juror decisions on e-commerce platforms.

Method: The authors define the E-commerce Dispute Verdicts (EDV) task and construct VerdictBench, a multimodal benchmark of 6,000 real cases reflecting crowdsourced jury decisions. They then design CyberJurors, a multi-agent system. At the individual level, it uses an Individual Verdict Chain-of-Thought that breaks the reasoning process into four structured stages for detailed evidence perception and causal reasoning. At the collective level, a Jury Consensus Verdict mechanism simulates multi-round deliberation and voting among agents, incorporating precedent verdicts to reduce bias toward either party.

Result: On the VerdictBench dataset, CyberJurors outperforms state-of-the-art large language models, multimodal large language models, and court simulator systems in predicting verdicts. It also shows better alignment with real-world jury voting patterns, indicating it more faithfully captures how crowdsourced jurors decide disputes.

Conclusion: The proposed EDV task, VerdictBench dataset, and CyberJurors framework together provide an effective approach for modeling and automating e-commerce dispute verdicts. CyberJurors delivers superior performance and better mimics human jury behavior, offering a promising direction for practical dispute resolution support on e-commerce platforms.

Abstract: E-commerce platforms have begun recruiting crowdsourced jurors to adjudicate massive volumes of transaction disputes. Unlike formal legal judgment, E-commerce dispute verdicts require grounding pivotal clues from redundant, multi-round, multimodal evidence and making decisions under flexible platform-specific conventions. These characteristics render existing methods insufficient for this scenario. To bridge this gap, we introduce a pioneering task, E-commerce Dispute Verdicts (EDV), and present VerdictBench, a multimodal benchmark comprising 6,000 real-world cases designed to reflect crowdsourced jury decisions. Building upon this, we propose CyberJurors, a multi-agent framework to clarify the dispute logic and regulate the verdict process. At the individual level, Individual Verdict Chain-of-Thought decomposes the EDV task into four structured reasoning stages, enabling fine-grained clue perception and clarifying causal logic between pivotal clues and the dispute focus. At the collective level, Jury Consensus Verdict simulates multi-round discussion and voting among jurors, while incorporating verdict precedents to mitigate cognitive biases toward either disputant. Experiments on VerdictBench show that CyberJurors outperforms state-of-the-art LLMs, MLLMs, and court simulators, while achieving stronger alignment with real-world jury voting patterns. Code and dataset are available at https://github.com/YanhuiS/CyberJurors and https://huggingface.co/datasets/piggi/VerdictBench.

</details>


### [242] [From paper to benchmark: agentic, framework-based reproduction of under-specified methods in machine health intelligence](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28371)
*Raffael Theiler,Ludovico Comito,David Leko,Leandro Von Krannichfeldt,Lev Telyatnikov,Olga Fink*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper presents an agent-based approach that translates PHM research papers into standardized implementations within a shared benchmark framework, improving reproducibility and comparability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reproducing industrial Prognostics and Health Management (PHM) methods from papers is hard because of limited access to industrial datasets, incomplete reporting of preprocessing/evaluation steps, and many implicit or under-specified design choices that heavily influence performance. Existing paper-to-code systems yield implementations that are not directly comparable due to inconsistent assumptions and evaluation settings. There is a need for a systematic, standardized way to turn papers into benchmark-ready, reproducible code while making assumptions explicit.

Method: The authors propose "agentic, framework-based PHM paper reproduction." An agent reads a PHM paper and maps its equations and protocol descriptions into a shared PHM benchmark framework via a slot-binding interface. This interface decomposes the paper into structured components, such as task definitions, dataset adapters, windowing strategies, target construction, models, and evaluators. It also records unresolved or ambiguous assumptions explicitly. Implementations are checked against standardized task contracts and evaluation hooks in the framework, ensuring that they adhere to common protocols. The authors then compare different agentic reproduction strategies—framework-enhanced, skill-based, and prompt-based—against a recent framework-free paper-reproduction agent.

Result: Applied to 16 PHM papers, the approach enables code generation that is validated within a shared framework, improving reproduction success, the quality of model-based code evaluation, and the explicit binding and documentation of paper assumptions. The produced implementations can be run under standardized protocols, making performance across papers directly comparable. Framework-based agents outperform a recent framework-free paper-reproduction agent on various measures related to successful reproduction and benchmark readiness.

Conclusion: Integrating agentic code generation with a shared PHM benchmark framework, via a slot-binding interface, turns paper reproduction from isolated, ad hoc code synthesis into a systematic process that yields executable, assumption-aware, and cross-paper comparable benchmark implementations. This demonstrates a promising direction for improving reproducibility and comparability in PHM and, potentially, in other applied machine learning domains.

Abstract: Industrial Prognostics and Health Management (PHM) provides a representative case study for a broader challenge in applied machine learning: translating published papers into executable, benchmark-ready implementations. Reproducing under-specified methods in PHM is particularly difficult due to restricted access to industrial datasets, incomplete reporting of preprocessing and evaluation protocols, and implicit design choices (e.g., windowing, target construction, data splits) that critically affect performance. Existing paper-to-code systems generate implementations for individual papers, but these artifacts are often not directly comparable due to inconsistencies in assumptions and evaluation settings. We introduce \emph{agentic, framework-based PHM paper reproduction}, where an agent translates a paper into a shared PHM benchmark framework via a \emph{slot-binding interface}. This interface maps equations and protocol descriptions into structured components (task definitions, dataset adapters, windowing, targets, models, and evaluators), while explicitly recording unresolved assumptions. The resulting implementations are validated against standardized task contracts and evaluation hooks, enabling consistent and comparable benchmarking. We evaluate this approach on 16 PHM papers, comparing framework-enhanced, skill-based and prompt-based agentic reproduction against a recent framework-free paper-reproduction agent. We assess reproduction success, model-based code evaluation, framework binding of paper assumptions, and cross-paper benchmark comparability under standardized protocols. Our results show that coupling agentic generation with a shared framework transforms paper reproduction from isolated code synthesis into executable, assumption-aware, and systematically comparable benchmark implementations.

</details>


### [243] [Mechanistically Interpreting the Role of Sample Difficulty in RLVR for LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28388)
*Yue Cheng,Jiajun Zhang,Xiaohui Gao,Weiwei Xing,Zheng Wang,Zhanxing Zhu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper studies how the difficulty of training problems affects Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Reward (RLVR) for reasoning in LLMs, finding a non-monotonic effect and proposing difficulty-adaptive training strategies.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: While RLVR improves LLM reasoning, it is unclear how the difficulty of training samples influences both performance and internal representations; understanding this is important to design more effective RL training curricula and avoid degradation of capabilities on very hard tasks.

Method: The authors perform difficulty-wise and single-sample analyses of RLVR training, measuring behavioral effects (answer quality, degeneracies) and internal representation changes using Temporal Sparse Autoencoders (T-SAE) across easy, medium, and hard problems; they then design and test difficulty-adaptive strategies such as backward-reasoning reformulations and T-SAE-guided signals for hard samples.

Result: They discover that easy and medium problems produce the strongest and most stable reasoning gains, whereas overly hard problems often yield weak learning signals, cause behaviors like answer repetition or skipping computation, and can harm prior capabilities; representation-wise, easy tasks reinforce direct-answer/basic-computation features, hard tasks only help when successful trajectories exist and primarily activate reasoning features, and medium tasks strengthen both computational and multi-step reasoning features.

Conclusion: Sample difficulty crucially shapes both optimization and representation dynamics in RLVR; effective use of RLVR requires tailoring training to emphasize easy/medium problems and using specialized strategies to extract useful signals from hard problems, improving reward density and credit assignment and ultimately leading to better reasoning-focused LLM training.

Abstract: Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Reward (RLVR) is empirically shown to notably enhance the reasoning performance of large language models (LLMs), particularly in mathematics and programming. However, the mechanistic role of Sample Difficulty in RLVR remains poorly understood. In this paper, we investigate RLVR through the lens of difficulty-wise and one-sample analysis. We find that sample difficulty has a non-monotonic effect on RLVR: easy and medium-difficulty problems yield the strongest and most stable reasoning improvements, whereas overly hard problems often provide weak learning signals, induce degenerate behaviors such as answer repetition or skipping necessary computation, and can ultimately degrade the model's pre-existing capabilities. Beyond the obverse of response, we further analyze the model's internal feature dynamics using Temporal Sparse Autoencoders (T-SAE). Easy problems mainly reinforce direct-answer and basic-computation features while suppressing deliberative-reasoning features; hard problems activate reasoning-related features but become useful only when successful trajectories are sampled; medium-difficulty problems provide a more balanced signal, strengthening both computation and multi-step reasoning features. Motivated by these findings, we propose difficulty-adaptive strategies for hard-sample utilization, using backward-reasoning reformulation and T-SAE-guided training signals to improve reward density and credit assignment during RLVR. Overall, our results identify sample difficulty as a key factor governing both the optimization dynamics and representation evolution of RLVR.

</details>


### [244] [HRBench: Benchmarking and Understanding Thinking-Mode Switch Strategies in Hybrid-Reasoning LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28398)
*Yansong Ning,Mianpeng Liu,Jingwen Ye,Weidong Zhang,Hao Liu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Introduces HRBench, a unified framework to systematically evaluate and compare strategies for switching between fast and slow reasoning modes in hybrid-reasoning LLMs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing adaptive thinking-mode selection methods for hybrid-reasoning LLMs (e.g., fast vs. slow reasoning) are hard to compare because they are evaluated on different models, datasets, and setups. This fragmentation obscures how switching strategies actually trade off quality and efficiency and what works best under which conditions.

Method: Designs HRBench, an evaluation framework that structures the design space of thinking-mode switching along two axes: (1) three switching strategy families—prompt-based selection, external routing, and speculative execution; (2) four training regimes—training-free, supervised fine-tuning (SFT), offline RL, and online RL. This yields 12 standardized evaluation settings. The authors reimplement 12+ prior methods within a single pipeline and test them across 6 LLMs of different scales and 5 reasoning benchmarks spanning math, science, and code, then analyze the efficiency–effectiveness trade-offs of each strategy class and training regime.

Result: Across controlled experiments, prompt-based switching tends to achieve good accuracy per token (favorable token-accuracy trade-offs), routing-based approaches yield more stable and predictable cost reductions, and speculative execution often boosts accuracy but at the price of increased token usage. The study also observes that the impact of training differs by strategy type and that the best-performing strategy depends on model size and task domain.

Conclusion: HRBench offers a standardized, reproducible benchmark and reference implementation suite for evaluating hybrid-reasoning LLM thinking-mode switching. It clarifies how different strategy families and training regimes occupy distinct regions of the cost–accuracy frontier and demonstrates that optimal choices are model- and task-dependent, enabling more controlled and comparable future research on efficient reasoning in LLMs.

Abstract: Hybrid-reasoning large language models (LLMs) expose explicit controls over reasoning effort, allowing users or systems to trade off answer quality against inference cost. However, existing methods for adaptive thinking-mode selection are typically evaluated under different models, datasets, and implementation assumptions, making it difficult to compare their practical behavior. We introduce HRBench, a unified evaluation framework for studying thinking-mode switching in hybrid-reasoning LLMs. HRBench organizes the design space along two axes: three switching strategy families, prompt-based selection, external routing, and speculative execution, and four training regimes, training-free, SFT, offline and online RL, yielding 12 controlled evaluation settings. We evaluate these settings across 6 LLMs, from Qwen3.5-2B to Kimi-K2.5-1.1T, and 5 reasoning benchmarks covering mathematics, science, and code, while reimplementing 12+ representative prior methods within the same pipeline. Our analysis characterizes how different switching strategies occupy distinct effectiveness-efficiency trade-off regions: prompt-based methods often provide favorable token-accuracy trade-offs, routing methods offer more stable cost reduction, and speculative methods tend to improve accuracy at higher token cost. We further find that training affects strategies differently, and that the preferred strategy varies with model scale and task domain. HRBench provides reference implementations and a unified evaluation platform to support more controlled research on efficient reasoning in hybrid-reasoning LLMs. Our data, code and repository are available at https://github.com/usail-hkust/HRBench.

</details>


### [245] [Measuring Progress Toward AGI: A Cognitive Framework](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28405)
*Ryan Burnell,Yumeya Yamamori,Orhan Firat,Kate Olszewska,Steph Hughes-Fitt,Oran Kelly,Isaac R. Galatzer-Levy,Meredith Ringel Morris,Allan Dafoe,Alison M. Snyder,Noah D. Goodman,Matthew Botvinick,Shane Legg*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: They propose a psychology-inspired framework and evaluation protocol to systematically measure AI progress toward AGI via human-like cognitive abilities.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current discussions about AGI lack a clear, shared way to quantify or track progress. This leads to subjective claims, makes scientific comparison of systems difficult, and complicates governance and safety decisions.

Method: They build a Cognitive Taxonomy of 10 core human cognitive faculties using insights from psychology, neuroscience, and cognitive science. Then they design an evaluation protocol where AI systems are tested on held-out tasks targeted at each faculty, producing a quantitative cognitive profile of capabilities and gaps.

Result: The paper delivers: (1) a 10-factor taxonomy of cognitive abilities relevant to general intelligence; and (2) a concrete testing protocol that maps system performance on specific tasks to that taxonomy, enabling systematic capability assessment rather than ad hoc benchmarks.

Conclusion: A structured, cognitively grounded taxonomy plus standardized evaluation can serve as an initial, practical roadmap for rigorous, empirical assessment of progress toward AGI, clarifying strengths, weaknesses, and remaining gaps in current systems.

Abstract: Despite widespread discussion of AGI, there is no clear framework for measuring progress toward it. This ambiguity fuels subjective claims, makes it difficult to track progress, and risks hindering responsible governance. As a starting point to address this gap, we present a framework for understanding system capabilities in relation to human cognitive abilities. Drawing from decades of research in psychology, neuroscience, and cognitive science, we introduce a Cognitive Taxonomy that deconstructs general intelligence into 10 key cognitive faculties. We then propose a rigorous evaluation protocol in which a system's performance is measured across a suite of targeted, held-out cognitive tasks, generating a 'cognitive profile' that can be used to understand a system's strengths and weaknesses. We hope this framework will provide a practical roadmap and an initial step toward more rigorous, empirical evaluation of AGI.

</details>


### [246] [Efficient Post-training of LLMs for Code Generation With Offline Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28409)
*Mingze Wu,Abhinav Anand,Shweta Verma,Mira Mezini*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper studies using offline reinforcement learning (RL) with existing code datasets to post-train code-generating LLMs, showing it effectively improves performance, especially for smaller models and harder coding tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Online RL for code generation requires repeated LLM inference plus verification of outputs, which is time- and resource-intensive. The authors are motivated to find a more efficient and scalable alternative that can still leverage RL-style training but without expensive online interaction, by exploiting abundant existing code data.

Method: They apply offline RL algorithms to code-generating language models, using pre-collected code datasets as the experience buffer instead of generating new trajectories online. They then compare the performance of models trained with offline RL to baselines, across model sizes and coding problem difficulties.

Result: Offline RL leads to measurable performance improvements of code LLMs relative to non-RL or standard fine-tuning baselines. Gains are particularly pronounced for smaller language models and for more difficult coding benchmarks, indicating that offline RL can extract more useful signal from fixed datasets under these conditions.

Conclusion: Offline RL is a viable and effective post-training strategy for code-generating LLMs, providing a more resource-efficient alternative to online RL. It is especially helpful when working with small models or challenging coding tasks, suggesting it can be a practical tool for improving LLM code quality without the full cost of online RL.

Abstract: Post-training using online reinforcement learning (RL) is an important training step for LLMs, including code-generating models. However, online RL for code generation involves LLM inference and verification of the generated output, which can take considerable time and resources. In this paper, we explore the application of offline RL to code-generating models by leveraging existing code datasets. Our experiments demonstrate that offline RL is an effective training strategy for improving LLM performance. We show that offline RL can be especially beneficial for small LLMs and challenging coding problems.

</details>


### [247] [DenoiseRL: Bootstrapping Reasoning Models to Recover from Noisy Prefixes](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28421)
*Caijun Xu,Changyi Xiao,Zhongyuan Peng,Yixin Cao*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: DenoiseRL is an RL framework that improves LLM reasoning by learning from the model’s own incorrect reasoning traces instead of relying on stronger teachers or heavily curated hard datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current RL methods for improving reasoning in LLMs often require stronger teacher models or highly curated difficult datasets, which are costly and limit scalability. There is a need for an approach that can leverage the weak model’s own failures as training signals, reducing dependence on external supervision and manual data curation while still improving reasoning and exploration efficiency.

Method: DenoiseRL uses reinforcement learning to treat incorrect reasoning traces from weak models as recoverable failures. It converts these incorrect traces into training signals by optimizing for recovery-oriented behavior, effectively denoising the model’s own mistakes. The framework focuses on leveraging imperfect model behavior to generate richer and more diverse feedback without external teacher supervision or curated hard data, thereby enhancing exploration and learning directly from failures.

Result: DenoiseRL empirically outperforms strong on-policy RL baselines on competitive mathematical and general reasoning benchmarks. It yields better reasoning performance, improved training efficiency, and requires less dependence on expensive data curation or stronger teacher models. Additionally, the method enhances the model’s self-corrective behavior, especially as the training difficulty increases.

Conclusion: DenoiseRL provides an effective and scalable alternative for improving reasoning in large language models by replacing external supervision with learning from the model’s own incorrect reasoning traces. This approach improves performance, efficiency, and self-correction, while reducing reliance on stronger teacher models and curated datasets, suggesting a promising direction for scalable RL-based reasoning enhancement in LLMs.

Abstract: Reinforcement learning has become a central paradigm for advancing reasoning in large language models, yet most existing methods still depend on stronger teacher models or heavily curated difficult datasets, limiting scalable capability improvement. In this paper, we introduce DenoiseRL, a reinforcement learning framework that substitutes external supervision with recovery-oriented optimization over failures from weak models. Instead of relying on stronger supervision or carefully engineered data, DenoiseRL learns directly from incorrect reasoning traces by converting them into opportunities for improvement, making training more scalable and less dependent on external resources. This yields a richer and more diverse learning signal, improving exploration efficiency from imperfect model behavior. As a result, DenoiseRL improves reasoning performance and overall training efficiency while reducing the need for expensive data curation or stronger teacher models. Empirically, DenoiseRL consistently outperforms strong on-policy RL baselines across competitive mathematical and general reasoning benchmarks and promotes stronger self-corrective behavior as training difficulty increases, highlighting an effective and scalable alternative pathway for improving reasoning in large language models.

</details>


### [248] [GONDOR to the Rescue: Satisficing Planning with Low Memory](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28454)
*Yonatan Vernik,Alexander Tuisov,Alexander Shleyfman*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Introduces GONDOR, a memory-efficient extension of Greedy Best-First Search that compresses search trees and later reconstructs paths, improving coverage under tight memory limits.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Greedy Best-First Search is widely used for heuristic search tasks but performs poorly when memory is strictly limited (e.g., edge devices), often running out of memory and failing to find solutions. There is a need for search algorithms that maintain GBFS’s effectiveness while drastically reducing memory usage and still being able to output full solution paths.

Method: Propose GONDOR, which periodically compresses the GBFS search tree by keeping a sparse set of anchor (outpost) states and discarding intermediate nodes to stay within a memory budget. When a goal is found, GONDOR reconstructs the full solution path by re-searching between consecutive outposts. They formalize and analyze the algorithm, define and compare multiple outpost selection strategies, and integrate Bloom-filter-based compact duplicate detection for the closed list to further reduce memory consumption.

Result: Across a range of numeric planning benchmarks and different heuristic settings, GONDOR achieves higher problem coverage than standard GBFS under tight memory limits. Variants using Bloom filters further save memory with acceptable trade-offs. The method successfully continues search where GBFS would exhaust memory and fail.

Conclusion: GONDOR is an effective, memory-efficient extension of GBFS for heuristic search in constrained environments. By compressing the search tree and reconstructing paths via re-search between selected outposts, it significantly improves coverage without major performance loss. The authors provide code to enable further work on memory-bounded heuristic search and refinement of outpost selection and duplicate detection strategies.

Abstract: Greedy Best-First Search (GBFS) is the dominant approach for solving search problems where the goal can be estimated with a heuristic, such as planning, route finding, navigation, and pathfinding. This is especially true when the memory is tightly constrained, such as planning on edge devices. To alleviate that, we present GONDOR (Greedy Online Navigation with Dynamic Outpost-based Re-search), a memory-efficient extension of GBFS that allows search to continue under strict memory limits by periodically compressing the search tree while retaining a sparse set of anchor states, then upon reaching the goal reconstructs the path by re-searching between the sparse states. We analyze the algorithm and discuss several variants defined by different outpost selection policies. In addition, we explore using Bloom filters for compact duplicate detection in the closed list. Experiments across numeric planning domains and heuristic configurations show that GONDOR consistently improves coverage under low memory budgets compared to standard GBFS. We release the implementation of GONDOR and the Bloom-filter variant to facilitate further research on memory-efficient heuristic search.

</details>


### [249] [Diffusion Large Language Models for Visual Speech Recognition](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28456)
*Jeong Hun Yeo,Chae Won Kim,Hyeongseop Rha,Yong Man Ro*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: They introduce DLLM-VSR, a diffusion-style large language model framework for visual speech recognition that decodes text via iterative masked denoising instead of left-to-right autoregression, plus a length-guided decoding strategy that uses video duration to hypothesize and rerank transcript lengths, achieving state-of-the-art WER on LRS3.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current visual speech recognition systems typically use left-to-right autoregressive decoding, which makes irreversible decisions on tokens before sufficient bidirectional visual context is available, leading to errors on visually ambiguous segments. There is a need for a decoding framework that can flexibly refine uncertain tokens using broader context and also better handle uncertainty in output length.

Method: They propose DLLM-VSR, which treats transcription as iterative masked denoising with flexible-order decoding in a diffusion-like large language model. During decoding, the model uses confidence-based unmasking: high-confidence token positions are fixed early and then serve as bidirectional context while the model repeatedly refines the remaining masked, ambiguous positions. Training uses a two-stage masked-denoising scheme: first to learn visual-to-text content alignment, and second to model transcript length separately. Observing a performance gap when transcript length is known (oracle length), they design length-guided candidate decoding, which leverages video duration to build several plausible transcript-length hypotheses, performs decoding under each, and reranks the resulting candidates using both length plausibility and decoding confidence.

Result: DLLM-VSR achieves a word error rate of 19.5% on the LRS3 dataset using only its labeled training split, outperforming prior methods and establishing a new state-of-the-art for that setting. They also quantify a noticeable gap between standard decoding and oracle-length decoding, demonstrating the importance of length uncertainty, and show that their length-guided candidate decoding narrows this gap.

Conclusion: Diffusion-style masked-denoising LLMs are effective for visual speech recognition and address limitations of left-to-right autoregressive decoding by allowing flexible-order refinement of ambiguous tokens. Separating content alignment from length modeling and explicitly guiding decoding with length hypotheses derived from video duration both improve performance. The combination yields state-of-the-art results on LRS3, suggesting that diffusion LLMs with length-aware decoding are a promising direction for VSR and possibly other sequence-to-sequence tasks.

Abstract: Existing Visual Speech Recognition (VSR) systems commonly rely on left-to-right autoregressive decoding, which can force premature decisions on visually ambiguous tokens before sufficient context is available. We propose DLLM-VSR, to the best of our knowledge, the first Diffusion Large Language Model (DLLM)-based VSR framework, formulating transcription as iterative masked denoising with flexible-order decoding. With confidence-based unmasking, DLLM-VSR commits high-confidence positions early and uses the committed tokens as bidirectional context to refine ambiguous ones. To adapt DLLMs to VSR, we introduce a two-stage masked-denoising training strategy that separates visual-to-text content alignment from length modeling. We further observe a performance gap with oracle-length decoding, which assumes access to the true transcript length, indicating that reducing target-length uncertainty can improve DLLM-based VSR. To reduce this gap, we develop length-guided candidate decoding, which uses video duration to construct plausible transcript-length hypotheses, decodes under multiple hypotheses, and reranks candidates using length plausibility and decoding confidence. The proposed method achieves a state-of-the-art WER of 19.5\% on LRS3 using only its labeled training data.

</details>


### [250] [From Learning Resources to Competencies: LLM-Based Tagging with Evidence and Graph Constraints](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28483)
*Ngoc Luyen Le,Marie-Hélène Abel,Bertrand Laforge*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper proposes an LLM-based, evidence-producing pipeline to automatically align LMS learning resources with a structured competency framework, achieving better performance and transparency than existing automatic baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Competency-based education requires linking learning resources (content and assessments) to explicit competency frameworks so that systems can support competency-based search, curriculum mapping, and analytics. Manual tagging of resources with competencies is costly and hard to scale, while fully automatic approaches either perform poorly or act as opaque black boxes without interpretable justifications. There is a need for a method that is both accurate and transparent, helping educators audit and trust the automatic alignment.

Method: The authors design an end-to-end alignment pipeline called LBG (LLM+BM25+Graph). First, LMS resources are segmented into pedagogically meaningful fragments. For each fragment, a traditional retrieval method (BM25) uses a graph-enriched competency profile to retrieve a small set of candidate competencies. Then a large language model, constrained to choose only from these candidates, selects the most relevant competencies for each fragment and extracts supporting evidence spans from the fragment text. These predictions are further refined by exploiting the structure of the competency graph and then aggregated from fragment level to full resource level. The approach is evaluated on a Computer Science competency referential from UTC, involving 22 competencies and multiple course materials.

Result: On the UTC Computer Science dataset, the LBG pipeline attains a micro-F1 of 0.57 and macro-F1 of 0.50 at the fragment level, 0.51 macro-F1 at the resource level, and an MRR of 0.82. It outperforms zero-shot and few-shot LLM baselines, retrieval/similarity-based baselines, and supervised classifiers. In addition to higher predictive performance, the system outputs explicit evidence spans from the learning resources that justify assigned competencies, making the results more auditable and useful for educational analysis.

Conclusion: The paper concludes that combining an LLM with classical retrieval (BM25) and graph-based competency context yields an effective and auditable pipeline for mapping learning resources to competency frameworks. This hybrid approach improves both accuracy and interpretability over standard automatic baselines, offering a practical solution for competency-based search and curriculum analytics in LMS environments. The evidence spans and graph-aware refinement support human oversight and could facilitate broader adoption of competency-based educational technologies.

Abstract: Linking learning resources to a structured competency framework is key to enabling competency-based search and curriculum analytics in Learning Management Systems (LMS). However, manual tagging is labor-intensive, and fully automatic methods often lack transparency. In this paper, we present an end-to-end alignment pipeline that uses a large language model (LLM) as a constrained, evidence-producing tagger. LMS resources -both instructional content and assessments -are first segmented into meaningful pedagogical fragments. For each fragment, a small set of candidate competencies is retrieved from structured competency profiles enriched with graph-based context. The LLM then selects the most relevant competencies from this set and provides supporting evidence spans from the fragment text. These predictions are refined using the structure of the competency graph and aggregated at the resource level. We evaluate our approach on a dataset built from the Computer Science department's competency referential at the Université de Technologie de Compiègne (UTC), covering 22 competencies across multiple course materials. Our LLM+BM25+Graph (LBG) pipeline achieves strong results, with a micro-F1 of 0.57 and macro-F1 of 0.50 at the fragment level, 0.51 macro-F1 at the resource level, and an MRR of 0.82outperforming zero-shot and few-shot LLM variants, retrieval/similarity baselines, and supervised classifiers -while also producing more mechanically traceable evidence spans to support human auditing and educational analysis.

</details>


### [251] [ProvMind: Provenance-grounded reasoning for materials synthesis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28487)
*Yiming Zhang,Ryo Tamura,Koji Tsuda*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A new benchmark and method for reasoning about materials processing procedures from literature-derived provenance graphs, enabling better evaluation and improved out-of-distribution generalization for process reasoning tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing computational approaches to materials process optimization often oversimplify synthesis procedures as plain text or linear steps, failing to capture complex routes, conditions, tools and causal dependencies. There is a need for a benchmark that reflects the true graph-structured provenance of materials processes and for methods that can reason over such structures, especially under distribution shifts over time and material classes.

Method: The authors build MatProcBench, a benchmark grounded in MatPROV provenance graphs mined from the literature, and define seven process-reasoning tasks that test route continuity, step-level variable inference, and global causal consistency. They evaluate models under standard same-split conditions and under shift-aware settings, including a strict dual out-of-distribution (OOD) split combining temporal and material-class distribution shifts. They also propose ProvMind, a process-memory reasoning framework: it retrieves analogous processes from training data, converts them into provenance-aware compatibility scores at the option level, and then employs a language model to make constrained final decisions based on these scores.

Result: ProvMind substantially outperforms baseline methods—including prompting-only, retrieval-augmented generation, and supervised fine-tuning—on the benchmark. On the most challenging dual-OOD split of MatProcBench, ProvMind achieves an accuracy of 52.84%, which is significantly higher than competing approaches, indicating better generalization under temporal and material-class distribution shifts.

Conclusion: The work shows that provenance-grounded, graph-based benchmarks like MatProcBench are effective for assessing process reasoning in materials synthesis, and that incorporating structured process memory via ProvMind improves models' ability to reason about complex procedures, especially under distribution shift.
