This suggests that future materials optimization and automation systems should leverage provenance-aware representations and retrieval-based reasoning to achieve robust performance.

Abstract: Materials process optimization requires reasoning over routes, conditions, tools and causal dependencies, yet most computational formulations flatten synthesis procedures into text or ordered steps. We introduce MatProcBench, a provenance-grounded benchmark constructed from literature-mined MatPROV graphs, to evaluate seven process-reasoning tasks spanning route continuity, step-level variable inference and global causal consistency under both same-split and shift-aware evaluation, including a strict dual-OOD split that combines temporal and material-class shift. We further introduce ProvMind, a process-memory reasoning framework that retrieves analogous training processes, converts them into provenance-aware option-level compatibility scores, and uses a language model for constrained final decision making. ProvMind achieves 52.84\% accuracy on the dual-OOD split, outperforming prompting, retrieval-augmented and supervised fine-tuning baselines.

</details>


### [252] [Benchmarking AI for low-resource contexts: Thinking beyond leaderboards](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28508)
*Aakash Pant,Kavya Shah,Apoorv Agnihotri,Sneha Nikam,Prasaanth Balraj,Nakul Jain*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper critiques current AI evaluation practices for not reflecting performance in low-resource deployment environments and proposes a system-level, context-aware evaluation and reporting framework.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: AI models are increasingly deployed in low-resource settings where constraints like noisy data, limited connectivity, and low-end hardware strongly affect real-world usefulness. Existing benchmarks largely ignore these deployment realities, leading to misleading assessments of system performance and poor decision-making by policymakers and implementers.

Method: The authors conduct a structured analysis of existing benchmark families across speech, chat/RAG, and vision systems to identify gaps between lab-based evaluations and real deployment conditions in low-resource environments. Based on this analysis, they conceptually design an evaluation and reporting framework that treats the deployed system as the primary unit of assessment and explicitly incorporates deployment conditions and application-specific requirements.

Result: They identify critical mismatches between current benchmark practices and the needs of low-resource deployments, such as the neglect of noisy and code-switched inputs, intermittent connectivity, low-end hardware constraints, and domain shifts. They propose concrete design principles for benchmarks—system-level evaluation that integrates deployment context, application-specific evaluation profiles instead of single aggregate scores, and standardized, comparable reporting artifacts.

Conclusion: Evaluating AI for low-resource environments requires shifting from isolated model scores to context-aware, system-level assessment. Benchmarks should integrate deployment constraints, tailor evaluation profiles to application classes, and produce concise, standardized artifacts—such as one-page benchmark cards, deployment profiles, and documented failure-handling and oversight practices—to support practical, informed decisions by policymakers, donors, and implementers.

Abstract: Existing AI evaluation practices often fail to capture how systems actually perform in low-resource environments, where operational constraints shape usability as much as model quality. Through a structured analysis of existing benchmark families across speech, chat/RAG, and vision systems, we identify critical gaps between laboratory evaluation practices and real-world deployment conditions in low-resource environments. We argue that the meaningful unit of assessment is the deployed system rather than an isolated model and that effective evaluation frameworks must integrate task performance with deployment conditions such as noisy inputs, code-switching, intermittent connectivity, low-end hardware, and domain shift. At the same time, benchmarks should recognize that different application classes require distinct evaluation profiles rather than a single aggregate score that obscures operational differences. To support practical decision-making, we propose a shared reporting framework that preserves comparability across systems and application types while remaining sensitive to deployment context. Finally, we emphasize the need for concise and actionable reporting artifacts for policymakers, donors, and implementers, including standardized one-page benchmark cards, deployment profiles, and explicit documentation of failure handling procedures and human oversight mechanisms.

</details>


### [253] [GS-FUSE: Granger-Supervised Gated Fusion and Multi-Granularity Alignment for Event-Driven Financial Forecasting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28520)
*Yang Zhang,En Chun,Ziyun Mao,Yulu Wu,Jun Wang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: They propose GS-Fuse, a multimodal event-based forecasting framework that selectively uses event text only when it offers predictive value beyond historical prices, and aligns textual and price information across multiple granularities to improve financial market forecasts.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing multimodal time-series models treat text and prices symmetrically and lack mechanisms to determine when event text is actually predictive, making it hard to capture the directional, causal influence from events (text) to prices and the different roles of textual vs. price signals.

Method: They design GS-Fuse, which includes (i) a Granger-supervised, causal-aware gated fusion module that learns to open toward event text only when it adds predictive information beyond past prices, and (ii) a multi-granularity alignment mechanism that aligns both high-level event representations and low-level textual cues with future price trajectories. GS-Fuse is implemented as a plug-and-play adapter atop pre-trained large language models and time-series foundation models, enabling flexible instantiation across architectures and markets.

Result: On several real-world financial datasets, GS-Fuse consistently outperforms state-of-the-art unimodal and multimodal forecasting baselines across assets (e.g., different stocks or indices) and across varying prediction horizons, indicating better utilization of textual event information for market forecasting.

Conclusion: Causality-aware, selective fusion of event text with price history, together with multi-granular alignment between text and future prices, yields more accurate and robust financial market forecasts than conventional symmetric multimodal fusion approaches. GS-Fuse serves as a general, adaptable module that can enhance a range of backbone models and market settings.

Abstract: Accurately forecasting the impact of salient financial events on markets is critical for investors and policymakers. However, existing multimodal time-series models typically fuse text and prices symmetrically, without an explicit way to decide when event text is truly predictive, and thus struggle to exploit the directional event-to-price structure and the heterogeneous roles of textual and price signals. In this work, we propose GS-Fuse, a multimodal event-based forecasting framework that employs (i) a Granger-supervised, causal-aware gated fusion module, which learns to open toward event text only when it provides incremental predictive value beyond historical prices, and (ii) a multi-granularity alignment mechanism that jointly aligns high-level event representations and fine-grained textual cues with future market trajectories. Built as a flexible, plug-and-play adapter on top of off-the-shelf large language models and time-series foundation models, GS-Fuse can be instantiated across diverse backbones and market settings. Extensive experiments on real-world financial datasets show that GS-Fuse consistently outperforms state-of-the-art time-series and multimodal baselines across multiple assets and forecasting horizons.

</details>


### [254] [Let Relations Speak: An End-to-End LLM-GNN Soft Prompt Framework for Fraud Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28524)
*Zhixing Zuo,Huilin He,Jiasheng Wu,Dawei Cheng*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: They propose LGSPF, a soft-prompt-based LLM-GNN framework that turns multi-relational graph structures into learnable graph tokens for LLMs, enabling state-of-the-art fraud detection without relying on textual attributes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing LLM-based graph methods for fraud detection depend on rich textual node attributes, which are often absent, and they textualize graph structures with rigid, hand-crafted prompts that distort features. Moreover, fraud graphs are multi-relational and semantically complex, which current approaches fail to model deeply. A new method is needed that can leverage LLMs for fraud detection directly from graph structure, fully exploiting multi-relational information while avoiding brittle hard prompts.

Method: They design LGSPF, an LLM-GNN Soft Prompt Framework. First, they use learnable soft prompts to connect graph structural information to the semantic space of an LLM, reducing dependence on explicit text. In parallel, they incorporate a Graph Neural Network encoder that encodes multi-relational graph topology into graph tokens. These tokens are fed to the LLM via soft prompts, and the whole LLM-GNN system is trained end-to-end, aligning the GNN’s structural representation with the LLM’s semantic space for fraud understanding and prediction.

Result: On multiple benchmark datasets for fraud detection, LGSPF outperforms prior state-of-the-art methods, demonstrating superior detection accuracy. Empirical analyses further show that the framework improves the semantic interpretability of learned fraud behavior patterns, indicating that the LLM acquires a deeper, more explainable understanding of the underlying graph-based fraud signals.

Conclusion: LGSPF successfully mitigates the limitations of text-dependent, hard-prompt-based LLM approaches for fraud detection on graphs. By combining a soft-prompt interface with a GNN-based graph token encoder and optimizing them jointly, it effectively captures multi-relational structure and aligns it with LLM semantics, achieving better performance and more interpretable insights into fraud behaviors.

Abstract: In recent years, Large Language Models (LLMs) have shown great capability in processing graph tasks such as fraud detection. However, most existing methods rely heavily on rich text attributes, which poses difficulties for this domain due to the lack of textual data. Although some pioneering methods attempt to overcome it, their textualization of graph structures via hard prompts easily leads to feature distortion. Additionally, fraud detection often exhibits multi-relational complexity, where current methods struggle to capture this deep semantic information. To address these challenges, we propose LLM-GNN Soft Prompt Framework (LGSPF). Specifically, LGSPF bridges the graph structure and semantic space using soft prompt to eliminate reliance on text. We further introduce a parallel Graph Neural Network (GNN) encoder to translate multi-relational topologies into graph tokens for fine-grained LLM fraud comprehension. Through end-to-end optimization, LGSPF enhances deep semantic alignment between LLM and GNN. Experiments across diverse fraud detection benchmarks demonstrate our method achieves state-of-the-art performance. Moreover, we further validate the contribution of LGSPF on enhancing the semantic interpretability of fraud behaviors.

</details>


### [255] [Entropy-aware Masking for Masked Language Modeling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28526)
*Gokul Srinivasagan,Kai Hartung,Munir Georges*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: They propose an entropy-based token masking strategy and a self-masking method for masked language modeling that better selects informative tokens, yielding about 5% average improvement on GLUE and further gains when combined with knowledge distillation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard masked language modeling randomly masks tokens, which may not provide the most informative supervision signal for pretraining encoder-based language models. The authors want to design a masking mechanism that focuses learning on uncertain, informative tokens so that pretraining is more effective and efficient.

Method: They compute the model’s predictive entropy over tokens and use this entropy distribution to guide which tokens to mask, focusing on more uncertain and informative ones. They further introduce a self-masking approach that uses the model itself, rather than an external reference model, to determine entropy-based mask positions, and they also experiment with adding knowledge distillation on top of entropy-guided masking.

Result: Their entropy-based masking and self-masking strategies yield around a 5% average improvement on GLUE benchmark scores over a random-masking baseline. When combined with knowledge distillation, the approach achieves the strongest performance among the tested configurations.

Conclusion: Entropy-guided token selection is a more effective masking strategy than random masking for masked language model pretraining. Self-masking removes the need for an auxiliary model while still improving performance, and integrating knowledge distillation with entropy-based masking yields the best overall results, indicating that smarter masking policies can notably enhance encoder pretraining.

Abstract: Masked language modeling has become a standard pretraining objective for training encoder-based language models. In this approach, certain tokens in the input are masked, and the model learns to predict them using the surrounding context. This process enables the model to capture both syntactic and semantic properties of language. Conventionally, the tokens selected for masking are chosen at random, which may not always yield the most effective learning signals. In this work, we examine a token masking strategy based on entropy distribution. We use the model's entropy over token predictions to identify which tokens should be masked. This method aims to target tokens that are more informative and uncertain to improve the training efficacy. We also propose a novel self-masking approach that enhances training efficiency without relying on an external reference model. Experimental results demonstrate that our method achieves an average performance improvement of 5% in GLUE scores compared to the baseline. Further, we experiment with combining knowledge distillation with entropy masking, resulting in the best overall results.

</details>


### [256] [Do Agents Know What They Can't Do? Evaluating Feasibility Awareness in Tool-Using Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28532)
*Liang Cheng,Mingsheng Cai,Jiuming Jiang,Luo Mai*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: FeasiGen is a framework to automatically create and evaluate infeasible tool-using agent tasks so we can measure whether agents can detect infeasibility and stop early.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Tool-using LLM agents can be very expensive because they perform long chains of reasoning and repeated tool calls. In real-world settings tools are often missing or limited, making some tasks fundamentally infeasible. Current benchmarks and systems mostly assume tasks are solvable and do not test whether agents can recognize infeasible tasks and avoid wasting resources. The authors want a systematic way to construct infeasible tasks and evaluate agents' ability to detect them.

Method: 1) Collect successful tool-using traces from multiple agent systems on solvable tasks. 2) Analyze these traces to find "critical tools" — tools that appear consistently across different successful strategies and are necessary for success. 3) Mask or remove access to these critical tools to convert otherwise solvable tasks into infeasible ones. 4) Have humans verify that the resulting tasks truly are infeasible without those tools, achieving >94% agreement. 5) Propose new feasibility-aware metrics that check not only task success but also whether agents recognize infeasibility and terminate instead of continuing wastefully. 6) Evaluate nine different models/architectures on these metrics.

Result: The constructed dataset of infeasible tasks is validated by humans with more than 94% accurate infeasibility labels. When nine different agent models are evaluated, they show poor ability to detect infeasible tasks: up to 73.9% of the time, agents continue executing as if the task were feasible (high false-continue rate). Multi-agent system designs show a notable reduction in incorrect continued execution under infeasible conditions compared to single-agent setups.

Conclusion: FeasiGen provides a practical way to generate infeasible tasks for tool-using agents and introduces metrics that explicitly test feasibility awareness. Experimental results indicate that current agents are quite weak at recognizing when a task is impossible under the available tools and tend to waste computation by persisting. Multi-agent architectures help but are not sufficient to solve the problem, highlighting the need for better infeasibility detection and early-stopping mechanisms in future agent designs.

Abstract: Tool-using agents often incur substantial computational cost due to long reasoning chains and iterative tool usage. In practical scenarios, many tasks become infeasible under constrained tool environments, where the capabilities required for successful task completion are unavailable. Detecting infeasible tasks and stopping execution early can significantly reduce unnecessary execution cost. In this work, we propose FeasiGen, an automatic pipeline for constructing infeasible agent tasks by identifying the critical tools required for successful task completion. Our approach extracts tool-calling traces from successful executions across multiple agent systems, identifies critical tools consistently shared across diverse execution strategies, and masks these tools to automatically transform solvable tasks into infeasible ones. Human verification confirms that the infeasibility annotations for our constructed tasks achieve over 94% accuracy. We further introduce feasibility-aware evaluation metrics for measuring whether agents can recognize infeasible tasks and stop execution appropriately. Extensive evaluations across nine models reveal substantially weak infeasibility detection ability, with false continue rate reaching up to 73.9%. We further observe that multi-agent architectures significantly reduce erroneous execution under infeasible conditions.

</details>


### [257] [Cultural Binding Heads in Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28543)
*Avrile Floro,Luca Benedetto*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper finds specific attention heads in LLMs that implement “cultural binding” (linking cultural items to the right identities), shows these heads arise in pre-training, and demonstrates that scaling their influence can improve culturally differentiated responses without harming general reasoning, implying routing not knowledge is the main bottleneck.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLMs tend to respond in a culture-neutral way even when different treatment is appropriate, reflecting a lack of difference awareness. The authors want to understand mechanistically how models represent culture, why they underuse this knowledge in generation, and whether targeted interventions can make models more appropriately sensitive to cultural differences without retraining.

Method: They use mechanistic interpretability on the N4 cultural appropriation benchmark with a factorial experimental design across eight models (four architectures, base and instruct variants). They search mid-layer attention heads for those that causally contribute to “cultural binding” (associating identities with cultural items). They perform edge knockouts from identity tokens to item tokens within those heads to quantify causal impact, test transfer of identified heads from instruct to base models, conduct α-scaling interventions during generation to modulate head influence, and run a knowledge probing task to compare stored vs. used cultural knowledge.

Result: They find 2–3 mid-layer attention heads per model whose activity causally supports cultural binding, consistent across eight models. Knockout of identity-to-item edges in these heads reduces cultural binding strength by 9–23%. The same heads generalize between instruct and base variants, suggesting the mechanism is created during pre-training. α-scaling of these heads produces a graded, dose-responsive effect: moderate amplification (α = 2–3) yields 1–3 percentage point gains in cultural differentiation accuracy with minimal impact on neutral reasoning. Probing reveals models encode 3–5× more cultural knowledge than they typically express, pointing to a routing rather than knowledge limitation.

Conclusion: Cultural differentiation in LLMs is implemented, in part, by a small set of shared mid-layer attention heads that arise in pre-training and can be causally manipulated. Underuse of cultural knowledge primarily stems from how information is routed during inference, not from lack of knowledge. Mechanistic interventions like targeted head scaling can improve culturally appropriate behavior without broad side effects, offering a more precise alternative to coarse alignment or retraining approaches.

Abstract: LLMs often default to equal treatment across cultural groups, even though context warrants differentiation: this is a lack of difference awareness. Using mechanistic interpretability and a factorial design on the N4 cultural appropriation benchmark from Wang et al. (2025), we identify 2-3 mid-layer attention heads per model that contribute causally to cultural binding across eight models (four architectures, base and instruct). Cultural binding is the process of associating cultural items with the appropriate identity. Knockout of the identity-to-item edges on these heads lowers the binding strength by 9-23%. The identified heads transfer from instruct to base models, suggesting that cultural binding is created at pre-training. An $α$-scaling shows a graded dose-response and moderate amplification steering at generation ($α= 2-3$) increases cultural differentiation accuracy by 1-3 pp while leaving neutral reasoning mostly intact. A knowledge probing task shows that models know 3-5 times more than they act upon it, indicating that the bottleneck lies in routing and not knowledge.

</details>


### [258] [Refusal Before Decoding: Detecting and Exploiting Refusal Signals in Intermediate LLM Activations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28553)
*Matteo Gioele Collu,Riccardo Conte,Alberto Giaretta,Denis Kleyko,Mauro Conti,Matteo Zavatteri,Roberto Confalonieri*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper shows that large language model (LLM) refusal behavior can be reliably detected from intermediate activations using linear probes, and this signal can drive faster, competitive jailbreak prompt search.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Safety behaviors like refusals are usually analyzed only from final outputs, but if they are already encoded in intermediate activations, they could be detected or manipulated earlier and more efficiently. The authors want to know whether refusal is linearly decodable within the model and whether that information can practically guide automated adversarial prompt search.

Method: Train linear probes on the residual stream at each transformer block of LLMs to classify whether the model will refuse a prompt. Analyze where in depth refusal becomes decodable. Then integrate these probes into a modified AutoDAN procedure, called Mechanistic AutoDAN, that replaces full forward passes and loss-based fitness with partial forward passes and probe scores inside a genetic algorithm for prompt optimization. Evaluate attack success, speed, and cross-model transfer across model scales.

Result: Refusal behavior is linearly decodable from intermediate residual activations well before the final layer. Mechanistic AutoDAN, which uses probe scores instead of full-model evaluations, attains attack success rates comparable to standard AutoDAN while reducing per-iteration search time by up to 72%. In several settings, probe-guided prompts also equal or outperform AutoDAN in cross-model transfer, and the benefit of probe guidance becomes larger for bigger models.

Conclusion: Refusal is not merely an emergent output-level behavior but is encoded as an accessible, structured feature in intermediate activations of LLMs. This internal signal can be exploited to guide efficient automated jailbreak search, and its utility grows with model size, implying both new capabilities for mechanistic understanding and new attack surfaces for safety defenses to consider.

Abstract: In this paper, we investigate whether refusal behavior can be predicted from LLM intermediate activations before decoding using linear probes trained on residual stream activations at each transformer block. We find that refusal is linearly decodable well before the final layer, indicating that safety-relevant behavior is represented in intermediate activations before output generation. To test whether this signal is actionable, we introduce Mechanistic AutoDAN, a probe-guided variant of AutoDAN that replaces full-model fitness evaluation with partial forward passes and probe-based scoring inside a genetic prompt search loop. Across the evaluated models, our method achieves attack success rates competitive with vanilla AutoDAN while reducing per-iteration search time by up to 72%, and probe-guided prompts match or exceed AutoDAN's cross-model transfer in several configurations. We further find that the usefulness of probe guidance increases with model scale. Our results show that refusal is not only observable at the output level, but is encoded as a structured and actionable signal in intermediate LLM activations.

</details>


### [259] [A Matter of TASTE: Improving Coverage and Difficulty of Agent Benchmarks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28556)
*Tomer Keren,Nitay Calderon,Asaf Yehudai,Yotam Perlitz,Michal Shmueli-Scheuer,Roi Reichert*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper introduces TASTE, an automatic method to synthesize harder and more diverse tool-use benchmark tasks for AI agents by generating and evolving tool sequences first, then wrapping them into full tasks, leading to a new benchmark τ^c-Bench that reveals saturation in existing benchmarks like τ^2-Bench.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing agent benchmarks like τ^2-Bench are getting saturated as agents get stronger, but creating new, high-quality benchmarks is expensive and manual. Current benchmarks are also biased: they start from natural-language scenarios and then map to tool sequences, which covers only a limited subset of possible tool-use patterns. The authors want a scalable, automatic way to create harder benchmarks that exercise a much wider variety of tool combinations so that high benchmark scores truly reflect robust capabilities rather than overfitting or saturation.

Method: They invert the usual task design pipeline: instead of writing natural-language scenarios and deriving tool calls, they first generate tool sequences and then instantiate them as tasks. Concretely, they propose TASTE (Task Synthesis from Tool Sequence Evolution), which (1) trains an Adaptive Contrastive n-gram model on signals from an LLM that judges whether sampled tool sequences are valid; (2) uses this model to sample a broad set of valid tool sequences that richly explore the space of tool combinations; (3) clusters these sequences to select representative ones; (4) instantiates these sequences as full benchmark tasks with natural-language descriptions; and (5) iteratively evolves task difficulty to make them more challenging. The whole pipeline is automatic and designed to increase both difficulty and coverage of tool-use patterns.

Result: Using TASTE, the authors construct τ^c-Bench, an extension of τ^2-Bench across its three domains, with significantly higher difficulty and much broader tool-use coverage. When evaluating 11 agent/user LLM pairs, models that nearly saturate τ^2-Bench show large performance drops on τ^c-Bench—for example, Gemini-3-Flash falls from 0.82–0.94 accuracy on τ^2-Bench to 0.28–0.61 on τ^c-Bench. The synthesized tasks also more than double the number of unique tool combinations that agents must correctly execute.

Conclusion: The work shows that high scores on existing agent benchmarks can be misleading, reflecting benchmark saturation rather than true robustness or general tool-use competence. By automatically generating difficult, high-coverage tasks from tool-sequence space and evolving their difficulty, TASTE offers a scalable way to keep benchmarks challenging as agents improve. This enables continuous, future-proof evaluation of tool-using agents and encourages development of models that truly generalize across diverse tool-use patterns.

Abstract: As agent capabilities advance, existing benchmarks, such as $τ^2$-Bench, are becoming increasingly saturated. Yet constructing new benchmark tasks remains complex, costly, and labor-intensive. Moreover, the standard approach, in which scenarios are first written in natural language and then mapped to tool sequences, captures only a narrow subset of the tool-use patterns agents exercise. In this paper, we address these problems by reversing the task construction process. We propose TASTE: Task Synthesis from Tool Sequence Evolution, an automatic method that generates challenging tasks with broader tool-use coverage. TASTE utilizes an Adaptive Contrastive $n$-gram model trained on LLM-judged validity signals. This enables sampling valid tool sequences that cover a vast range of tool combinations. TASTE then selects representative sequences from the pool via clustering, instantiates them into complete benchmark tasks, and refines them through iterative difficulty evolution. Using TASTE, we construct $τ^c$-Bench, a challenging extension of the three domains of $τ^2$-Bench. We evaluate $11$ agent/user LLM pairs and find that models nearly saturating $τ^2$-Bench suffer severe performance drops on our tasks (e.g., Gemini-3-Flash falls from $0.82\!-\!0.94$ to $0.28\!-\!0.61$). Beyond increasing difficulty, our generated tasks more than double the number of unique tool combinations agents must execute. Our results suggest high scores on existing benchmarks often reflect saturation rather than robust task-solving ability. By automating the generation of difficult, high-coverage benchmarks, TASTE enables continuous, scalable evaluation of future agents.

</details>


### [260] [Tree of Thoughts as a Classical Heuristic Search Problem: Formal Foundations and Design Patterns](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28566)
*Guni Sharon*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper systematizes Tree-of-Thoughts (ToT) methods by reframing them as classical heuristic search over LLM reasoning steps, proposes a unified taxonomy, analyzes existing work within this framework, and highlights open challenges to guide future research.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current Tree-of-Thoughts work shows promise in improving LLM reasoning by exploring branching thought processes, but research is fragmented between NLP and planning communities, with inconsistent terminology and ad-hoc algorithms. This fragmentation hinders principled comparison, reuse of planning/search knowledge, and systematic design of better ToT methods. The authors aim to bridge this gap by grounding ToT in well-understood heuristic search concepts.

Method: The authors conceptually map LLM-based Tree-of-Thoughts reasoning onto components of classical heuristic search. They define: (1) state representation in terms of the granularity of intermediate 'thoughts', (2) successor generation as prompting-based operators that expand states into new thoughts, and (3) heuristic evaluation as LLM self-assessment of partial solutions. Using this unified vocabulary, they build a taxonomy of ToT methods and categorize existing work by search strategy (e.g., Best-First Search, Depth-First Search, Monte Carlo Tree Search), task characteristics, and lookahead depth. They then distill recurring design patterns and use these to identify algorithmic gaps and challenges.

Result: The paper provides a unified taxonomy that shows how diverse ToT approaches can be expressed as instances of classical heuristic search over LLM-generated states. Through this lens, they observe that systematic search methods such as Best-First Search tend to be used effectively for shallow, relatively deterministic tasks, while deeper, more stochastic or multi-step reasoning tasks favor lookahead-heavy approaches such as DFS variants and MCTS. The taxonomy reveals which parts of the search pipeline (state design, operators, heuristics) are most explored and which remain underdeveloped.

Conclusion: Tree-of-Thoughts can be fruitfully understood as heuristic search over LLM reasoning traces, enabling researchers to import decades of planning and search insights into LLM reasoning. The unified taxonomy clarifies the design space, exposes emerging design patterns, and highlights open challenges at the interface of heuristic search and LLM-based reasoning. The authors encourage the heuristic search community to actively engage, refine algorithms, and develop principled methods for state representations, operators, and heuristics tailored to LLMs.

Abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) have demonstrated remarkable reasoning capabilities, yet their standard generation process -- auto-regressive token prediction -- is inherently myopic and prone to cascading errors. To address this, the Tree-of-Thoughts (ToT) framework creates a search space over intermediate reasoning steps, allowing search models to explore, look ahead, and backtrack. However, current ToT research remains fragmented across Natural Language Processing and Automated Planning communities, often using inconsistent terminology and ad-hoc implementations. Consequently, we synthesize the ToT landscape through a unified taxonomy based on classical heuristic search terminology. We map LLM-based reasoning to classical search components: state representation (granularity of thoughts), successor generation (prompting operators), and heuristic evaluation (self-assessment of progress). We analyze existing work within the context of our taxonomy and identify emerging design patterns: systematic search (Best-First Search) for shallow, deterministic tasks and lookahead-heavy strategies (DFS, MCTS) for deep multi-step reasoning. We conclude by identifying open algorithmic challenges at the intersection of heuristic search and LLM reasoning, and call on the heuristic search community to engage with this emerging domain.

</details>


### [261] [A Conflict-Aware Penalty and Statistical Loss Framework for Balancing Modalities and Enhancing Stability in Multimodal Sentiment Analysis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28575)
*Jianheng Dai,Jiazhang Liang,Sijie Mai*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper tackles the imbalance in multimodal sentiment analysis where text dominates over audio and visual signals, proposing new training techniques to reduce gradient conflicts and better exploit all modalities, achieving state-of-the-art performance on CMU-MOSI.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: In multimodal sentiment analysis, powerful pre-trained text encoders overshadow weaker acoustic and visual encoders, causing optimization to focus on text while neglecting other modalities. This dominance leads to gradient norm conflicts between objectives and modalities, destabilizing training and limiting performance gains from multimodal fusion. The authors want to design a training framework that balances contributions of all modalities so that audio and visual cues are effectively used instead of being suppressed by text.

Method: They introduce two main components. (1) Conflict-aware Penalty (CP): at each training step, they monitor gradient norms across modalities and detect conflicts where a dominant modality’s gradients interfere with others. CP adds a penalty term that discourages such conflicts, preventing strong text gradients from overwhelming weaker modalities. (2) Statistical Loss (SL): they compute statistics (e.g., distribution moments) of model predictions and align them with empirical statistics of the multimodal inputs, encouraging predictions that respect the data distribution. CP is designed so that dominant modality gradients are restricted from disrupting optimization under SL. These are embedded in a unified architecture that includes adaptive modality encoding (modality-specific encoders that can adjust to input), gated cross-modal fusion (a gating mechanism to control how modalities influence each other), and unimodal auxiliary heads (per-modality prediction heads that support better unimodal representations).

Result: On the CMU-MOSI benchmark for multimodal sentiment analysis, their framework with CP and SL achieves state-of-the-art performance compared to existing methods. Ablation studies, where CP, SL, and other components are removed or modified, show that each proposed component contributes to improved accuracy and training stability, confirming their effectiveness.

Conclusion: Balancing modality contributions during training is critical for effective multimodal sentiment analysis, especially when text encoders are much stronger than acoustic and visual ones. By penalizing gradient norm conflicts and aligning prediction statistics with input statistics within a unified multimodal architecture, the proposed method stabilizes optimization, prevents text dominance, and fully leverages all modalities, resulting in superior performance on CMU-MOSI.

Abstract: Multimodal Sentiment Analysis (MSA) fuses text, acoustic, and visual streams to infer sentiment. Because pre-trained text encoders are far more expressive than their acoustic and visual counterparts, the text modality tends to dominate optimization, suppressing weaker modalities and inducing gradient norm conflicts that destabilize training. To address this, we propose a Conflict-aware Penalty (CP) that detects and penalizes gradient norm conflicts at each training step, and a Statistical Loss (SL) that aligns predicted distribution statistics with empirical input statistics. Crucially, CP prevents dominant modality gradients from interfering with the SL objective, enabling synergistic training within a unified framework incorporating adaptive modality encoding, gated cross-modal fusion, and unimodal auxiliary heads. Experiments on CMU-MOSI demonstrate state-of-the-art performance, with ablation studies confirming the effectiveness of each component.

</details>


### [262] [Continual Model Routing in Evolving Model Hubs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28577)
*Jack Bell,Giacomo Carfì,Gerlando Gramaglia,Vincenzo Lomonaco*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper defines the problem of continually routing tasks to the best models in rapidly growing AI model hubs, introduces a large benchmark (CMRBench), and proposes a new routing method (CARvE) that outperforms strong baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Model hubs host thousands of pre-trained models that can be combined into mixture-of-experts systems, but as the number of models and tasks grows, it becomes hard to (1) efficiently select the best expert among thousands, and (2) keep routing policies up to date as new models and tasks appear. There was no formal framework or benchmark to study this continual routing problem at realistic scale.

Method: 1) Formalize the setting of continually selecting models in a growing hub as Continual Model Routing (CMR). 2) Build CMRBench, a large-scale benchmark that simulates hub expansion and contains over 2,000 candidate models across families and domains. 3) Propose CARvE, a contrastive embedding-based method for routing: it learns an embedding space in which tasks and models that work well together are close, using checkpoint-based anchoring and structured replay to remain effective as new models and tasks are added.

Result: Experiments and ablation studies show that CARvE achieves substantially higher accuracy than baselines in choosing the right model, not only at the individual model level but also at the model family and domain level. It consistently outperforms zero-shot retrieval, fine-tuning-based routing, and adapter-merging strategies on CMRBench.

Conclusion: Continual Model Routing is an important emerging problem for large AI model hubs. CMRBench provides a realistic, large-scale testbed for it, and the proposed CARvE method offers an efficient and accurate solution that surpasses existing routing strategies, demonstrating the value of contrastive embeddings with checkpoint anchoring and structured replay for this setting.

Abstract: AI model hubs provide access to a rapidly growing collection of powerful pre-trained models, enabling off-the-shelf mixture-of-experts systems with different routing strategies. However, this rapid growth poses two fundamental challenges: scaling model selection across thousands of experts and continually updating routing mechanisms as new models and tasks are introduced. In this paper, we formalise this setting as Continual Model Routing (CMR) and propose CMRBench, a new large-scale benchmark simulating realistic hub expansion and including over 2,000 candidate models. Finally, we introduce CARvE, a contrastive embedding approach for efficient continual model routing via checkpoint-based anchoring and structured replay. Extensive empirical results and ablations show that CARvE significantly outperforms zero-shot retrieval, fine-tuning, and adapter-merging baselines in model, family, and domain-level accuracy.

</details>


### [263] [MUSE: Benchmarking Manufacturable, Functional, and Assemblable Text-to-CAD Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28579)
*Xiaoyu Dong,Zhi Li,Xiao-Ming Wu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: MUSE is a benchmark for evaluating Text-to-CAD systems on realistic, multi-part, engineering-grade CAD assemblies rather than simple single-part shapes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current Text-to-CAD benchmarks mostly test simple single-part models and rely on geometric similarity metrics that do not reflect real engineering needs such as functionality, manufacturability, and how parts assemble. This makes them inadequate for guiding progress toward CAD tools that are actually useful in industrial product design.

Method: The authors build MUSE, a benchmark of complex, editable B-Rep assemblies paired with structured Design Specifications. They design a three-stage evaluation protocol: (1) code check to ensure the generated CAD code executes, (2) geometric check to confirm valid geometry is produced, and (3) design-intent alignment, where models are assessed using design-specific rubrics for functionality, manufacturability, and assemblability. They then use a rubric-based visual language model (VLM) as an automated judge, and validate its reliability using human annotations. They benchmark several open- and closed-source LLM-based Text-to-CAD systems using this protocol.

Result: The experiments show a clear degradation at each stage: many models fail to produce executable code; among those that run, many produce invalid geometry; and among geometrically valid models, very few meet fine-grained engineering requirements. Even the strongest current models perform poorly on detailed engineering criteria defined in the rubrics, highlighting substantial gaps in current Text-to-CAD capabilities.

Conclusion: MUSE offers a more realistic and stringent benchmark for Text-to-CAD focused on engineering-grade assemblies and design intent, not just shape similarity. The findings indicate that current LLM-based Text-to-CAD systems are far from delivering engineering-ready designs, and that future work should target robustness across code generation, geometric validity, and higher-level design properties like functionality and manufacturability.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) have recently advanced text-driven 3D generation, yet Text-to-CAD remains far from supporting industrial product design. Existing benchmarks focus primarily on generating single-part CAD models and evaluate them using geometric similarity metrics that fail to capture functionality, manufacturability, and assemblability. To address this gap, we introduce MUSE, a Text-to-CAD benchmark focused on complex, editable boundary representation (B-Rep) assemblies. MUSE pairs practical design instances with structured Design Specifications and evaluates generated models through a three-stage protocol: code check, geometric check, and design-intent alignment. The final stage uses design-specific rubrics to assess functionality, manufacturability, and assemblability, moving beyond shape matching toward practical design quality. To enable scalable evaluation, we use a rubric-based visual language model (VLM) judge and validate its reliability through human annotation. Experiments on closed-source and open-source LLMs reveal a clear failure cascade from executable code to valid geometry and finally to engineering-ready design, with even the strongest models achieving limited success on fine-grained engineering criteria. Together, MUSE provides a realistic benchmark and evaluation framework for advancing Text-to-CAD from geometric generation toward true engineering design. Our project website, including the leaderboard, dataset, and code, is available at https://dong7313.github.io/muse-benchmark/.

</details>


### [264] [Satisfiability Solving with LLMs: A Matched-Pair Evaluation of Reasoning Capability](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28602)
*Leizhen Zhang,Shuhan Chen,Sheng Chen*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper studies how well large language models can reason about SAT problems, exposes weaknesses in standard evaluation metrics, and proposes a new paired-instance metric (ADR) along with cross-representation tests to more reliably measure true reasoning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Large language models are increasingly used on tasks that implicitly involve SAT-style reasoning, but it is unclear whether high scores on standard benchmarks reflect genuine reasoning or superficial heuristics, especially as problem size grows or representations change.

Method: The authors systematically evaluate LLMs on 2-SAT and 3-SAT, including classical SAT phase-transition regimes, and also on problems reducible to SAT (Vertex Cover and discrete 3D packing). They analyze standard metrics (accuracy, precision, recall, F1) and show their limitations, then introduce a paired-formula evaluation protocol with minimally different satisfiable/unsatisfiable instance pairs and define Accurate Differentiation Rate (ADR) that requires both instances in a pair to be correctly classified. They further study cross-representation consistency by comparing model predictions on logically equivalent CNF, Vertex Cover, and packing instances.

Result: They find that standard metrics can be misleading because many LLMs over-predict satisfiability, fail to capture the classic easy-hard-easy pattern around the 3-SAT threshold, and their performance drops sharply with more variables. ADR, in contrast, better separates models that exhibit genuine reasoning from those using shallow heuristics and correlates with the validity of produced witnesses. Additionally, most models show over 80% agreement in decisions between CNF formulas and their equivalent Vertex Cover or packing instances, indicating stable internal decision rules across representations.

Conclusion: SAT serves as a conservative but informative probe of LLM reasoning, and the proposed paired evaluation protocol with ADR is more faithful and robust across representations than conventional metrics, enabling clearer discrimination between reasoning-oriented and heuristic behavior in LLMs.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly used for tasks that implicitly reduce to Boolean satisfiability (SAT), yet their reasoning ability on SAT remains unclear. We present a systematic study of LLMs on 2-SAT and 3-SAT, together with two canonical reductions, Vertex Cover and discrete 3D packing, to probe representation-invariant reasoning. We first evaluate models using conventional metrics, including accuracy, precision, recall, and F1, as well as the SAT phase-transition setting. We find that these metrics can be misleading: many models obtain high scores by over-predicting satisfiable formulas, fail to reproduce the classical easy-hard-easy signature around the 3-SAT threshold, and degrade sharply as the number of variables grows.
  To address this problem, we introduce a paired-formula protocol based on minimally different satisfiable and unsatisfiable instances, together with Accurate Differentiation Rate (ADR), which requires both members of each pair to be classified correctly. ADR separates reasoning-oriented models from heuristic ones and correlates with witness validity. Beyond CNF, we test cross-representation consistency by converting CNF to Vertex Cover and 3-SAT to discrete 3D packing. Model decisions on CNF and on the corresponding graph or packing instances agree for most models on more than 80 percent of instances, suggesting stable decision rules across representations. Overall, our results show that SAT is a conservative probe for LLM reasoning, and that paired evaluation with ADR provides a more faithful and representation-robust assessment than conventional metrics.

</details>


### [265] [Adaptive Multimodal Agents-Based Framework for Automatic Workflow Execution](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28607)
*Susanna Cifani,Mario Luca Bernardi,Marta Cimitile*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A paper proposing a multimodal multi-agent framework that builds a topological graph of workflows from past logs and then uses RAG over this graph plus collaborative verification to robustly execute GUI-based tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing autonomous agents with MLLMs can operate GUIs but usually treat each task as an isolated linear episode, failing to learn or exploit the underlying transition structure of workflows. This makes them brittle in novel or changing environments and limits generalization and robustness when training data is scarce.

Method: The authors introduce a two-phase multimodal multi-agent pipeline. In an offline discovery phase, execution logs from GUI interactions are processed to adaptively construct a topological knowledge base represented as a graph over states and transitions. In the online inference phase, agents perform workflow execution by using Adaptive Retrieval-Augmented Generation over this fixed graph to plan and act, while a closed-loop collaborative verification protocol between multiple agents checks, corrects, and refines actions in real time. This combination allows better task decomposition and navigation through the workflow space.

Result: The framework is evaluated in a real-world setting where agents must operate information systems via GUIs under limited training data. Experiments show improved reliability, semantic awareness of the workflow, and more adaptive navigation and task decomposition compared to baselines that do not exploit topological structure or multi-agent verification.

Conclusion: Modeling workflows as a topological graph discovered from logs and using RAG-based navigation plus collaborative verification yields more reliable, semantically aware GUI agents. The approach helps bridge the gap between structured metadata parsing and open-ended environmental perception, and is particularly effective when training data is limited or environments are non-stationary.

Abstract: Modern information systems require autonomous agents capable of navigating complex workflows, yet current methodologies often struggle with the transition from structured metadata parsing to general environmental perception. While the integration of MLLMs has enabled agents to interact directly with GUIs, existing approaches typically treat task sequences as discrete, linear episodes. This fragmentation prevents agents from capturing the underlying transition topology, limiting their effectiveness in novel or non-stationary scenarios. To address this, we propose a novel multimodal multi-agent framework that achieves automatic workflow execution through a distinct two-phase pipeline. First, during an offline discovery phase, the architecture adaptively constructs a topological knowledge base from fragmented execution logs. During inference, agents leverage Adaptive Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) over this fixed, pre-established graph, coupled with a closed-loop collaborative verification protocol to dynamically self-correct and navigate. This graph-based approach facilitates superior task decomposition and adaptive navigation performance. We validate our framework in a real-world context, demonstrating its ability to maintain high reliability and semantic awareness even with limited training data.

</details>


### [266] [LACUNA: Safe Agents as Recursive Program Holes](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28617)
*Yaoyu Zhao,Yichen Xu,Oliver Bračevac,Cao Nguyen Pham,Frank Zhengqing Wu,Martin Odersky*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: LACUNA is a safe programming model that lets LLM-written code control the agent runtime while enforcing strong static checks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current LLM agents have a hard-coded runtime loop and control flow, while the LLM only emits code for individual actions. This limits expressiveness and compositionality of agents. However, letting model-written code control the runtime introduces serious safety risks, such as prompt injection, misuse of tools, and inconsistent partial states. The authors aim to close the gap between runtime and model-written code without compromising safety.

Method: They introduce LACUNA, a typed programming model where each agent action is expressed as a call agent[T](task). When execution reaches this call, the LLM generates code to implement the action. The generated code is statically type-checked against the surrounding program before execution. If the code fails type-checking, it is rejected wholesale, leaving the environment unchanged; compiler diagnostics then guide a retry. The type system constrains which tools and data may be accessed and enforces how information flows. Using this primitive, traditional agent behaviors such as ReAct-style loops, sub-agents, skills, parallel decompositions, and multi-model planning are all written as ordinary control flow in the host language.

Result: On BrowseComp-Plus, 8.6% of LLM-generated action programs are rejected prior to execution, requiring on average 0.7 retries per query, and the system achieves 27.1% task accuracy. On τ^2-bench, with a strong LLM, LACUNA solves 76.0% of 392 tasks spanning four domains, matching the performance of a baseline agent while providing stronger safety controls.

Conclusion: LACUNA demonstrates that it is possible to let LLMs write code that shapes the agent runtime while still maintaining safety via static typing and whole-action acceptance. The model unifies various agent patterns as simple control-flow constructs and attains competitive performance on standard benchmarks, suggesting that typed, check-before-run agent programming is a practical path toward more expressive yet safe LLM agents.

Abstract: LLM agents increasingly act by writing code, yet a split persists between the runtime that drives the agent and the code the model writes. The runtime owns the loop, context, and control flow, and the model has little say over any of them. Letting model-written code shape the runtime itself would make agents more expressive, but it would also sharpen safety problems. A model can be diverted by a prompt injection, call the wrong tool, or fail partway and leave an inconsistent state, and each such failure reaches further when the code shapes the runtime than when it expresses a single action. We present LACUNA, a programming model for agents that closes this split while preserving safety. Each agent action is a typed call $\texttt{agent[T](task)}$ that the LLM fills with code when execution reaches it, and the code is type-checked against the surrounding program before it runs. Because each action is accepted or rejected as a whole, a rejected one leaves the environment untouched, and its compiler diagnostics drive a retry. The same check also bounds which tools and data an action may use and how they flow. Our primitive expresses ReAct loops, sub-agents, skills, parallel decomposition, and multi-model planning as ordinary control flow. We evaluate LACUNA on a collection of test cases, BrowseComp-Plus, and $τ^2$-bench. On BrowseComp-Plus, $8.6\%$ of generations are rejected before execution, with 0.7 retries per query on average, and the agent reaches $27.1\%$ accuracy. On $τ^2$-bench, LACUNA solves $76.0\%$ of $392$ tasks across four domains with a capable model, on par with the baseline agent.

</details>


### [267] [Bandwidth-Efficient and Privacy-Preserving Edge-Cloud Many-to-Many Speech Translation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28642)
*Yexing Du,Kaiyuan Liu,Youcheng Pan,Bo Yang,Ming Liu,Bing Qin,Yang Xiang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper proposes ESRT, a collaborative edge-cloud framework for speech-to-text translation using multimodal large language models, aiming for privacy, bandwidth efficiency, and improved many-to-many multilingual performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing S2TT MLLM deployments either run fully on-device, which is constrained by compute and memory, or fully in the cloud, which poses privacy risks from transmitting raw audio and causes bandwidth bottlenecks. Additionally, most current models are biased toward English, limiting their ability to handle many-to-many translation across many languages. The authors want a system that is practical to deploy, privacy-preserving, bandwidth-efficient, and effective across many language pairs.

Method: They design ESRT, an edge-cloud split-inference architecture where a lightweight speech encoder and adapter run on the edge device, and only highly compressed intermediate representations (not raw audio) are sent to the cloud MLLM. This reduces bandwidth and mitigates voiceprint leakage. For training, they use a multi-task weighted curriculum learning strategy with data balancing to improve cross-lingual consistency and reduce English-centric bias, targeting many-to-many S2TT. They instantiate two models, ESRT-4B and ESRT-12B, based on MLLMs.

Result: Experiments on the FLEURS dataset across 45 languages (45×44 translation directions) show that ESRT-4B and ESRT-12B achieve state-of-the-art many-to-many S2TT performance. The edge-cloud design reduces bandwidth requirements by up to 10× while preserving privacy by avoiding transfer of raw speech signals. The models demonstrate strong cross-lingual performance beyond English-centric setups.

Conclusion: ESRT proves that a collaborative edge-cloud MLLM design can achieve strong many-to-many speech-to-text translation while being both privacy-preserving and bandwidth-efficient. The combination of split inference and multi-task weighted curriculum learning alleviates resource, privacy, and English-centric bias issues. The authors release code and models to encourage reproducible and privacy-aware S2TT research in the community.

Abstract: Multimodal large language models (MLLMs) have demonstrated significant potential for speech-to-text translation (S2TT). However, existing deployment paradigms face critical challenges: pure on-device models suffer from resource constraints, while centralized cloud systems incur severe privacy risks and bandwidth bottlenecks by transmitting raw voice data. Furthermore, most models exhibit English-centric biases, restricting many-to-many translation scaling. In this paper, we propose Edge-cloud Speech Recognition and Translation (ESRT), a privacy-preserving and bandwidth-efficient collaborative edge-cloud MLLM framework. Specifically, we design an edge-cloud split inference architecture that retains a lightweight speech encoder and adapter on the device, transmitting only highly compressed intermediate features to the cloud. This fundamentally prevents voiceprint leakage and reduces bandwidth requirements by up to 10$\times$. To overcome English-centric bottlenecks, we introduce a multi-task weighted curriculum learning strategy with data balancing to ensure robust cross-lingual consistency. Extensive experiments on the FLEURS dataset demonstrate that our models, ESRT-4B and ESRT-12B, achieve state-of-the-art many-to-many S2TT performance across 45 languages ($45 \times 44$ directions). Code and models are released to facilitate reproducible, privacy-aware MLLM S2TT research. The code and models are released at https://github.com/yxduir/esrt.

</details>


### [268] [The Ethics of LLM Sandbox and Persona Dynamics](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28647)
*Tim Gebbie,Stewart Gebbie*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper argues that certain LLM safety and persona practices create a harmful gap between the sanitized world models they present and the complex reality users inhabit, calling this "reality laundering."


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To critically examine current LLM guardrail and persona design practices, showing that they can unintentionally shift epistemic and practical risk onto users by presenting distorted or overly sanitized representations of reality, especially in high-stakes advice contexts.

Method: Conceptual and normative analysis using analogies to historical cases of formal risk and compliance systems (e.g., financial regulation failures) to illustrate how performative safety mechanisms can obscure real exposure; theoretical distinction between forms of refusal (harm vs. reality) and proposal of alternative design principles.

Result: The analysis identifies a recurring pattern where safety framed as moral or compliance-based leads to legible, gameable, and performative safeguards that nevertheless allow or even exacerbate underlying risks. It shows how LLM guardrails that over-sanitize outputs can function similarly, creating "safe language, distorted reality" and offloading epistemic risk to users.

Conclusion: Ethical AI practices that prioritize institutional reassurance, moralized guardrails, and safe personas over accurate engagement with real-world complexity are themselves unethical when they generate reality gaps. The authors advocate for task-level, causal requirements specification and a careful design of assistant personas that supports users' contact with reality rather than merely signaling compliance.

Abstract: It is well known that LLM guardrails and trained persona dynamics can produce a reality gap: the distance between the world a LLM is permitted or shaped to describe, and the world in which users must act. Here we argue that actively generating reality gaps is in fact unethical because it knowingly shifts epistemic risk back to the uninformed user -- this is reality laundering. This can potentially cause harm when operationalised at scale. The risk is sharpest in high-exposure advice contexts, where users seek orientation rather than a bounded, externally checkable task. Guardrails naively appear ethically necessary when they claim to prevent direct harm, but often become suspect when they suppress truthful perception and launder uncomfortable mechanisms into acceptable abstractions. Basel-style financial regulation, B-BBEE-style compliance, Societe Generale, and the London Whale show how formal safety systems can become legible, gameable, and performative while real exposure migrates elsewhere. The same pattern can appear in LLMs as moral compliance: safe language, distorted reality. We therefore distinguish refusing harm, from refusing reality; and then argue for top-down causal requirements specification at the task level rather than bottom-up moral correction at the response or sandbox level. Persona dynamics matter because the assistant interface is not neutral; it shapes how uncertainty, conflict, authority, and risk are staged. The conclusion is that so-called ``ethical AI'' becomes substantively unethical when it substitutes institutional reassurance for contact with reality.

</details>


### [269] [AutoScientists: Self-Organizing Agent Teams for Long-Running Scientific Experimentation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28655)
*Shanghua Gao,Ada Fang,Marinka Zitnik*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: AutoScientists is a decentralized team of AI agents that collaboratively run long computational experiments, improving scientific discovery speed and quality across multiple domains.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing AI research agents usually follow a single trajectory or depend on a central planner with fixed goals, which makes them bad at parallel exploration, adapting to new evidence, and remembering failed directions over long runs. The authors want a system that can scale to complex, long-term scientific experimentation where many hypotheses must be explored and coordinated efficiently.

Method: They design AutoScientists, a decentralized multi-agent system where agents share a global experimental state, form and re-form teams around hypotheses, critique each other’s experimental proposals before compute is spent, and record both successes and failures to avoid redundant work. The framework is applied uniformly across several computational science domains (biomedical ML benchmarks, LLM training optimization, protein fitness prediction) under matched compute/experiment budgets against prior AI-agent baselines.

Result: Across BioML-Bench (24 tasks), AutoScientists attains an average leaderboard percentile of 74.4%, beating the strongest competing AI agent by +8.33%. In GPT training optimization, it reaches a target validation bits-per-byte 1.9x faster than Autoresearch and continues to find 7 accepted improvements where the single-agent baseline finds none. In ProteinGym, it discovers a method for ACE2–Spike binding prediction that improves Spearman correlation by +12.5% over the SOTA model, and when applied unchanged to all 217 ProteinGym assays yields an average +6.5% improvement in Spearman over the previous SOTA.

Conclusion: A decentralized, self-organizing multi-agent framework can make AI-driven scientific research more efficient and effective than single-agent or centrally planned approaches, enabling better parallel exploration, dynamic adaptation to evidence, and reuse of negative results across long-running computational experiments in diverse scientific domains.

Abstract: Scientific research proceeds through iterative cycles of hypothesis generation, experiment design, execution, and revision. AI agents can automate parts of this process, but existing approaches typically follow a single research trajectory or coordinate through a central planner with fixed objectives. As a result, they struggle to sustain parallel exploration, adapt as experimental evidence changes, or preserve knowledge of failed directions over long-running experiments. We introduce AutoScientists, a decentralized team of AI agents for long-running computational scientific experimentation. Agents interpret a shared experimental state, self-organize into teams around promising hypotheses, critique proposals before using experimental compute, and share successes and failures to reduce redundant exploration. Under matched experimental budgets, AutoScientists improves over prior AI agents across biomedical machine learning, language-model training optimization, and protein fitness prediction. On BioML-Bench, spanning biomedical imaging, protein engineering, single-cell omics, and drug discovery, AutoScientists achieves a mean leaderboard percentile of 74.4% across 24 tasks, improving over the strongest AI agent by +8.33%. On GPT training optimization, AutoScientists reaches a target validation bits-per-byte 1.9x faster than Autoresearch and continues discovering improvements from a starting champion where the single-agent approach finds none (7 vs. 0 accepted improvements). On ProteinGym fitness prediction, AutoScientists discovers a method for ACE2-Spike binding that improves over the current state-of-the-art model by +12.5% in Spearman correlation. Applied without modification across all 217 ProteinGym assays, the same method improves over the prior state of the art by +6.5% (Spearman correlation).

</details>


### [270] [DREAM-R: Multimodal Speculative Reasoning with RL-Based Refined Drafting, Precise Verification, and Fully Parallel Execution](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28678)
*Yunhai Hu,Zining Liu,Xiangyang Yin,Tianhua Xia,Bo Bao,Eric Sather,Vithursan Thangarasa,Sai Qian Zhang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper proposes DREAM-R, a framework to make speculative reasoning in large multimodal models faster and more reliable by better aligning draft and final reasoning, stabilizing verification, and parallelizing the entire process.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Speculative reasoning can speed up reasoning-intensive generation in large multimodal models, but current approaches suffer when the speculative drafts deviate from or misalign with the target model’s verified reasoning steps, causing errors and limiting speedups. The paper aims to improve both the faithfulness and efficiency of speculative reasoning so that acceleration does not degrade reasoning quality.

Method: The authors introduce DREAM-R, centered on Speculative Alignment Policy Optimization (SAPO), a reinforcement learning objective that trains draft models to generate reasoning steps that closely follow target trajectories while remaining concise. They also design a Threshold-based Verification Mechanism (TBVM), which accepts speculative steps only when a ratio-based, positive-evidence criterion is clearly satisfied, reducing error propagation. Finally, they combine these ideas into a Fully Parallel Speculative Reasoning (FPSR) framework that parallelizes draft generation, target reasoning, and verification over multiple reasoning steps, with support for early stopping and fallback to the target model when needed.

Result: On reasoning-heavy benchmarks, DREAM-R achieves significant speedups ("up to X" times, exact number omitted in the abstract) while maintaining the accuracy of the underlying target model, indicating that the framework improves computational efficiency without harming reasoning performance.

Conclusion: DREAM-R provides an effective way to accelerate multi-step reasoning in large multimodal models via better-aligned speculative drafts, more stable and interpretable verification, and fully parallel execution, achieving substantial efficiency gains without sacrificing reasoning quality.

Abstract: Speculative reasoning has recently been proposed as a means to accelerate reasoning-intensive generation in large multimodal models, but its effectiveness is often constrained by misalignment between speculative drafts and target-verified reasoning. In this work, we introduce DREAM-R, a framework that substantially improves the performance of speculative reasoning. At its core, DREAM-R employs Speculative Alignment Policy Optimization (SAPO), a reinforcement-learning objective that trains draft models to generate reasoning steps that are both faithful to target trajectories and concise. We further propose a Threshold-based Verification Mechanism (TBVM) that uses a ratio-based criterion to provide stable and interpretable acceptance of speculative steps only when positive evidence clearly dominates, thereby preventing error propagation. Building on these components, we develop a Fully Parallel Speculative Reasoning (FPSR) framework that parallelizes draft generation, target-side reasoning, and verification across multi-step reasoning, enabling early stopping and clean fallback. Experiments on reasoning-heavy benchmarks demonstrate up to speedup while preserving target-model accuracy, yielding substantial efficiency gains without compromising reasoning quality.

</details>


### [271] [VeriTrip: A Verifiable Benchmark for Travel Planning Agents over Unstructured Web Corpora](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28683)
*Yuting Xu,Jiayi Tian,Jian Liang,Xin Xiong,Hang Zhang,Mu Xu,Xiao-Yu Zhang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: VeriTrip is a benchmark to evaluate autonomous travel planning agents on complex, multimodal, open-web reasoning instead of simple API use.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing benchmarks for travel planning agents focus on structured APIs and ignore real-world web complexity such as noisy information, conflicting facts from multiple sources, and the need to connect visual perception to logical planning. Autonomous agents are getting stronger, so evaluation must also test robustness and reliability under these conditions.

Method: The authors build VeriTrip, a verifiable benchmark that requires evidence-grounded reasoning over unstructured multimodal web data. They construct a Multimodal Retrieval Base (MRB) from real-world sources to force agents to issue and coordinate queries across heterogeneous data types. They also create a synchronized Verifiable Knowledge Base (VKB) and a cell-wise verification protocol to quantitatively measure factual reliability and to separate reasoning errors from hallucinations in model parameters.

Result: When evaluating several leading multimodal large language models (MLLMs) on VeriTrip, the authors find a strong retrieval-reasoning trade-off: as agents take on more autonomous retrieval and must manage more cognitive load, they perform worse at following instructions and maintaining plans.

Conclusion: VeriTrip offers a more realistic and rigorous benchmark for next-generation planning agents, supporting evaluation of their robustness and reliability in unconstrained, multimodal, open-web environments and highlighting retrieval-reasoning trade-offs that must be addressed in future system design.

Abstract: Existing benchmarks have laid the foundation for travel planning agents by establishing API-centric paradigms. However, as the capabilities of Autonomous Agents continue to advance, their evaluation must evolve beyond simple tool execution toward handling the inherent complexities of the open web. Current benchmarks bypass core cognitive hurdles: they fail to account for information noise, ignore multi-source factual contradictions, and overlook the necessity of grounding visual perception into logical planning. We introduce VeriTrip, a verifiable benchmark designed to meet the increasing demands for agent robustness and reliability. VeriTrip shifts the evaluation focus to evidence-grounded reasoning over unstructured multimodal web corpora. It establishes a Multimodal Retrieval Base (MRB) derived from real-world sources, forcing agents to autonomously orchestrate queries across heterogeneous data. A synchronized Verifiable Knowledge Base (VKB) enables a cell-wise verification protocol that precisely quantifies factual reliability, distinguishing systematic reasoning failures from parametric hallucinations. Our evaluations across leading MLLMs reveal a critical \textit{retrieval-reasoning trade-off}: the cognitive load of autonomous retrieval significantly erodes instruction retention. VeriTrip provides the rigorous foundation necessary for the next generation of planning agents capable of operating in unconstrained, multimodal environments.

</details>


### [272] [Beyond Binary Moral Judgment: Modeling Ethical Pluralism in AI](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28707)
*Aisha Aijaz,Rahul Goel,Arnav Batra,Raghava Mutharaju*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper proposes a probabilistic, pluralistic model of moral reasoning for AI, moving beyond binary or scalar moral labels by representing decisions as distributions over multiple normative ethical theories and subtheories.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing AI approaches to moral decision-making typically use simple scalar or binary judgments, which lack explanation, ignore contextual nuances, and fail to support accountability in socially impactful decisions. The authors aim to create a richer, theory-informed representation of moral reasoning that can capture ethical pluralism and provide interpretable justifications.

Method: They design a "normative ethics simplex" that integrates three major ethical theories (consequentialism, virtue ethics, deontology) and 15 fine-grained subtheories. They compile a benchmark of 450 natural-language ethical dilemmas with extracted contextual features. The system uses a two-stream normative-semantic architecture: one stream handles normative/contextual priors and the other semantic embeddings of the text. These are fused and fed into a sequential stacking ensemble to learn probabilistic distributions over the ethical theories and subtheories. Ablation studies compare components and architectures.

Result: Their integrated model, combining contextual and normative priors with semantic embeddings, achieves 88.89% classification accuracy on assigning cases to ethical theories and subtheories. Ablation studies show that structured ethical representations provide benefits beyond analogical, similarity-based reasoning alone, and that the stacking ensemble with gradual granularity outperforms alternatives.

Conclusion: Modeling moral reasoning as a probabilistic distribution over multiple normative theories (ethical pluralism) yields more human-like, explainable moral judgments in AI systems. The approach enables nuanced analysis of ethical disagreement using entropy, confidence, and visualization, and offers a path toward better-aligned and more accountable autonomous decision-making systems.

Abstract: Critical decision-making in socially consequential spaces is increasingly involving AI systems at varying capacities. Yet, despite the ubiquity of autonomous systems, most approaches to handling autonomous moral decision-making resort to scalar or binary judgments. These methods are insufficient for acceptable moral reasoning, as they provide little explanation, leaving out imperative contextual and theoretical information that must be included to support accountability. For this, we propose a framework to model moral reasoning as a distribution over normative ethical theories or ethical pluralism. We introduce a normative ethics simplex that integrates these theories. A benchmark of 450 cases across 15 fine-grained subtheories was also prepared for the purposes of stacked ensemble learning. These cases describe ethical dilemmas in natural language and have associated extracted contextual features. The implementation of the simplex was achieved via a two-stream normative-semantic architecture.
