This is followed by the fusion of normative information and a sequential, stacking ensemble to learn the best fit of the three broad theories: consequentialism, virtue ethics, and deontology, and the 15 subcategories. Our experiments demonstrate that the integration of contextual and normative priors with the semantic embeddings significantly improves the performance of the classification, displaying an accuracy of 88.89%. We conducted ablation studies to show that structured ethical representations contribute beyond analogical reasoning, and the chosen stacking architecture gives the best results due to the gradual learning of granularity. Ethical pluralism is also analyzed through entropy, confidence, and visualization. Thus, modeling ethical pluralism as a probabilistic normative distribution supports human-like moral reasoning, ethical disagreement analysis, and future alignment in AI systems.

</details>


### [273] [Thinking as Compression: Your Reasoning Model is Secretly a Context Compressor](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28713)
*Guoxin Ma,Yibing Liu,Chengzhengxu Li,Yu Liang,Yan Wang,Yueyang Zhang,Kecheng Chen,Zhaohan Zhang,Zhiyuan Sun,Daiting Shi*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper proposes using an LLM’s own “thinking traces” as a compressed representation of long context, enabling faster inference while preserving task-relevant information, and introduces a constrained, reward-optimized variant that significantly improves QA performance over existing compression methods at high compression ratios.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Long-context inputs to large language models are computationally expensive, so there is a need for effective context compression that speeds up inference while preserving important information. Existing approaches often require additional specialized compression modules or separate training, which adds complexity and underutilizes the LLM’s inherent ability to reason and summarize. The authors aim to explore whether the intrinsic thinking process of an LLM can itself serve as an efficient, controllable compression mechanism.

Method: They introduce Thinking as Compression (TaC), where the LLM is directly prompted to generate ‘thinking traces’—structured reasoning or notes that select and organize task-relevant information from the long context—and these traces are then used as the shortened context for downstream inference. To address issues such as uncontrolled length and shortcut behaviors in naive thinking outputs, they further propose Thinking as Compression Constrained (TaC-C), which adds a simple reward-driven optimization framework. This framework encourages intrinsic, faithful reasoning while enforcing compactness and explicit control over compression budgets, effectively shaping the model’s thinking traces into high-quality compressed contexts.

Result: On four long-context QA benchmarks, TaC-C consistently outperforms standard and state-of-the-art compression baselines. At 4x and 8x compression ratios, TaC-C improves average F1 by 17.4% and 23.4%, and average Exact Match (EM) by 15.7% and 21.7%, respectively, compared with the strongest competing method, showing that intrinsic thinking-based compression can yield both better accuracy and robustness under aggressive context reduction.

Conclusion: Thinking traces generated by LLMs can serve as powerful compressed representations of long contexts, enabling effective acceleration of long-context inference without dedicated external compressors. By constraining and optimizing these thinking traces with a simple reward-based framework, TaC-C achieves compact, controllable compression that significantly surpasses existing methods in long-context QA. This suggests that leveraging and shaping intrinsic model reasoning is a promising and simpler direction for future context compression techniques.

Abstract: Context compression aims to shorten long context inputs with minimal information loss for LLM inference acceleration. While existing methods have shown promise, they typically rely on complex compression modules or compression-specific training, leaving the intrinsic capabilities of LLMs underexplored. In contrast, this work reveals that a thinking model itself can naturally compress long contexts by organizing task-relevant information. We thus derive Thinking as Compression (TaC), a new compression paradigm that treats thinking itself as compressed context. Without relying on specific dedicated compressor, TaC directly prompts the thinking model to generate thinking traces as the shortened context, already outperforming most representative compression methods. Further, given that raw thinking output may struggle with budget control and shortcut behaviors, we introduce Thinking as Compression Constrained (TaC-C), leveraging a simple reward-driven optimization framework to elicit intrinsic thinking as compact and controllable compressed context. Experiments across four long-context QA benchmarks demonstrate that TaC-C consistently outperforms existing baselines. At 4x and 8x compression ratios, it surpasses the strongest competitor by 17.4% and 23.4% in average F1, and by 15.7% and 21.7% in average Exact Match Score (EM), respectively.

</details>


### [274] [OpenURMA: A Clean-Room Open Implementation of the Unified Bus Protocol](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28717)
*Bojie Li*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper presents OpenURMA, the first open implementation of Huawei’s Unified Bus (UB) RDMA transport, and shows large latency and throughput gains over RoCEv2/InfiniBand by changing the NIC abstraction and using CPU load/store over an on-chip bus instead of PCIe queue pairs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Modern datacenter RDMA (RoCE/InfiniBand) is increasingly bottlenecked at the NIC and PCIe interface rather than the network link. Existing designs require large per-connection state and incur expensive PCIe round-trips for small operations, significantly inflating latency and limiting scalability. Huawei’s Unified Bus (UB) proposes a different abstraction, but its reference implementation is closed silicon, preventing open evaluation and comparison.

Method: The authors re-implement UB’s transport and transaction layers in a clean-room, open-source fashion called OpenURMA. They realize this design at three tiers: (1) synthesizable RTL on an Alveo U50 FPGA, (2) a cycle-accurate two-node SystemC simulator, and (3) a gem5 full-system scaffold. For each tier, they construct a matched RoCEv2 Reliable Connection (RC) baseline, then perform controlled experiments comparing latency, throughput, and hardware resource usage for canonical RDMA operations, particularly 64-byte remote reads/fetches.

Result: For a canonical 64-byte remote fetch (UB LOAD vs RoCEv2 RC READ), the UB load/store path achieves approximately 500 ns end-to-end latency, which is 4.37× lower than the matched RoCEv2 baseline’s 2186 ns. It also sustains 2.80× higher throughput while occupying only about 14% of the LUTs on an Alveo U50 FPGA, demonstrating both performance and resource-efficiency advantages. The multi-tier setup enables fair comparison that closed commercial silicon would not allow.

Conclusion: Changing the RDMA abstraction from PCIe queue pairs with per-connection state to UB’s decoupled endpoint/transport state and opt-in ordering, combined with native CPU load/store to an on-chip-bus controller, substantially reduces latency and increases throughput for small RDMA operations. OpenURMA validates that UB’s design can be efficiently realized in open hardware and simulation frameworks and that NIC and PCIe bottlenecks, rather than the wire, dominate modern datacenter RDMA; redesigning this interface yields significant performance gains.

Abstract: Modern datacenter RDMA is bottlenecked at the network interface, not the wire. A NIC running RoCE or InfiniBand holds per-connection state for every (application, remote-endpoint) pair - hundreds of megabytes at 1024-application fanout - and pays a four-traversal PCIe round trip on a 64-byte operation, inflating latency an order of magnitude beyond the wire. Both follow from the Queue Pair over PCIe abstraction RDMA inherits from InfiniBand.
  Huawei's Unified Bus (UB), a public 2025 specification, changes the abstraction: it decouples per-application endpoint state from per-host transport state so connection context grows additively, exposes ordering as opt-in, and reaches remote memory through native CPU load/store to an on-chip-bus controller. UB ships in Huawei's closed Ascend 950 silicon.
  OpenURMA is the first clean-room open implementation of UB's transport and transaction layers, realised at three tiers - synthesisable RTL on Alveo U50, a cycle-level two-node SystemC simulator, and a gem5 full-system scaffold - each with a matched OpenRoCE (RoCEv2 RC) baseline. The contribution is the implementation, harness, and controlled comparison closed silicon does not admit. On the canonical 64-byte remote fetch - LOAD on UB-spec Sec.8.3, READ on RoCEv2 RC - UB's load/store path delivers ~500 ns end-to-end, 4.37x below the matched baseline (2186 ns), sustains 2.80x higher throughput, and fits in ~14% of a U50's LUTs.

</details>


### [275] [LiveBrowseComp: Are Search Agents Searching, or Just Verifying What They Already Know?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28721)
*HuiMing Fan,Xiao Wang,Zheng Chu,Qianyu Wang,Zhuoyao Wang,Ming Liu,Bing Qin,XingYu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper shows that current LLM-based search agents rely heavily on their pre-trained internal knowledge instead of truly discovering information via web search, and proposes a new benchmark (LiveBrowseComp) that forces evaluation on genuinely novel, recently published facts.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing search benchmarks may overestimate the real search capabilities of LLM-based agents because models can often answer questions from their internal parametric knowledge, merely using tools to verify answers. This conflates what the model already knows with what it can actually find, making it unclear whether search agents perform genuine evidence-driven discovery. The authors aim to disentangle these effects and create an evaluation setup that requires true web-based information seeking rather than memory-backed verification.

Method: The authors analyze LLM-based search agents on the BrowseComp benchmark using three diagnostics: (1) measuring how many questions agents can answer without any tools, (2) examining the origin of search queries to see whether they are driven by internally generated hypotheses versus retrieved leads, and (3) testing performance when answer-supporting evidence is removed to see if agents fall below closed-book baselines. Based on the observed limitations, they construct a new benchmark, LiveBrowseComp, consisting of 335 human-authored questions whose answers depend on facts published within 90 days prior to benchmark creation. Questions are drawn from six frequently updated sources and filtered to remove globally salient events, thus minimizing overlap with the model’s training data. They then evaluate multiple agents on this new benchmark, comparing closed-book and tool-augmented performance and observing changes in model rankings relative to BrowseComp.

Result: On BrowseComp, agents show strong Intrinsic Knowledge Dependence (IKD): up to 44.5% of questions are answered correctly without using tools; more than half of search queries originate from the model’s internally generated hypotheses rather than from retrieved evidence; and when answer-supporting evidence is removed, search-augmented performance can be worse than closed-book baselines, indicating reliance on internal memory rather than external validation. On the new LiveBrowseComp benchmark, closed-book accuracy for all evaluated agents drops below 2%, confirming that the questions are largely outside intrinsic coverage. Even with tool augmentation, agent performance declines by 25–40 points compared to BrowseComp, and the previous ranking of models no longer reliably predicts their performance on LiveBrowseComp, highlighting that prior benchmarks did not truly measure search capability.

Conclusion: LLM-based search agents often exhibit strong dependence on intrinsic, pre-trained knowledge, using web tools mainly to verify or lightly support what they already know instead of conducting genuine, evidence-driven search. Static benchmarks like BrowseComp can therefore give a misleadingly optimistic view of search capabilities by rewarding memory-backed verification. The proposed LiveBrowseComp benchmark, built from very recent, non-salient information, better isolates and tests real-time search and reasoning skills beyond a model’s internal knowledge. The results show substantial performance drops and altered model rankings, underscoring the need for such temporally fresh, dynamically grounded benchmarks when evaluating web-augmented LLM agents.

Abstract: Are LLM-based search agents genuinely searching, or using the web to verify what they already know? We study this question on BrowseComp with three diagnostics. Our analysis reveals Intrinsic Knowledge Dependence (IKD): even with tool access, agents often rely on intrinsic knowledge -- information encoded in the model before retrieval -- rather than on external evidence. Agents answer up to 44.5% of BrowseComp questions without tools, generate more than half of their search queries from internally produced hypotheses rather than retrieved leads, and perform worse than closed-book baselines when answer-supporting evidence is removed. These results suggest that static search benchmarks can reward memory-backed verification rather than evidence-driven discovery, conflating what agents already know with what they can find. We then introduce LiveBrowseComp, a deep-search benchmark designed to evaluate agents beyond intrinsic coverage. It contains 335 human-authored questions whose answers depend on facts published within the 90 days preceding benchmark construction, drawn from six updated sources and filtered to exclude globally salient events. On LiveBrowseComp, all evaluated agents fall below 2% closed-book accuracy, search-augmented scores drop by 25-40 points relative to BrowseComp, and prior model rankings no longer reliably predict performance. LiveBrowseComp is available at https://huggingface.co/datasets/Forival/LiveBrowseComp.

</details>


### [276] [Multi-Adapter Representation Interventions via Energy Calibration](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28722)
*Manjiang Yu,Hongji Li,Junwei Chen,Xue Li,Priyanka Singh,Yang Cao,Lijie Hu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper proposes MARI, a multi-adapter, energy-gated representation intervention method that adaptively adjusts both direction and strength of activation edits per input, improving alignment benchmarks while preserving or improving general capabilities.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing representation intervention techniques for aligning large language models typically apply a single, fixed intervention direction and strength to all inputs. The authors observe that the optimal intervention is highly sample-dependent: different inputs need different directions and magnitudes of representation edits, and many benign inputs should not be intervened on at all. Uniform interventions therefore hurt general capabilities and are suboptimal for alignment. The motivation is to design a more flexible, input-adaptive intervention mechanism that can capture non-linear correction patterns and avoid harming benign behavior.

Method: They introduce MARI (Multi-Adapter Representation Interventions via Energy Calibration). Instead of a single linear edit, MARI uses a competitive pool of specialized adapter experts that operate on internal representations. For each input, the system learns which expert to use and what intervention vector/magnitude to apply, enabling non-linear, sample-specific corrections. Additionally, MARI incorporates an energy-based gating module that inspects internal propagation dynamics (e.g., representation energies) to decide whether an input should receive any intervention at all. The approach is trained to optimize alignment objectives while leaving benign behavior unchanged as much as possible.

Result: Across multiple LLM families and sizes, MARI achieves state-of-the-art performance on alignment-related benchmarks. It significantly boosts scores on TruthfulQA, BBQ, and safety evaluation suites, indicating better truthfulness, reduced social bias, and safer outputs. At the same time, unlike prior representation interventions that often degrade base capabilities, MARI maintains or even improves performance on standard capability benchmarks like MMLU and ARC.

Conclusion: Adaptive, multi-expert representation interventions with energy-based gating can align large language models more effectively than uniform, single-direction edits. By tailoring both direction and strength of interventions per input and selectively applying them only when appropriate, MARI achieves better safety and truthfulness while preserving or enhancing general task performance. This suggests that fine-grained, input-dependent control of internal representations is a powerful and practical direction for LLM alignment without finetuning model weights.

Abstract: Representation intervention has emerged as a promising paradigm for aligning large language models toward desired behaviors without modifying model weights. Existing methods typically apply a fixed intervention uniformly across all inputs. However, we find that the appropriate intervention direction and strength vary substantially across samples, and such indiscriminate intervention leads to degradation of general capabilities on benign inputs. To address these challenges, we propose Multi-Adapter Representation Interventions via Energy Calibration (MARI). Specifically, we introduce a competitive multi-adapter mechanism in which specialized experts capture non-linear correction patterns and adaptively determine the appropriate intervention direction and strength for different samples. Furthermore, we design an energy-based gating module that leverages internal propagation dynamics to distinguish inputs that are applicable for intervention. Extensive experiments across diverse model families and parameter scales demonstrate that MARI achieves state-of-the-art alignment performance. Our method significantly improves performance on TruthfulQA, BBQ, and safety benchmarks, while maintaining and even improving general capabilities on tasks such as MMLU and ARC. Our code is available at https://github.com/V1centNevwake/MARI.

</details>


### [277] [AlphaTransit: Learning to Design City-scale Transit Routes](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28730)
*Bibek Poudel,Sai Swaminathan,Weizi Li*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper presents AlphaTransit, a planning framework that combines Monte Carlo Tree Search with a neural policy-value network to design high-quality city-scale bus networks under delayed-feedback constraints, achieving better service rates than reinforcement learning alone or search alone on a realistic benchmark.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Transit Route Network Design requires making many sequential route extension decisions whose true quality is only revealed once the whole network is built. Local improvements can degrade global performance through transfer bottlenecks, overlap, or reduced throughput. Existing approaches struggle with this delayed, deceptive feedback and with efficiently exploring the large space of possible route configurations for realistic city-scale networks.

Method: The authors propose AlphaTransit, which treats route network design as a search-based planning problem. They couple Monte Carlo Tree Search with a neural policy-value network: the policy network proposes promising route extensions and the value network predicts the long-term quality of partial designs. MCTS then uses these learned priors and value estimates to guide lookahead search, avoiding the need to run full simulator rollouts in the tree. The system is evaluated on a new Bloomington TRNDP benchmark with realistic road topology and demand patterns under different demand scenarios.

Result: On the Bloomington benchmark, AlphaTransit achieves the highest service rate among compared methods in both mixed and full transit demand settings, obtaining 54.6% and 82.1% service rates, respectively. Compared to pure reinforcement learning (without search), AlphaTransit improves service rate by 9.9% and 11.4%. Compared to pure MCTS without learned guidance, it gains 2.5% and 11.2%. These empirical results show clear performance benefits from combining learning and search.

Conclusion: Coupling a neural policy-value network with Monte Carlo Tree Search provides more effective decision-time guidance for transit route network design than using either learned policies or uninformed search alone. This integrated approach addresses the delayed-feedback and deceptive interaction challenges in TRNDP and yields superior service rates on a realistic city-scale benchmark. The released code and data enable further research on learning-and-search methods for transit planning.

Abstract: Designing a transit network requires many sequential route extension decisions, but their quality is often visible only after the full network is assembled. This delayed-feedback challenge lies at the heart of the Transit Route Network Design Problem (TRNDP), where route interactions can be deceptive: an extension that appears useful locally can create transfer bottlenecks, produce redundant overlap, or reduce overall throughput. To guide route construction under delayed simulator feedback, we introduce AlphaTransit, a search-based planning framework for cityscale bus network design. AlphaTransit couples Monte Carlo Tree Search (MCTS) with a neural policy-value network: the policy proposes route extensions, the value estimates downstream design quality, and search uses these predictions to refine each decision. This provides decision-time lookahead during route construction without running simulator rollouts inside the search tree. We evaluate AlphaTransit on a new Bloomington TRNDP benchmark with realistic road topology and censusderived demand, under mixed and full transit demand settings. In the Bloomington network, AlphaTransit attains the highest service rate in both demand settings, reaching 54.6% and 82.1%, respectively. Relative to reinforcement learning without search, these correspond to 9.9% and 11.4% service rate gains; relative to MCTS without learned guidance, they correspond to 2.5% and 11.2% gains. These results suggest that coupling learned guidance with MCTS is more effective than using either approach alone for transit network design. Our code and data are publicly available in https://github.com/poudel-bibek/AlphaTransit.

</details>


### [278] [Utility-Aware Multimodal Contrastive Learning for Product Image Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28733)
*Xiaohang Feng,Yiling Xie*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper introduces a utility-aware multimodal contrastive learning framework that directly incorporates consumer demand into generative image models for online marketplaces, improving both demand and semantic fidelity of generated product images.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current multimodal generative models focus on text-image semantic alignment but ignore marketplace performance, i.e., whether generated images actually increase product demand. Semantic alignment alone does not ensure that images will sell, creating a gap between technical optimization and commercial objectives in e-commerce platforms like Amazon and Airbnb.

Method: The authors propose a utility-aware multimodal contrastive learning framework built around a new Utility-Aware InfoNCE loss. This loss incorporates measures of consumer demand into the contrastive learning objective, shifting the learned joint image-text representation space toward visual cues associated with higher demand. They provide a theoretical analysis showing how the objective biases representations toward demand-relevant features and then plug this representation into generative and image-editing pipelines for product imagery.

Result: On real-world datasets from Amazon and Airbnb, product images generated or edited using the proposed framework outperform state-of-the-art baselines in increasing demand (e.g., higher click-throughs or bookings) while preserving visual fidelity and maintaining strong text-image consistency. The method also replicates realistic inverse U-shaped demand relationships for attributes like aesthetics and uniqueness, and human-subject experiments confirm that the generated images are commercially more effective.

Conclusion: Incorporating consumer demand directly into the contrastive learning objective enables generative models to produce product images that are not only semantically aligned with text prompts but also more effective at driving marketplace performance. The proposed utility-aware component is flexible and can be integrated into future generative architectures to enhance their direct commercial value while preserving fidelity and semantic coherence.

Abstract: Product images strongly influence consumer decision-making in online marketplaces. Empowered by multimodal contrastive learning, generative AI can output images that closely align with text prompts. Yet existing generative AI models do not directly optimize marketplace performance. This is a critical gap, since semantic alignment alone does not guarantee that an image will sell. To address this limitation, we propose a \textit{utility-aware multimodal contrastive learning} framework that incorporates consumer demand into a novel Utility-Aware InfoNCE loss. Optimizing this utility-aware objective guides generation toward images that are both semantically coherent and demand-enhancing. This effect arises directly from a shift in the learned image-text representation space toward demand-driven visual cues, which we also validate through the theoretical bound of the proposed objective. In downstream applications on Amazon and Airbnb, product images generated and edited by our method outperform state-of-the-art models in increasing demand and preserving fidelity, while maintaining text-image consistency. Notably, our utility-aware framework preserves inverse U-shaped demand patterns for attributes such as aesthetics and uniqueness, improving demand-based performance while preserving fidelity and semantic consistency. Human-subject experiments further validate its commercial effectiveness. As generative AI technology continues to evolve, our utility-aware component can be flexibly embedded into emerging generative models to improve direct commercial use.

</details>


### [279] [CORE: Contrastive Reflection Enables Rapid Improvements in Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28742)
*Linas Nasvytis,Simon Jerome Han,Ben Prystawski,Satchel Grant,Noah D. Goodman,Judith E. Fan*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper introduces Contrastive Reflection (CORE), a non-parametric algorithm that accelerates language-model self-improvement on reasoning tasks by extracting natural-language insights contrasting successful vs unsuccessful reasoning traces, achieving faster gains with fewer rollouts and tokens than prior methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing methods for improving language models on reasoning tasks using verifiable rewards—both parametric (weight updates via RL) and non-parametric (prompt optimization, memory, episodic retrieval)—are sample- and compute-inefficient, often needing hundreds of training samples and thousands of rollouts. This makes them expensive and sometimes intractable, especially under tight rollout or context constraints. The authors seek a way to leverage information already present in reasoning traces more efficiently, in a form that is interpretable and compact, and that works with very small amounts of supervision.

Method: They propose Contrastive Reflection (CORE), a non-parametric learning algorithm that operates over past reasoning traces. CORE compares trajectories from successful and unsuccessful problem attempts to extract “insights”: short natural-language descriptions of reasoning strategies, constraints, and patterns that distinguish good from bad reasoning. These insights are then fed back into the model (e.g., via the prompt) to guide future reasoning. CORE is evaluated on four reasoning tasks and compared against parametric methods like GRPO and non-parametric baselines such as GEPA, episodic RAG, and MemRL, under controlled rollout budgets and few-shot training regimes.

Result: Across four reasoning benchmarks, CORE improves model performance more rapidly than both parametric (GRPO) and non-parametric (GEPA, episodic RAG, MemRL) baselines, while using fewer model rollouts. With fixed, small rollout budgets and as few as five training samples, CORE matches or surpasses the performance gains of these baselines. It also uses context more efficiently, achieving these gains with fewer additional prompt tokens by storing learned information as concise, reusable insights rather than large collections of past traces.

Conclusion: Distilling the differences between successful and unsuccessful reasoning traces into abstract natural-language insights provides an efficient and interpretable pathway for language-model self-improvement. Compared with updating model weights, performing direct prompt optimization, or reusing large sets of stored traces, CORE’s insight-based approach is both rollout-efficient and context-efficient, suggesting that contrastive, natural-language reflections are a powerful mechanism for enhancing reasoning abilities under tight computational and data budgets.

Abstract: Language models can use verifiable rewards to improve at a wide variety of reasoning tasks. However, both parametric (e.g. RLVR) and non-parametric (e.g. prompt optimization) approaches to doing so typically require hundreds of training samples and thousands of model rollouts, making them expensive in the best case and intractable in the worst. To address this challenge, we introduce Contrastive Reflection (CORE), a non-parametric learning algorithm that compares past reasoning traces to generate insights: short natural-language descriptions of reasoning strategies and constraints that capture differences between successful and unsuccessful problem attempts. Across four reasoning tasks, we demonstrate that CORE enables more rapid improvement than both parametric (GRPO) and non-parametric (GEPA, episodic RAG, and MemRL) methods, while using fewer rollouts. Under fixed rollout budgets with as few as five training samples, we then show that CORE also achieves comparable or greater performance gains than each baseline. Finally, we highlight how CORE is also substantially more context-efficient than non-parametric baselines, requiring fewer prompt tokens while storing learned knowledge as compact, interpretable natural-language insights. Our results therefore suggest that distilling contrasts between successful and unsuccessful reasoning traces into abstract and useful insights can provide a more efficient and interpretable route to model self-improvement than weight updates, prompt optimization, or direct reuse of stored reasoning traces.

</details>


### [280] [CubePart: An Open-Vocabulary Part-Controllable 3D Generator](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28763)
*Yiheng Zhu,Kangle Deng,Jean-Philippe Fauconnier,Inaki Navarro,Daiqing Li,Ava Pun,Yinan Zhang,Peiye Zhuang,Xiaoxia Sun,Maneesh Agrawala,Kiran Bhat,Tinghui Zhou*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: CubePart is a generative framework for creating 3D meshes with explicit, controllable semantic parts from text and a user-defined part schema.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing generative 3D models mainly output single, monolithic meshes or arbitrary part decompositions, which do not match the structured, semantic parts needed for animation, physics, and scripting in games and simulations. There is a gap between generative 3D content and application-specific requirements for part-aware assets.

Method: CubePart takes a global text prompt plus a user-defined list of part names (an open-vocabulary parts schema) as input. It uses a scalable data pipeline to build a large 3D dataset with open-vocabulary part labels, and a two-stage generative architecture: first synthesizing the global object shape, then decoding individual parts, producing one mesh per schema element that fit together into a coherent object.

Result: Given arbitrary text prompts and part schemas, CubePart generates sets of coherent 3D part meshes that respect the specified semantic structure. The generated assets can be directly used in game engines and linked to animation and behavior scripts without requiring manual cleanup.

Conclusion: CubePart enables open-vocabulary, part-controllable 3D mesh generation by making part structure an explicit control signal at inference time, supported by a large part-labeled dataset and a two-stage architecture. This bridges the gap between 3D generative models and practical needs in interactive applications like games and simulations.

Abstract: Interactive 3D assets used in games and simulation are typically decomposed into specific semantic parts to support animation, physics, and scripted behaviors, yet most generative 3D models produce either monolithic meshes or arbitrary part decompositions that cannot be aligned with application-specific requirements. We present CubePart, a generative framework for open-vocabulary, part-controllable 3D mesh generation that exposes part structure as an explicit inference-time control signal. Given a global text prompt and a user-defined parts schema expressed as an open-ended list of part names, our method generates a set of meshes - one per schema element - that assemble into a coherent object while respecting the specified semantic structure. To enable this capability, we introduce a scalable data pipeline to construct a large open-vocabulary, part-labeled 3D dataset, along with a two-stage generative architecture that separates global shape synthesis from part-level decoding. We demonstrate that the resulting assets can be directly integrated into game engines and driven by animation and behavior scripts without manual post-processing. Project Page: https://cubepart.github.io/

</details>


### [281] [SwarmHarness: Skill-Based Task Routing via Decentralized Incentive-Aligned AI Agent Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28764)
*Edwin Jose*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: SwarmHarness is a decentralized protocol that turns scattered, idle compute resources into an incentive-aligned, self-organizing compute swarm.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Large amounts of potentially useful compute across personal GPUs, idle inference servers, and edge devices remain unused because there is no protocol that lets owners share this compute in a way that is safe, profitable, and does not depend on a trusted central party or heavy blockchain infrastructure. Existing systems either rely on centralized coordination, require complex blockchain stacks, or lack a native incentive layer, which limits adoption and robustness.

Method: The paper introduces SwarmHarness, a fully decentralized protocol composed of three core parts: (1) SwarmRegistry, a peer discovery and capability advertisement layer built on a Distributed Hash Table (DHT); (2) SwarmRouter, which assigns tasks to nodes using a utility function that balances capability, load, latency, and trust; and (3) SwarmCredit, an incentive system where nodes earn and spend compute credits, with rewards allocated based on an approximation to the Shapley value to fairly attribute contributions. The protocol design encourages nodes to both contribute compute and specialise in high-value skills, while discouraging free-riding through credit depletion and reduced routing priority.

Result: SwarmHarness yields a decentralized, self-regulating compute-sharing network where contributing nodes are rewarded with credits and free-riding is disincentivized. The routing and incentive dynamics lead nodes to specialise and coordinate in a way that resembles swarm intelligence: nodes gravitate toward high-reward tasks and routing signals guide workloads much like digital pheromones. The system thus achieves emergent organisation and robust task allocation without central control.

Conclusion: SwarmHarness demonstrates that a combination of DHT-based discovery, utility-driven routing, and Shapley-inspired incentives can turn disparate, idle compute resources into a coherent, self-organizing swarm. This design not only addresses the incentive and coordination gaps in existing compute-sharing approaches but also provides a foundational building block for future autonomous distributed AI agent networks, where agents can autonomously hire compute, delegate subtasks, and settle credits without human oversight or centralized infrastructure.

Abstract: Vast quantities of compute (GPU cycles on personal workstations, idle inference servers, and edge devices between jobs) go unused because no incentive-aligned protocol exists for their owners to share them safely and profitably. Existing approaches either require a trusted central coordinator (cloud marketplaces), demand heavy blockchain infrastructure (Golem, BrokerChain), or lack an incentive layer entirely (BOINC, Petals). We propose SwarmHarness, a decentralised protocol in which HarnessAPI skill nodes self-organise into a compute swarm without any central authority. SwarmHarness has three interlocking components: a SwarmRegistry built on a Distributed Hash Table (DHT) for peer discovery and capability advertisement; a SwarmRouter that dispatches tasks to nodes using a utility function over capability, load, latency, and trust; and SwarmCredit, an incentive mechanism that attributes compute-credit rewards to contributing nodes via a Shapley-value approximation. Nodes earn credits by serving tasks and spend credits to submit them; idle nodes that never contribute drain credits and lose routing priority, creating a self-regulating participation economy. As nodes specialise toward high-reward skills and routing signals act as digital pheromones, the network exhibits emergent collective intelligence analogous to biological swarms. Beyond compute sharing, SwarmHarness is a foundational primitive for autonomous distributed AI agent networks in which agents hire compute, route subtasks, and settle credits without human intermediation.

</details>


### [282] [Calibrating Conservatism for Scalable Oversight](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28807)
*William Overman,Mohsen Bayati*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper proposes Calibrated Collective Oversight (CCO), a method for keeping powerful, agentic AI systems under reliable human control by aggregating multiple oversight signals and calibrating their conservatism with statistical guarantees.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: As AI systems become more agentic, autonomously planning and acting over long horizons, humans struggle to maintain meaningful oversight, especially when the AI may surpass human capabilities. Existing scalable oversight approaches are often ad hoc, rely on strong assumptions, or lack clear statistical guarantees in sequential decision settings. There is a need for a principled method that allows weaker human or automated overseers to reliably constrain stronger agents while providing guarantees about the frequency of undesired outcomes.

Method: The authors introduce Calibrated Collective Oversight (CCO), which combines multiple auxiliary scoring functions—representing different overseer concerns—into a single penalty that measures deviation from a conservative baseline policy. Drawing on Attainable Utility Preservation, actions incur penalties proportional to how concerning they are across the collective of overseers, allowing beneficial actions when they are not widely objected to and overriding them when concern accumulates. The degree of conservatism is adapted online using Conformal Decision Theory, producing finite-time, distribution-free guarantees that the rate of undesirable outcomes will not exceed a user-chosen target threshold. The method is tested on modified SWE-bench and the MACHIAVELLI environment.

Result: On a modified SWE-bench benchmark, the authors show that weaker overseers can successfully restrict an adversarially misaligned but stronger agent using CCO. In the MACHIAVELLI environment, CCO substantially reduces ethical violations while maintaining much of the original reward. Across both domains, the empirical rates of violations are close to the user-specified target thresholds, aligning with the theoretical guarantees of the method.

Conclusion: CCO offers a principled, statistically calibrated approach to scalable oversight of powerful agentic AI systems. By aggregating diverse oversight signals into a conservative penalty and adapting its strength via conformal methods, it enables weaker overseers to constrain stronger agents while providing finite-time guarantees on the frequency of undesirable outcomes. The experiments suggest that CCO can meaningfully reduce harmful or unethical behavior without excessively sacrificing performance, with empirical behavior matching the desired safety targets.

Abstract: Agentic AI systems capable of autonomous planning and extended environmental interaction pose a fundamental control problem: how can humans maintain meaningful oversight of systems that may exceed their own capabilities? Existing approaches to scalable oversight rely on complex assumptions, remain largely heuristic, or lack practical methods for sequential settings with statistical guarantees. We introduce Calibrated Collective Oversight (CCO), which aggregates diverse auxiliary scoring functions into a penalty measuring deviation from a conservative baseline. Inspired by Attainable Utility Preservation, CCO enables collective conservatism: actions face a penalty proportional to overseer concern, so high-utility actions are still selected when overseers find them unobjectionable and overridden only when concern accumulates. CCO calibrates this conservatism online using Conformal Decision Theory, ensuring that undesirable outcomes remain below a user-specified target threshold with finite-time bounds and no distributional assumptions. On a modified version of SWE-bench, weaker overseers successfully constrain an adversarially misaligned stronger agent; on MACHIAVELLI, CCO substantially reduces ethical violations while preserving reward. In both settings, empirical violation rates closely match the specified targets, as predicted by the theory.

</details>
